definition: A person is free if and only if (i) they are not subject to physical coercion, (ii) a range of options is available to them, and (iii) they are able to choose among those options.
conditions: not subject to physical coercion
range of options available
able to choose among options
counterexample: A man is not physically coerced, has a wide range of options, and is able to choose among them, but his preferences have been systematically manipulated through propaganda so that he invariably chooses what the state desires. The three conditions are met, yet he is not free in any meaningful sense.
missing_condition: The person's preferences and deliberative faculties must not be the product of manipulation or indoctrination.
passage: It is commonly held that a person is free if and only if he is not subject to physical coercion, a range of options is available to him, and he is able to choose among those options. This definition, however, proves insufficient. Consider a citizen of an authoritarian state who is subjected from birth to pervasive propaganda. He is never physically coerced; indeed, he moves about as he pleases. A range of options is available to him — he may choose his occupation, his residence, his associates. And he is perfectly able to choose among these options. Yet his preferences have been so thoroughly shaped by propaganda that he invariably chooses what the state desires him to choose, and he does so with every appearance of spontaneity. The three conditions are met in full. Yet one feels strongly that this man is not free, for his choices, though uncoerced and unconstrained, are not genuinely his own. The definition has failed to account for the possibility that freedom requires not merely the absence of external constraint but the integrity of the deliberative faculties by which choices are made.

definition: A political system is democratic if and only if (i) political decisions are made by majority vote, (ii) all adult citizens have the right to vote, and (iii) elections are held at regular intervals.
conditions: decisions by majority vote
universal adult suffrage
regular elections
counterexample: A state holds regular elections in which all adults may vote and decisions are made by majority rule, but only one party is permitted to stand for election. The three conditions are satisfied, yet the system is not genuinely democratic.
missing_condition: There must be genuine competition among candidates or parties, so that the electorate faces a real choice.
passage: One might propose that a political system is democratic if and only if political decisions are made by majority vote, all adult citizens have the right to vote, and elections are held at regular intervals. Yet this analysis is too permissive. Consider a state in which elections are held every four years with perfect regularity, in which every adult citizen is entitled to vote, and in which all decisions are taken by majority rule. However, only one political party is permitted to stand for election; all others are banned. The citizens dutifully cast their votes, and the ruling party wins, as it must, by an overwhelming majority. The three conditions are satisfied — there is majority voting, universal suffrage, and regular elections — yet no one would call this arrangement democratic. What is missing is the element of genuine choice: democracy requires not merely the mechanism of voting but the availability of alternatives among which the electorate may choose.

definition: A government is legitimate if and only if (i) it has the consent of the governed, (ii) it rules according to law, and (iii) it protects the fundamental rights of its citizens.
conditions: consent of the governed
rules according to law
protects fundamental rights
counterexample: A colonial government obtains the nominal consent of a subject people through a plebiscite, rules meticulously according to its own laws, and protects those rights it chooses to recognise. Yet the consent was obtained under conditions of ignorance and duress, and we should doubt the government's legitimacy.
missing_condition: Consent must be informed and uncoerced, not merely formally expressed.
passage: The view that a government is legitimate if and only if it has the consent of the governed, rules according to law, and protects the fundamental rights of its citizens is attractive in its simplicity. Yet it proves insufficient upon examination. Imagine a colonial power which, upon conquering a territory, conducts a plebiscite among the inhabitants. The people, unfamiliar with the political alternatives and overawed by military force, vote to accept the colonial government. The government then rules meticulously according to its own legal code and protects those rights which it has chosen to enumerate. The three conditions appear to be met: there is consent (expressed by plebiscite), rule of law (the government follows its own statutes), and protection of rights (such as they are). Yet we should be reluctant to call this government legitimate, for the consent was obtained under conditions of ignorance and implicit coercion, and the rights protected are defined by the conqueror rather than by the conquered. The definition has failed to require that consent be genuinely informed and freely given.

definition: A person has authority over others if and only if (i) they occupy a recognised institutional position, (ii) they have the power to issue directives, and (iii) those subject to their directives generally comply.
conditions: occupies recognised position
power to issue directives
general compliance
counterexample: A gangster occupies a recognised position within his criminal organisation, issues directives that are generally obeyed, yet we should not say he possesses legitimate authority.
missing_condition: The institutional position must be constituted by morally acceptable rules, not merely by effective power.
passage: It may be suggested that a person has authority over others if and only if he occupies a recognised institutional position, he has the power to issue directives, and those subject to his directives generally comply. But this analysis is vulnerable to the following case. Consider the head of a criminal organisation. He occupies a recognised position within the organisation — his subordinates acknowledge him as their leader. He has the power to issue directives — his word is, within his domain, law. And those subject to his directives generally comply, for the consequences of disobedience are severe. The three conditions are satisfied, yet we should not say that the gangster has authority in any morally significant sense. He has power, certainly, and his power is effective, but authority requires something more: that the institutional position be constituted by morally acceptable rules, and that compliance be grounded in recognition of the legitimacy of those rules rather than in mere fear of punishment.

definition: Citizens are treated equally if and only if (i) the same laws apply to all, (ii) no person receives preferential treatment from the state, and (iii) all have equal access to public institutions.
conditions: same laws apply to all
no preferential treatment
equal access to public institutions
counterexample: A law prohibiting sleeping under bridges applies equally to the rich and the poor, no one receives preferential treatment, and all have equal access to courts. Yet the law burdens only the poor, for only they have occasion to sleep under bridges.
missing_condition: Equality requires attention to the differential impact of formally equal rules upon persons in different circumstances.
passage: One is inclined to say that citizens are treated equally if and only if the same laws apply to all, no person receives preferential treatment from the state, and all have equal access to public institutions. Yet this purely formal conception of equality proves insufficient. Consider a law which prohibits sleeping beneath the bridges of Paris. This law applies to all citizens without distinction: the rich man and the poor man are alike forbidden to sleep under bridges. No one receives preferential treatment; the law is enforced impartially. And all citizens have equal access to the courts should they wish to challenge the law. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet there is something deeply unequal about the arrangement, for the law burdens only the poor, since only they have occasion to sleep under bridges. The rich man's formal equality with the poor man is, in this instance, a mockery. The definition has failed to account for the substantive dimension of equality: that formally identical treatment may produce grossly unequal effects when applied to persons in materially different circumstances.

definition: An event constitutes a political revolution if and only if (i) the existing government is overthrown, (ii) the transfer of power is effected by force or the credible threat of force, and (iii) the new regime institutes a fundamentally different political order.
conditions: existing government overthrown
transfer effected by force or credible threat
new regime institutes fundamentally different order
counterexample: In 1688 the English Parliament invited William of Orange to assume the throne. James II fled without battle. The government was overthrown, force was credibly threatened by William's fleet and army, and the resulting constitutional settlement was fundamentally different from the Stuart absolutism it replaced. The three conditions are met, yet the event is universally called the 'Glorious Revolution' precisely because it lacked the popular uprising and violent rupture we ordinarily associate with revolution.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require popular participation or mass mobilisation; a revolution may be distinguished from a mere coup or palace intrigue by the involvement of the populace at large.
passage: It is commonly held that an event constitutes a political revolution if and only if the existing government is overthrown, the transfer of power is effected by force or the credible threat of force, and the new regime institutes a fundamentally different political order. This analysis captures the salient features of the French Revolution of 1789 and the Russian Revolution of 1917, yet it proves insufficiently discriminating when applied to certain other cases. Consider the events of 1688 in England. James II was effectively deposed; William of Orange arrived with a substantial fleet and army, and the threat of force was entirely credible; and the constitutional settlement that followed transformed the balance of power between Crown and Parliament in ways that may fairly be called fundamental. The three conditions are thus satisfied. Yet the event bore almost none of the marks we customarily associate with revolution: there was no popular insurrection, no barricades, no mass mobilisation. It was, rather, an affair conducted by a narrow political elite. One is led to suspect that the definition has omitted a condition of popular participation — that a genuine revolution must involve, in some substantial degree, the active involvement of the people whose political order is being transformed.

definition: An event constitutes a political revolution if and only if (i) it results in a change of the ruling group, (ii) the change is sudden rather than gradual, and (iii) it is motivated by an ideological vision of a better society.
conditions: change of ruling group
change is sudden
motivated by ideological vision
counterexample: A military junta seizes power from a civilian government in a single night, installing a new ruling group. The change is sudden, and the junta publicly declares its vision of national renewal and social order. The three conditions are met. Yet a military coup undertaken principally for the personal enrichment and power of a handful of officers is not what we mean by a revolution, even if the officers profess an ideological programme.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the change involve a transformation of the political and social structure, rather than a mere substitution of one ruling clique for another.
passage: One might propose that an event constitutes a political revolution if and only if it results in a change of the ruling group, the change is sudden rather than gradual, and it is motivated by an ideological vision of a better society. This formulation has initial plausibility, yet it admits of a case which tells against it. Consider a military coup in which a junta of senior officers removes the civilian president in the space of a single night. A new ruling group is installed; the change is as sudden as one could wish; and the junta issues a proclamation setting forth its vision of national renewal, social discipline, and modernisation. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet one should hesitate to call this event a revolution. The political structure remains essentially unchanged — the same parliament sits, the same laws are enforced, the same classes retain their privileges. Only the personnel at the summit have been exchanged. It appears that the definition has conflated two things: the replacement of one governing faction by another, which is a mere coup d'état, and the transformation of the political and social order itself, which is the distinguishing mark of a genuine revolution.

definition: A state practises toleration if and only if (i) it refrains from suppressing practices of which it disapproves, (ii) it recognises the liberty of its subjects to hold divergent views, and (iii) its forbearance is a matter of settled policy rather than mere neglect.
conditions: refrains from suppressing disapproved practices
recognises liberty of divergent views
forbearance is settled policy not neglect
counterexample: A weak state lacks the administrative apparatus to suppress a religious minority, though the government openly declares its wish to do so. It refrains from suppression, it permits divergent views to persist, and its forbearance is in practice consistent and long-standing, thus appearing to be settled policy. The three conditions are met, yet the state does not tolerate the minority; it merely lacks the power to persecute.
missing_condition: Toleration requires that the forbearance proceed from a principled acceptance of diversity, not from incapacity or indifference.
passage: It is sometimes maintained that a state practises toleration if and only if it refrains from suppressing practices of which it disapproves, it recognises the liberty of its subjects to hold divergent views, and its forbearance is a matter of settled policy rather than mere neglect. Yet this analysis may be shown to be insufficient by the following case. Imagine a state whose government is openly hostile to a particular religious sect. The rulers declare, in public pronouncements and in law, that the sect is pernicious and ought to be suppressed. But the state is weak: its police force is small, its administrative apparatus rudimentary, and it simply lacks the power to enforce its hostility. The sect flourishes unmolested. Over time, the pattern of forbearance becomes so regular that it acquires the appearance of settled policy. The three conditions are nominally satisfied: the state refrains from suppression, divergent views persist, and the forbearance is consistent. Yet one would not say that this state practises toleration in any meaningful sense. Its restraint proceeds not from any recognition of the value of liberty or diversity, but from sheer incapacity. The definition, it appears, has failed to distinguish between toleration as a principled posture and toleration as an accidental consequence of weakness.

definition: A person is a political representative if and only if (i) they have been chosen by a group of citizens, (ii) they act on their behalf in a legislative or deliberative body, and (iii) the citizens have the power to remove them at the next election.
conditions: chosen by group of citizens
acts on their behalf in legislative body
citizens can remove them at next election
counterexample: A member of an hereditary House of Lords has not been chosen by citizens, yet he acts on behalf of the nation in a legislative body, and his position is not subject to electoral removal. Despite the failure of conditions (i) and (iii), he is customarily regarded as a representative of the realm in the constitutional sense. Conversely, a delegate chosen by citizens, acting in a legislature, and subject to removal, who votes exclusively according to his personal convictions and never consults his constituents, satisfies all three conditions yet seems to represent no one but himself.
missing_condition: Genuine representation requires some form of responsiveness or accountability to those represented, not merely formal selection and eligibility for removal.
passage: One might propose that a person is a political representative if and only if he has been chosen by a group of citizens, he acts on their behalf in a legislative or deliberative body, and the citizens have the power to remove him at the next election. This definition seems natural enough, yet it admits of troublesome cases in both directions. On the one hand, consider a member of an hereditary second chamber. He has not been chosen by any group of citizens; he sits by right of birth. And the citizens cannot remove him by any electoral mechanism. Yet he participates in the legislative process and is customarily described as representing certain interests of the nation. On the other hand, consider a delegate who has been duly elected and who may be removed at the next election, yet who votes exclusively according to his own philosophical convictions, never consults his constituents, and disregards their wishes entirely. The three conditions are satisfied in full: he was chosen, he acts in the legislature, and he is removable. Yet we should hesitate to say that he represents his constituents in any but the most formal sense. The definition, it appears, has captured the procedural trappings of representation whilst missing its substance — which is, one suspects, some form of genuine responsiveness to the interests or wishes of those on whose behalf the representative purports to act.

definition: A person is a citizen if and only if (i) they owe allegiance to a particular state, (ii) they are entitled to the protection of that state's laws, and (iii) they have the right to participate in its political processes.
conditions: owes allegiance to state
entitled to state's legal protection
has right to participate in political processes
counterexample: A permanent resident alien owes certain duties to the state in which he resides, is entitled to the protection of its laws, and in some jurisdictions may vote in local elections. All three conditions are substantially met, yet he is not a citizen. He is, by the state's own classification, an alien.
missing_condition: Citizenship requires a formal recognition of membership in the political community, typically conferred by birth or naturalisation, which is distinct from the mere possession of rights and duties within the state.
passage: A natural analysis of citizenship holds that a person is a citizen if and only if he owes allegiance to a particular state, is entitled to the protection of that state's laws, and has the right to participate in its political processes. Yet this definition proves too broad. Consider a permanent resident alien — a person who has lived in a country for many years, pays its taxes, obeys its laws, and in that sense owes it a form of allegiance. He is entitled, under the law, to the same protections as any native: he may bring suit, he may not be deprived of property without due process, and he enjoys the protections of the criminal law. In certain jurisdictions he may even vote in local elections, thereby participating in the political process. The three conditions are substantially met. Yet the state itself does not regard him as a citizen, and he himself may feel no identification with the political community. The definition has confused the possession of certain rights and duties that happen to accompany citizenship with the status of citizenship itself. What is missing, it seems, is a formal condition of recognised membership — conferred by birth or by a deliberate act of naturalisation — which constitutes the person as a member of the body politic.

definition: A citizen is under a political obligation to obey a law if and only if (i) the law was enacted by a legitimate authority, (ii) the citizen has received benefits from the legal order, and (iii) they consented, at least tacitly, to be governed.
conditions: law enacted by legitimate authority
citizen has received benefits from legal order
citizen consented tacitly to be governed
counterexample: A person born and raised in a state has received the benefits of public roads, education, and police protection. The state is governed by a legitimate authority, and the citizen has never emigrated, which is often taken as tacit consent. The three conditions are met. Yet if the law in question commands him to inform on his neighbours for private political opinions, one feels that no obligation to obey arises, notwithstanding the satisfaction of the three conditions.
missing_condition: The definition omits a substantive moral constraint on the content of the law: political obligation does not extend to laws that violate fundamental moral principles.
passage: One familiar account holds that a citizen is under a political obligation to obey a law if and only if the law was enacted by a legitimate authority, the citizen has received benefits from the legal order, and he consented, at least tacitly, to be governed. This analysis appears to capture the principal grounds of political obligation. Yet consider the following case. A citizen has lived his entire life within a well-ordered state. He has received the benefits of public education, the protection of the police, and the use of public roads and hospitals. The government is legitimate by any recognised standard. And by remaining within the state, enjoying its benefits and participating in its institutions, he has tacitly consented to be governed. The three conditions are satisfied. Now suppose the state enacts a law requiring citizens to report their neighbours' private political opinions to the authorities. One feels strongly that the citizen is not obligated to obey this law, despite the satisfaction of the three conditions. The definition, it appears, has treated political obligation as arising entirely from the procedural and reciprocal features of the relationship between citizen and state, whilst omitting a substantive moral constraint upon the content of the laws to which obligation attaches. No amount of legitimate authority, benefit, or consent can generate an obligation to obey a law that commands a grave moral wrong.

definition: A distribution of resources is just if and only if (i) it arises from a procedure to which all participants could agree, (ii) no participant is made worse off than they would be in the absence of the arrangement, and (iii) inequalities benefit the least advantaged members.
conditions: arises from procedure to which all could agree
no participant worse off than without arrangement
inequalities benefit least advantaged
counterexample: A society distributes resources according to a procedure all could agree to, no one is made worse off than in the state of nature, and the inequalities that arise do benefit the poorest members. Yet the distribution was achieved by exploiting the natural resources of a neighbouring people without their consent. The three conditions regarding the internal distribution are satisfied, but the distribution is unjust because the wealth upon which it rests was unjustly acquired.
missing_condition: The definition addresses only the pattern and procedure of distribution, not the justice of the original acquisition of the resources distributed.
passage: It is sometimes proposed that a distribution of resources is just if and only if it arises from a procedure to which all participants could agree, no participant is made worse off than he would be in the absence of the arrangement, and inequalities benefit the least advantaged members. This analysis has considerable appeal, yet it may be challenged by the following case. Suppose a society distributes its wealth according to a procedure which all its members could rationally endorse. No member is worse off than he would be without the cooperative arrangement, and the inequalities that arise do in fact benefit the poorest. The three conditions are fully satisfied. Yet suppose further that the wealth of this society was acquired by the systematic exploitation of a neighbouring people — by seizing their lands, extracting their minerals, and appropriating the fruits of their labour, all without consent or compensation. The internal distribution satisfies the stated conditions, yet one feels that the arrangement is profoundly unjust, for the resources being distributed were themselves unjustly obtained. The definition, it appears, has attended only to the pattern and procedure of distribution and has neglected entirely the question of whether the goods distributed were justly acquired in the first instance.

definition: An act of suppression constitutes censorship if and only if (i) a public authority prevents or restricts the dissemination of expression, (ii) the restriction is imposed prior to or independently of any judicial finding of wrongdoing, and (iii) the purpose of the restriction is to prevent the expression from reaching its intended audience.
conditions: public authority prevents or restricts dissemination
restriction imposed independently of judicial finding
purpose is to prevent expression reaching audience
counterexample: A large private corporation that controls the principal channels of public communication removes certain political writings from its platforms, preventing them from reaching their audience. No public authority is involved, and no judicial proceeding has occurred. The first condition is not met, since the suppressor is a private body, not a public authority. Yet the effect on the writer and his audience is identical to that of state censorship.
missing_condition: The definition restricts censorship to acts by public authorities and fails to account for the capacity of powerful private entities to suppress expression with effects equivalent to state censorship.
passage: One might define censorship as follows: an act of suppression constitutes censorship if and only if a public authority prevents or restricts the dissemination of expression, the restriction is imposed prior to or independently of any judicial finding of wrongdoing, and the purpose of the restriction is to prevent the expression from reaching its intended audience. This analysis captures the paradigm cases of censorship — the state banning a book, the censor excising passages from a play before performance, the government suppressing a newspaper. Yet consider the following case. A large private corporation controls the principal means by which writings are disseminated to the public. This corporation, acting on its own commercial or ideological motives, removes certain political writings from its platforms, ensuring that they cannot reach their intended audience. No public authority has acted; no judicial proceeding has occurred. The first condition fails, for the suppressor is not a public body. Yet the effect upon the author and his readers is precisely the same as if the state had banned the work. The definition, it appears, has identified censorship exclusively with the actions of public authorities, and in so doing has failed to accommodate the possibility that private entities, when they wield sufficient power over the channels of expression, may exercise a censorship no less real for being unofficial.

definition: A government is tyrannical if and only if (i) power is concentrated in the hands of a single ruler or a small group, (ii) the rulers govern without the consent of the governed, and (iii) the rulers govern in their own interest rather than the common good.
conditions: power concentrated in single ruler or small group
govern without consent of governed
govern in own interest not common good
counterexample: A democratically elected majority, governing with the formal consent of the people and dispersing power across multiple institutions, enacts laws that systematically oppress a racial or religious minority for the benefit of the majority. Power is not concentrated, consent has been given, and the majority may sincerely believe it acts for the common good. The three conditions fail, yet the oppressed minority experiences the arrangement as tyranny.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the tyranny of the majority, in which democratic institutions operating with popular consent may nonetheless inflict systematic oppression upon a vulnerable group.
passage: It has been maintained that a government is tyrannical if and only if power is concentrated in the hands of a single ruler or a small group, the rulers govern without the consent of the governed, and the rulers govern in their own interest rather than the common good. This definition captures the classical conception of tyranny admirably. Yet it fails to accommodate a case which ought to give us pause. Suppose a democratic state in which power is dispersed across multiple institutions, elections are free and fair, and the government acts with the consent of the majority. The majority, however, enacts laws that systematically deprive a racial or religious minority of its rights: its members are excluded from certain professions, confined to particular districts, and subjected to humiliating restrictions. The majority sincerely believes it acts for the common good. None of the three conditions is satisfied — power is dispersed, consent is present, and the majority perceives itself as governing for the general welfare. Yet the minority experiences this regime as tyrannical in the most concrete sense. The definition, it appears, has conceived of tyranny too narrowly, identifying it with the concentration of power in a few hands and overlooking the possibility that a democratic majority, acting through perfectly regular institutions, may oppress a minority no less effectively than any despot.

definition: A group has a right to secede from a state if and only if (i) the group occupies a distinct and contiguous territory, (ii) the group possesses a shared identity that distinguishes it from the rest of the state, and (iii) the group's members have expressed, by plebiscite or equivalent means, a clear desire to form a separate state.
conditions: occupies distinct contiguous territory
possesses shared distinguishing identity
expressed clear desire by plebiscite
counterexample: A wealthy region occupies a distinct territory, shares a cultural and linguistic identity, and votes overwhelmingly in a plebiscite for independence. The three conditions are met. Yet the region's motive for secession is entirely to avoid sharing its mineral wealth with the poorer regions of the state, and its departure would reduce the remaining population to destitution. One may doubt whether a right of secession arises in such circumstances.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that secession not impose a grave injustice upon those who remain; the right to secede may be constrained by duties of distributive fairness owed to fellow members of the existing state.
passage: One might hold that a group has a right to secede from a state if and only if it occupies a distinct and contiguous territory, possesses a shared identity distinguishing it from the rest of the state, and has expressed, by plebiscite or equivalent means, a clear desire to form a separate state. This analysis has a certain liberal appeal. Yet consider the following case. A coastal region of a large state is exceptionally wealthy, owing to its mineral deposits. It has a distinct culture and language, and a plebiscite reveals that ninety per cent of its inhabitants wish to secede. The three conditions are satisfied. But the region's wealth was developed in part through infrastructure financed by the central government and through markets sustained by the larger state, and its secession would deprive the interior provinces — which are already poor — of revenues upon which they depend for basic services. One hesitates to say that a right of secession arises in these circumstances, for the exercise of that right would impose a grave injustice upon millions of people who had no say in the matter. The definition, it appears, attends only to the claims of the seceding group and ignores the interests and claims of those who would be left behind. A right of secession, if it exists, may be constrained by duties of distributive fairness to fellow members of the existing political community.

definition: A state discharges its obligation of welfare if and only if (i) it provides material assistance to those unable to provide for themselves, (ii) the assistance is sufficient to meet basic needs, and (iii) the assistance is available to all who qualify without arbitrary discrimination.
conditions: provides material assistance to those unable to provide for themselves
assistance sufficient for basic needs
available to all who qualify without arbitrary discrimination
counterexample: A state provides generous material assistance to all who qualify, sufficient to meet basic needs and free from discrimination. However, the system is administered in so degrading a manner — requiring public declarations of destitution, compulsory inspections of private dwellings, and humiliating interrogations — that recipients are reduced to a condition of shame and dependence. The three conditions are met, yet the state has not adequately discharged its obligation of welfare.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that welfare be administered in a manner consistent with the dignity of the recipient.
passage: It is often supposed that a state discharges its obligation of welfare if and only if it provides material assistance to those unable to provide for themselves, the assistance is sufficient to meet basic needs, and the assistance is available to all who qualify without arbitrary discrimination. This analysis, though it identifies three important conditions, may be shown to be insufficient. Consider a state which provides generous assistance to every citizen who meets the qualifying conditions. The assistance is ample: it covers food, shelter, and medical care. There is no discrimination. Yet the system of administration is designed in such a way as to humiliate the recipient. He must make a public declaration of his destitution at a government office. His dwelling is subject to compulsory inspection at any hour. He is interrogated about his expenditures and must justify every purchase. The three conditions are satisfied in full. Yet one feels that the state has not genuinely discharged its obligation of welfare, for it has provided material aid at the cost of the recipient's dignity. The definition, it appears, has attended only to the quantum and distribution of assistance and has overlooked the manner in which assistance is provided — a matter which is, one suspects, no less important than the assistance itself.

definition: A state respects minority rights if and only if (i) it refrains from persecuting members of minority groups, (ii) it accords them equal treatment under the law, and (iii) it permits them to practise their customs and traditions.
conditions: refrains from persecuting minority members
accords equal treatment under law
permits practice of customs and traditions
counterexample: A state neither persecutes a linguistic minority nor treats them unequally under the law, and it permits them to speak their language at home. However, the state conducts all public education, all official proceedings, and all public signage exclusively in the majority language, and offers no support for the minority language. Over a generation the minority language falls into disuse and the minority's distinctive culture is effectively extinguished. The three conditions are met, yet the minority's rights have not been genuinely respected.
missing_condition: Mere non-interference and formal equality may be insufficient; the state may be required to take positive measures to sustain the conditions under which minority culture can survive.
passage: One might hold that a state respects minority rights if and only if it refrains from persecuting members of minority groups, accords them equal treatment under the law, and permits them to practise their customs and traditions. This negative or non-interference conception has considerable intuitive force, yet it admits of a troublesome case. Consider a state in which a linguistic minority constitutes a small fraction of the population. The state does not persecute this group; its members enjoy the same legal rights as every other citizen; and they are permitted to speak their language and observe their customs at home. However, the state conducts all public education exclusively in the majority language, all official proceedings are in the majority language, and no public resources are devoted to the preservation or transmission of the minority tongue. Over the course of a generation, the minority language falls into disuse, and with it the customs and traditions that depended upon it. The three conditions are met throughout this process: there is no persecution, no legal inequality, and no prohibition of minority practices. Yet the minority's distinctive culture has been effectively extinguished by the sheer weight of majority institutions. The definition, it appears, has conceived of rights too narrowly as rights against interference, failing to recognise that the survival of a minority culture may require positive measures of support.

definition: A state S is a democracy if and only if (i) the adult citizens of S periodically elect their rulers, (ii) more than one candidate stands for each office, and (iii) the elected rulers enact laws reflecting the preferences of the majority.
conditions: the adult citizens periodically elect their rulers
more than one candidate stands for each office
the elected rulers enact laws reflecting the preferences of the majority
counterexample: Consider a state in which elections are held regularly, multiple candidates contest every office, and the winning candidates faithfully legislate according to the majority's expressed wishes. Yet the electorate comprises only male property-owners above a certain income, amounting to perhaps eight per cent of the adult population. Women, labourers, and the poor are altogether excluded from the franchise. The state satisfies all three conditions, yet one would hesitate to call it a democracy in any robust sense, for the vast preponderance of adult inhabitants have no share in the selection of their rulers.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the franchise be extended to all or nearly all adult members of the political community. A state in which a narrow propertied class elects rulers and those rulers serve that class's preferences satisfies the stated conditions yet excludes the majority of the population from political participation.
passage: One might propose that a state is a democracy if and only if its adult citizens periodically elect their rulers, more than one candidate stands for each office, and the elected rulers enact laws reflecting the preferences of the majority. This formulation appears to capture the essential features of democratic governance: popular election, genuine choice among candidates, and responsiveness to the will of the greater number. It would seem to distinguish democracies from monarchies, aristocracies, and tyrannies in a tolerably precise fashion. Yet one must consider a state of the following description. Elections are held at regular intervals, and at each election several candidates present themselves for every office. The victorious candidates then proceed to legislate in strict accordance with the wishes of those who elected them. The majority is faithfully served. But the electorate itself is narrowly circumscribed: only male property-holders above a stipulated income may vote, and this class constitutes no more than eight per cent of the adult population. Women are excluded, as are labourers, tenant farmers, and all those beneath the property qualification. The state satisfies every condition of the definition, yet to call it a democracy seems gravely misleading. The difficulty is that the definition speaks of citizens electing rulers and of the majority's preferences being enacted, but it places no constraint upon who counts as a citizen or how broad the franchise must be. A definition that permits a small oligarchy to satisfy the conditions of democracy has, it would appear, failed to capture something essential about the concept it purports to analyse.

definition: A person P is free if and only if (i) no other person physically restrains P, (ii) no law of the state prohibits P's actions, and (iii) P is not subject to imprisonment or detention.
conditions: no other person physically restrains P
no law of the state prohibits P's actions
P is not subject to imprisonment or detention
counterexample: Consider a person who is neither restrained, nor subject to any legal prohibition, nor imprisoned, yet who lives in a community where powerful landowners have purchased every available plot of land and every source of employment. He may go where he pleases, for no one physically prevents him; no statute forbids his movements; and he is not detained. Yet he cannot obtain food, shelter, or livelihood without submitting to whatever terms the landowners dictate. His liberty, though formally complete, is substantively hollow, for the conditions of his material existence place him under a dependence scarcely distinguishable from servitude.
missing_condition: The definition attends only to the absence of physical and legal coercion, neglecting the role of material conditions and economic dependence. A person who is formally uncoerced but who lacks the material means to exercise meaningful choice is not free in any substantive sense.
passage: It might be supposed that a person is free if and only if no other person physically restrains him, no law of the state prohibits his actions, and he is not subject to imprisonment or detention. This definition captures what many writers have regarded as the core of liberty: the absence of external interference and legal compulsion. A person who walks about without chains, who faces no statutory prohibitions, and who is not confined to a gaol is, on this account, a free man. The conditions appear jointly sufficient. But consider a man in the following circumstances. He inhabits a district in which a small number of landowners have acquired every parcel of land, every mill, every source of employment. No law compels him to work for these landowners; no one lays hands upon him; he is not imprisoned. He may walk the roads at his leisure. Yet if he wishes to eat, to have a roof, to clothe himself against the weather, he must accept whatever terms the landowners offer, for there is no alternative source of sustenance. He cannot refuse, not because the law forbids refusal, but because refusal means starvation. His formal freedom is unimpaired, yet his actual capacity for independent action is negligible. The definition, by attending solely to physical restraint, legal prohibition, and imprisonment, has overlooked the possibility that material dependence may constrain a man's liberty as effectively as any chain or statute. What is wanted is a conception of freedom that takes account not merely of the absence of coercion but of the presence of genuine alternatives, and no definition that ignores the conditions of material life can be adequate to this purpose.

definition: Two citizens A and B are equal before the law if and only if (i) the same statutes apply to both A and B, (ii) A and B are subject to the same judicial procedures, and (iii) the penalties prescribed for identical offences are the same for A and B.
conditions: the same statutes apply to both
both are subject to the same judicial procedures
the penalties prescribed for identical offences are the same
counterexample: Consider two citizens who are formally subject to the same statutes, the same judicial procedures, and the same penalties. Yet one is wealthy and can retain the most accomplished barristers, engage investigators, and postpone proceedings indefinitely through procedural motions, whilst the other is indigent and must rely upon whatever counsel the state provides, often an overworked solicitor with scant time for preparation. The law on the books treats them identically, but the practical operation of the legal system yields systematically different outcomes. Both satisfy all three conditions, yet their equality before the law is, in substance, illusory.
missing_condition: The definition addresses only formal legal equality — identical statutes, procedures, and penalties — but neglects the requirement that citizens possess roughly comparable means of availing themselves of the law's protections. Without some measure of substantive access, formal equality may coexist with systematic practical inequality.
passage: One might define equality before the law as obtaining between two citizens when the same statutes apply to both, both are subject to the same judicial procedures, and the penalties prescribed for identical offences are the same for each. This formulation is attractive in its clarity and captures a principle that has been central to liberal political thought since at least the eighteenth century. It excludes systems in which nobles are tried by different courts than commoners, or in which different punishments attach to the same offence depending upon the offender's station. So far, so good. Yet consider two citizens of a state in which these formal conditions are perfectly satisfied. One is a woman of considerable wealth; the other possesses almost nothing. The wealthy citizen retains accomplished barristers, commissions expert witnesses, and employs procedural devices to delay and complicate any proceeding brought against her. The indigent citizen, by contrast, must accept whatever representation the state assigns, and that representation is frequently perfunctory. The statutes that apply to each are identical; the procedures are formally the same; the penalties on the books are uniform. All three conditions are met. But the practical likelihood that each will receive a just outcome differs enormously. The wealthy citizen's resources afford her advantages that the indigent citizen cannot match, and these advantages are not incidental but systematic. The definition has captured the form of legal equality whilst omitting its substance. What seems to be wanted is not merely that the same rules apply, but that citizens possess a roughly comparable capacity to invoke those rules effectively.

definition: A person or body X has political authority over a population P if and only if (i) X issues commands to P, (ii) P generally obeys those commands, and (iii) X possesses the capacity to enforce compliance through coercion.
conditions: X issues commands to P
P generally obeys those commands
X possesses the capacity to enforce compliance through coercion
counterexample: Consider a brigand who occupies a mountain pass and commands all travellers to surrender a portion of their goods. The travellers generally comply, for the brigand has armed men at his disposal and can enforce his demands. He issues commands; the population of travellers obeys; and he possesses coercive capacity. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the brigand does not possess political authority in any sense that a philosopher of politics ought to recognise. His power is mere domination, and his commands carry no moral weight or claim to legitimacy.
missing_condition: The definition omits any requirement that X's commands be regarded as legitimate or that P's obedience rest upon something more than mere fear of coercion. Political authority, properly understood, involves a claim to the right to command, not merely the power to compel.
passage: Suppose one defines political authority as follows: a person or body X has political authority over a population P if and only if X issues commands to P, P generally obeys those commands, and X possesses the capacity to enforce compliance through coercion. This definition appears to identify the salient features of political authority. Governments issue commands in the form of laws, citizens generally obey, and the state maintains police and military forces to compel the recalcitrant. The three conditions seem to describe the phenomenon adequately. But consider a brigand who establishes himself at a mountain pass. He commands every traveller who approaches to surrender one-third of his goods. The travellers, seeing his armed retainers, comply without resistance. The brigand issues commands; the travellers obey; and the brigand possesses ample coercive capacity. Every condition of the definition is met. Yet it would be most peculiar to say that the brigand has political authority over the travellers. His power is naked domination; his commands carry no claim to rightness; and the obedience he secures rests upon nothing but the travellers' rational fear of violence. What distinguishes the authority of a legitimate government from the power of a brigand is not merely the fact of command, obedience, and coercion, but the presence of a normative element — a claim, however contested, that the commands ought to be obeyed, and that the obedience rendered is not merely prudent but in some sense obligatory. The definition, by confining itself to descriptive conditions, has failed to distinguish authority from mere power.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G came to power through established constitutional procedures, (ii) G governs in accordance with the existing body of law, and (iii) G maintains public order within its territory.
conditions: G came to power through established constitutional procedures
G governs in accordance with the existing body of law
G maintains public order within its territory
counterexample: Consider a government that ascended to power through the constitutional procedures established by a previous regime — a regime that had itself enacted a constitution conferring supreme authority upon a single party. The government governs strictly in accordance with the body of law bequeathed by that constitution, which includes provisions permitting the indefinite detention of dissidents and the prohibition of rival political associations. Public order is maintained with great efficiency. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the government's legitimacy is, to say the least, doubtful, for the constitutional procedures and laws upon which it relies are themselves instruments of oppression.
missing_condition: The definition requires only conformity with established procedures and laws but places no constraint upon the justice or moral acceptability of those procedures and laws themselves. A government may satisfy purely procedural criteria of legitimacy whilst governing under a constitution that is fundamentally unjust.
passage: It might be proposed that a government is legitimate if and only if it came to power through established constitutional procedures, it governs in accordance with the existing body of law, and it maintains public order within its territory. This definition has the merit of precision and of grounding legitimacy in observable institutional facts rather than in contestable moral judgements. A government that seizes power by force, that rules by decree in defiance of the law, or that permits the collapse of civil order would fail to satisfy the conditions, and one might reasonably deny its legitimacy on those grounds. Yet consider the following case. A previous regime enacted a constitution that conferred supreme power upon a single party and that authorised the suppression of all rival organisations. Under this constitution, a new government comes to power through the prescribed procedures — there is an orderly succession within the ruling party, exactly as the constitution stipulates. The new government governs strictly in accordance with the existing body of law, a body of law that includes provisions permitting the detention of political opponents without trial and the censorship of the press. Public order is maintained with great efficiency; there are no disturbances. Every condition of the definition is satisfied. But one might reasonably ask whether a government whose constitutional foundation is itself an instrument of tyranny can be called legitimate merely because it adheres to the procedures that tyranny has laid down. The definition, by attending only to procedural regularity and the maintenance of order, has neglected the possibility that the procedures and laws themselves may stand in need of justification.

definition: A state S is sovereign if and only if (i) S exercises supreme legislative authority within its territory, (ii) no external power dictates the laws of S, and (iii) S commands a military force sufficient to defend its borders.
conditions: S exercises supreme legislative authority within its territory
no external power dictates the laws of S
S commands a military force sufficient to defend its borders
counterexample: Consider a small state that exercises supreme legislative authority within its borders, that receives no dictation from any external power regarding its laws, and that maintains a military force adequate to its defence. Yet this state has entered into a treaty of alliance with a larger neighbour, under which it has undertaken never to conclude separate agreements with certain other states. The treaty was freely accepted, and no external power compels the state's domestic legislation. But the treaty substantially constrains the state's foreign policy, and should it violate the treaty, the larger neighbour would withdraw military assistance upon which the small state's continued security in fact depends. The three conditions are satisfied, yet the state's sovereignty is, in practice, significantly compromised by its treaty obligations.
missing_condition: The definition addresses only legislative supremacy, absence of external dictation of laws, and military capacity, but overlooks the possibility that treaty obligations, economic dependencies, or other voluntary but constraining arrangements may substantially limit a state's effective sovereignty without formally violating any of the three conditions.
passage: One might define sovereignty by saying that a state is sovereign if and only if it exercises supreme legislative authority within its territory, no external power dictates its laws, and it commands a military force sufficient to defend its borders. This definition captures several features traditionally associated with sovereignty: internal legislative supremacy, freedom from external legal imposition, and the military capacity to preserve territorial integrity. It would exclude vassal states, protectorates, and occupied territories. Yet consider a small state that satisfies all three conditions. Its legislature is supreme within its borders; no foreign power prescribes its domestic statutes; and its army, though modest, is adequate to its defence. But this state has entered into a treaty of alliance with a much larger neighbouring power, under which it has bound itself never to conclude separate agreements with certain other states and to align its foreign policy with that of its ally. The treaty was freely negotiated and freely ratified. No external power dictates the small state's laws. But the treaty obligations are substantial: should the small state violate them, the larger power would withdraw the military cooperation upon which the small state's continued security, in the final analysis, depends. The state's formal sovereignty is intact — the three conditions are satisfied — yet its freedom of action is severely constrained. The definition has captured the formal attributes of sovereignty but has failed to account for the ways in which voluntary but binding international commitments can restrict a state's effective independence without formally abrogating its legislative supremacy or imposing external laws upon it.

definition: An act A is an act of civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a deliberate violation of a law, (ii) the person performing A acts publicly and accepts the legal consequences, and (iii) A is motivated by the desire to change a law or policy.
conditions: A is a deliberate violation of a law
the person performing A acts publicly and accepts the legal consequences
A is motivated by the desire to change a law or policy
counterexample: Consider a merchant who publicly and deliberately violates a tariff regulation by importing goods without paying the prescribed duty. He accepts the legal consequences — the fine, the confiscation of goods — as part of his calculated strategy. His motivation is to bring about a change in the tariff policy, which he regards as ruinous to trade. All three conditions are met. Yet his aim is entirely commercial: he wishes the tariff lowered so that his profits may increase. There is no appeal to justice, no moral principle at stake, no concern for the common good. His act resembles a business stratagem more than an exercise of civic conscience.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the violation be motivated by a moral or principled objection to the law, as distinguished from mere self-interest. Civil disobedience, as commonly understood, involves an appeal to shared principles of justice, not merely a desire for policy change that serves the agent's private advantage.
passage: Suppose one defines civil disobedience as follows: an act is an act of civil disobedience if and only if it is a deliberate violation of a law, the person performing it acts publicly and accepts the legal consequences, and the act is motivated by the desire to change a law or policy. The definition captures several features that are widely thought to distinguish civil disobedience from ordinary criminality: the violation is intentional rather than accidental, it is performed openly rather than covertly, the agent submits to punishment rather than evading it, and the aim is political reform rather than personal gain. These conditions seem, at first, to mark out the concept with tolerable precision. Yet consider a merchant who publicly and deliberately imports goods in violation of a tariff regulation. He makes no effort to conceal his actions; indeed, he wishes them to be known. He accepts the fine imposed upon him, regarding it as a cost of his strategy. And his motivation is to bring about a change in the tariff — he wishes the duty reduced or abolished. Every condition of the definition is met. But the merchant's concern is entirely commercial. He objects to the tariff not because it is unjust, not because it oppresses any group, not because it violates any principle that the community professes, but because it diminishes his profits. His public violation of the law is a calculated business manoeuvre designed to generate controversy and thereby pressure the legislature into altering the tariff. One hesitates to call this civil disobedience, for the concept seems to require something that the merchant's act conspicuously lacks: an appeal to moral principle, a claim that the law in question is not merely inconvenient but wrong.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) P has a legal entitlement to X recognised by the state, (ii) the state provides mechanisms for P to enforce this entitlement, and (iii) P's entitlement to X is codified in statute or constitutional text.
conditions: P has a legal entitlement to X recognised by the state
the state provides mechanisms for P to enforce this entitlement
P's entitlement to X is codified in statute or constitutional text
counterexample: Consider a person living under a regime that has enacted no statute and adopted no constitutional provision recognising the right not to be tortured. The state provides no mechanism for enforcing such a right, and no codified text mentions it. All three conditions fail. Yet it seems deeply implausible to conclude that the person therefore has no right not to be tortured. If rights are exhaustively constituted by legal recognition, enforcement mechanisms, and codification, then any state that fails to recognise a right thereby ensures that the right does not exist — a conclusion that many would find morally abhorrent.
missing_condition: The definition identifies rights entirely with positive legal entitlements and thereby excludes the possibility of moral or natural rights that exist independently of state recognition. A person may have a right — in the sense that others are morally bound to respect it — even when no legal system has acknowledged or codified that right.
passage: One might propose that a person has a right to something if and only if the person has a legal entitlement recognised by the state, the state provides mechanisms for enforcing that entitlement, and the entitlement is codified in statute or constitutional text. This definition has the considerable advantage of precision: one can determine whether a person has a right simply by consulting the relevant legal documents and ascertaining whether enforcement mechanisms exist. It eliminates the vagueness that attends more expansive conceptions of rights. Yet consider a person living under a regime that has enacted no statute and adopted no constitutional provision recognising the right not to be tortured. The state provides no mechanism for enforcing such a right, and the legal code is silent on the matter. According to the definition, this person has no right not to be tortured, for none of the three conditions is satisfied. But surely this conclusion is unsatisfactory. If the state may extinguish a right simply by declining to recognise it, then the most fundamental moral protections depend upon the will of the very power from which they are most needed. The definition, by identifying rights entirely with positive legal entitlements, has closed off the possibility that there are moral or natural rights — rights that persons possess by virtue of their humanity, or by virtue of moral principles that are independent of any particular legal order. The person who is tortured by a state that has not prohibited torture is not merely the victim of a legal lacuna; he is the victim of a moral wrong, and the language of rights seems indispensable in explaining why.

definition: A political system is democratic if and only if (i) all laws are enacted by a legislative assembly, (ii) members of the assembly are chosen by popular vote, and (iii) each voter casts exactly one vote.
conditions: all laws are enacted by a legislative assembly
members of the assembly are chosen by popular vote
each voter casts exactly one vote
counterexample: Consider a system in which a legislative assembly enacts all laws, its members are chosen by popular vote, and each voter casts exactly one ballot. Yet the assembly's districts have been drawn in such a manner that one party, commanding perhaps forty per cent of the total vote, consistently wins seventy per cent of the seats. The boundaries are not accidental but have been deliberately contrived to concentrate the opposing party's supporters into a small number of districts. Every condition of the definition is met, yet the legislature does not reflect the will of the electorate in any proportionate fashion.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the translation of votes into legislative seats bear a reasonable relationship to the distribution of preferences among the electorate. Gerrymandering can render elections formally correct yet substantively unrepresentative.
passage: Suppose one defines a political system as democratic if and only if all laws are enacted by a legislative assembly, the members of that assembly are chosen by popular vote, and each voter casts exactly one vote. The definition appears to identify the essential mechanisms of representative democracy: legislative enactment, popular election, and equal suffrage. It excludes systems in which laws are decreed by an executive or in which some voters possess plural votes. Yet one must consider a system of the following kind. The legislative assembly enacts all laws, its members are chosen by popular vote, and every voter casts a single ballot. The formal conditions are impeccably satisfied. But the electoral districts have been drawn with great cunning, so that the supporters of one party are packed into a small number of districts whilst the supporters of the other are spread thinly across many. The result is that a party commanding no more than forty per cent of the total vote consistently wins seventy per cent of the seats in the assembly. The laws enacted by this assembly reflect the preferences of the minority party, for it is that party that controls the chamber. Each voter has cast one vote; the assembly was chosen by popular election; all laws were enacted by that assembly. Yet the system, considered as a whole, fails to translate the preferences of the electorate into legislative outcomes in any manner that could reasonably be called representative. The definition has specified the machinery of democracy but has neglected to require that the machinery operate in such a way as to give effect to the popular will it purports to register.

definition: A citizen C is free in a state S if and only if (i) C may express any opinion without legal penalty, (ii) C may associate with any other citizen without restriction, and (iii) C may practise any religion or none.
conditions: C may express any opinion without legal penalty
C may associate with any other citizen without restriction
C may practise any religion or none
counterexample: Consider a citizen who may express any opinion, associate with anyone, and practise any religion. Yet the state requires him to obtain a permit before travelling from one district to another, forbids him from changing his occupation without official approval, and dictates where he shall reside. He enjoys the freedoms of expression, association, and worship in full, but his freedom of movement, of occupation, and of domicile are severely curtailed. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied, yet his overall liberty is substantially restricted.
missing_condition: The definition enumerates certain specific freedoms — expression, association, and religion — but omits others that are equally essential to a free life, such as freedom of movement, freedom of occupation, and freedom to choose one's place of residence. A person may enjoy the listed freedoms whilst being unfree in other fundamental respects.
passage: One might propose that a citizen is free in a state if and only if he may express any opinion without legal penalty, may associate with any other citizen without restriction, and may practise any religion or none. These three freedoms — of expression, of association, and of worship — are indeed among the most frequently celebrated liberties in the tradition of political thought, and a state that denied any of them would commonly be judged unfree. The definition, however, treats these three as exhaustive of the conditions of freedom. Consider a citizen who enjoys all three in their fullest measure. He publishes pamphlets criticising the government without fear of prosecution. He joins whatever societies he pleases. He worships according to his conscience, or abstains from worship altogether. Yet the state in which he lives requires that he obtain official permission before travelling from one district to another. He may not change his occupation without the approval of a government bureau. He is assigned a place of residence and may not move without authorisation. His freedom of expression, association, and worship is unimpaired, and all three conditions of the definition are satisfied. But his freedom of movement is restricted, his choice of livelihood is controlled, and his domicile is dictated. It would be exceedingly odd to call such a citizen free, for substantial dimensions of his life are subject to state direction. The definition has identified certain important liberties but has mistaken them for the whole of freedom, when in truth freedom is a concept of much wider scope, encompassing the capacity to direct one's own life across a broad range of activities and choices.

definition: A society is egalitarian if and only if (i) all citizens possess the same legal rights, (ii) no hereditary titles or ranks are recognised by the state, and (iii) public offices are open to all citizens without restriction of birth.
conditions: all citizens possess the same legal rights
no hereditary titles or ranks are recognised
public offices are open to all citizens without restriction of birth
counterexample: Consider a society that has abolished all hereditary titles, that confers identical legal rights upon every citizen, and that permits any citizen to hold any public office regardless of birth. Yet the distribution of wealth is such that a small number of families control the preponderance of land, capital, and productive enterprise, whilst the majority of citizens live in conditions of poverty. The children of the wealthy receive superior education, establish social connexions that ease their entry into positions of influence, and inherit fortunes that place them beyond the reach of economic insecurity. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the society is marked by deep and persistent inequality.
missing_condition: The definition addresses only legal and formal political equality, omitting any consideration of economic inequality. A society may satisfy all formal conditions of egalitarianism whilst tolerating vast disparities in wealth, education, and social advantage that render the formal equality largely nominal.
passage: It might be supposed that a society is egalitarian if and only if all citizens possess the same legal rights, no hereditary titles or ranks are recognised by the state, and public offices are open to all citizens without restriction of birth. This definition captures the principal demands of formal political equality: the abolition of legal privilege, the elimination of aristocratic rank, and the opening of public office to talent rather than lineage. A society satisfying these conditions would differ markedly from a feudal or caste-based order. But consider a society of the following character. Every hereditary title has been abolished. The law confers identical rights upon every citizen. Any citizen may stand for any public office. The three conditions are met in full. Yet a small number of families control the greater part of the society's wealth — its land, its factories, its banks. The children of these families attend superior schools, form connexions with one another, and inherit fortunes that insulate them from the vicissitudes that afflict their less fortunate compatriots. The children of the poor, though possessed of identical legal rights, find that their formal entitlements avail them little against the practical advantages that wealth confers. They may stand for public office, but they lack the resources to conduct a campaign. They possess the same legal rights, but they cannot afford to engage a solicitor. The society is formally egalitarian and substantively oligarchic. The definition has grasped the form of equality whilst leaving its substance untouched, and no analysis of the concept that ignores the distribution of material resources can be regarded as complete.

definition: A person X has authority over person Y if and only if (i) X occupies a recognised office or station, (ii) Y acknowledges X's position, and (iii) X's directives pertain to matters within the customary scope of that office.
conditions: X occupies a recognised office or station
Y acknowledges X's position
X's directives pertain to matters within the customary scope of that office
counterexample: Consider a colonial governor who occupies a recognised office established by the metropolitan power, whose position is acknowledged by the colonised population in the sense that they are aware of his office and do not deny its existence, and whose directives pertain to matters — taxation, public works, criminal law — that fall within the customary scope of governorship. All three conditions are met. Yet the colonised population did not consent to the creation of the office, did not choose its occupant, and regards the entire arrangement as an imposition by a foreign power. Their acknowledgement of the governor's position is mere recognition of a fact, not acceptance of a claim to rightful rule.
missing_condition: The definition requires only that Y acknowledge X's position but does not require that this acknowledgement amount to genuine consent or acceptance of X's right to command. Recognition of a fact is not the same as endorsement of a normative claim, and authority without consent is, arguably, mere domination.
passage: Suppose one defines authority by saying that a person X has authority over person Y if and only if X occupies a recognised office or station, Y acknowledges X's position, and X's directives pertain to matters within the customary scope of that office. The definition seems to capture the structural features of authority: an institutional role, recognition by those subject to it, and limitation to an appropriate domain. It would explain why a magistrate has authority over litigants in his court but not over their private affairs, and why a military officer has authority over his subordinates but not over civilians. Yet consider a colonial governor. He occupies a recognised office, established by the laws of the metropolitan power and acknowledged by the colonial administration. The colonised population acknowledges his position in the sense that they are perfectly aware of it — they know that he is the governor, that he issues decrees, that he commands troops. His directives concern taxation, public works, the administration of justice — all matters within the customary scope of a governor's office. Every condition is satisfied. But the colonised population did not consent to the establishment of the office, played no part in selecting its occupant, and regards the entire arrangement as an alien imposition. Their acknowledgement of the governor's position is mere recognition of a fact, not acceptance of a normative claim. One must ask whether such acknowledgement suffices for authority, or whether authority properly so called requires something closer to consent — a willingness to be bound that goes beyond the mere awareness that one is, in fact, being commanded.

definition: A political regime is legitimate if and only if (i) it enjoys the approval of the majority of its citizens, (ii) it provides basic security of person and property, and (iii) it permits citizens to petition for redress of grievances.
conditions: it enjoys the approval of the majority of its citizens
it provides basic security of person and property
it permits citizens to petition for redress of grievances
counterexample: Consider a regime that enjoys overwhelming majority approval, provides excellent security of person and property, and permits citizens to petition for redress. Yet this regime maintains a policy of systematic persecution against a small ethnic minority, confiscating their property, excluding them from public life, and subjecting them to humiliation. The majority approves of these policies, or at least does not object. The regime satisfies every condition of the definition, for the majority is content, security is maintained for most citizens, and petitioning is permitted. Yet a regime that persecutes a defenceless minority can scarcely be called legitimate without qualification.
missing_condition: The definition grounds legitimacy entirely in majority approval and the provision of security and petitioning, but places no constraint upon how the regime treats minorities. A regime that satisfies the majority whilst oppressing a minority may meet the stated conditions yet fail a more fundamental test of legitimacy rooted in the equal moral standing of all persons.
passage: One might propose that a political regime is legitimate if and only if it enjoys the approval of the majority of its citizens, provides basic security of person and property, and permits citizens to petition for redress of grievances. This definition ties legitimacy to popular endorsement, the fulfilment of the basic protective functions of government, and the maintenance of a channel through which citizens may seek remedy for wrongs. Each condition is plausible, and their conjunction appears to describe a regime that one would ordinarily regard as having a reasonable claim to the allegiance of its subjects. Yet consider a regime of the following description. It commands the enthusiastic support of eighty per cent of the population. It maintains excellent police and military forces, and crimes against person and property are swiftly punished. Citizens may present petitions to the government, and many petitions are acted upon. All three conditions are met. But the regime pursues a systematic policy of persecution against a small ethnic minority. Members of this minority are excluded from public office, their property is subject to confiscation without compensation, and they are required to reside in designated districts. The majority approves of these measures, or at least does not protest them. The regime, on the proposed definition, is legitimate, for the majority approves, security is provided, and petitioning is permitted. But a regime that subjects an innocent minority to organised persecution seems to lack something essential to legitimacy, however enthusiastically the majority may endorse its actions. The definition has made legitimacy a function of majority satisfaction whilst ignoring the rights of those whom the majority chooses to oppress.

definition: A political community is sovereign if and only if (i) it possesses a determinate territory, (ii) it has a permanent population residing within that territory, and (iii) it maintains a government capable of entering into relations with other states.
conditions: it possesses a determinate territory
it has a permanent population residing within that territory
it maintains a government capable of entering into relations with other states
counterexample: Consider a political community that possesses a clearly demarcated territory, has a permanent population, and maintains a government that conducts diplomatic relations with other states. Yet within its borders, a powerful religious institution exercises jurisdiction over matters of family law, education, and personal status, and the state lacks the authority to override these ecclesiastical rulings.
