The community satisfies all three conditions, yet its sovereignty is importantly divided, for there are domains within its own territory over which it does not exercise supreme authority.
missing_condition: The definition specifies territory, population, and a government capable of foreign relations, but omits the requirement that the government exercise supreme or undivided authority within its territory. A community may meet the stated conditions whilst lacking effective supremacy over significant domains of governance within its own borders.
passage: It might be suggested that a political community is sovereign if and only if it possesses a determinate territory, has a permanent population residing within that territory, and maintains a government capable of entering into relations with other states. These conditions are sometimes called the constitutive elements of statehood, and they have the advantage of being relatively clear and verifiable. A community that lacked a territory, or a population, or a government able to conduct foreign affairs would not, on any ordinary understanding, be a sovereign state. But consider a political community that satisfies all three conditions. Its territory is clearly bounded; its population is permanent and substantial; its government exchanges ambassadors with foreign powers and concludes treaties. Within its borders, however, a powerful religious institution exercises jurisdiction over family law, education, and matters of personal status. The state's government cannot override the rulings of the ecclesiastical authorities in these domains, and any attempt to do so would meet with effective resistance. The community possesses territory, population, and a government capable of foreign relations, yet it does not exercise supreme authority within its own borders. There are domains of governance over which it has, in practice, ceded control. The definition has identified necessary conditions for statehood in the international sense but has failed to capture the notion of sovereignty as supreme internal authority — the idea that a sovereign body is one that has the final word on all matters within its jurisdiction, answering to no rival authority within its own territory.

definition: An act of civil disobedience obtains when (i) the agent violates a specific law that they consider unjust, (ii) the violation is nonviolent, and (iii) the agent seeks to persuade fellow citizens of the law's injustice.
conditions: the agent violates a specific law that they consider unjust
the violation is nonviolent
the agent seeks to persuade fellow citizens of the law's injustice
counterexample: Consider a citizen who regards a particular tax as unjust but who recognises that refusing to pay the tax would harm only himself without attracting public notice. He therefore violates a different law — he stages a peaceful sit-in at a government building, blocking the entrance — in order to draw attention to the tax he opposes. His violation is nonviolent, and he seeks to persuade his fellow citizens that the tax is unjust. But the law he violates — the law against obstruction of public buildings — is not the law he considers unjust. He has no objection to the obstruction statute per se. The first condition fails, yet his act seems a clear instance of civil disobedience.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the agent violate the very law they consider unjust, but civil disobedience may involve the violation of a different law chosen for its strategic visibility. Indirect civil disobedience — violating law A to protest law B — is a recognised form of the practice that the definition excludes.
passage: Suppose one says that an act of civil disobedience obtains when the agent violates a specific law that he considers unjust, the violation is nonviolent, and the agent seeks to persuade fellow citizens of the law's injustice. This formulation captures a natural picture of civil disobedience: the conscientious citizen, confronted with a law he deems unjust, refuses to comply with that law, does so peacefully, and appeals to the moral sense of his community. The first condition ensures a direct connexion between the law violated and the injustice protested; the second ensures that the protest does not degenerate into mere criminality; the third ensures that the act has a communicative purpose. Yet consider a citizen who regards a particular tax as deeply unjust — he believes it falls disproportionately upon the poorest members of society. He recognises, however, that quietly refusing to pay the tax would attract no attention and accomplish nothing. He therefore stages a peaceful sit-in at the entrance to a government building, an act that violates not the tax law but the statute against obstruction of public buildings. His purpose is to draw attention to the injustice of the tax, and he makes this purpose plain to all who will listen. He has no objection whatsoever to the obstruction statute; he violates it solely as a means of making his protest visible. The violation is nonviolent, and he seeks to persuade his fellow citizens. But the law he violates is not the law he considers unjust, and the first condition therefore fails. If civil disobedience may be indirect — if one may violate one law in order to protest a different and unrelated law — then the definition is too narrow, for it excludes what would seem to be a perfectly intelligible and indeed common form of conscientious protest.

definition: A right R is a natural right if and only if (i) R is possessed by every human being, (ii) R does not depend upon any convention or institution for its existence, and (iii) R is inalienable — it cannot be surrendered or forfeited.
conditions: R is possessed by every human being
R does not depend upon any convention or institution for its existence
R is inalienable
counterexample: Consider the right of self-defence. It is plausibly possessed by every human being and does not depend upon any convention or institution for its existence; it seems to arise from the very nature of a being capable of preserving its own life. Yet a person who commits certain grave offences may, on many views, forfeit the right of self-defence in certain circumstances — a prisoner lawfully detained, for instance, may not resist his gaolers by force, even to preserve his comfort, for he has forfeited this right by his criminal act. The first two conditions are satisfied, but the third fails if forfeiture is possible. Yet the right of self-defence is among the most frequently cited natural rights.
missing_condition: The definition requires absolute inalienability, but some rights that are plausibly natural — self-defence being a prominent example — may be subject to forfeiture under certain conditions. The requirement of inalienability is too strong if it excludes rights that are natural in origin but defeasible in application.
passage: One might define a natural right as follows: a right is a natural right if and only if it is possessed by every human being, it does not depend upon any convention or institution for its existence, and it is inalienable — it cannot be surrendered or forfeited. The definition reflects a venerable tradition in political philosophy that distinguishes natural rights from conventional or positive rights on the grounds of universality, independence from human artifice, and permanence. A natural right, on this view, is one that belongs to a man simply in virtue of his humanity, not in virtue of any social arrangement, and that cannot be taken from him or given away. Yet consider the right of self-defence. It is plausible that every human being possesses this right, not because any legislature has conferred it, but because any creature capable of preserving its own existence has, by nature, a right to resist threats to that existence. The right does not depend upon convention; it is antecedent to all civil society. These features commend it as a paradigmatic natural right. But is it truly inalienable? A person who has been lawfully convicted of a grave offence and placed in confinement has, on many accounts, forfeited certain rights, among them the right to resist his gaolers by force. If the right of self-defence can be forfeited through criminal conduct, then it is not inalienable in the sense the definition requires. Yet it remains, by hypothesis, a natural right — one that arises from human nature and not from convention. The definition, by insisting upon absolute inalienability, has drawn the boundaries of the natural too narrowly and has excluded rights that most theorists of the natural law tradition would wish to include.

definition: A decision-making procedure is democratic if and only if (i) every member of the group affected by the decision has an equal vote, (ii) the decision is determined by majority rule, and (iii) the decision is binding upon all members.
conditions: every affected member has an equal vote
the decision is determined by majority rule
the decision is binding upon all members
counterexample: Consider a small community that votes, by simple majority and with equal suffrage, to expel a particular family from the community and confiscate their home. Every member has an equal vote; the majority carries the motion; and the decision is binding upon all, including the expelled family. All three conditions are met. Yet the decision is an exercise of majoritarian tyranny rather than a defensible instance of democratic governance, for it employs democratic procedures to inflict an arbitrary harm upon a minority.
missing_condition: The definition imposes no substantive constraint upon the content of democratic decisions. Without a requirement that democratic decisions respect certain fundamental rights or principles of justice, the definition permits the majority to use formally democratic procedures to oppress individuals or minorities.
passage: It might be proposed that a decision-making procedure is democratic if and only if every member of the group affected by the decision has an equal vote, the decision is determined by majority rule, and the decision is binding upon all members. This is a procedural definition of democracy, and it has the merit of clarity and simplicity. It specifies the mechanism — equal votes, majority determination, binding force — without importing substantive moral requirements that might be thought extraneous to the concept of democracy as such. But consider a small community of fifty families that employs this procedure to decide all collective matters. At a meeting, the community votes, by simple majority and with equal suffrage, to expel one particular family and to confiscate their dwelling. The family in question has committed no offence; they are simply unpopular. Thirty families vote in favour; twenty vote against. The decision is binding. Every condition of the definition is satisfied: each family had one vote, the majority ruled, and the decision binds all. Yet the outcome is an act of collective injustice — the arbitrary dispossession of an innocent family by the sheer weight of numbers. To call this procedure democratic is, at a minimum, to acknowledge that democracy thus defined is compatible with grave injustice. The definition has specified what makes a procedure formally democratic but has failed to distinguish between the legitimate and the tyrannical exercise of majority power. If democracy is to be a term of approbation and not merely a description of a voting mechanism, it must, it would seem, incorporate some constraint upon the substance of the decisions that majorities may impose.

definition: A people is self-governing if and only if (i) the laws under which they live are enacted by their own representatives, (ii) no foreign power exercises authority over their internal affairs, and (iii) they possess the right to amend or repeal any law.
conditions: the laws are enacted by their own representatives
no foreign power exercises authority over internal affairs
they possess the right to amend or repeal any law
counterexample: Consider a people whose laws are enacted by their own elected representatives, over whom no foreign power exercises authority, and who possess the right to amend or repeal any statute. Yet the representatives, once elected, are subject to no effective check: there is no independent judiciary, no constitutional limitation upon legislative power, and no mechanism by which the people may recall a representative between elections. The legislature, being supreme and unchecked, enacts laws that serve the interests of the representatives themselves at the expense of the electorate. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the people are, in a meaningful sense, governed by an oligarchy of their own creation rather than governing themselves.
missing_condition: The definition requires that representatives enact laws but imposes no requirement of accountability, constitutional limitation, or institutional checks upon those representatives. Self-government requires not merely that laws be enacted by representatives but that the representatives remain answerable to the people and constrained by fundamental principles.
passage: Suppose one says that a people is self-governing if and only if the laws under which they live are enacted by their own representatives, no foreign power exercises authority over their internal affairs, and they possess the right to amend or repeal any law. This definition unites two prominent themes in the theory of political freedom: representative government and national independence. A people whose laws are imposed by a foreign sovereign, or whose legislature is a mere puppet, would fail to satisfy the conditions and would not count as self-governing. The definition seems adequate. But consider a people whose representatives are freely elected, whose state is fully independent of foreign control, and whose constitution grants the legislature the power to amend or repeal any law. Once elected, however, the representatives discover that there is no institution capable of restraining them. There is no independent judiciary to strike down their enactments, no constitutional provision that limits their legislative power, and no procedure by which the electorate can recall a wayward representative before the next election. The representatives proceed to enact laws that enrich themselves, exempt themselves from taxation, and extend their own terms of office indefinitely. The people retain the formal right to amend these laws, but the amendment procedure itself requires a two-thirds vote of the very legislature whose members benefit from the status quo. Every condition of the definition is satisfied, yet the people are governed, in effect, by a self-serving oligarchy that they cannot dislodge. The definition has failed to require that the machinery of representation be supplemented by mechanisms of accountability and constitutional restraint without which representation degenerates into a new form of domination.

definition: A law has authority if and only if (i) it was enacted by the recognised legislative body, (ii) it does not conflict with any prior law of higher standing, and (iii) it has been duly promulgated to the public.
conditions: it was enacted by the recognised legislative body
it does not conflict with any prior law of higher standing
it has been duly promulgated to the public
counterexample: Consider a law enacted by the recognised legislature of a state, consistent with all prior laws of higher standing, and duly promulgated in the official gazette. The law requires that all citizens of a particular religious confession register with the authorities and wear a distinguishing badge in public. It was enacted through proper legislative channels, it conflicts with no prior constitutional provision (the constitution being silent on the matter), and it has been widely publicised. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might question whether such a law possesses genuine authority, for it commands something that is, by any reasonable moral standard, an outrage against the dignity of the persons it targets.
missing_condition: The definition treats authority as a purely procedural matter — enacted by the proper body, consistent with prior law, publicly promulgated — but places no constraint upon the moral content of the law. A law that commands something manifestly unjust may satisfy all procedural requirements yet lack the moral authority that would ground a genuine obligation to obey.
passage: One might propose that a law has authority if and only if it was enacted by the recognised legislative body, it does not conflict with any prior law of higher standing, and it has been duly promulgated to the public. This is a purely procedural account of legal authority, and it has the considerable merit of determinacy. Whether a law has authority is, on this view, a question that can be settled by consulting the legislative record, the hierarchy of legal norms, and the official gazette. No controversial moral judgements are required. Yet consider a law that satisfies all three conditions. It was duly enacted by the legislature through the prescribed voting procedure. It is consistent with the constitution, which is silent on the subject matter. And it has been published in the official gazette for all to read. The law requires that all citizens belonging to a particular religious confession register with the authorities and wear a distinguishing badge upon their garments whenever they appear in public. The purpose, one gathers, is to mark them out for the convenience of the state. The law is procedurally impeccable. But it commands something that most persons of moral sensibility would regard as a grave injustice — the public humiliation and stigmatisation of persons on account of their religious identity. If authority is entirely a matter of procedural correctness, then this law possesses it in full. But if authority carries with it any claim to obedience, one must ask whether a law that commands injustice can truly claim to be obeyed. The definition, by confining itself to procedure, has severed the connexion between authority and the moral quality of what is commanded.

definition: A ruler is sovereign if and only if (i) their will is the ultimate source of law within the territory, (ii) they are not subject to any higher human authority, and (iii) their subjects owe them unconditional obedience.
conditions: their will is the ultimate source of law
they are not subject to any higher human authority
their subjects owe them unconditional obedience
counterexample: Consider a constitutional monarch whose will was historically the ultimate source of law but who has, over generations, ceded legislative power to an elected parliament. He retains a formal role — laws are enacted in his name, and he gives royal assent — but his assent is a formality, and he may not refuse it. He is not subject to any higher human authority; no foreign prince commands him. Yet his subjects do not owe him unconditional obedience, for the laws they obey are made by parliament, not by the monarch, and they would rightly refuse a royal command that contradicted a statute. The first and third conditions fail, though the second is satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes an absolutist conception of sovereignty in which the ruler's will is the sole source of law and obedience is unconditional. It cannot accommodate constitutional or limited sovereignty, in which supreme authority is divided among institutions or constrained by fundamental law.
passage: Suppose one defines a sovereign ruler as one whose will is the ultimate source of law within the territory, who is not subject to any higher human authority, and whose subjects owe him unconditional obedience. This definition reflects a tradition of political thought that locates sovereignty in an absolute and undivided will — a single authority from which all law flows and to which all subjects must submit without condition. It may describe certain historical forms of monarchy with tolerable accuracy. But consider a constitutional monarch of the kind familiar in modern European states. His will was, in earlier centuries, the source of law, and the vestiges of this power survive in ceremonial forms: laws are enacted in his name, and he formally assents to parliamentary statutes. But the reality is otherwise. The laws are made by an elected parliament; the monarch's assent is a formality that cannot be withheld; and the subjects obey the laws not because they emanate from the royal will but because they have been enacted by their representatives. The monarch is not subject to any higher human authority — no foreign prince commands him, and no superior office exists within the state. But his will is not the ultimate source of law, and his subjects do not owe him unconditional obedience. The definition, framed with an absolutist conception in view, cannot accommodate the phenomenon of limited or constitutional sovereignty, in which the supreme authority of the state is exercised not by a single will but by a complex arrangement of institutions, each constrained by the others and by fundamental law.

definition: A political protest is an instance of civil disobedience if and only if (i) it involves a refusal to comply with a governmental directive, (ii) the participants are citizens of the state whose directive they refuse, and (iii) the refusal is collective rather than individual.
conditions: it involves a refusal to comply with a governmental directive
the participants are citizens of the state
the refusal is collective rather than individual
counterexample: Consider a single individual who refuses to comply with a conscription order because she believes the war to be unjust. She acts alone; her refusal is individual, not collective. She is a citizen of the state whose directive she refuses, and her refusal is a deliberate act of noncompliance with a governmental directive. Two conditions are met, but the third — collectivity — fails. Yet her solitary refusal, undertaken on grounds of conscience and at great personal cost, seems a paradigmatic instance of civil disobedience. The definition excludes it solely because she acts alone.
missing_condition: The definition requires that civil disobedience be collective, but an individual acting alone on grounds of conscience may engage in civil disobedience no less genuinely than a group. The moral character of the act — its conscientiousness, its publicity, its acceptance of consequences — does not depend upon the number of participants.
passage: One might define civil disobedience as obtaining when a political protest involves a refusal to comply with a governmental directive, the participants are citizens of the state whose directive they refuse, and the refusal is collective rather than individual. The requirement of collectivity might be motivated by the thought that civil disobedience is, at its core, a political act — an act aimed at influencing public opinion and governmental policy — and that such influence requires the concerted action of a group rather than the isolated gesture of a single person. A lone dissenter, one might think, is merely a conscientious objector, not a practitioner of civil disobedience. But consider a woman who receives a conscription order directing her to serve in a war that she believes to be profoundly unjust. She is a citizen of the state that has issued the order. She refuses to comply, not covertly but openly, declaring her reasons to any who will listen. She does not flee; she presents herself to the authorities and accepts whatever punishment they impose. She acts entirely alone; no organisation supports her, and no group has coordinated with her. Her refusal is individual, not collective, and the third condition therefore fails. Yet her act bears every other mark of civil disobedience: it is deliberate, public, conscientious, and submissive to legal consequences. To exclude it from the category of civil disobedience solely because she acts alone seems arbitrary, for the moral seriousness of her act does not depend upon whether others happen to share her convictions or join her refusal.

definition: A citizen has a right to political participation if and only if (i) the citizen is entitled to vote in elections, (ii) the citizen is eligible to stand for public office, and (iii) the citizen may freely join political associations.
conditions: the citizen is entitled to vote in elections
the citizen is eligible to stand for public office
the citizen may freely join political associations
counterexample: Consider a citizen who possesses the right to vote, who is eligible to stand for office, and who may join any political association. Yet the state controls all newspapers, radio broadcasting, and public meeting halls, and permits no independent source of political information. The citizen votes, but on the basis of information curated by the government. She may stand for office, but she cannot communicate her views to the electorate through any medium not controlled by the state. She may join political associations, but those associations cannot publish pamphlets, hold public meetings, or otherwise make their case to the public. All three conditions are satisfied, yet her right to political participation is, in practice, hollow.
missing_condition: The definition enumerates formal entitlements — voting, standing for office, joining associations — but neglects the informational and communicative conditions without which these entitlements cannot be effectively exercised. Meaningful political participation requires access to independent information and the ability to communicate one's views to the public.
passage: Suppose one defines the right to political participation as follows: a citizen has this right if and only if the citizen is entitled to vote in elections, is eligible to stand for public office, and may freely join political associations. These three conditions identify the principal formal entitlements associated with democratic citizenship, and a state that denied any of them would be widely regarded as undemocratic. Yet consider a citizen in a state that satisfies all three conditions. She votes in regular elections. She is eligible to stand for any office. She belongs to a political association of her choosing. But the state exercises a monopoly over all media of public communication — newspapers, radio, public meeting halls — and permits no independent source of political information. The citizen votes, but the information upon which she bases her vote has been filtered, curated, and in many cases fabricated by the government. She may stand for office, but she cannot reach the electorate with her own message, for every channel of communication is controlled by the very state she seeks to challenge. She may join a political association, but that association cannot publish its views, hold public rallies, or distribute literature. The formal entitlements are intact, yet the substance of political participation — the informed exercise of judgement, the public exchange of arguments, the genuine competition of ideas — is absent. The definition has mistaken the formal skeleton of political participation for the living body, and no adequate account of the right can omit the conditions that make its exercise meaningful rather than merely ceremonial.

definition: A revolution is legitimate if and only if (i) the existing regime has persistently violated the fundamental rights of its citizens, (ii) all peaceful means of redress have been exhausted, and (iii) the revolutionary movement commands the support of the majority of the population.
conditions: the existing regime has persistently violated fundamental rights
all peaceful means of redress have been exhausted
the revolutionary movement commands majority support
counterexample: Consider a regime that has persistently and egregiously violated the fundamental rights of a religious minority constituting fifteen per cent of the population, whilst treating the majority tolerably well. The minority has exhausted every peaceful avenue of redress — petition, legal challenge, appeal to international bodies — without result. A revolutionary movement arises among the minority, but it commands the support of only fifteen per cent of the population; the majority, whose rights are not at issue, is indifferent or opposed. The first two conditions are satisfied, but the third fails. Yet it seems difficult to deny legitimacy to a revolution undertaken by a systematically oppressed group merely because that group is a numerical minority.
missing_condition: The definition requires majority support, but a revolution may be legitimate even when undertaken by a minority, provided that the minority's rights have been grievously and persistently violated and all other remedies have failed. The requirement of majority support privileges the interests of the many over the rights of the few in a manner that may be morally indefensible.
passage: One might propose that a revolution is legitimate if and only if the existing regime has persistently violated the fundamental rights of its citizens, all peaceful means of redress have been exhausted, and the revolutionary movement commands the support of the majority of the population. The three conditions together impose a demanding standard: the regime must be genuinely oppressive, not merely imperfect; the revolutionaries must have tried every peaceful alternative before resorting to force; and the movement must represent the will of the greater number rather than the ambitions of a faction. These seem reasonable requirements. But consider a regime that has persistently and egregiously violated the fundamental rights of a religious minority comprising fifteen per cent of the population. Members of this minority have been subjected to forced displacement, confiscation of property, and systematic exclusion from public life. They have petitioned the government, challenged its policies in the courts, and appealed to international bodies, all without effect. A revolutionary movement arises among the minority and prepares to resist the regime by force. But the movement commands the support of only those fifteen per cent whose rights have been violated; the remaining eighty-five per cent of the population, whose rights the regime has not infringed, are either indifferent or actively hostile to the revolution. The third condition fails. Yet the moral case for the revolution seems compelling: a group whose fundamental rights have been persistently violated, and which has exhausted every peaceful remedy, resorts to force as a last recourse. To deny the legitimacy of this revolution solely because the oppressed group is a numerical minority is to make the rights of the few hostage to the indifference of the many.

definition: Two persons are political equals if and only if (i) each has an equal vote in collective decisions, (ii) each is equally eligible for public office, and (iii) each is subject to the same laws.
conditions: each has an equal vote
each is equally eligible for public office
each is subject to the same laws
counterexample: Consider two citizens of a state in which each has an equal vote, each is eligible for public office, and each is subject to the same laws. Yet one citizen is a prominent landowner whose wealth enables him to fund political campaigns, endow newspapers sympathetic to his views, and entertain legislators at his estate, whilst the other is a labourer who can barely afford to take a day from his work to cast his vote. The conditions of political equality are formally met, yet the landowner's influence upon the political process vastly exceeds that of the labourer, not by virtue of any legal privilege but by virtue of his economic position.
missing_condition: The definition captures formal political equality but omits any requirement concerning the distribution of political influence. When economic resources translate into political power, formal equality of vote, eligibility, and legal status may coexist with profound inequality of actual political influence.
passage: Suppose one defines political equality as obtaining between two persons when each has an equal vote in collective decisions, each is equally eligible for public office, and each is subject to the same laws. These conditions embody the core commitments of formal political equality: no one's vote counts for more than another's, no one is barred from office by legal disability, and the law applies uniformly. A state satisfying these conditions would differ strikingly from an aristocracy or a caste society. Yet consider two citizens of such a state. One is a person of great wealth — a landowner, an industrialist, a man whose fortune enables him to fund the campaigns of sympathetic candidates, to establish newspapers that promote his political views, and to entertain legislators whose goodwill he cultivates. The other is a labourer whose income scarcely covers the necessities of life and who finds it difficult even to take the hours from work required to cast his ballot. Each has one vote; each is eligible for office; each is subject to the same statutes. The three conditions are met. But the landowner exercises an influence upon the political process that the labourer cannot remotely approach. His wealth is convertible into political power — not through bribery, which the law forbids, but through the perfectly legal employment of resources that the labourer simply does not possess. The definition has secured the form of political equality whilst leaving its substance to be determined by the distribution of economic resources, a distribution that may be radically unequal.

definition: A constitution has supreme authority if and only if (i) all legislation must conform to its provisions, (ii) an independent judiciary has the power to invalidate legislation that conflicts with it, and (iii) the constitution can be amended only through a special procedure more demanding than ordinary legislation.
conditions: all legislation must conform to its provisions
an independent judiciary can invalidate conflicting legislation
amendment requires a special procedure more demanding than ordinary legislation
counterexample: Consider a constitution that satisfies all three conditions: legislation must conform to it, an independent judiciary reviews and may invalidate statutes, and amendment requires a supermajority and a ratification process beyond the ordinary legislative procedure. Yet the constitution itself was drafted and ratified by a narrow assembly that excluded women, the unpropertied, and certain racial groups from participation. The document reflects the interests and prejudices of those who wrote it, and its amendment procedure is so demanding that the excluded groups, even after gaining the franchise, cannot muster the supermajority required to alter the provisions that disadvantage them. The constitution has supreme authority in the formal sense, yet its authority rests upon a foundation of exclusion.
missing_condition: The definition specifies formal features of constitutional supremacy — conformity, judicial review, and a demanding amendment procedure — but places no requirement upon the inclusiveness or representativeness of the constitution's original enactment. A constitution drafted by a narrow and unrepresentative body may satisfy all formal conditions whilst lacking the democratic legitimacy that would ground its claim to supreme authority.
passage: One might propose that a constitution has supreme authority if and only if all legislation must conform to its provisions, an independent judiciary has the power to invalidate legislation that conflicts with it, and the constitution can be amended only through a special procedure more demanding than ordinary legislation. This definition captures the structural features commonly associated with constitutional supremacy: the constitution stands above ordinary law, its supremacy is enforced by an independent body, and its alteration requires an exceptional degree of consensus. These are, indeed, important features of constitutionalism as it is practised in many states. But consider a constitution that satisfies all three conditions. It was drafted, however, by a constituent assembly from which women, the unpropertied, and certain racial groups were entirely excluded. The document that this assembly produced reflects its composition: it protects the interests of the propertied classes, it is silent on the rights of those who were excluded from its drafting, and certain of its provisions entrench the political dominance of the group that wrote it. The amendment procedure is exceedingly demanding, requiring a three-quarters supermajority in both chambers of the legislature and ratification by three-quarters of the constituent provinces. The excluded groups, having subsequently gained the franchise, find that they cannot assemble the supermajority needed to amend the provisions that disadvantage them. The constitution satisfies every condition of the definition; its formal supremacy is unimpeachable. Yet its authority rests upon a foundation of exclusion, and the very stringency of its amendment procedure perpetuates the injustices embedded in its original text. The definition has identified the formal architecture of constitutional supremacy but has neglected the question of whether the constitution's origin confers upon it the moral authority that its formal supremacy presupposes.

definition: A political system S is a democracy if and only if (i) S provides for the direct participation of every adult citizen in the making of all laws, (ii) S requires that every decision be reached by a unanimous vote of those participating, and (iii) the citizens of S assemble in a single physical location to deliberate and vote.
conditions: S provides for the direct participation of every adult citizen in the making of all laws
S requires that every decision be reached by a unanimous vote of those participating
the citizens of S assemble in a single physical location to deliberate and vote
counterexample: Consider the parliamentary system of Great Britain. Citizens do not directly participate in the making of laws; they elect representatives who legislate on their behalf. Decisions are reached not by unanimity but by majority vote, and a vigorous opposition is regarded as essential rather than as a defect. Citizens do not assemble in a single physical location; the elected representatives meet in the Palace of Westminster, whilst the citizenry is dispersed across the kingdom. Yet Great Britain is a democracy, and has been regarded as such for the better part of two centuries.
missing_condition: The definition requires direct participation, unanimity, and physical assembly, yet democratic governance may be exercised through elected representatives, may proceed by majority rule, and may operate across vast territories whose citizens never meet in a single place. What is wanted is a conception of democracy that accommodates representative institutions and majority decision.
passage: One might propose that a political system is a democracy if and only if it provides for the direct participation of every adult citizen in the making of all laws, requires that every decision be reached by a unanimous vote, and arranges for the citizens to assemble in a single physical location to deliberate and vote. The definition has a certain historical respectability; one thinks of the Athenian ekklesia, in which the citizens of Athens gathered on the Pnyx, debated matters of state, and voted directly upon the measures proposed. The image of the assembled citizenry, deliberating face to face and reaching decisions together, has exercised a powerful hold upon the democratic imagination. Yet one must attend to the case of Great Britain. The British system is democratic — it has been so recognised by political observers from Montesquieu onwards — yet it satisfies none of the three conditions. British citizens do not directly participate in the making of laws; they elect members to the House of Commons, who legislate on their behalf. The system does not require unanimity; decisions are reached by a majority of those voting, and the existence of a loyal opposition — a body of members who disagree with the government and vote against its measures — is regarded not as a deficiency but as an essential feature of the constitution. And the citizens of Great Britain do not assemble in a single location; the kingdom stretches from Cornwall to Caithness, and the vast majority of citizens have never set foot in the Palace of Westminster. The definition, in requiring direct participation, unanimity, and physical assembly, takes as its model one historically specific form of democratic practice — the small city-state of classical antiquity — and mistakes the features of that particular form for the necessary conditions of democracy as such.

definition: A person P is free if and only if (i) no external authority exercises coercive power over P's actions, (ii) P is subject to no legal prohibitions or obligations imposed by the state, and (iii) P is at liberty to act upon any desire or impulse without interference from other persons or institutions.
conditions: no external authority exercises coercive power over P's actions
P is subject to no legal prohibitions or obligations imposed by the state
P is at liberty to act upon any desire or impulse without interference from other persons or institutions
counterexample: Consider a citizen living under a constitutional government that maintains a system of just laws. He is subject to legal prohibitions — he may not steal, he may not assault his neighbour, he is obliged to pay taxes. The state exercises coercive power over certain of his actions; if he violates the law, he will be punished. He is not at liberty to act upon every desire without interference. Yet he is free. He lives under laws that he has had a share in making through his elected representatives, that protect his person and property, and that secure the conditions under which he may pursue his own conception of the good life.
missing_condition: The definition equates freedom with the complete absence of external constraint, yet freedom may be compatible with, and indeed may require, a framework of just laws and legitimate authority. What is wanted is a conception of freedom that distinguishes between arbitrary coercion and the rule of law, recognising that subjection to just laws is not a deprivation of liberty but a condition of it.
passage: One might propose that a person is free if and only if no external authority exercises coercive power over his actions, he is subject to no legal prohibitions or obligations imposed by the state, and he is at liberty to act upon any desire or impulse without interference. The definition appeals to a deeply felt intuition: to be free is to be unconstrained, to act as one pleases, to acknowledge no master. One thinks of the solitary individual in a state of nature, answerable to no sovereign, bound by no law, free to go where he will and do as he likes. The three conditions appear to identify what freedom essentially is — the absence of all external constraint. Yet one must attend to the citizen of a well-ordered constitutional state. An Englishman living under the rule of law in the nineteenth century is subject to a multitude of legal prohibitions and obligations. He may not steal his neighbour's property; he may not commit assault; he is obliged to pay taxes and to serve on a jury when summoned. The state exercises coercive power over certain of his actions; if he breaks the law, the magistrate will impose a penalty. He is not at liberty to act upon every desire without interference; the law constrains him at a hundred points. Yet he is free — and he is free precisely because of the law rather than in spite of it. The laws under which he lives protect his person, his property, and his liberty of thought and expression. They were made by a parliament in which he is represented, and they may be altered by the same process. They constrain him, but the constraints are not the constraints of arbitrary power; they are the constraints of a legal order that secures the conditions under which he and his fellow citizens may live together as free persons. The definition, in equating freedom with the complete absence of external constraint, mistakes one possible meaning of freedom — what might be called natural or anarchic freedom — for the only meaning, and thereby excludes the political freedom of the citizen under law, which is the form of freedom most relevant to the actual conditions of social life.

definition: Two persons A and B are equal if and only if (i) A and B possess the same natural talents and capacities, (ii) A and B occupy the same social and economic position, and (iii) A and B are treated by all other persons in precisely the same manner.
conditions: A and B possess the same natural talents and capacities
A and B occupy the same social and economic position
A and B are treated by all other persons in precisely the same manner
counterexample: Consider two citizens of a republic — a gifted physician and an unskilled labourer. Their natural talents differ markedly; the physician possesses intellectual capacities that the labourer lacks. Their social and economic positions are unequal; the physician is wealthier and more esteemed. They are not treated in precisely the same manner by all persons; the physician receives deference that the labourer does not. Yet in the relevant political sense they are equal: each has one vote, each is equal before the law, each possesses the same civil rights, and neither may be subjected to arbitrary treatment by the state.
missing_condition: The definition equates equality with identity of talents, position, and treatment, yet the equality that matters in political life is equality of standing — equality before the law, equality of civil rights, equality of political participation — which is perfectly compatible with differences in natural endowment, social position, and the manner in which persons happen to be treated in daily life.
passage: One might propose that two persons are equal if and only if they possess the same natural talents and capacities, occupy the same social and economic position, and are treated by all other persons in precisely the same manner. The definition appears to capture a robust and demanding conception of equality. If two persons are identical in talent, identical in station, and treated identically by everyone they encounter, then surely they are equal in every sense that matters. Yet the definition proves far too demanding. Consider two citizens of a well-ordered republic — a gifted physician and an unskilled labourer. The physician possesses intellectual capacities that the labourer lacks; she has a subtle and disciplined mind, trained through years of study, whilst the labourer's skills are of a wholly different kind. They do not occupy the same social and economic position; the physician earns a larger income, lives in a finer house, and moves in more cultivated circles. And they are not treated in precisely the same manner by all persons; the physician receives a deference in social life that the labourer does not, and their neighbours address them differently. None of the three conditions is satisfied. Yet in the relevant political sense, these two citizens are equal. Each has one vote in the election of representatives. Each is equal before the law; the same courts, the same procedures, the same rights of appeal are available to both. Each possesses the same civil rights — the right to speak freely, to assemble peaceably, to practise the religion of his choice, to be secure against arbitrary arrest. Neither may be subjected to treatment by the state that is not equally applicable to the other. The definition, in requiring identity of natural talent, social position, and treatment, confuses equality with uniformity, and thereby makes equality an impossible ideal rather than a political principle capable of realisation in a society of diverse and unequal human beings.

definition: A person or body X possesses authority over a group G if and only if (i) X has the power to compel obedience from the members of G by the threat or use of physical force, (ii) the members of G obey X's commands out of fear of the consequences of disobedience, and (iii) X's position is maintained by a monopoly on the instruments of coercion within the territory occupied by G.
conditions: X has the power to compel obedience from the members of G by the threat or use of physical force
the members of G obey X's commands out of fear of the consequences of disobedience
X's position is maintained by a monopoly on the instruments of coercion within the territory occupied by G
counterexample: Consider the authority of a respected elder in a traditional village community. The elder possesses no instruments of physical coercion; he commands no armed men and has no power to compel obedience by force. The villagers do not obey him out of fear; they defer to his judgement because they regard his wisdom and experience as giving him a right to guide their conduct. His position is maintained not by a monopoly on force but by the respect and trust of those over whom he exercises authority. Yet he possesses genuine authority — his pronouncements settle disputes, his counsel shapes the community's decisions, and his disapproval carries a weight that no threat of force could match.
missing_condition: The definition equates authority with coercive power, yet authority may rest upon respect, trust, and the recognition of wisdom rather than upon the capacity to inflict punishment. What is wanted is a conception of authority that distinguishes it from mere power and that accommodates the authority of those who govern by moral suasion and recognised competence rather than by force.
passage: One might propose that a person or body possesses authority over a group if and only if it has the power to compel obedience by the threat or use of physical force, the members of the group obey out of fear of the consequences of disobedience, and the authority's position is maintained by a monopoly on the instruments of coercion. The definition identifies authority with the capacity for compulsion, and it is not without plausibility. One thinks of the absolute monarch whose word is law and whose subjects obey because they know that disobedience will be met with imprisonment or worse. One thinks of the military commander whose orders are enforced by the discipline of the army and the sanction of the court-martial. In such cases, authority and coercive power appear to coincide. Yet one must attend to the authority of the village elder. In many traditional communities, disputes are settled and collective decisions are guided by the counsel of an elder — a man or woman of advanced years, long experience, and recognised wisdom. The elder possesses no instruments of physical coercion. He commands no soldiers, employs no constables, and has no power to imprison or punish those who disregard his advice. The villagers do not defer to him out of fear; they defer to him because they trust his judgement, because they recognise in his long experience a source of practical wisdom that they do not themselves possess, and because they regard his moral standing in the community as giving him a right to speak with authority on matters of common concern. His position is maintained not by a monopoly on force but by the continued respect and confidence of those over whom he exercises authority. Yet his authority is real and effective. His pronouncements settle disputes that might otherwise fester for years; his counsel shapes the community's decisions on matters of importance; and his disapproval carries a moral weight that no threat of physical punishment could match. The definition, in equating authority with coercive power, mistakes one means by which authority is sometimes maintained for the essence of authority itself.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G came to power through a process of election in which all adult citizens were entitled to vote, (ii) G governs with the ongoing consent of a majority of the governed, as expressed through periodic elections, and (iii) the authority of G is limited by a written constitution that enumerates the rights of citizens and the powers of government.
conditions: G came to power through a process of election in which all adult citizens were entitled to vote
G governs with the ongoing consent of a majority of the governed, as expressed through periodic elections
the authority of G is limited by a written constitution that enumerates the rights of citizens and the powers of government
counterexample: Consider the British government in the eighteenth century. It came to power not through universal adult suffrage but through an electoral system in which only a small fraction of the adult population was entitled to vote. Its authority was not limited by a written constitution; Great Britain possessed no single constitutional document enumerating rights and powers. Yet the government was legitimate — it was recognised as such by its subjects and by foreign observers, it governed under the rule of law, and its authority rested upon a fabric of custom, convention, and constitutional practice that, though unwritten, was no less binding for that.
missing_condition: The definition requires universal suffrage and a written constitution as necessary conditions of legitimacy, yet legitimate government may rest upon custom, convention, and constitutional practice that have never been reduced to a single written document, and may derive its authority from sources other than universal popular election.
passage: One might propose that a government is legitimate if and only if it came to power through universal adult suffrage, governs with the ongoing consent of the majority as expressed through periodic elections, and is limited by a written constitution enumerating rights and powers. The definition captures the features of one important form of legitimate government — the modern constitutional democracy with a written charter of rights. The government of the United States, for instance, satisfies all three conditions: it is elected by universal adult suffrage, it governs with the ongoing consent of the electorate, and its authority is limited by the Constitution and the Bill of Rights. One might suppose that these conditions identify what legitimacy essentially requires. Yet one must attend to the British government of the eighteenth century. The parliament that governed Great Britain in the age of Walpole and the elder Pitt came to power through an electoral system in which only a small fraction of the adult male population possessed the franchise; the great majority of men and all women were excluded from the vote. The government's authority was not limited by a written constitution; Great Britain had no single constitutional document — no charter enumerating the rights of citizens and the powers of the state in the manner of the American Constitution. The British constitution was, and remains, unwritten — a fabric of statutes, judicial decisions, conventions, and customary practices that has evolved over centuries. Yet the British government was legitimate. It was recognised as such by its subjects, who submitted to its laws and acknowledged its right to govern. It was recognised as such by foreign observers, who regarded the British system as a model of balanced and lawful government. Its authority rested upon the rule of law, upon the independence of the judiciary, upon the conventions of parliamentary government, and upon a tradition of constitutional practice stretching back to Magna Carta. The definition, in requiring universal suffrage and a written constitution, mistakes the features of one historically recent form of legitimate government for the necessary conditions of legitimacy as such.

definition: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme and undivided authority over all persons and associations within its territory, (ii) no external power exercises any jurisdiction or influence over the internal affairs of E, and (iii) E possesses sufficient military force to defend its territory against any external aggression.
conditions: E exercises supreme and undivided authority over all persons and associations within its territory
no external power exercises any jurisdiction or influence over the internal affairs of E
E possesses sufficient military force to defend its territory against any external aggression
counterexample: Consider a small European state — Luxembourg, let us say — that is a member of several international organisations and has entered into treaties that limit its freedom of action in matters of trade, monetary policy, and military affairs. External powers exercise jurisdiction over certain of its internal arrangements through treaty obligations. It does not possess sufficient military force to defend itself against a determined aggressor; its security depends upon alliances rather than upon its own arms. Yet Luxembourg is a sovereign state, recognised as such by the international community, with its own government, its own laws, and its own seat in the councils of nations.
missing_condition: The definition requires absolute internal supremacy, complete freedom from external influence, and military self-sufficiency, yet sovereignty may be compatible with the voluntary limitation of authority through treaties, with participation in international organisations that exercise jurisdiction over certain internal matters, and with military dependence upon allies. What is wanted is a conception of sovereignty that admits of the conventional limitations that sovereign states voluntarily accept in the course of international life.
passage: One might propose that a political entity is sovereign if and only if it exercises supreme and undivided authority over all persons and associations within its territory, no external power exercises any jurisdiction over its internal affairs, and it possesses sufficient military force to defend its territory against any external aggression. The definition captures an ideal of absolute sovereignty that has exercised a powerful influence upon political thought. One thinks of the great European monarchies of the seventeenth century — France under Louis XIV, for instance — in which the sovereign claimed supreme authority over all subjects and associations within the realm, acknowledged no external jurisdiction, and maintained armies capable of defending the kingdom against any aggressor. Yet one must attend to the case of a small European state. Luxembourg is a sovereign state. It has its own government, its own laws, its own parliament, its own diplomatic service. It is recognised as sovereign by every other state in the international system, and it occupies its own seat in the United Nations. Yet it does not exercise supreme and undivided authority over all matters within its territory; it has ceded certain powers to international organisations and has accepted treaty obligations that constrain its freedom of action in matters of trade, monetary policy, and the regulation of industry. External powers do exercise a form of jurisdiction over certain of its internal arrangements; the decisions of international courts and the rules of international organisations to which Luxembourg has bound itself take precedence, in specified domains, over the enactments of the Luxembourg parliament. And Luxembourg does not possess sufficient military force to defend itself against a determined aggressor; its security depends upon its membership in military alliances rather than upon its own armed strength. The definition, in requiring absolute internal supremacy, complete insulation from external jurisdiction, and military self-sufficiency, describes an ideal that no modern state fully realises and mistakes this ideal for the necessary conditions of sovereignty as such.

definition: An act A constitutes civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a public and deliberate violation of a specific law, (ii) A is performed in order to bring about the repeal of that specific law, and (iii) the person performing A willingly accepts the legal punishment prescribed for the violation.
conditions: A is a public and deliberate violation of a specific law
A is performed in order to bring about the repeal of that specific law
the person performing A willingly accepts the legal punishment prescribed for the violation
counterexample: Consider the case of a group of citizens who publicly and deliberately refuse to pay a portion of their taxes in protest against their government's prosecution of a war that they regard as unjust. They are not seeking the repeal of the tax law itself; they have no objection to taxation as such. Their disobedience is directed not at the law they violate but at a policy that the law's revenue supports. They willingly accept the legal consequences of their refusal. Yet their act is one of civil disobedience.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the act aim at the repeal of the specific law violated, yet civil disobedience may involve the violation of one law as a means of protesting a different law or a broader policy to which the violated law bears only an instrumental relation. What is wanted is a conception that admits indirect civil disobedience, in which the law violated is not itself the object of protest.
passage: One might propose that an act constitutes civil disobedience if and only if it is a public and deliberate violation of a specific law, is performed in order to bring about the repeal of that specific law, and the person performing it willingly accepts the legal punishment prescribed for the violation. The definition captures a familiar picture of civil disobedience: the protester who breaks an unjust law, openly and deliberately, precisely in order to challenge that law and bring about its repeal, and who submits to punishment as a demonstration of his respect for the legal order as a whole. One thinks of the American civil rights movement, in which protestors deliberately violated segregation ordinances — sitting at white-only lunch counters, riding in the front of buses — in order to bring about the repeal of those very ordinances, and willingly accepted arrest and imprisonment. The three conditions appear to describe the essential structure of civil disobedience. Yet one must attend to the case of the tax refuser. During a period of war, a group of citizens publicly and deliberately refuse to pay a portion of their income tax. They do not object to the tax law as such; they acknowledge the legitimacy of taxation and would pay their taxes in full if the revenue were not being used to prosecute a war that they regard as profoundly unjust. Their disobedience is directed not at the tax law but at the war policy that the tax revenue supports. The law they violate is not the law they wish to see repealed; the connection between their act of violation and the object of their protest is indirect. They accept the legal consequences of their refusal — fines, seizure of property, possibly imprisonment — as a demonstration of the depth of their conviction. Their act is one of civil disobedience, yet it does not satisfy the second condition: it is not performed in order to bring about the repeal of the law that is violated. The definition, in requiring that the violated law be the object of the protest, excludes a large and important class of acts of civil disobedience in which the law broken is not itself unjust but serves as the instrument through which a broader injustice is resisted.

definition: A person P possesses a right R if and only if (i) R is explicitly enumerated in a written legal code or constitutional document, (ii) there exists an institutional mechanism — a court, a tribunal, or some analogous body — empowered to enforce R on P's behalf, and (iii) P is aware of the existence of R and is capable of claiming it.
conditions: R is explicitly enumerated in a written legal code or constitutional document
there exists an institutional mechanism empowered to enforce R on P's behalf
P is aware of the existence of R and is capable of claiming it
counterexample: Consider an infant born in a society that recognises no written legal code and possesses no courts or tribunals. The infant is unaware of any rights and is incapable of claiming anything. Yet we should be reluctant to say that the infant possesses no rights whatever. We regard the infant as possessing a right not to be killed, a right not to be starved, and a right to such care as is necessary for its survival — rights that obtain independently of any written code, any enforcement mechanism, and any capacity on the infant's part to claim them.
missing_condition: The definition makes the possession of rights dependent upon written enumeration, institutional enforcement, and the right-holder's awareness and capacity to claim, yet rights may exist independently of all three. What is wanted is a conception of rights that admits of natural or moral rights — rights that persons possess by virtue of their humanity rather than by virtue of positive law and institutional machinery.
passage: One might propose that a person possesses a right if and only if the right is explicitly enumerated in a written legal code or constitutional document, there exists an institutional mechanism empowered to enforce the right on the person's behalf, and the person is aware of the right and capable of claiming it. The definition captures an important conception of rights — the positivist conception, according to which rights are creatures of positive law and have no existence apart from the legal and institutional framework that creates and sustains them. One thinks of the rights enumerated in the American Bill of Rights or the French Declaration of the Rights of Man — rights that are written down, that are enforceable through the courts, and that citizens may invoke and claim. The three conditions appear to identify what it is for a person to possess a right. Yet one must attend to the case of the infant. A child is born in a remote community that possesses no written legal code, no courts, no institutional machinery for the enforcement of rights. The infant is unaware of any rights and is wholly incapable of claiming anything; it is a helpless creature, dependent upon the care of others for its very survival. None of the three conditions is satisfied. Yet we should be reluctant — profoundly reluctant — to conclude that the infant possesses no rights whatever. We regard the infant as possessing a right not to be killed, a right not to be starved, a right to such care as is necessary for its survival and its development into a mature human being. These are rights that we attribute to the infant by virtue of its humanity, not by virtue of any written code or institutional mechanism. The definition, in making the possession of rights dependent upon positive law, institutional enforcement, and the capacity to claim, excludes the possibility of natural or moral rights — rights that persons possess simply by being human, independently of any legal system — and thereby fails to account for the rights of those who are most vulnerable and most in need of protection.

definition: A political system S is a democracy if and only if (i) in S the will of the majority prevails on every question of public policy without exception, (ii) S places no limits upon the range of questions that may be decided by majority vote, and (iii) the decisions of the majority in S are final and may not be reviewed or overturned by any other body.
conditions: in S the will of the majority prevails on every question of public policy without exception
S places no limits upon the range of questions that may be decided by majority vote
the decisions of the majority in S are final and may not be reviewed or overturned by any other body
counterexample: Consider a constitutional democracy in which the majority may not, by vote, abolish the freedom of the press, strip a religious minority of its civil rights, or impose retrospective criminal penalties. The will of the majority does not prevail on every question without exception; certain questions are placed beyond the reach of majority decision. The decisions of the majority may be reviewed and overturned by a constitutional court. Yet the system is a democracy — indeed, it may be argued that it is more fully democratic than a system of unrestricted majority rule, for it protects the conditions under which democratic deliberation itself can take place.
missing_condition: The definition equates democracy with unlimited majority rule, yet democracy may require — and in practice typically does require — constitutional limits upon what the majority may decide, in order to protect the rights of minorities and to preserve the conditions of democratic governance itself. What is wanted is a conception of democracy that accommodates the principle of constitutionally limited majority rule.
passage: One might propose that a political system is a democracy if and only if the will of the majority prevails on every question of public policy without exception, no limits are placed upon the range of questions that may be decided by majority vote, and the decisions of the majority are final and may not be reviewed or overturned by any other body. The definition has a certain radical simplicity; it identifies democracy with the unqualified supremacy of the popular will, and it does so without equivocation. One might suppose that any limitation upon majority rule is, by definition, a limitation upon democracy. Yet one must attend to the constitutional democracy. In the United States, in Switzerland, and in a number of other states, the will of the majority is subject to constitutional constraints. The majority may not, by vote, abolish the freedom of speech; it may not strip a religious or ethnic minority of its civil rights; it may not impose retrospective criminal penalties or suspend the writ of habeas corpus except in cases of invasion or rebellion. Certain questions are placed beyond the reach of majority decision — not because the majority's will is disregarded but because the constitution recognises that there are principles more fundamental than the preference of any transient majority. The decisions of the majority may be reviewed by a constitutional court and overturned if they are found to violate these fundamental principles. The system is a democracy — the government derives its authority from the people, elections are free and regular, and the majority's will prevails on the vast range of ordinary questions of public policy. But it is a democracy that limits itself, that places certain rights and principles beyond the reach of majoritarian decision, and that entrusts to an independent judiciary the task of ensuring that the majority does not destroy the very conditions that make democratic governance possible. The definition, in equating democracy with unlimited majority rule, mistakes one possible form of popular government for the only form, and overlooks the possibility that constitutional limits upon the majority may be not a departure from democracy but a condition of its survival.

definition: A person P is free if and only if (i) P possesses the material resources necessary to act upon their choices, (ii) P has access to a range of genuinely valuable options among which to choose, and (iii) P's choices are not shaped or constrained by the social circumstances into which they were born.
conditions: P possesses the material resources necessary to act upon their choices
P has access to a range of genuinely valuable options among which to choose
P's choices are not shaped or constrained by the social circumstances into which they were born
counterexample: Consider a monk who has taken a vow of poverty and entered a cloistered religious order. He possesses no material resources beyond the barest necessities; he has renounced property, income, and worldly goods. His range of options has been radically narrowed by his own deliberate choice; within the cloister he may pray, study, and work, but the life of the world is closed to him. His choice to enter the religious life was profoundly shaped by his upbringing in a devout family and a Catholic community. Yet he is free — he has chosen his life with the fullest deliberation, and he regards his vows not as constraints upon his freedom but as the very form of its exercise.
missing_condition: The definition requires material resources, a range of valuable options, and freedom from the influence of social circumstances, yet a person may be free in voluntarily renouncing resources and options, and all human choices are shaped by the circumstances of birth and upbringing. What is wanted is a conception of freedom that accommodates the voluntary limitation of one's own options and that recognises the inescapable role of social formation in shaping the self that chooses.
passage: One might propose that a person is free if and only if he possesses the material resources necessary to act upon his choices, has access to a range of genuinely valuable options, and his choices are not shaped or constrained by the social circumstances into which he was born. The definition captures an important positive conception of freedom — the idea that freedom requires not merely the absence of external interference but the presence of real opportunities and real capacities. A person who is formally free to travel but who lacks the money to buy a ticket is not, on this view, truly free to travel. The definition is generous and humane. Yet one must attend to the case of the contemplative monk. A young man, reared in a devout Catholic family in a small town in rural France, enters a Trappist monastery at the age of twenty-two. He takes vows of poverty, chastity, and obedience. He renounces all material possessions; his personal property is reduced to the habit he wears, a few books, and a rosary. His range of options is narrowed to the austerest minimum; within the cloister, he may pray, work in the fields, study, and attend the divine office, but the vast range of options available to the person of the world — travel, career, marriage, political engagement — is closed to him by his own deliberate act. And his choice to enter the religious life was profoundly shaped by the social circumstances of his birth; he was raised in a community where the religious vocation was honoured, where monks were respected, and where the monastic ideal was presented to him from childhood as the highest form of human life. None of the three conditions is satisfied. Yet the monk is free. He chose his life with full deliberation; he entered the monastery not under compulsion but in response to what he understood as a calling. He regards his vows not as constraints upon his freedom but as the very form of its exercise — the means by which he has freed himself from the distractions and attachments that, as he sees it, prevent most men from living as they truly wish to live. The definition, in requiring material resources, a wide range of options, and independence from social formation, fails to accommodate the freedom of the person who voluntarily renounces resources and options, and who regards the very circumstances that shaped his choice as the conditions of his liberation.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G was established through a social contract explicitly agreed to by all members of the political community, (ii) G acts solely in the interests of those who consented to its authority, and (iii) any member of the community may withdraw their consent and thereby dissolve their obligation to obey G.
conditions: G was established through a social contract explicitly agreed to by all members of the political community
G acts solely in the interests of those who consented to its authority
any member of the community may withdraw their consent and thereby dissolve their obligation to obey G
counterexample: Consider a person born into a long-established political community — the kingdom of Denmark, let us say. He did not explicitly agree to any social contract; the political order into which he was born was established centuries before his birth, and he was never asked to consent to it. The government does not act solely in the interests of those who consented; it acts, or purports to act, in the interests of all, including those who were born into its jurisdiction without being consulted. And the citizen may not simply withdraw his consent and dissolve his obligation to obey the law; emigration is possible, but the state does not recognise a unilateral right of individual withdrawal from political obligation. Yet the Danish government is legitimate, and its legitimacy does not depend upon the explicit consent of each individual citizen.
missing_condition: The definition makes legitimacy depend upon an explicit act of consent by each individual, yet no actual government has been established through such a process. What is wanted is a conception of legitimacy that accommodates the fact that political authority is typically inherited rather than contracted for, and that obligation may arise from membership in a political community rather than from an explicit act of individual agreement.
passage: One might propose that a government is legitimate if and only if it was established through a social contract explicitly agreed to by all members of the political community, acts solely in the interests of those who consented, and allows any member to withdraw his consent and dissolve his obligation to obey. The definition has a distinguished philosophical pedigree; it draws upon a tradition of contractarian thought that stretches from Hobbes and Locke to the present day — a tradition whose assumptions Margaret MacDonald subjected to searching criticism in her analysis of the language of political theory. The idea that legitimate government rests upon the consent of the governed is among the most powerful ideas in the history of political thought, and the social contract provides a vivid and intuitively appealing model of how such consent might be given. Yet one must attend to the ordinary citizen of an established state. A man is born in Denmark in the year 1890. He did not choose to be born there; he did not explicitly agree to any social contract; the political order under which he lives was established centuries before his birth, through processes of conquest, settlement, custom, and constitutional evolution in which he had no part and to which he was never asked to consent. The government does not act solely in the interests of those who consented to its authority, for no one living has consented; it acts, or claims to act, in the interests of all its subjects, including those who were born into its jurisdiction without being consulted.
