And the citizen may not simply withdraw his consent and dissolve his obligation to obey the law; emigration is a practical possibility, but the Danish state does not recognise a right of individual withdrawal from political obligation whilst the citizen remains within its territory. None of the three conditions is satisfied. Yet the Danish government is legitimate — it is a government under law, recognised by its subjects and by the international community as possessing lawful authority. The definition, in requiring explicit individual consent, mistakes a philosophical idealisation for a necessary condition and thereby renders every actual government illegitimate, which is a reductio of the definition rather than a discovery about the nature of political authority.

definition: An act A constitutes civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is performed by an individual acting alone and on their own initiative, (ii) A is motivated by the individual's personal moral or religious convictions, and (iii) the individual performing A seeks no political alliance or collective support but acts as a solitary witness to their own conscience.
conditions: A is performed by an individual acting alone and on their own initiative
A is motivated by the individual's personal moral or religious convictions
the individual performing A seeks no political alliance or collective support but acts as a solitary witness to their own conscience
counterexample: Consider the organised refusal of a group of factory workers to comply with a new law requiring them to inform upon their colleagues' political affiliations. The workers act collectively; they have discussed the matter among themselves and have agreed upon a common course of action. Their refusal is motivated not solely by personal conscience but by a shared political conviction about the injustice of the law and its threat to the solidarity of their community. They seek collective support and present their refusal as the act of a group rather than of isolated individuals. Yet their act is one of civil disobedience.
missing_condition: The definition restricts civil disobedience to solitary, conscience-driven acts, yet civil disobedience may be — and historically has most often been — a collective and politically motivated enterprise. What is wanted is a conception that admits of organised, collective disobedience undertaken for reasons that are political as well as moral.
passage: One might propose that an act constitutes civil disobedience if and only if it is performed by an individual acting alone and on his own initiative, is motivated by personal moral or religious convictions, and involves the individual seeking no political alliance or collective support but acting as a solitary witness to his own conscience. The definition captures a certain romantic image of the conscientious objector — the lone individual who, moved by an inner conviction that a law is unjust, refuses to comply with it, accepting whatever consequences follow, and making no effort to enlist others in his cause. One thinks perhaps of Thoreau, who refused to pay his poll tax as a solitary act of moral witness against slavery and the Mexican war. Yet one must attend to the case of collective refusal. A group of factory workers in a European city are confronted with a new law requiring them to report the political affiliations and associations of their colleagues to the authorities. The workers regard the law as an instrument of tyranny — a device for identifying and persecuting those who hold disfavoured political views. They discuss the matter among themselves, in their homes and in the back rooms of public houses, and they agree upon a common course of action: they will refuse, collectively and publicly, to comply with the law. Their refusal is motivated not solely by personal conscience but by a shared political conviction — a conviction about the injustice of the law, about the value of solidarity among working people, and about the duty of citizens to resist measures that would make them instruments of oppression. They seek collective support; they present their refusal as the act of a group, and they draw strength and courage from the knowledge that they are not acting alone. Their act is one of civil disobedience — it is public, deliberate, principled, and nonviolent. But it is not the act of a solitary individual; it is the act of a community. The definition, in restricting civil disobedience to the solitary witness of individual conscience, mistakes one possible form of principled disobedience for the only form, and thereby excludes the collective, politically motivated acts of resistance that constitute the most historically significant instances of civil disobedience.

definition: A state S is a democracy if and only if (i) the government of S is chosen through elections in which all adult citizens may vote, (ii) elections are held at regular intervals specified by law, and (iii) the party or coalition that receives the most votes forms the government.
conditions: the government of S is chosen through elections in which all adult citizens may vote
elections are held at regular intervals specified by law
the party or coalition that receives the most votes forms the government
counterexample: Consider a state in which universal adult suffrage obtains, elections are held punctually every four years as the constitution prescribes, and the party that receives the greatest number of votes duly forms the government. Yet between elections, the ruling party suppresses all opposition newspapers, imprisons dissident politicians, and forbids public assemblies critical of the government. When the next election arrives, the electorate has no access to independent information and no means of organising an effective opposition. All three conditions are formally satisfied, yet the state is not a democracy in any meaningful sense; it is an authoritarian regime that has adopted the outward trappings of electoral procedure.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require the protection of civil liberties — freedom of speech, freedom of the press, freedom of assembly — that are necessary for elections to be genuinely free and fair. Without such liberties, the electoral mechanism becomes an empty formality.
passage: One might propose that a state is a democracy if and only if its government is chosen through elections in which all adult citizens may vote, elections are held at regular intervals specified by law, and the party or coalition receiving the most votes forms the government. The definition appears to identify the essential procedural features of democratic governance. In a state satisfying these conditions, the people choose their rulers, the process is regular and predictable, and the popular will is translated into governmental power. Yet consider a state in which these conditions are met to the letter. Universal adult suffrage obtains; elections are held every four years as the written constitution prescribes; and the party that wins the most votes assumes office without impediment. But between elections, the ruling party systematically suppresses the opposition. Independent newspapers are shut down or brought under state control. Politicians who criticise the government are arrested and imprisoned. Public meetings at which dissenting views might be aired are prohibited. When election day arrives, the citizenry possesses no independent source of information about the government's record, no organised opposition to which it might turn, and no assurance that a vote for an alternative party will not bring retribution. All three conditions of the definition are formally satisfied — universal franchise, regular elections, majority rule — yet the state is plainly not a democracy. The definition has attended only to the mechanical procedure of voting and has overlooked the conditions under which voting becomes meaningful, namely the protection of those civil liberties without which an informed and free choice is impossible.

definition: A person P is free if and only if (i) no external agent physically prevents P from performing any action P wishes to perform, (ii) no law or regulation prohibits P from performing any action P wishes to perform, and (iii) P is not subject to coercion — that is, no one threatens P with harm as a consequence of P's performing any action.
conditions: no external agent physically prevents P from performing any action P wishes to perform
no law or regulation prohibits P from performing any action P wishes to perform
P is not subject to coercion
counterexample: Consider a person who has been raised from infancy in a closed community and has been so thoroughly indoctrinated that he desires only what his masters wish him to desire. No one physically restrains him; the community has no written laws prohibiting any action; and no one threatens him with harm, for there is no need — he never wishes to do anything of which his masters would disapprove. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one would hesitate to call this man free, for his very desires have been shaped by those who control him, and he lacks the capacity to form independent purposes.
missing_condition: The definition attends only to the absence of external impediments and neglects the internal conditions of freedom. A person whose desires have been formed by manipulation, indoctrination, or extreme deprivation may face no external obstacle yet still lack freedom in any robust sense. A complete account of freedom must require some degree of autonomy in the formation of one's own desires and purposes.
passage: One might propose that a person is free if and only if no external agent physically prevents him from performing any action he wishes to perform, no law or regulation prohibits any action he wishes to perform, and no one coerces him by threatening harm. The definition identifies freedom with the absence of external impediment, in a manner broadly consonant with the tradition that runs from Hobbes to the modern liberal theorists. A citizen of a tolerant state, facing no physical barriers, no legal prohibitions upon his preferred activities, and no threats from other persons, would count as free under this analysis, and rightly so. Yet consider the following case. A man has been raised from earliest infancy in a closed community governed by a small group of elders. From childhood he has been subjected to a comprehensive regime of indoctrination designed to ensure that he desires only what the elders wish him to desire. He has been told that the outside world is dangerous and wicked, that the community's way of life is the sole path to well-being, and that to wish for anything the elders have not sanctioned would be a mark of corruption. The indoctrination has succeeded perfectly. The man desires nothing that the elders would prohibit. No one physically restrains him, for there is nothing he wishes to do that would require restraint. No law prohibits any action he wishes to perform, for he wishes only to perform those actions the community encourages. No one threatens him, for compliance is willing and complete. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one has the strongest intuition that this man is not free. His desires themselves have been manufactured by those who control him, and he lacks the very capacity to form purposes of his own.

definition: A society S achieves equality if and only if (i) every member of S possesses the same legal rights and duties, (ii) no member of S is accorded a higher social rank or status than any other by virtue of birth, and (iii) every member of S has equal access to public offices and positions of honour.
conditions: every member possesses the same legal rights and duties
no member is accorded a higher social rank by virtue of birth
every member has equal access to public offices and positions of honour
counterexample: Consider a society in which the law recognises no distinctions of birth — there is no hereditary nobility, no caste system, no legal privileges attaching to family origin. Every citizen possesses the same legal rights, bears the same legal duties, and is eligible for any public office. Yet the distribution of wealth in this society is so extreme that a small number of families control the vast preponderance of productive land and capital, while the majority live in penury. The wealthy can afford the education, leisure, and connections necessary to secure public office; the poor cannot. All three conditions of the definition are formally satisfied, yet the society is manifestly unequal.
missing_condition: The definition attends only to formal legal and status equality and ignores the material conditions that render formal equality effective. Substantive equality requires not merely equal rights and the absence of hereditary rank but also a distribution of resources sufficient to give every member a genuine opportunity to exercise those rights and access those offices.
passage: One might propose that a society achieves equality if and only if every member possesses the same legal rights and duties, no member is accorded a higher social rank by virtue of birth, and every member has equal access to public offices and positions of honour. The definition captures what might be termed formal equality: equality before the law, the abolition of hereditary privilege, and open competition for public positions. A society that abolished its aristocracy, extended the franchise to all, and opened its civil service to merit would satisfy these conditions. Yet consider a society in which these formal conditions are met. The law draws no distinctions of birth. Every citizen has the right to vote, to own property, to seek legal redress, and to stand for public office. Hereditary titles have been abolished, and no caste or estate enjoys special legal privileges. But the distribution of material wealth in this society is extraordinarily unequal. A handful of families possess the great bulk of the land and industrial capital, while the majority of citizens own nothing beyond the bare necessities of subsistence. The children of the wealthy attend excellent schools, form connexions with persons of influence, and enter public life with every advantage. The children of the poor receive a meagre education, must begin labouring in early youth, and have neither the leisure nor the resources to compete for public office. Formal access to public positions is universal, but effective access is confined to a narrow stratum. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied, yet the society is profoundly unequal. The definition has mistaken the absence of legal barriers for the achievement of equality itself.

definition: An agent A has authority over a group G if and only if (i) A issues commands to the members of G, (ii) the members of G habitually obey A's commands, and (iii) A possesses the physical power to compel obedience should any member of G refuse.
conditions: A issues commands to the members of G
the members of G habitually obey A's commands
A possesses the physical power to compel obedience
counterexample: Consider a bandit who has seized control of a mountain pass. She issues commands to all travellers — they must surrender a portion of their goods — and the travellers habitually comply, for the bandit commands a band of armed women who will enforce her demands. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the bandit has no authority in any legitimate sense. She has power, certainly, but authority implies a right to command and a corresponding obligation to obey, neither of which the bandit possesses. The definition has confused authority with mere coercive power.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that A's power to command be grounded in some normative basis — law, consent, tradition, or moral right — that would distinguish legitimate authority from the naked exercise of force. Without such a normative foundation, what the definition describes is domination, not authority.
passage: One might propose that an agent has authority over a group if and only if the agent issues commands to the members of the group, the members habitually obey, and the agent possesses the physical power to compel obedience should anyone refuse. The definition appears to capture the essential features of authority: command, compliance, and the capacity to enforce. A military officer commands her soldiers, the soldiers obey, and the officer may discipline those who do not comply. The conditions seem jointly sufficient. Yet one must consider the case of a bandit who has established herself at a mountain pass. The bandit issues commands to every traveller who approaches: she demands a portion of their goods and directs them to lay down their arms. The travellers habitually obey, for the bandit is attended by a band of armed ruffians who will beat, rob, or kill anyone who resists. The bandit possesses ample physical power to compel obedience. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. But no one would say that the bandit has authority over the travellers in any philosophically interesting sense. She has power over them — that is undeniable — but authority implies something more than the capacity to compel. It implies a right to command, and a corresponding obligation on the part of the subject to obey, grounded in some normative basis such as law, consent, or tradition. The definition has failed to distinguish between legitimate authority and mere domination, between the magistrate who commands by right and the highwayman who commands by force.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G came to power through a process recognised as lawful by the constitution of the state, (ii) G governs with the express consent of the majority of its citizens, and (iii) G has been recognised as legitimate by other sovereign states.
conditions: G came to power through a lawful constitutional process
G governs with the express consent of the majority of its citizens
G has been recognised as legitimate by other sovereign states
counterexample: Consider a revolutionary government that has overthrown a corrupt and tyrannical regime. The revolution was not contemplated by the old constitution, so the new government did not come to power through any process that the prior constitutional order would recognise as lawful. The first condition fails. Yet the revolutionary government has the overwhelming support of the population, governs justly, and proceeds to establish a new constitutional order. Few would deny it legitimacy on the sole ground that it did not observe the procedures of the constitution it displaced.
missing_condition: The definition makes constitutional continuity a necessary condition of legitimacy, but legitimate governments may arise from revolutionary breaks with prior constitutional orders. What matters is not procedural continuity with the old order but whether the new government rules justly and with the genuine support of the people it governs.
passage: One might propose that a government is legitimate if and only if it came to power through a process recognised as lawful by the state's constitution, it governs with the express consent of the majority of its citizens, and it has been recognised as legitimate by other sovereign states. The definition captures the procedural aspect of legitimacy — constitutional succession — and adds both popular consent and international recognition. A government elected under the provisions of a written constitution, enjoying the confidence of the people, and acknowledged by the community of nations would satisfy all three conditions, and rightly so. But consider a revolutionary government that has come to power by overthrowing a regime universally regarded as tyrannical. The old constitution was the creature of the deposed tyrant and made no provision for its own supersession by popular revolution. The revolutionary government therefore did not come to power through any process the old constitution would recognise as lawful; the first condition fails. Yet the new government commands the enthusiastic support of the vast preponderance of the population. It governs justly, establishes the rule of law, and convenes a constituent assembly to draft a new constitution reflecting the will of the people. To deny this government legitimacy merely because it violated the constitutional procedures of the regime it displaced would be to elevate procedural form above substantive justice. The definition has treated constitutional continuity as essential to legitimacy, when in truth a government may acquire legitimacy through the justice of its cause and the consent of those it governs, even if it has broken entirely with the constitutional order that preceded it.

definition: A state S is sovereign if and only if (i) S possesses a supreme authority within its territory that is not subordinate to any higher political body, (ii) S controls all armed forces operating within its borders, and (iii) S maintains exclusive jurisdiction over all persons within its territory.
conditions: S possesses a supreme authority within its territory not subordinate to any higher political body
S controls all armed forces operating within its borders
S maintains exclusive jurisdiction over all persons within its territory
counterexample: Consider a state that has entered into a military alliance under the terms of which allied forces are stationed within its territory and are subject to the military jurisdiction of the alliance command rather than to the courts of the host state. Foreign soldiers accused of offences while on duty are tried by their own military tribunals. The second and third conditions fail: the state does not control all armed forces within its borders, and it does not maintain exclusive jurisdiction over all persons on its soil. Yet the state is sovereign; it entered the alliance voluntarily and may withdraw from it at will.
missing_condition: The definition requires exclusive control of armed forces and exclusive jurisdiction over all persons, but sovereign states routinely consent to limitations upon these powers through treaties, alliances, and international agreements without thereby ceasing to be sovereign. What is essential to sovereignty is the capacity to consent to or withdraw from such arrangements, not the absence of all shared or delegated authority.
passage: One might propose that a state is sovereign if and only if it possesses a supreme authority within its territory not subordinate to any higher political body, it controls all armed forces operating within its borders, and it maintains exclusive jurisdiction over all persons within its territory. The definition has the merit of capturing the classical absolutist conception of sovereignty. A state that is supreme within its borders, commands every soldier on its soil, and subjects every person within its territory to its own courts would seem to be sovereign in the fullest sense. But consider a state — an independent and self-governing nation — that has entered into a military alliance for the purpose of collective defence. Under the terms of the alliance treaty, soldiers from allied nations are stationed on the state's territory. These foreign soldiers are subject not to the host state's criminal courts but to the military jurisdiction of their own countries; an allied soldier accused of an offence is tried by a tribunal of his own nation. The state does not control the allied forces stationed within its borders — they answer to an integrated command structure directed by the alliance as a whole. The second and third conditions of the definition are violated. Yet no one would deny that the host state is sovereign. It entered the alliance by its own free decision, it may withdraw from the treaty at any time, and the presence of allied forces is a consequence of its own sovereign act. The definition has confused the absolute and unrestricted exercise of state power with sovereignty itself, when in truth a sovereign state may voluntarily limit the exercise of its powers without ceasing to be sovereign.

definition: An act A is an instance of civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a deliberate violation of a law, (ii) A is performed publicly and with full transparency as to the agent's identity and motives, and (iii) the agent who performs A willingly accepts the legal punishment that follows.
conditions: A is a deliberate violation of a law
A is performed publicly and with full transparency
the agent willingly accepts the legal punishment that follows
counterexample: Consider the case of a network of citizens who, during a period of unjust conscription, secretly forge identity documents so that young men may avoid being called up for a war they regard as wicked. The forgers are deliberately violating the law; they regard their actions as a form of moral protest against an unjust policy. But they act in secret rather than publicly, for publicity would lead to the immediate arrest of the young men they are trying to protect and would defeat the purpose of the enterprise. The second condition is not satisfied. Yet many moral philosophers would regard such clandestine resistance to an unjust law as a genuine form of civil disobedience, motivated by conscience and directed against injustice.
missing_condition: The definition requires publicity and transparency, but there are cases of conscientious law-breaking in which secrecy is essential to the moral purpose of the act. What distinguishes civil disobedience from ordinary criminality is not publicity but the conscientious character of the motivation and the aim of protesting or remedying injustice.
passage: One might propose that an act is an instance of civil disobedience if and only if it is a deliberate violation of a law, it is performed publicly and with full transparency as to the agent's identity and motives, and the agent willingly accepts the legal punishment that follows. The definition captures the paradigmatic cases admirably. When a group of citizens occupies a segregated lunch counter, deliberately violating the local ordinance that forbids them from being served, they act publicly and make no attempt to conceal their identities or motives. When they are arrested, they submit to the legal process without resistance. This is civil disobedience as ordinarily understood. Yet one must consider a case of a different character. During a period of unjust conscription — suppose a state requires its young men to serve in a war that is widely regarded as aggressive and wicked — a network of citizens begins to forge identity documents so that conscripted men may escape service. The forgers are deliberately violating the law; they regard their actions as an act of moral protest against a policy they consider deeply unjust. But they act in strict secrecy, for if they were to forge documents publicly and transparently, the young men they seek to protect would be immediately identified and arrested. The very purpose of the act requires concealment. The second condition of the definition is violated. Yet one might well regard this clandestine resistance as a genuine form of civil disobedience: it is motivated by conscience, directed against injustice, and undertaken at considerable personal risk. The definition has taken a feature of certain conspicuous forms of civil disobedience — publicity — and treated it as essential, when in truth what is essential is the conscientious refusal to comply with a law one judges to be unjust.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) there exists a law enacted by the state that entitles P to X, (ii) the state possesses institutions capable of enforcing P's entitlement to X, and (iii) P is a citizen of the state that enacted the law.
conditions: there exists a law enacted by the state that entitles P to X
the state possesses institutions capable of enforcing P's entitlement
P is a citizen of the state that enacted the law
counterexample: Consider a foreign traveller passing through a state of which he is not a citizen. He is set upon by brigands and robbed. One would say, without hesitation, that his right not to be robbed has been violated, even though he is not a citizen of the state in which the robbery occurred. The third condition fails — P is not a citizen — yet the right plainly exists. Moreover, the tradition of natural rights holds that certain rights belong to persons as such, regardless of what any state has enacted.
missing_condition: The definition restricts rights to citizens and to what positive law has enacted, but rights may belong to persons irrespective of citizenship, and some rights — if the natural-rights tradition is correct — exist prior to and independently of any state's enactment. The definition has conflated the legal positivist account of rights with the concept of rights itself.
passage: One might propose that a person has a right to something if and only if there exists a law enacted by the state that entitles the person to it, the state possesses institutions capable of enforcing that entitlement, and the person is a citizen of the state that enacted the law. The definition has the advantage of precision: it locates rights firmly within the domain of positive law and institutional enforcement. A citizen of a state that has enacted a law guaranteeing freedom of worship, and that possesses courts and police to enforce that guarantee, has a right to freedom of worship under this analysis. But consider a foreign traveller who is passing through a state of which he is not a citizen. He is attacked and robbed by brigands. One would say, with considerable confidence, that the traveller's rights have been violated — specifically, his right not to be despoiled of his property by violence. Yet the traveller is not a citizen of the state in which the robbery occurred; the third condition fails. Furthermore, many philosophical traditions hold that certain rights — the right to life, the right to liberty, the right not to be subjected to torture — belong to human beings as such, irrespective of what any particular state has enacted and irrespective of whether any state possesses institutions capable of enforcement. A person held in slavery has a right to liberty even if no law in his country recognises that right and no institution exists to enforce it. The definition has identified rights with their legal positivisation, when in truth the concept of a right is broader than the concept of a legally enacted and enforced entitlement.

definition: An act A is an instance of civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a violation of a law that the agent regards as unjust, (ii) A is non-violent, and (iii) A is motivated by a desire to change the law in question.
conditions: A is a violation of a law that the agent regards as unjust
A is non-violent
A is motivated by a desire to change the law in question
counterexample: Consider the case of a Quaker who, during a period of compulsory military service, refuses to report for duty. He violates the conscription law, and he does so non-violently — he simply fails to appear. But his motive is not to change the law. He has no expectation that his refusal will lead to the repeal of conscription; he acts solely from a conscientious conviction that it would be wicked for him, personally, to take up arms. His aim is to preserve the integrity of his own moral convictions, not to alter the legal order. The third condition fails. Yet his refusal is widely regarded as a form of civil disobedience — indeed, as one of its most venerable forms.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the agent be motivated by a desire to change the law, but some acts of civil disobedience are motivated by personal conscience rather than by any ambition to reform the legal system. What distinguishes civil disobedience from ordinary criminality is the conscientious character of the agent's refusal, not necessarily a reformist intention.
passage: One might propose that an act is an instance of civil disobedience if and only if it is a violation of a law the agent regards as unjust, it is non-violent, and it is motivated by a desire to change the law in question. The definition captures many paradigmatic cases: the sit-in at a segregated lunch counter, the march in defiance of an unjust prohibition, the public burning of a draft card — in each case the agent violates a law he considers unjust, does so without violence, and aims thereby to bring about the law's repeal or amendment. But consider the case of a member of the Society of Friends — a Quaker — who, during a period of compulsory military service, simply refuses to report for duty. He is violating the conscription law, and he regards that law as unjust, for he believes that all war is contrary to the will of God. His refusal is non-violent in the most literal sense. Yet his motive is not to change the law. He has no programme of legal reform; he does not expect his solitary refusal to bring about the repeal of conscription. He acts from a conviction of personal conscience — the conviction that it would be sinful for him to take up arms, regardless of what the law may require. His aim is to preserve the integrity of his own soul, not to alter the statute book. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet the Quaker's conscientious refusal is widely and rightly regarded as a form of civil disobedience, and it has been cited as such by philosophers and jurists for centuries. The definition has confused a feature of certain politically motivated acts of civil disobedience — the desire to change the law — with an essential condition, when in truth civil disobedience may be motivated by personal conscience without any reformist ambition.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) P's right to X is recognised by the customs and traditions of the community to which P belongs, (ii) the community possesses informal mechanisms — social censure, ostracism, or collective action — for enforcing P's entitlement to X, and (iii) the right to X has been exercised by members of P's community for a substantial period of time.
conditions: P's right to X is recognised by the customs and traditions of the community
the community possesses informal mechanisms for enforcing P's entitlement
the right to X has been exercised by members of the community for a substantial period of time
counterexample: Consider a community in which it has long been customary for the eldest son to inherit the entirety of his father's property. This custom is enforced by social censure — any family that departs from it is ostracised — and it has been practised for generations. By the proposed definition, the eldest son has a right to the whole inheritance. Now consider the younger sons and the daughters, who receive nothing. They have no customary right to a share of the inheritance; no tradition supports their claim; and no informal mechanism enforces any entitlement on their behalf. By the definition, they have no right to a share. Yet one might well contend that they do have such a right — a right grounded not in custom but in a principle of fairness that the custom violates.
missing_condition: The definition grounds rights entirely in established custom and tradition, but customs may themselves be unjust. A satisfactory account of rights must allow for the possibility that persons possess rights that their communities have never recognised and that no tradition supports — rights grounded in principles of justice or human dignity that transcend local practice.
passage: One might propose that a person has a right to something if and only if the right is recognised by the customs and traditions of the person's community, the community possesses informal mechanisms for enforcing the entitlement, and the right has been exercised by members of the community for a substantial period. The definition anchors rights in established social practice and avoids the difficulties of appealing to abstract principles. A villager's right to graze cattle on the common land, for instance, is recognised by long-standing custom, enforced by the collective censure of those who overgraze, and has been exercised for generations. The conditions are satisfied, and the right is genuine. But consider a community in which it is the immemorial custom that the eldest son inherits the whole of his father's estate. The custom is enforced by vigorous social censure: any family that divides the inheritance contrary to custom is shunned. The practice has persisted for as long as the community's records extend. By the proposed definition, the eldest son has a right to the entire inheritance. Now consider his younger brothers and his sisters. They receive nothing under the custom. No tradition recognises their claim to a share; no informal mechanism enforces any entitlement on their behalf. By the definition, they have no right to any portion of the estate. Yet one has the strong intuition that the younger children are being treated unjustly — that they have a right to a fair share of the inheritance, a right that the community's customs happen not to recognise. The definition has identified rights with customs, but customs may be unjust, and the concept of a right must be capable of reaching beyond established practice to ground claims that prevailing tradition has failed to acknowledge.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G promotes the material welfare of the population it governs, (ii) G maintains public order and security within its territory, and (iii) G enforces contracts and protects property rights.
conditions: G promotes the material welfare of the population
G maintains public order and security
G enforces contracts and protects property rights
counterexample: Consider a benevolent despotism in which the ruler has seized power by military force, has never consulted the people, and permits no political participation of any kind. The despot governs efficiently: he builds roads, establishes hospitals, suppresses banditry, and maintains a rigorous system of contract enforcement and property protection. The population is materially prosperous and secure. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet many would deny the government's legitimacy, for it rests upon no foundation of popular consent or constitutional authority. The people have had no say in their governance and possess no means of changing their rulers.
missing_condition: The definition identifies legitimacy with effective governance — material welfare, order, and the protection of rights — but a government may provide all these benefits and yet lack legitimacy because it has not been authorised by those it governs. Legitimacy requires some connexion between the government and the will or consent of the people, not merely the efficient delivery of public goods.
passage: One might propose that a government is legitimate if and only if it promotes the material welfare of the population, maintains public order and security, and enforces contracts and protects property rights. The definition has the appeal of practicality: it judges a government by its results rather than by the manner in which it acquired power. A government that feeds the hungry, keeps the peace, and upholds the rule of law would seem to have established its right to govern by its very performance. Yet consider a ruler who has come to power by a military coup, dismissing the elected assembly and suspending the constitution. He permits no elections, no free press, and no political opposition. But he governs with great efficiency. He invests in public works — roads, hospitals, schools — and the material condition of the population improves markedly under his rule. He maintains order with a firm hand, and crime declines. He enforces commercial contracts faithfully and protects property with scrupulous care. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet many would deny that this government is legitimate. It rules without the consent of the governed; it has no constitutional basis; and the people possess no peaceful means of replacing their ruler should they wish to do so. The definition has confused effective governance with legitimate governance — an understandable confusion, perhaps, but a confusion nonetheless. A government may deliver admirable results and yet lack the authority that comes from the consent or participation of those it governs.

definition: A state S is sovereign if and only if (i) S has the sole authority to make and enforce laws within its territory, (ii) no external power exercises any governmental function within S's borders, and (iii) S controls all trade and commerce that crosses its frontiers.
conditions: S has the sole authority to make and enforce laws within its territory
no external power exercises any governmental function within S's borders
S controls all trade and commerce that crosses its frontiers
counterexample: Consider a state that has joined a customs union with several of its neighbours. Under the terms of the treaty, external tariffs are set not by any single member state but by the union as a whole, and goods move freely across the internal borders of the union without customs inspection. The state no longer controls all trade and commerce that crosses its frontiers; the third condition fails. Yet the state has entered the customs union by its own sovereign decision, and no one would deny that it remains a sovereign state. Many of the most powerful nations in the world belong to such arrangements.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the state control all cross-border trade, but sovereign states routinely delegate or share authority over trade through treaties and international organisations. Sovereignty consists in the ultimate authority to enter into or withdraw from such arrangements, not in the actual exercise of control over every governmental function at every moment.
passage: One might propose that a state is sovereign if and only if it has the sole authority to make and enforce laws within its territory, no external power exercises any governmental function within its borders, and it controls all trade and commerce that crosses its frontiers. The definition captures a maximalist conception of sovereignty — the state as the sole and unqualified master of its domain. A state that makes its own laws, admits no foreign governmental activity on its soil, and inspects and taxes every item of goods that enters or leaves its territory would satisfy all three conditions. Yet consider a state that has joined a customs union with its neighbours. Under the treaty establishing the union, external tariffs on imported goods are determined not by any individual member state but by a council representing all the member states collectively. Goods produced within any member state cross the internal frontiers of the union without customs inspection or duties. The state in question no longer controls all trade and commerce crossing its frontiers; the external tariff is set by the union, and internal trade is free. The third condition of the definition fails. But the state is sovereign nonetheless. It entered the customs union by an act of its own parliament; it may withdraw from the union by the same means. Its participation in the arrangement is an exercise of sovereignty, not an abrogation of it. The definition has confused the sovereign state's authority to control its trade with the requirement that it actually exercise exclusive control at every moment, overlooking the possibility that a sovereign state may voluntarily share or delegate certain functions without thereby ceasing to be sovereign.

definition: A system of government G is a democracy if and only if (i) the adult citizens of the polity have the right to vote in periodic elections, (ii) those elections determine who shall hold political office and exercise legislative power, and (iii) all adult citizens are eligible to stand as candidates for office.
conditions: The adult citizens have the right to vote in periodic elections
Those elections determine who holds political office and exercises legislative power
All adult citizens are eligible to stand as candidates for office
counterexample: Consider ancient Athens in the fifth century before Christ, which is widely regarded as the original and paradigmatic democracy. The citizens of Athens did not elect representatives to hold office; instead, they assembled in person to deliberate and vote upon legislation directly. Most public offices were filled not by election but by lot — a random selection from among the citizen body. The second condition — that elections determine who holds office — fails entirely, for elections played a minor role in Athenian governance. Yet Athens is the very model of a democracy, and to deny it that title would be to distort the concept beyond recognition.
missing_condition: The definition requires that elections determine officeholders, but democratic self-governance may operate through direct assembly, sortition, or other mechanisms that bypass elections altogether, as in the Athenian original.
passage: One might define a democracy as a system of government in which the adult citizens of the polity have the right to vote in periodic elections, those elections determine who shall hold political office and exercise legislative power, and all adult citizens are eligible to stand as candidates for office. This definition has the advantage of precision and appears to describe the modern representative democracies with tolerable accuracy. In such systems, citizens go to the polls at regular intervals, choose among candidates, and the successful candidates take office and legislate. All three conditions are satisfied in the case of, say, the British Parliament after the Reform Acts, or the French Republic after the establishment of universal manhood suffrage. Yet one must consider the case of ancient Athens in the fifth century before Christ. Athens is universally acknowledged to be the paradigmatic democracy — indeed, the very word demokratia is of Athenian origin. But the Athenian system bore little resemblance to the definition given above. The citizens of Athens did not elect representatives to hold office and legislate on their behalf; they assembled in person — in the Pnyx, on the hill facing the Acropolis — to deliberate and vote upon legislation directly. The assembly was not a body of elected representatives but a gathering of the citizen body itself. Moreover, most public offices in Athens were filled not by election but by lot. The members of the Council of Five Hundred, the magistrates, the jurors — all were selected by a random process from among the eligible citizens. Election was reserved for a few specialised offices, such as the generalship, where particular competence was thought to be required. The second condition of the definition — that elections determine who holds political office — fails entirely, for the Athenian system relied upon direct participation and sortition rather than upon elections. Yet to deny that Athens was a democracy would be to deny the name to the very polity that invented the thing. The definition has taken the institutions of modern representative democracy and treated them as necessary conditions of democracy as such.

definition: A person P is free if and only if (i) P is not subject to physical restraint — that is, P is not imprisoned, chained, or otherwise physically prevented from moving as P wishes, (ii) P is not subject to legal prohibition that prevents P from performing the actions P wishes to perform, and (iii) no other person or institution is issuing commands that P is compelled to obey.
conditions: P is not subject to physical restraint
P is not subject to legal prohibition preventing desired actions
No other person or institution is issuing commands that P is compelled to obey
counterexample: Consider a person who lives in a society without physical restraints upon his movement, without legal prohibitions upon his desired activities, and without any person or institution issuing commands to him. He is, by all external measures, free. Yet he is addicted to opium. His addiction dominates his life; he cannot refrain from seeking and consuming the drug, though he wishes desperately to stop. He recognises that the addiction has destroyed his health, his relationships, and his capacity for the pursuits he once valued. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied — there is no physical restraint, no legal prohibition, no external compulsion — yet we should hesitate to say that this man is free.
missing_condition: The definition captures only external obstacles to freedom — restraints, prohibitions, commands — but ignores internal obstacles such as addiction, compulsion, and psychological incapacity, which may deprive a person of freedom as effectively as any chain or law.
passage: It appears natural to define freedom by saying that a person is free if and only if the person is not subject to physical restraint, is not subject to legal prohibition that prevents desired actions, and is not compelled by any other person or institution to obey commands. This analysis captures much of what we ordinarily mean by freedom. A prisoner in gaol is not free, for he is physically restrained. A citizen who is forbidden by law from speaking his mind is not free, at least in respect of speech. A soldier under the orders of a tyrannical officer is not free, for he is compelled to obey commands not of his choosing. Each of these cases involves the failure of one of the three conditions, and in each case the denial of freedom seems warranted. Yet one must consider a case of a rather different kind. A man lives in a society that imposes no physical restraints upon his movement — he may go where he pleases. The law does not prohibit any of the activities he wishes to pursue. No person or institution issues commands that he is compelled to obey. By every external measure, he is free. Yet this man is addicted to opium. His addiction dominates his waking life with a tyranny more absolute than that of any despot. He cannot refrain from seeking and consuming the drug, though he wishes desperately to stop. He has tried many times; each time, the compulsion has overcome his resolution. He recognises that the addiction has destroyed his health, alienated his family, and extinguished his capacity for the scholarly pursuits he once valued above all things. He is enslaved — so he himself would say — not by chains or laws or commands, but by a compulsion arising from within his own nature. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. There is no physical restraint, no legal prohibition, no external compulsion. Yet we should hesitate to call this man free. The definition has confined its attention to external obstacles — the chains, the laws, the commands of others — and has overlooked the possibility that the most effective obstacles to freedom may be internal, arising from the subject's own disordered desires.

definition: A society S achieves equality if and only if (i) all members of S possess the same legal rights and are subject to the same legal obligations, (ii) no member of S is formally excluded from any office, profession, or social position on the basis of birth, rank, or inherited status, and (iii) the laws of S are applied impartially — that is, without favour or discrimination on the basis of any personal characteristic.
conditions: All members possess the same legal rights and obligations
No member is formally excluded from any position on the basis of birth, rank, or inherited status
The laws are applied impartially without discrimination
counterexample: Consider a society in which all citizens have identical legal rights, no one is formally excluded from any office or profession, and the laws are applied with scrupulous impartiality. Yet the distribution of wealth is such that the children of the rich receive an education of incomparably greater quality than the children of the poor, and the latter, though legally entitled to enter any profession, lack the training, the connections, and the resources to do so in practice. The son of a factory owner may become a barrister or a physician; the son of the factory worker, though no law bars his way, cannot. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied, yet the society is not equal in any sense that would satisfy a serious enquirer into the matter.
missing_condition: The definition captures formal or legal equality but ignores substantive inequality — the material conditions of wealth, education, and social advantage that determine whether formal rights can be exercised in practice.
passage: One might define equality in a society by stipulating that all members possess the same legal rights and are subject to the same legal obligations, no member is formally excluded from any office, profession, or social position on the basis of birth, rank, or inherited status, and the laws are applied impartially, without favour or discrimination. This definition has a distinguished pedigree; it captures what the French revolutionaries meant by equality before the law and what the liberal tradition has taken to be the essence of the equal society. A society that satisfies all three conditions has abolished legal privilege: the nobleman has no rights that the commoner lacks, the law treats the rich man and the poor man alike, and every position is open to talent regardless of birth. Yet consider a society that satisfies all three conditions and yet is profoundly unequal. All citizens have the same legal rights. No one is formally excluded from any office or profession. The judges apply the law with rigorous impartiality. But the distribution of wealth is enormously unequal. The children of the rich attend schools of the highest quality, are taught by the best masters, have access to libraries, laboratories, and tutors, and enter adult life with every advantage that money can confer. The children of the poor attend schools that are starved of resources, taught by overworked and underpaid teachers, and enter adult life without the education, the social connections, or the material resources necessary to compete for the positions that are, in law, open to them. The son of a wealthy manufacturer may aspire to become a barrister, a physician, or a member of Parliament; the son of a mill worker, though no statute bars his way, cannot realistically aspire to any of these things, for he lacks the prerequisites. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied; the society is, in the formal sense, equal. Yet the inequality is palpable and pervasive. The definition has captured the shell of equality — its legal and formal expression — while ignoring the substance: the material conditions that determine whether formal rights can be translated into real opportunities.

definition: A person or institution A has authority over a person B if and only if (i) A issues directives to B regarding B's conduct, (ii) B has an obligation to comply with A's directives, and (iii) A's authority derives from B's voluntary consent — that is, B has agreed, explicitly or tacitly, to submit to A's directives.
conditions: A issues directives to B regarding B's conduct
B has an obligation to comply with A's directives
A's authority derives from B's voluntary consent
counterexample: Consider a parent's authority over a young child. The parent issues directives — do not touch the fire, eat your vegetables, go to bed at eight o'clock — and the child has an obligation to comply, or at any rate, the parent's issuing of such directives is widely regarded as legitimate. But the child has not consented, voluntarily or otherwise, to the parent's authority. The child is too young to consent to anything; he did not choose his parents, did not agree to be governed by them, and may in fact protest their directives vigorously. The third condition fails, yet parental authority is among the oldest and most widely recognised forms of authority.
missing_condition: The definition requires voluntary consent, but some of the most fundamental forms of authority — parental authority, the authority of a guardian over a ward — arise from the nature of the relationship and the needs of the dependent, not from any act of consent.
passage: It might be proposed that a person or institution has authority over another if and only if the first issues directives regarding the second's conduct, the second has an obligation to comply, and the authority derives from the second's voluntary consent. This analysis reflects the contractarian tradition in political thought, according to which legitimate authority rests upon the consent of the governed. A citizen who has consented — explicitly, by taking an oath of allegiance, or tacitly, by remaining within the territory and accepting the benefits of the legal order — is subject to the authority of the state, and the state's directives carry an obligation of compliance. The definition excludes the commands of a bandit, which carry no obligation, and the demands of a tyrant, whose authority rests upon coercion rather than consent. Yet one must consider the authority of a parent over a young child. A father says to his three-year-old son: do not touch the fire. This is a directive regarding the child's conduct, and the child has an obligation — or, more precisely, the parent has a right, widely recognised in law and morals — to issue such directives and to enforce compliance. But the child has not consented to the parent's authority. He did not choose to be born into this family; he did not agree, explicitly or tacitly, to submit to his father's governance. He is too young to understand what consent means, let alone to give it. If one asks whether he consents, the question is absurd — as absurd as asking whether he consents to the law of gravity. Yet the parent's authority is among the oldest and most widely recognised forms of human authority. Every society acknowledges it; every legal system provides for it; and the welfare of the child depends upon its exercise. The definition has taken a feature of political authority in the contractarian tradition — the consent of the governed — and made it a necessary condition of authority as such, when in truth some of the most important forms of authority arise not from consent but from the nature of the relationship and the needs of the dependent.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G came to power through a process that is recognised as lawful by the constitutional arrangements of the polity, (ii) G exercises power within the limits prescribed by those constitutional arrangements, and (iii) G is accepted by the majority of the population as having the right to govern.
conditions: G came to power through a lawful constitutional process
G exercises power within constitutional limits
G is accepted by the majority of the population as having the right to govern
counterexample: Consider a colonial government imposed upon a subject people by a foreign power. The colonial power may establish a constitution for the colony and may install a government through a process that is lawful according to that constitution. The colonial government may exercise its powers within the limits that constitution prescribes. And the majority of the colonised population may, through resignation, habit, or the absence of any perceived alternative, accept the colonial government as the authority under which they live. All three conditions may be satisfied. Yet the colonial government is not legitimate in any morally significant sense, for it rules without the genuine self-determination of the people it governs — it was imposed from outside, and the acceptance of the population reflects acquiescence rather than authentic endorsement.
missing_condition: The definition captures procedural and majoritarian legitimacy but ignores the requirement of genuine self-determination — the distinction between a people's authentic endorsement of their government and their mere acquiescence to an imposed regime.
passage: One might define legitimate government by stipulating that a government is legitimate if and only if it came to power through a process recognised as lawful by the polity's constitutional arrangements, it exercises power within the limits prescribed by those arrangements, and it is accepted by the majority of the population as having the right to govern. This definition captures a great deal of what we mean by legitimacy in the context of established democratic states. A government that wins an election conducted according to the established rules, governs within the limits of the constitution, and enjoys the acceptance of the citizenry is legitimate in the fullest sense. A government that seizes power by force, or that exercises powers beyond its constitutional remit, or that rules in the face of widespread popular rejection, fails one or more of the conditions and is, to that extent, illegitimate. Yet one must consider the case of a colonial government. A European power establishes a colony in a distant land and imposes upon the subject population a constitution — drafted in the metropole, enacted without the participation of the colonised people, but nonetheless a constitution that provides for the orderly exercise of governmental power. A governor is appointed through the process that this constitution prescribes. She exercises her powers within the limits the constitution sets. And the majority of the colonised population, having no alternative, no means of effective resistance, and no experience of any other form of government, accepts the colonial administration as the authority under which they live — they pay their taxes, obey the regulations, and go about their affairs. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet it would be an extraordinary distortion to call this government legitimate. It was imposed from without by a foreign power. The constitution it operates under was not the product of the people's own deliberation. The acceptance of the population reflects not genuine endorsement but resignation — the acquiescence of those who see no alternative. The definition has confused procedural regularity and passive acceptance with the deeper requirement that a legitimate government must arise from the genuine self-determination of the people it governs.

definition: A state S is sovereign if and only if (i) S exercises supreme authority within its territory — that is, there is no higher political authority to which S is subordinate, (ii) S has the capacity to enforce its laws throughout its territory, and (iii) S is recognised as sovereign by other states in the international community.
conditions: S exercises supreme authority within its territory with no higher subordinating authority
S has the capacity to enforce its laws throughout its territory
S is recognised as sovereign by other states
counterexample: Consider Taiwan. Taiwan exercises supreme authority within its territory — there is no higher political authority to which the government in Taipei is subordinate in the day-to-day governance of the island. Taiwan has the capacity to enforce its laws throughout its territory; it maintains courts, police, armed forces, and an effective civil administration. But Taiwan is not recognised as sovereign by the great majority of the states in the international community; most states recognise the government in Beijing as the sole legitimate government of China, of which Taiwan is held to be a part. The third condition fails, yet Taiwan functions as a sovereign state in every practical respect.
missing_condition: The definition requires international recognition, but a state may exercise all the practical attributes of sovereignty — supreme internal authority, effective governance, territorial control — without being recognised by other states, suggesting that recognition is a political fact rather than a constitutive condition.
passage: It is commonly held that a state is sovereign if and only if it exercises supreme authority within its territory, has the capacity to enforce its laws throughout that territory, and is recognised as sovereign by other states in the international community. The definition reflects the Westphalian conception of sovereignty and captures the standard cases well. France is sovereign: it exercises supreme authority within its borders, it enforces its laws from Lille to Marseille, and it is recognised as sovereign by every other state. A province within a federal system — Bavaria, let us say, or Ontario — is not sovereign, for it is subordinate to the federal government in certain matters. The definition correctly distinguishes sovereign states from sub-state entities. Yet one must consider the case of Taiwan. The government in Taipei exercises supreme authority within the territory it controls — the island of Taiwan and several smaller islands. There is no higher political authority to which it is subordinate in the day-to-day governance of these territories. It has an effective legislature, an independent judiciary, a professional civil service, and armed forces capable of defending the island. It enforces its laws with the same thoroughness as any other well-governed state. But Taiwan is not recognised as sovereign by the great majority of states in the international community. Most states maintain diplomatic relations with the government in Beijing and recognise it as the sole legitimate government of China, of which Taiwan is regarded as a part. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet to say that Taiwan is not sovereign is to say something that bears no relation to the practical reality. Taiwan governs itself; it makes its own laws; it collects its own taxes; it maintains its own army; it conducts its own foreign policy, albeit under considerable constraints. The definition has made international recognition — which is a political fact, subject to the pressures and calculations of diplomacy — a constitutive condition of sovereignty, when in truth sovereignty may be a matter of practical capacity rather than of diplomatic acknowledgement.

definition: An act A constitutes civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a deliberate violation of a law or governmental directive, (ii) A is performed publicly and openly, with no attempt at concealment, and (iii) the agent who performs A is motivated by a desire to change the law or policy that A violates — that is, A is a form of political protest.
conditions: A is a deliberate violation of a law or governmental directive
A is performed publicly and openly with no attempt at concealment
The agent is motivated by a desire to change the law or policy that A violates
counterexample: Consider the case of persons who, during the period of fugitive slave laws, deliberately harboured escaped slaves in violation of the law. Their actions were a deliberate violation of the law, and they were motivated by a moral conviction that the law was unjust — they were, in the relevant sense, engaged in political protest. But they did not act publicly and openly. They concealed the escaped slaves in cellars and attics; they operated in secret, often under cover of darkness, taking elaborate precautions to avoid detection. The second condition fails, yet their acts are among the most celebrated instances of civil disobedience in the historical record.
missing_condition: The definition requires that civil disobedience be public and open, but some of the most morally significant acts of civil disobedience — the harbouring of fugitives, the hiding of persecuted persons — must be performed in secret to be effective, and their secrecy does not diminish their character as principled resistance to unjust law.
passage: One might define civil disobedience as an act that constitutes a deliberate violation of a law or governmental directive, is performed publicly and openly with no attempt at concealment, and is motivated by a desire to change the law or policy in question. The requirement of publicity is usually thought to be essential: the civil disobedient acts in the open precisely in order to draw attention to the injustice of the law, to invite public debate, and to demonstrate a willingness to accept the legal consequences of the violation. A person who sits at a lunch counter in defiance of a segregation ordinance, or who refuses to move to the back of a bus, acts in full public view and invites arrest. The openness of the act is part of its moral force. Yet one must consider the case of those persons who, during the period of the fugitive slave laws in the United States, deliberately harboured escaped slaves in their homes in violation of the law. These persons were motivated by a deep moral conviction that the law requiring the return of fugitive slaves was profoundly unjust. They violated the law deliberately, and their violation was a form of political protest — a refusal to cooperate with an institution they regarded as an abomination. But they did not act publicly and openly. They concealed the escaped slaves in cellars, attics, and hidden rooms; they operated in secret, often under cover of darkness; they took elaborate precautions to avoid detection, for discovery would have meant the return of the fugitive to bondage and severe punishment for the harbourers. The second condition of the definition fails. Yet the underground railway and the acts of those who sustained it are among the most celebrated instances of civil disobedience in the historical record. To deny them that title on the ground that they were performed in secret would be to confuse a feature of one important form of civil disobedience — the public protest — with a necessary condition of civil disobedience as such.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) there exists a legal system that formally recognises P's entitlement to X, (ii) that legal system provides a mechanism for the enforcement of P's entitlement — that is, P can seek redress through courts or other institutions if the entitlement is violated, and (iii) the entitlement to X is enjoyed by all persons within the jurisdiction of the legal system, not merely by P as a matter of special privilege.
conditions: A legal system formally recognises P's entitlement to X
The legal system provides a mechanism for enforcement of the entitlement
The entitlement is enjoyed by all persons within the jurisdiction, not merely by P
counterexample: Consider a man living under a despotic regime that recognises no right of free expression. No law protects his speech; no court will hear a complaint on his behalf; the entitlement to speak freely is not recognised by the legal system in any form. Yet if he is silenced by the police for expressing a political opinion, we should wish to say that his right to free expression has been violated. The definition's conditions are all unsatisfied — there is no legal recognition, no enforcement mechanism, no universal entitlement — yet the right, we feel, exists and has been transgressed.
missing_condition: The definition reduces rights to positive law, but the concept of natural or moral rights — rights that persons possess independently of any legal system — has a long and important philosophical history, and it is precisely in cases where the law fails to recognise a right that the assertion of that right is most urgent.
passage: It might be supposed that a person has a right to something if and only if there exists a legal system that formally recognises the entitlement, that legal system provides a mechanism for its enforcement, and the entitlement is enjoyed universally within the jurisdiction rather than as a matter of special privilege. This analysis has the virtue of concreteness. When we say that a British subject has a right to trial by jury, we mean that the legal system recognises this entitlement, that the courts will enforce it, and that it is enjoyed by all persons accused of serious crimes, not merely by the wealthy or the well-connected. The definition correctly identifies this as a right and excludes mere aspirations or wishes that lack legal recognition. Yet one must consider a man living under a despotic regime. The regime recognises no right of free expression. No statute protects his speech; no court will entertain a complaint from him if he is silenced. The police suppress political dissent as a matter of course, and the legal system, far from providing a mechanism of enforcement, is itself the instrument of suppression. If this man is arrested and imprisoned for expressing a political opinion, we should wish to say — and the tradition of political thought from the Stoics through Locke and beyond has said — that his right to free expression has been violated. Not his legal right, for he has none, but his right — his moral or natural right, the right he possesses by virtue of being a rational and autonomous being. The definition's conditions are all unsatisfied. There is no legal recognition, no enforcement mechanism, no universal entitlement within the jurisdiction. Yet the right exists and has been transgressed. The definition has reduced rights to positive law and has thereby excluded the entire category of natural or moral rights — rights that persons possess independently of any legal system, rights that may be violated precisely by the legal system that ought to protect them. It is in such cases, where the law fails to recognise a right, that the assertion of that right is most urgently needed.

definition: A political decision D is democratic if and only if (i) D is made by a vote of the citizens or their elected representatives, (ii) the vote is conducted according to the principle of majority rule — the option that receives the most votes prevails, and (iii) the citizens who participate in the vote have had access to relevant information about the matter being decided.
conditions: D is made by a vote of the citizens or their elected representatives
The vote follows majority rule — the option with the most votes prevails
Citizens have had access to relevant information about the matter
counterexample: Consider a community in which a decision is to be made about the construction of a dam that will flood a valley inhabited by a small minority. The majority of the citizens vote in favour of the dam; the vote is conducted properly; all citizens have access to the relevant information. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the decision will destroy the homes and livelihoods of the minority in the valley, who voted against it unanimously. The majority has used the democratic process to impose a devastating cost upon a vulnerable group. One might observe that this decision, though procedurally democratic, violates something essential about the democratic ideal — namely, that the rights of minorities are not to be overridden by the mere will of the majority.
missing_condition: The definition captures the procedural dimension of democracy — voting, majority rule, informed participation — but neglects the substantive dimension: the protection of minority rights, without which majority rule may become a form of tyranny.
passage: One might propose that a political decision is democratic if and only if it is made by a vote of the citizens or their elected representatives, the vote is conducted according to the principle of majority rule, and the citizens who participate have had access to relevant information about the matter at hand. This analysis captures the procedural essence of democratic decision-making. When a legislature debates a bill, the members vote, the majority prevails, and the bill becomes law, the process is democratic in the sense that the definition specifies. The requirement of access to information ensures that the vote is not merely a counting of uninformed heads but an exercise of judgement by persons who have had the opportunity to consider the question. Yet one must consider a case that satisfies all three conditions and yet strikes us as troubling. A community is to decide whether to construct a dam. The dam will provide water and electricity for the majority of the population, but it will flood a valley in which a small minority lives — let us say fifty families who have farmed the valley for generations. A vote is held; all citizens have access to the relevant information; the majority votes in favour of the dam. The fifty families in the valley vote unanimously against it. The decision is carried by majority rule. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. The decision is, in the procedural sense, democratic. Yet the majority has used the democratic process to destroy the homes and livelihoods of a vulnerable minority. The fifty families will lose their farms, their houses, and the way of life that has sustained them. They have been outvoted, and that is the end of the matter. One might observe that this decision, though procedurally democratic, violates something that lies at the heart of the democratic ideal — the conviction that the rights and interests of minorities are not to be simply overridden by the will of the greater number. The definition has captured the mechanics of democracy — the voting, the counting, the majority rule — while neglecting the substantive commitment to the protection of those who find themselves in the minority on any given question.

definition: A right R is inalienable if and only if (i) R cannot be transferred from its holder to any other person or institution, (ii) R cannot be forfeited by any action of its holder, and (iii) R cannot be overridden by any governmental authority, regardless of the circumstances.
conditions: R cannot be transferred from its holder to another
R cannot be forfeited by any action of its holder
R cannot be overridden by any governmental authority regardless of circumstances
counterexample: Consider the right to life, which is often cited as the paradigmatic inalienable right. Yet if a man attacks another with murderous intent, and the defender kills the attacker in self-defence, we do not say that the defender has violated an inalienable right; we say that the attacker, by his own aggression, has brought about a situation in which lethal force is justified. In a case of self-defence, the right to life of the attacker is, in effect, overridden — or at any rate, it does not prohibit the use of lethal force against him. The third condition fails, for the right has been overridden by the circumstance of the holder's own aggression. Yet we continue to call the right to life inalienable.
missing_condition: The definition requires absolute immunity from override, but even paradigmatically inalienable rights — the right to life, the right to liberty — may be overridden in extreme circumstances such as self-defence or the prevention of serious harm, and their inalienability consists not in their absolute inviolability but in the fact that they cannot be voluntarily surrendered or bargained away.
passage: One might define an inalienable right as a right that cannot be transferred from its holder to any other person or institution, cannot be forfeited by any action of its holder, and cannot be overridden by any governmental authority regardless of the circumstances.
