This definition captures the rhetorical force of the concept: to call a right inalienable is to place it beyond the reach of any power — personal, social, or governmental — and to insist that it belongs to its holder unconditionally and without exception. The right to life, the right to liberty, the right to the pursuit of happiness — these are often cited as inalienable rights, and the definition seems to capture what is meant by calling them so. Yet one must consider what happens when the right to life — the paradigmatic inalienable right — encounters the hard realities of human conflict. A man attacks another with the clear intention of killing him. The defender, having no other means of saving his own life, kills the attacker. In this case, the attacker's right to life has been, in effect, overridden. The defender is not condemned for what he has done; on the contrary, every legal and moral tradition recognises his right to defend himself with lethal force if necessary. The right to life of the attacker, though inalienable, has not prevented the use of lethal force against him; the circumstances of his own aggression have brought about a situation in which his right yields to the defender's right of self-preservation. The third condition of the definition fails: the right has been overridden, not by governmental authority, but by the force of circumstance and the competing right of the defender. Yet we do not conclude that the right to life is not inalienable; we say rather that it is inalienable but not absolute — that it cannot be surrendered or bargained away, but that it may yield in extreme cases to other rights or to the necessities of self-defence. The definition has confused inalienability with absolute inviolability, when the true force of calling a right inalienable is that it cannot be voluntarily given up or taken away by ordinary political processes, not that it can never under any circumstances be overridden.

definition: A political system G is a democracy if and only if (i) the holders of supreme political authority are chosen by periodic elections in which all adult citizens may vote, (ii) there exist at least two competing candidates or parties, and (iii) the government is accountable to the electorate and may be removed by electoral means.
conditions: Holders of supreme authority are chosen by periodic elections with universal adult suffrage
At least two competing candidates or parties exist
The government is accountable and removable by electoral means
counterexample: Consider a state in which elections are held periodically with universal suffrage, multiple parties compete, and the government can in principle be removed by the electorate. Yet the government controls the press, suppresses dissent, and manipulates the electoral process so thoroughly that no opposition party has any realistic prospect of victory. All three conditions are formally satisfied, yet one might observe that the system is a democracy in name only. The electorate cannot exercise genuine choice when the conditions for informed and free deliberation are absent.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that elections be conducted under conditions of genuine freedom — freedom of speech, assembly, and the press — without which the formal apparatus of electoral competition is an empty shell.
passage: A standard definition of democracy holds that a political system G is a democracy if and only if the holders of supreme political authority are chosen by periodic elections in which all adult citizens may vote, there exist at least two competing candidates or parties, and the government is accountable to the electorate and may be removed by electoral means. This definition captures the institutional core of democratic governance: popular elections, competition, and accountability. It distinguishes democracies from hereditary monarchies, military dictatorships, and one-party states. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is not difficult to describe. Consider a state in which elections are indeed held at regular intervals, in which all adults may cast a ballot, in which several parties formally compete, and in which the government is in principle removable by vote. Yet the government controls all major organs of the press. It suppresses public assembly by opposition groups. It manipulates voter rolls, intimidates candidates, and deploys state resources in its own campaigns. The formal conditions of the definition are met in every particular. Yet no disinterested observer would describe this state as a genuine democracy. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring that the electoral process be conducted under circumstances of genuine freedom — freedom of expression, freedom of assembly, and access to diverse sources of information — without which the mechanism of popular choice is rendered meaningless.

definition: A citizen C is free with respect to action A if and only if (i) no law prohibits C from performing A, (ii) no other person or institution physically prevents C from performing A, and (iii) C faces no legal penalty for performing A.
conditions: No law prohibits C from performing A
No person or institution physically prevents C from performing A
C faces no legal penalty for performing A
counterexample: Consider a citizen in a state where no law prohibits travel, no person or institution physically blocks the roads, and no legal penalty attaches to leaving one's district. Yet the citizen is so impoverished that she cannot afford the transport necessary to leave. She is legally and physically unimpeded, yet she cannot travel. All three conditions are satisfied, but it appears wrong to say she is free to travel in any substantive sense. Her poverty constrains her as effectively as a locked gate.
missing_condition: The definition attends only to legal and physical impediments and neglects the role of material conditions; it should include a condition requiring that the citizen possess the resources or capacities necessary to perform the action in question.
passage: One might propose that a citizen C is free with respect to action A if and only if no law prohibits C from performing A, no other person or institution physically prevents C from performing A, and C faces no legal penalty for performing A. This definition articulates a purely negative conception of freedom: freedom is the absence of legal prohibition and physical obstruction. It has the virtues of clarity and of conformity with a long tradition in liberal political thought. The first and third conditions address the legal dimension; the second addresses the physical. Together, they seem to account for the principal ways in which a person might be unfree. Yet the definition is vulnerable to a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider a citizen who lives in a state where no law prohibits travel between districts, where no checkpoint or barricade stands in her way, and where no penalty whatsoever attaches to movement. She is, by the definition's lights, entirely free to travel. Yet she is destitute. She lacks the means to purchase a rail ticket or to hire a vehicle. She cannot walk the distance involved. She is as effectively confined to her district as if a wall surrounded it. The definition, by attending exclusively to the absence of external legal and physical impediments, overlooks the possibility that material deprivation may constrain freedom as decisively as any law or barrier. A definition adequate to the full range of cases would need to include a condition addressing the agent's possession of the material resources or capacities requisite for the exercise of the freedom in question.

definition: A society exhibits political equality if and only if (i) every adult citizen has one and only one vote of equal weight, (ii) every citizen is eligible to stand for public office, and (iii) no citizen is accorded legal privileges on the basis of birth, rank, or hereditary status.
conditions: Every adult citizen has one equally weighted vote
Every citizen is eligible to stand for public office
No citizen is accorded legal privileges based on birth, rank, or hereditary status
counterexample: Consider a society in which every citizen has an equal vote, every citizen may stand for office, and no hereditary legal privileges exist. Yet the conduct of electoral campaigns requires such enormous sums of money that only the wealthy can realistically compete. A poor citizen is eligible in law but excluded in practice. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the society is characterised by a marked inequality in the effective exercise of political power.
missing_condition: The definition addresses only formal or legal equality and neglects the material conditions necessary for the effective exercise of political power; a condition requiring roughly equal access to the resources needed for political participation is absent.
passage: A standard formulation of political equality holds that a society exhibits political equality if and only if every adult citizen has one and only one vote of equal weight, every citizen is eligible to stand for public office, and no citizen is accorded legal privileges on the basis of birth, rank, or hereditary status. This definition captures the formal dimension of political equality with admirable precision. The first condition ensures numerical equality in the franchise. The second opens the corridors of power to all. The third abolishes the aristocratic principle. Together, they describe a society that has removed the most obvious legal barriers to equal participation. Yet the definition is insufficient. Consider a society that satisfies all three conditions perfectly. Every citizen votes, every citizen may stand for office, and no hereditary distinctions exist in law. But the cost of mounting an electoral campaign is so great that only the independently wealthy or those with access to large financial supporters can realistically compete. A citizen of modest means is eligible for office in the strictest legal sense, yet she faces obstacles so formidable as to render her eligibility largely nominal. The society is formally equal but substantively unequal. The definition, by confining itself to legal and procedural criteria, fails to address the material conditions — wealth, education, social connections — that determine whether formal political equality translates into genuine equality of political power and opportunity.

definition: A person or body P possesses political authority over a population if and only if (i) P has the power to issue commands that the population is expected to obey, (ii) P's commands are backed by the credible threat of sanctions for non-compliance, and (iii) P occupies a recognised institutional position within the political order.
conditions: P has the power to issue commands expected to be obeyed
P's commands are backed by credible threat of sanctions
P occupies a recognised institutional position
counterexample: Consider a military commander who seizes power by force, dissolving existing institutions and installing himself in no recognised office. He issues commands and enforces obedience through credible threats of violence. He satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) entirely. Yet he occupies no recognised institutional position within the prior political order — he has destroyed that order. If we insist on condition (iii), we must deny that he possesses political authority, yet he exercises power indistinguishable from authority in its practical effects. Alternatively, if his new regime is rapidly recognised, condition (iii) becomes trivially satisfied, and the definition provides no purchase on the question of legitimacy.
missing_condition: The definition conflates authority with effective power and fails to include a condition addressing the moral or legal legitimacy of P's position; without such a condition, rule by brute force, once institutionally formalised, counts as authority.
passage: A familiar definition holds that a person or body P possesses political authority over a population if and only if P has the power to issue commands that the population is expected to obey, P's commands are backed by the credible threat of sanctions for non-compliance, and P occupies a recognised institutional position within the political order. The definition aims to distinguish authority from mere influence or persuasion by insisting upon the coercive element, whilst anchoring it in institutional structure to distinguish it from the power of a bandit or a bully. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account is readily at hand. Consider a military commander who seizes power by force, dissolving the existing constitution and installing himself at the apex of a new order. He issues commands; they are obeyed. He enforces compliance through credible threats of imprisonment and worse. Conditions (i) and (ii) are fully satisfied. As for condition (iii), if the definition requires recognition within the prior political order, then the commander fails the test — but then the definition must deny authority to every revolutionary government that has ever existed, which seems too strong. If instead the definition allows that the commander's new office counts as a recognised institutional position, then condition (iii) is trivially satisfied, and the definition draws no distinction between legitimate authority and successful usurpation. What is missing is a condition addressing the legitimacy of P's position — the question of whether P's authority rests on something more than the effective exercise of power.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G has been established through procedures accepted by the governed population, (ii) G governs in accordance with a recognised body of law, and (iii) the governed population generally acquiesces in G's rule.
conditions: G was established through accepted procedures
G governs according to recognised law
The governed population generally acquiesces in G's rule
counterexample: Consider a colonial government imposed upon a subject people. Over the course of generations, the colonised population comes to acquiesce in the regime's rule, not from approval but from exhaustion and the absence of alternatives. The government administers a body of law, recognised at least by the colonial power, and was established through procedures accepted by the colonising nation if not by the colonised. One might argue that all three conditions are met — particularly if 'accepted procedures' and 'recognised law' are read from the perspective of the ruling power. Yet it appears deeply wrong to call such a government legitimate, for its authority rests on conquest rather than consent.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the acceptance of procedures and the acquiescence of the population be genuinely voluntary and uncoerced; without such a requirement, governments sustained by subjugation and resignation qualify as legitimate.
passage: One might define the legitimacy of a government by saying that a government G is legitimate if and only if G has been established through procedures accepted by the governed population, G governs in accordance with a recognised body of law, and the governed population generally acquiesces in G's rule. This definition has the merit of combining procedural, legal, and popular elements. The first condition ensures that the government has a proper origin. The second ensures that it operates within a framework of law rather than arbitrary decree. The third connects legitimacy to the attitudes of those who are governed. Yet the definition is susceptible to a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider a colonial government established over a conquered people. The colonising power follows its own constitutional procedures in establishing the government — procedures accepted by the colonists if not by the colonised. The government administers a body of law, drawn from the legal tradition of the colonial power and recognised by its courts. Over time, the subject population, worn down by decades of rule and seeing no realistic prospect of change, acquiesces. All three conditions may be said to be met, at least on a generous reading. Yet the suggestion that such a government is legitimate offends our deepest political intuitions. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring that the acceptance and acquiescence in question be voluntary and uncoerced — that they arise from genuine consent rather than from exhaustion, intimidation, or the sheer absence of alternatives.

definition: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme authority within a defined territory, (ii) E is not subject to the authority of any external political power, and (iii) E possesses the capacity to enter into relations with other sovereign entities.
conditions: E exercises supreme authority within a defined territory
E is not subject to external political authority
E possesses the capacity to enter into relations with other sovereign entities
counterexample: Consider a small state that has signed a series of international treaties obligating it to comply with the rulings of an international tribunal, to maintain certain trade policies, and to submit its military forces to the command of an alliance. The state exercises authority within its territory, and it has entered into relations with other sovereigns. Yet it is subject, in significant respects, to the authority of external bodies. If condition (ii) is read strictly, the state has surrendered its sovereignty; if read loosely, no state in the modern world is fully sovereign. The definition provides no principled threshold for distinguishing sovereignty from its absence.
missing_condition: The definition treats freedom from external authority as an absolute condition, when in practice sovereignty is a matter of degree; it should specify the domains or extent of authority that must remain free from external control for sovereignty to obtain.
passage: A traditional account of sovereignty holds that a political entity E is sovereign if and only if E exercises supreme authority within a defined territory, E is not subject to the authority of any external political power, and E possesses the capacity to enter into relations with other sovereign entities. This tripartite definition has deep roots in the theory of the state and captures the essential idea that a sovereign entity is self-governing, independent, and capable of acting as a member of the international community. Yet the second condition — independence from external authority — raises a difficulty that the definition cannot resolve. Consider a small state that has voluntarily entered into a network of international treaties and institutional arrangements. It has submitted itself to the jurisdiction of an international court, agreed to binding trade regulations promulgated by a supranational body, and placed its military forces under the operational command of a multinational alliance. This state exercises authority within its territory and has plainly entered into relations with other sovereign entities. But it is, in non-trivial respects, subject to the authority of external powers. If condition (ii) is read as requiring absolute independence, then this state is not sovereign — but then neither is any state in the modern world, since all are enmeshed in networks of international obligation. If the condition is weakened, the definition provides no guidance as to how much external constraint is compatible with sovereignty. The definition, it appears, needs a more nuanced account of what it means to be free from external authority.

definition: An act A by citizen C constitutes civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a deliberate violation of a law, (ii) A is performed publicly and with the intention of drawing attention to an injustice, and (iii) C accepts the legal consequences of A without resistance.
conditions: A is a deliberate violation of a law
A is performed publicly and with intention to draw attention to injustice
C accepts legal consequences without resistance
counterexample: Consider an activist who deliberately violates a law she regards as unjust by distributing prohibited literature. She does so in secret, passing the literature to trusted contacts in private settings, precisely because public action would result in immediate suppression and would fail to reach its intended audience. She is prepared to accept legal consequences if discovered. She satisfies conditions (i) and (iii), yet she fails condition (ii), for her act is covert. Nevertheless, her action has the moral and political character that we associate with civil disobedience: it is conscientious, principled, and directed against an injustice.
missing_condition: The definition's requirement of publicity is too rigid; it should allow that an act may qualify as civil disobedience even when performed covertly, provided it is motivated by conscientious opposition to injustice and the agent is prepared to accept the consequences if their act becomes known.
passage: A widely accepted definition holds that an act A by citizen C constitutes civil disobedience if and only if A is a deliberate violation of a law, A is performed publicly and with the intention of drawing attention to an injustice, and C accepts the legal consequences of A without resistance. The first condition distinguishes civil disobedience from lawful protest. The second ensures that the act is communicative — that it aims to address the political community rather than merely to serve the agent's private interests. The third connects civil disobedience to a tradition of principled submission to the legal order, even as one contests a particular law. This definition captures many paradigm cases of civil disobedience admirably. Yet it is too narrow in its insistence on publicity. Consider an activist who distributes prohibited literature in a society where public dissent is met with immediate and violent suppression. She violates the law deliberately and from conscientious conviction. She accepts that if she is caught she will face prosecution, and she does not intend to resist. But she acts in secret, distributing her materials through private channels, because public distribution would result in the confiscation of the literature and the arrest of her contacts before the material could reach its audience. Her act is covert not from self-interest but from strategic necessity. It has every moral and political characteristic we associate with civil disobedience — conscientiousness, principled opposition, willingness to bear consequences — except publicity. The definition, by making publicity a necessary condition, excludes cases that are, in every relevant respect, instances of the very phenomenon it purports to define.

definition: A citizen C possesses a right to X if and only if (i) there exists a law or constitutional provision guaranteeing C's entitlement to X, (ii) corresponding duties are imposed upon others to respect or provide X, and (iii) C has access to institutional mechanisms for the enforcement of the entitlement.
conditions: A law or constitutional provision guarantees C's entitlement to X
Corresponding duties are imposed on others
C has access to institutional enforcement mechanisms
counterexample: Consider a constitution that guarantees every citizen the right to a fair trial. The law exists, duties are imposed upon the judiciary and prosecution to act fairly, and courts are in principle available for the enforcement of these duties. Yet in practice, the courts are so corrupt and the legal system so dysfunctional that no citizen can in fact obtain a fair trial. All three conditions are formally met — the law exists, the duties are prescribed, the mechanisms are nominally available — yet the right is wholly ineffective. It appears inadequate to say that the right exists when it can never be exercised.
missing_condition: The definition addresses only the formal existence of legal guarantees, duties, and enforcement mechanisms, and fails to require that these mechanisms function effectively; a condition demanding that the right be practically realisable is needed.
passage: One might define a right by saying that a citizen C possesses a right to X if and only if there exists a law or constitutional provision guaranteeing C's entitlement to X, corresponding duties are imposed upon others to respect or provide X, and C has access to institutional mechanisms for the enforcement of the entitlement. This definition is attractive in its juridical precision. The first condition locates rights in positive law. The second captures the correlative structure of rights and duties that has been a staple of legal theory since at least Hohfeld. The third ensures that rights are not mere paper declarations but are backed by enforceable institutional arrangements. Yet the definition encounters a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider a state whose constitution solemnly guarantees every citizen the right to a fair trial. The criminal code imposes duties upon prosecutors, judges, and other officers of the court to conduct proceedings fairly. Courts exist and are, in principle, open to all citizens who seek to vindicate their rights. All three conditions are satisfied in their letter. Yet the judiciary is profoundly corrupt. Judges are appointed on the basis of political loyalty. Prosecutorial decisions are made at the direction of the executive. No citizen can, in fact, obtain a fair trial. The right exists on paper, the duties are prescribed, the enforcement mechanisms are nominally in place, and yet the right is entirely empty. What the definition fails to capture is the distinction between the formal existence of a right and its effective realisation — between a right that exists in law and one that exists in practice.

definition: A distribution of goods is just if and only if (i) the distribution was produced by a procedure to which all affected parties consented, (ii) the procedure treated all parties impartially — that is, without favouring any party over another, and (iii) the resulting distribution satisfies the basic needs of all parties.
conditions: The distribution was produced by a consented-to procedure
The procedure treated all parties impartially
The resulting distribution satisfies the basic needs of all parties
counterexample: Consider a group of castaways who agree unanimously to divide their salvaged supplies by lot. The lottery is scrupulously fair, treating every castaway identically. The resulting distribution is such that everyone's basic needs are met — each receives sufficient food and water. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet by chance, one castaway receives a quantity of supplies ten times greater than any other, including rare medicines he does not need while another castaway, whose needs are met at the bare minimum, will soon require those very medicines. The distribution seems unjust despite satisfying the three conditions.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the proportionality or reasonableness of the resulting distribution; a just distribution may require not merely the satisfaction of basic needs but some constraint upon extreme disparities that arise even from fair procedures.
passage: One might propose that a distribution of goods is just if and only if it was produced by a procedure to which all affected parties consented, the procedure treated all parties impartially, and the resulting distribution satisfies the basic needs of all parties. This definition combines procedural and substantive elements in an appealing way. The consent condition ensures that no one is subjected to an arrangement she did not accept. The impartiality condition prevents the procedure from being rigged. And the basic-needs condition sets a substantive floor beneath which no one may fall. Together, these conditions seem to capture a robust conception of distributive justice. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the account may be constructed without difficulty. Consider a group of castaways who unanimously agree to divide their salvaged supplies by lot. The lottery is conducted with perfect fairness — each person draws from the same pool under identical conditions. The outcome is such that everyone receives enough food and water to survive. All three conditions are met. But chance produces a distribution in which one castaway receives a supply of rare medicines vastly exceeding his own needs, while another castaway, whose basic needs are just barely met, is developing a condition for which those medicines are essential. The distribution was consented to, it was impartial, and it meets basic needs, yet it strikes one as unjust. The definition, it appears, needs a further condition addressing the proportionality or reasonableness of the distribution, beyond the bare satisfaction of minimum needs.

definition: An act of censorship occurs if and only if (i) a government or governmental body suppresses or restricts the expression of ideas, (ii) the suppression is carried out prior to or at the point of publication or dissemination, and (iii) the suppression is directed at the content of the expression rather than at its time, place, or manner.
conditions: A government or governmental body suppresses or restricts expression
The suppression occurs prior to or at the point of dissemination
The suppression targets the content of the expression
counterexample: Consider a large corporation that owns the only newspaper in a region and systematically refuses to publish any criticism of its commercial practices. A journalist who writes a critical article finds it suppressed before publication; the suppression is directed at the content. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, yet condition (i) is not, for the suppression is carried out by a private entity rather than a governmental body. Nevertheless, the effect upon public discourse is indistinguishable from governmental censorship. The sole channel of communication in the region is controlled by a private power that silences dissent as effectively as any state censor.
missing_condition: The definition restricts censorship to governmental action and thereby fails to account for cases in which private entities with monopolistic control over channels of communication suppress expression with effects equivalent to state censorship.
passage: A common definition holds that an act of censorship occurs if and only if a government or governmental body suppresses or restricts the expression of ideas, the suppression is carried out prior to or at the point of publication or dissemination, and the suppression is directed at the content of the expression rather than at its time, place, or manner. This definition captures the paradigmatic case of state censorship — the government blue-pencilling a manuscript before it reaches the public. The first condition confines censorship to state action. The second distinguishes censorship from post-publication punishment, such as libel proceedings. The third ensures that the concept targets content-based restrictions rather than reasonable regulations of how and where expression occurs. Yet the definition is narrower than it ought to be. Consider a region in which the sole newspaper is owned by a large corporation. A journalist employed by the paper writes an article criticising the corporation's commercial practices. The article is suppressed before publication by the paper's editorial management, acting on instructions from the corporation's directors. The suppression is directed squarely at the content of the article. Its effect upon public discourse is identical to what would obtain if a government censor had struck the piece. The public in the region has no alternative source of information, and the critical perspective is simply silenced. Yet the definition does not classify this as censorship, because the suppressing agent is a private entity rather than a governmental body. The definition, it appears, has drawn the boundary of censorship too narrowly by confining it to state action, when the phenomenon it aims to capture — the suppression of ideas before they reach the public — may be accomplished just as effectively by private powers.

definition: A political system is a democracy if and only if (i) all adult citizens have the right to vote in elections for their representatives, (ii) elections are held at regular intervals and are conducted by secret ballot, and (iii) the outcome of elections determines who holds political office — the candidate or party receiving the most votes takes power.
conditions: All adult citizens have the right to vote
Elections are held at regular intervals by secret ballot
The outcome of elections determines who holds office
counterexample: Consider a political system in which all adult citizens vote in regular, secret-ballot elections, and the winning candidate takes office. But the winning candidate, once in office, uses her power to control the media, suppress opposition parties, and manipulate the conditions under which the next election will be held — all without formally abolishing elections or restricting the franchise. All three conditions continue to be met at each successive election: citizens vote, the ballot is secret, and the winner takes office. Yet the system has ceased to be a democracy in any meaningful sense; it has become an elected autocracy. The conditions describe the form of democracy but not its substance.
missing_condition: The definition captures the procedural form of democracy but fails to require that the conditions for meaningful democratic competition — a free press, the ability of opposition parties to organise and campaign, and the absence of state manipulation of the electoral process — be maintained between elections.
passage: One might suppose that a political system is a democracy if and only if all adult citizens can vote, elections are held at regular intervals by secret ballot, and the election results determine who holds office. These conditions capture the essential procedural features of democracy: universal suffrage, regular and secret elections, and the translation of electoral outcomes into political power. The suffrage condition ensures inclusivity; the regularity and secrecy conditions guard against manipulation and intimidation; and the outcome condition ensures that elections have real consequences. Together, they describe the mechanism by which the will of the people is translated into governance. Yet one might observe that a system can satisfy all three conditions and still fail to be a democracy in any meaningful sense. Consider a system in which elections are held on schedule, all citizens vote by secret ballot, and the winner takes office. But the incumbent uses the power of the state to control the press, harass opposition candidates, restrict the activities of rival parties, and gerrymander electoral boundaries. Each successive election satisfies the letter of all three conditions. But the elections are not genuinely competitive: the incumbent's control over the informational and organisational environment renders the outcome a foregone conclusion. The system has the form of democracy — elections, ballots, transfers of power — without its substance. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring that the background conditions for meaningful democratic competition be maintained: a free press, the ability of opposition parties to organise, and the absence of state interference in the electoral process.

definition: A person P is free with respect to an action A if and only if (i) no external agent prevents P from performing A, (ii) no law or regulation prohibits P from performing A, and (iii) P has the physical capacity to perform A.
conditions: No external agent prevents P from performing A
No law or regulation prohibits A
P has the physical capacity to perform A
counterexample: Consider a worker in a factory town where there is only one employer. No one physically prevents the worker from quitting his job. No law forbids him from leaving. He has the physical capacity to walk out the door. All three conditions are met. But if he quits, he will be unable to find alternative employment, will lose his home (which is owned by the company), and will be unable to feed his family. The worker is 'free' to quit in the sense captured by the definition, but it would be misleading to say that he is free in any substantive sense. The constraints upon him are economic and structural rather than physical or legal, and the definition does not recognise such constraints.
missing_condition: The definition recognises only physical prevention, legal prohibition, and physical incapacity as restrictions on freedom, but economic and structural constraints — poverty, dependence, lack of alternatives — can restrict freedom just as effectively. A condition addressing the role of economic and social circumstances in enabling or constraining action is needed.
passage: One might define freedom with respect to an action as the absence of external prevention, legal prohibition, and physical incapacity. This negative conception of freedom — freedom as the absence of obstacles — has a long and distinguished history. It focuses on what is not done to the agent: no one blocks her path, no law forbids her action, and her body is capable of performing it. The definition has the considerable merit of clarity: one can, in principle, determine for any agent and any action whether the three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the definition captures only the most visible and dramatic forms of unfreedom. Consider a worker in a company town — a settlement built around a single factory, where the employer owns the housing, controls the only shop, and is the sole source of income. No one prevents the worker from quitting his job. No law forbids it. He is physically able to walk away. All three conditions are satisfied: on the definition, he is free to quit. But if he quits he will have no income, no home, and no means of supporting his family, for there is no other employer within reach. It would be deeply misleading to say that this worker is free to leave his job. The constraints upon him are as real and as effective as any physical barrier, but they are economic and structural rather than physical or legal, and the definition is blind to them. What the definition lacks is a recognition that freedom may be constrained not only by coercion and physical incapacity but by the social and economic conditions in which the agent is situated.

definition: A society S achieves equality if and only if (i) all members of S are equal before the law — the same legal rules apply to all, (ii) all members have equal access to public institutions such as courts, schools, and hospitals, and (iii) no member is subject to discrimination on the basis of birth, rank, or inherited status.
conditions: All members are equal before the law
All members have equal access to public institutions
No member is subject to discrimination based on birth, rank, or inherited status
counterexample: Consider a society in which the law applies equally to all, public institutions are formally open to everyone, and no one is discriminated against on the basis of birth or rank. But vast disparities in wealth mean that some citizens can afford the finest legal representation, the best schools, and the most advanced medical care, while others cannot afford any of these. The wealthy citizen's access to the courts is substantively different from the poor citizen's access, even though the doors of the courthouse stand equally open to both. All three conditions are formally met, yet the society is characterised by profound inequality.
missing_condition: The definition captures formal equality — equality of legal status, institutional access, and non-discrimination — but ignores substantive equality. A condition requiring that material circumstances not render formal equality nugatory — that citizens possess the resources necessary to make effective use of their equal rights — is needed.
passage: One might propose that a society achieves equality if and only if all members are equal before the law, all have equal access to public institutions, and no one is subjected to discrimination on the basis of birth, rank, or inherited status. The first condition establishes legal equality: the law does not create privileged classes. The second condition ensures that the institutions through which the state serves its citizens are open to all on equal terms. The third condition abolishes the hereditary distinctions that characterised the ancien régime. Together, these conditions describe a society that has removed the formal barriers to equality. Yet one might observe that formal equality is compatible with profound substantive inequality. Consider a society that satisfies all three conditions. The law applies to all; the courts, the schools, and the hospitals are open to all; and no hereditary privilege obtains. But the distribution of wealth is so unequal that the richest citizens can afford legal teams, private schools, and the most advanced medical treatments, while the poorest can afford none of these. The formal equality of access to the courts is rendered hollow by the inability of the poor to afford competent representation. The formal openness of schools is rendered hollow by the vast difference between a well-funded private school and an impoverished public one. The society satisfies every condition of the definition, yet no reasonable observer would describe it as equal. The definition, it appears, has mistaken the removal of formal barriers for the achievement of equality. What is needed is a condition requiring that material circumstances not undermine the effective exercise of equal rights.

definition: A person or body X has authority over a group G if and only if (i) X issues directives to G, (ii) the members of G generally comply with those directives, and (iii) X possesses the capacity to impose sanctions upon members of G who fail to comply.
conditions: X issues directives to G
Members of G generally comply with those directives
X can impose sanctions on non-compliant members
counterexample: Consider a charismatic religious leader who issues directives to her congregation. The members of the congregation generally comply — they attend the services she prescribes, observe the dietary restrictions she imposes, and contribute financially as she requests. But she possesses no capacity to impose sanctions upon those who fail to comply. She cannot fine them, imprison them, or expel them from the community. Her authority rests entirely upon the voluntary devotion of her followers, not upon any coercive power. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) is not. Yet the religious leader manifestly has authority over her congregation — an authority that is, in many respects, more complete and more deeply felt than that of a government official who relies upon the threat of sanctions.
missing_condition: The definition requires the capacity to impose sanctions, but authority may rest on moral, charismatic, or traditional grounds rather than on coercive power. A condition permitting authority to be grounded in legitimacy, respect, or voluntary recognition, rather than exclusively in the capacity for enforcement, is needed.
passage: One might suppose that a person or body has authority over a group if and only if it issues directives to the group, the members generally comply, and the authority possesses the capacity to impose sanctions upon those who do not. This definition has the merit of being empirically grounded: one can observe the issuance of directives, the compliance of the group, and the availability of sanctions. The compliance condition ensures that the authority is effective, not merely nominal. And the sanctions condition provides the mechanism by which compliance is maintained: the threat of punishment deters disobedience. Yet one might observe that the sanctions condition is at once too narrow and too crude. Consider a charismatic religious leader whose congregation obeys her directives with a fervour that no secular authority could inspire. She prescribes rites, prohibitions, and contributions, and her followers comply not out of fear of sanctions but out of devotion, reverence, and a sincere belief that her directives express the will of the divine. She has no power to fine, imprison, or expel anyone. Her authority rests entirely upon the voluntary recognition of her followers. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met; condition (iii) is not. Yet her authority is manifestly real, and in many respects more complete than that of a government official who commands obedience only because of the threat of punishment. The definition, by requiring the capacity for sanctions, excludes forms of authority — moral, charismatic, traditional — that operate without coercion and are, if anything, more deeply rooted than coercive authority. What is needed is a broader account of the grounds of authority, encompassing legitimacy and voluntary recognition alongside the capacity for enforcement.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G came to power through procedures that are accepted by the governed population, (ii) G governs in accordance with established laws rather than by arbitrary decree, and (iii) G provides for the security and basic welfare of its citizens.
conditions: G came to power through accepted procedures
G governs in accordance with established laws
G provides for security and basic welfare
counterexample: Consider a colonial government imposed upon a subject population by a foreign power. The colonial government came to power through procedures accepted by the colonising nation — parliamentary authorisation, royal charter — but these procedures were not accepted by the colonised population. Suppose, however, that the colonial government governs in accordance with established laws and provides effectively for the security and welfare of the population — building hospitals, schools, and infrastructure. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met, and condition (i) is met with respect to the colonising nation, though not with respect to the governed population. If we interpret 'accepted by the governed population' strictly, the government fails condition (i). But if the colonial government is efficient and benevolent, providing better security and welfare than any available alternative, one faces the uncomfortable question of whether procedural legitimacy alone can ground a judgement of illegitimacy.
missing_condition: The definition does not adequately specify whose acceptance is required for procedural legitimacy, nor does it address whether effective governance can confer a degree of legitimacy even in the absence of procedural consent by the governed. A condition clarifying that the acceptance must come from the governed population itself, and addressing the relationship between procedural consent and effective governance, is needed.
passage: One might define a legitimate government as one that came to power through accepted procedures, governs in accordance with established laws, and provides for the security and welfare of its citizens. The procedural condition ensures that the government's origin is not tainted by usurpation or fraud. The rule-of-law condition guards against tyranny: a legitimate government governs by law, not by whim. And the welfare condition ensures that the government fulfils its basic obligations to those it governs. These conditions jointly describe a government that is both procedurally sound and substantively adequate. Yet one might observe that the procedural condition is ambiguous in a crucial respect. Consider a colonial government established by a foreign power through procedures entirely regular by the standards of the colonising nation. The colonising parliament votes to annex the territory; a governor is duly appointed; laws are enacted and enforced. The colonial government governs by law and provides effectively for the security and welfare of the colonised population, building hospitals, roads, and schools where none existed before. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met. And the procedures by which the government came to power were accepted — but accepted by the colonisers, not by the colonised. The definition does not specify whose acceptance is required. If acceptance by the governed population is demanded, the colonial government fails, however benevolent it may be. If acceptance by any relevant political authority suffices, the colonial government may pass. The definition leaves this question unresolved, and yet it is precisely this question that determines whether colonial rule can ever be legitimate.

definition: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme authority within a defined territory — no higher authority overrides its decisions, (ii) E controls the use of force within that territory, and (iii) E is recognised as sovereign by other sovereign entities.
conditions: E exercises supreme authority within a defined territory
E controls the use of force within that territory
E is recognised as sovereign by other sovereign entities
counterexample: Consider Taiwan. Taiwan exercises supreme authority within its territory — it has its own government, legislature, judiciary, and constitution. It controls the use of force within its borders through its own military and police. But a significant number of sovereign states do not recognise Taiwan as a sovereign entity, owing to the political position of the People's Republic of China. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) is only partially met. The definition implies that Taiwan's sovereignty depends upon the recognition of other states — that a political entity's sovereignty can be conferred or withheld by external parties whose decisions may be driven by political expediency rather than by any assessment of the entity's actual governance.
missing_condition: The definition makes sovereignty partly dependent on external recognition, but the case reveals that a political entity may exercise all the substantive attributes of sovereignty while being denied recognition for reasons unrelated to its actual governance. A condition grounding sovereignty in the entity's effective exercise of authority rather than in the opinions of other states is needed.
passage: One might propose that a political entity is sovereign if and only if it exercises supreme authority within a defined territory, controls the use of force within that territory, and is recognised as sovereign by other sovereign entities. The first condition captures the internal dimension of sovereignty: within its territory, the sovereign's word is final. The second condition ensures that the sovereign has the means to enforce its authority. The third condition adds an external dimension: sovereignty is not merely an internal matter but requires acknowledgement by the community of states. Together, the conditions describe a political entity that is internally supreme, capable of self-defence, and externally acknowledged. Yet one might observe that the recognition condition introduces a peculiar circularity and an uncomfortable political dimension. Consider the case of Taiwan. Taiwan has a functioning government that exercises supreme authority within its territory. It has a military and a police force that control the use of force within its borders. It conducts its own foreign policy, issues its own currency, and administers its own laws. By conditions (i) and (ii), Taiwan appears to be sovereign. But a significant number of states withhold recognition, largely for reasons of political accommodation with the People's Republic of China. If the definition is applied strictly, Taiwan is not sovereign — not because it lacks any substantive attribute of sovereignty, but because certain foreign governments find it politically convenient to deny its status. The definition, it appears, has placed sovereignty partly in the hands of external parties whose judgements may reflect political calculation rather than any assessment of the entity's actual governance.

definition: An act A constitutes civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a deliberate violation of a law, (ii) A is performed publicly and openly, not in secret, and (iii) A is motivated by a moral or political objection to the law being violated.
conditions: A is a deliberate violation of a law
A is performed publicly and openly
A is motivated by moral or political objection to the violated law
counterexample: Consider the case of a group of citizens who deliberately and publicly violate a racial segregation law by entering a whites-only establishment. Their action is motivated by a moral objection — not, however, to the segregation law they are violating, but to the broader system of racial injustice of which the segregation law is merely one expression. They have no particular objection to the specific statute they violate; they violate it as a means of drawing public attention to the wider injustice. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met. But condition (iii) requires that the moral objection be to the law being violated, and the protestors' objection is to the broader system, not to the specific statute. The definition too narrowly links the objection to the particular law that is broken.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the moral objection be directed at the specific law being violated, but many acts of civil disobedience involve the violation of a particular law as a means of protesting a broader injustice or a different law altogether. A condition permitting the moral objection to be directed at the wider legal or political order, rather than exclusively at the specific law being broken, is needed.
passage: One might define civil disobedience as a deliberate, public violation of a law motivated by a moral or political objection to the law being violated. The deliberateness condition distinguishes civil disobedience from accidental or negligent lawbreaking. The publicity condition distinguishes it from ordinary criminality, which is typically conducted in secret. And the moral motivation condition distinguishes it from lawbreaking motivated by self-interest. Together, these conditions describe an act that is intentional, transparent, and principled. Yet one might observe that the third condition, as stated, is too restrictive. Consider the sit-in protests against racial segregation. The protestors deliberately and publicly violated segregation ordinances by entering establishments reserved for whites. Their action was unquestionably motivated by moral conviction. But the moral objection was not necessarily directed at the specific ordinance being violated. The protestors were not primarily concerned with the particular statute that forbade them to sit at a particular lunch counter; they were concerned with the entire system of racial injustice, of which the segregation ordinance was merely one expression. They chose to violate that ordinance because it provided a visible and dramatic means of challenging the broader system. The definition, by requiring that the objection be directed at the violated law, fails to accommodate this common and arguably paradigmatic form of civil disobedience, in which a particular law is violated as a strategic means of protesting a wider injustice.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) there exists a corresponding duty on the part of others to refrain from interfering with P's pursuit or possession of X, (ii) the duty is recognised and enforceable by the legal or political institutions of P's society, and (iii) P can voluntarily waive the right — that is, P can release others from their duty.
conditions: Others have a duty to refrain from interfering with P's pursuit of X
The duty is recognised and enforceable by legal or political institutions
P can voluntarily waive the right
counterexample: Consider the right not to be enslaved. There is a corresponding duty on others to refrain from enslaving a person, and this duty is recognised and enforceable by the legal institutions of most societies. But the right not to be enslaved is generally held to be inalienable — a person cannot voluntarily waive it. A contract by which a person consents to be enslaved is void in most legal systems, precisely because the right is not one that the holder can waive. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) is not, and yet the right not to be enslaved is perhaps the most fundamental of all rights. The definition, by requiring waivability, excludes inalienable rights from the category of rights altogether.
missing_condition: The definition requires that all rights be waivable, but some of the most important rights — the right not to be enslaved, the right not to be tortured — are inalienable and cannot be voluntarily surrendered. A condition distinguishing waivable from inalienable rights, rather than treating waivability as a universal requirement, is needed.
passage: One might propose that a person has a right to something if and only if others have a duty to refrain from interfering with the person's pursuit or possession of it, the duty is recognised and enforceable by the legal institutions of the society, and the right-holder can voluntarily waive the right. The first condition captures the correlativity of rights and duties that has been a cornerstone of rights theory since Hohfeld. The second condition ensures that the right has institutional backing — it is not a mere moral aspiration but a legally protected entitlement. The third condition reflects the principle that rights belong to their holders, who may dispose of them as they see fit: one may waive one's right to privacy by consenting to a search, or one's right to silence by choosing to speak. The three conditions together describe a right that is correlative, institutionally grounded, and at the disposal of its holder. Yet one might observe that the waivability condition is too strong. Consider the right not to be enslaved. Others have a duty to refrain from enslaving any person; this duty is recognised and enforced by the legal institutions of virtually every modern society. But the right not to be enslaved is inalienable. A person cannot validly consent to enslavement; a contract purporting to effect such consent is void. Condition (iii) fails. The definition therefore implies that the right not to be enslaved is not a right at all — a conclusion that is plainly absurd. What the definition has overlooked is that some rights are precisely those that cannot be waived, and that their inalienability is a feature of their importance, not evidence of their non-existence.

definition: A citizen C has a political obligation to obey the laws of them state S if and only if (i) C has consented — explicitly or tacitly — to the authority of S, (ii) C receives benefits from the legal and institutional order maintained by S, and (iii) S is a reasonably just state — its laws do not systematically violate the basic interests of its citizens.
conditions: C has consented to the authority of S
C receives benefits from S's legal and institutional order
S is a reasonably just state
counterexample: Consider an immigrant who has been brought to a country as a child and has never been offered the opportunity to consent to its authority — no oath of allegiance has been administered, no naturalisation ceremony performed, and the conditions under which she lives do not plausibly constitute tacit consent, since she has no realistic opportunity to leave. She receives benefits from the state's legal order — protection, education, infrastructure. And the state is reasonably just. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met, but condition (i) is doubtful at best. The definition implies that she has no political obligation, yet she may feel — and others may believe — that she does. The consent condition proves to be both too demanding and too artificial as a foundation for political obligation.
missing_condition: The definition grounds political obligation in consent, but many citizens have never consented in any meaningful sense. A condition grounding political obligation in the fairness of the social arrangement — the idea that those who benefit from a cooperative scheme have an obligation to bear their share of its burdens, regardless of whether they have consented — would provide a more adequate foundation.
passage: One might define political obligation as follows: a citizen has an obligation to obey the laws of her state if and only if she has consented to its authority, she receives benefits from its legal order, and the state is reasonably just. The consent condition reflects the social-contract tradition: political authority is legitimate only when those subject to it have agreed to accept it. The benefits condition ensures reciprocity: the citizen who enjoys the protections and services of the state owes something in return. The justice condition limits the scope of obligation: no one is obliged to obey a state that systematically oppresses its citizens. These conditions together describe a political obligation that is voluntary, reciprocal, and morally conditioned. Yet one might observe that the consent condition is, in practice, almost impossible to satisfy. Consider a person who was brought to a country as a young child. She did not choose to come; no one asked her consent; she has grown up within the legal order of the state without ever being offered the opportunity to accept or refuse its authority. The notion of tacit consent — that by remaining in the territory she implicitly consents — is strained, for she may have no realistic opportunity to leave. She receives benefits from the state's order, and the state is reasonably just. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met. But condition (i) is not. The definition implies that she has no political obligation. This conclusion seems unsatisfying: she participates in the cooperative arrangements of the society, benefits from them, and arguably owes her fair share of the cooperative burden regardless of whether she has ever consented. The definition, it appears, has placed too much weight on the fiction of consent and too little on the reality of fair reciprocity.

definition: A distribution of goods in a society is just if and only if (i) the goods were acquired through legitimate means — no theft, fraud, or coercion was involved, (ii) any transfer of goods from one party to another was voluntary and uncoerced, and (iii) the initial distribution from which all subsequent transfers proceeded was itself fair.
conditions: Goods were acquired through legitimate means
Transfers of goods were voluntary and uncoerced
The initial distribution was fair
counterexample: Consider a society that began with a fair distribution of resources and in which all subsequent acquisitions and transfers have been voluntary and legitimate. Over several generations, small differences in talent, luck, and preference compound into vast inequalities: a few families possess enormous wealth while the majority live in deprivation. No theft or fraud has occurred; every transfer was voluntary. All three conditions are met. Yet the resulting distribution — in which a small minority controls the bulk of the resources while the majority lacks the means for a decent life — seems unjust, even though no injustice was committed at any individual step.
missing_condition: The definition evaluates the justice of a distribution solely by the legitimacy of the process that produced it, without regard to the outcome. A condition requiring that the resulting distribution not fall below some threshold of fairness — that cumulative processes, however individually legitimate, not be permitted to produce outcomes that deny a substantial portion of the population the means for a decent life — is needed.
passage: One might define a just distribution of goods as one in which all goods were acquired through legitimate means, all transfers were voluntary and uncoerced, and the initial distribution was fair. This definition embodies a procedural or historical conception of justice: a distribution is just if it arose from just processes, regardless of what the distribution looks like. The legitimacy condition excludes ill-gotten gains. The voluntariness condition ensures that no one is forced to part with what is rightfully hers. And the fairness of the initial distribution ensures that the process began from a just starting point. If the starting point is fair and every subsequent step is legitimate, the result — whatever it may be — is just. Yet one might observe that this reasoning leads to conclusions that are difficult to accept. Consider a society that begins with a fair distribution and in which all subsequent transactions are voluntary and above reproach. Over many generations, small differences in ability, industry, and fortune compound: some families grow immensely wealthy while others fall into destitution. No injustice was committed at any step; every acquisition was earned, every transfer freely made. All three conditions are met. Yet the result is a society in which a handful of citizens possess more than they could consume in a lifetime while a great many lack the necessities of life. The definition declares this distribution just, since the process was just at every step. But many would judge the outcome profoundly unjust, for the cumulative effect of individually legitimate transactions has been to create a society in which a large portion of the population is denied the material conditions for a decent existence. What the definition lacks is a condition evaluating the outcome as well as the process.

definition: A political system P is a democracy if and only if (i) the holders of governmental power are selected by periodic elections, (ii) all adult citizens are entitled to vote in those elections, and (iii) the elections are conducted by secret ballot.
conditions: Holders of governmental power are selected by periodic elections
All adult citizens are entitled to vote
Elections are conducted by secret ballot
counterexample: Consider a political system in which elections are held periodically, all adult citizens may vote, and the ballot is secret, but in which a single party controls all media, prohibits opposition candidates from campaigning, and uses the apparatus of the state to intimidate dissenters. The elections are, in form, free, but in substance they offer no genuine choice. All three conditions of the definition are met, yet one would hesitate to call such a system a democracy.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that elections involve genuine competition among candidates or parties, such that voters have a meaningful choice, as opposed to a merely formal right to cast a ballot in a contest whose outcome is predetermined.
passage: A natural analysis of democracy requires periodic elections, universal adult suffrage, and secret balloting. This analysis captures the procedural features that distinguish democratic from non-democratic systems and has the virtue of providing clear, observable criteria. Yet there is a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider a state in which elections are held every five years, in which all adult citizens are entitled to vote, and in which voting is conducted by secret ballot. The formal conditions of the definition are met. But suppose further that a single party controls the entirety of the press, radio, and public discourse. Opposition candidates are permitted to stand but are denied access to media, forbidden to hold public meetings, and subject to harassment by the security services. The elections are a pageant whose outcome is never in doubt. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied — there are elections, universal suffrage, and secret ballots — yet the system is manifestly not a democracy in any substantive sense. What the definition lacks is a requirement that elections involve genuine competition: that voters be presented with meaningful alternatives, that opposition candidates be permitted to campaign freely, and that the electorate be able to make an informed choice among genuinely different programmes.

definition: An individual I is free with respect to an action X if and only if (i) no external agent prevents I from performing X, (ii) no law or regulation prohibits I from performing X, and (iii) I is not subject to the threat of punishment for performing X.
conditions: No external agent prevents I from performing X
No law prohibits I from performing X
I is not subject to threat of punishment for X
counterexample: Consider a man in a society with no laws against travel, no agents preventing him from leaving his town, and no threat of punishment for doing so. Yet the man is destitute — he has no money for transport, no shoes for walking, and no food for the journey. He is free, by the definition, to travel wherever he pleases. But his freedom is entirely notional; the absence of external constraint is of no use to one who lacks the material means to exercise it. The definition declares him free, yet he cannot go anywhere.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that I possess the material means or capacities necessary to perform X, thereby confusing the absence of prohibition with the genuine ability to act.
passage: One might analyse freedom as the absence of external prevention, legal prohibition, and the threat of punishment. This analysis — which captures what has been called the negative conception of liberty — has a long and distinguished pedigree. It provides clear criteria: one asks whether any agent blocks the action, whether any law forbids it, and whether any sanction attends it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is readily at hand. Consider a man living in a society that places no legal restrictions on travel, employs no agents to prevent his movement, and threatens no punishment for departure. He is, by the definition, perfectly free to travel. But the man is penniless. He owns no vehicle, has no money for a fare, possesses no shoes adequate for a long walk, and has no provisions for the journey. His freedom to travel is entirely formal — the absence of obstruction is of no practical value to one who lacks the means to move. The definition declares him free, yet he is as effectively confined as if a wall surrounded his town. What is missing is a condition requiring that the individual possess the material means or effective capacities necessary to exercise the freedom in question. Without such a condition, the analysis mistakes the absence of interference for the genuine ability to act.

definition: A society S achieves equality if and only if (i) the laws of S treat all citizens identically without regard to social position, (ii) all citizens have the same formal rights and legal protections, and (iii) no citizen is barred by law from any public office or occupation.
conditions: Laws treat all citizens identically
All citizens have the same formal rights
No citizen is barred by law from any office or occupation
counterexample: Consider a society in which the laws are scrupulously identical for all citizens, all possess the same formal rights, and no legal barrier prevents anyone from holding any office. Yet the society contains vast disparities of inherited wealth, and in practice only the children of the rich can afford the education necessary for professional or political life. The laws are impartial, but the social reality is profoundly unequal. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the equality is a fiction.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that citizens possess roughly comparable opportunities to exercise their formal rights, thereby confusing legal equality with substantive equality of condition.
passage: One might propose that equality is achieved when the laws treat all citizens identically, all possess the same formal rights, and no one is barred by law from any office or occupation. This proposal captures an important dimension of equality — equality before the law — and has the merit of providing criteria that can be assessed by inspecting the statute books. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is uncomfortably familiar. Consider a society whose laws satisfy all three conditions. There is no legal distinction among citizens; every right available to one is available to all; the highest offices are open to anyone. But the society is marked by vast inequalities of inherited wealth. The sons of the rich attend excellent schools, receive private tuition, make influential connexions, and proceed to the professions and to political office as a matter of course. The sons of the poor attend inadequate schools, leave education early, and spend their lives in manual labour. The formal rights are the same, but the practical ability to exercise them is not. The equality proclaimed by the law is, in substance, a sham. What the definition overlooks is that formal legal equality is compatible with profound substantive inequality, and that genuine equality requires not merely identical laws but roughly comparable conditions under which the laws may be exercised.

definition: An agent A has authority over a group G if and only if (i) A issues directives to the members of G, (ii) the members of G generally comply with A's directives, and (iii) A possesses the capacity to impose sanctions upon non-compliant members.
conditions: A issues directives to members of G
Members of G generally comply
A possesses the capacity to impose sanctions
counterexample: Consider an armed bandit who occupies a village. He issues directives — demanding tribute, forbidding certain activities, commanding labour. The villagers comply, for the bandit has the capacity and the will to punish those who disobey. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to say the bandit has authority; he has power, certainly, but authority implies a right to issue directives, not merely the capacity to enforce compliance through violence.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that A's directives be regarded by the members of G as rightful or legitimate, thereby confusing mere coercive power with genuine authority.
passage: One might analyse authority as the issuing of directives, general compliance, and the capacity to impose sanctions. This analysis captures an important feature of authority — that it involves a practical capacity to direct behaviour and to enforce compliance. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is not far to seek. Imagine an armed bandit who descends upon a small village. He issues directives: he demands a portion of the harvest, forbids the villagers from leaving, and commands them to labour on his behalf. The villagers comply — they have little choice, for the bandit has weapons and henchmen capable of punishing disobedience. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. The bandit issues directives, the villagers comply, and the bandit can impose sanctions. Yet it would be deeply misleading to say the bandit has authority over the villagers. He has power — brute, coercive power — but authority is something different. Authority implies a right to direct, not merely the capacity to compel. The villagers obey the bandit not because they recognise his right to command but because they fear the consequences of resistance. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring that the directives be regarded by the subjects as rightful or legitimate — that compliance be grounded in a recognition of right rather than mere submission to force.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G came to power through procedures recognised as lawful within the political order, (ii) G governs in accordance with established constitutional norms, and (iii) G maintains public order and provides basic security to its citizens.
conditions: G came to power through lawful procedures
G governs according to constitutional norms
G maintains public order and provides security
counterexample: Consider a colonial government imposed upon a subject people by a foreign power. The colonial administration may have been established through procedures lawful under the imperial constitution, may govern according to established norms, and may maintain order and security with considerable efficiency. All three conditions are met. Yet the subject people may regard the government as wholly illegitimate, on the ground that no foreign power has the right to rule them without their consent. Legitimacy, in their eyes, requires not merely procedural correctness but the consent of the governed.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the governed consent to or accept the government's right to rule, thereby allowing governments imposed by external force to count as legitimate despite the manifest absence of popular endorsement.
passage: One might define legitimate government as government that came to power lawfully, governs constitutionally, and maintains public order. This definition captures many of the features we associate with legitimate governance: the peaceful transfer of power, the rule of law, the provision of security. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the history of colonial administration. Consider a colonial government installed by a European power over an African or Asian territory. The government may have been established through procedures perfectly lawful under the imperial constitution — an act of parliament, a royal charter, a treaty among European powers. It may govern the territory according to well-established administrative norms. And it may maintain public order with considerable efficiency, providing roads, courts, and police. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet the subject people may regard the government as wholly illegitimate. They did not choose it; they were not consulted; their consent was neither sought nor given. The government was imposed upon them by force, and its lawfulness under the imperial constitution is, from their perspective, entirely beside the point. What the definition overlooks is the requirement that the governed accept or consent to the government's right to rule. Without this condition, a government may satisfy every formal criterion of legitimacy while being regarded by those it governs as an alien imposition.

definition: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme legislative authority within its territory, (ii) E is not subject to a higher political authority, and (iii) E controls the legitimate use of force within its borders.
conditions: E exercises supreme legislative authority
E is not subject to a higher political authority
E controls the legitimate use of force within its borders
counterexample: Consider a member state of a federal union — one of the constituent states of a federation. Such a state does not exercise supreme legislative authority, for the federal constitution and federal statutes override its own; it is subject to a higher political authority, namely the federal government; and the control of force is divided between the state and the federation.
