Yet such states are frequently described as possessing a form of sovereignty — residual sovereignty, or sovereignty within their sphere of competence. The definition, framed for the unitary nation-state, cannot accommodate this divided or layered sovereignty.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that sovereignty may be divided, shared, or layered rather than unitary, and thus cannot accommodate federal systems in which authority is distributed across multiple levels of government.
passage: A standard analysis of sovereignty requires that a political entity exercise supreme legislative authority, be subject to no higher authority, and control the use of force within its territory. This analysis captures the concept of sovereignty as it was developed in the early modern period — the absolute, undivided authority of the nation-state. Yet there is a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider a member state within a federal system — one of the constituent states of a federation such as existed in various forms throughout the nineteenth century. Such a state does not exercise supreme legislative authority: the federal constitution and federal statutes may override its own laws. It is subject to a higher political authority: the federal government may intervene in its affairs on specified grounds. And control of force is divided: the state may maintain its own militia or police, but the federal government commands the army. None of the three conditions is fully satisfied. Yet the member state is commonly described as possessing sovereignty — sovereignty within its allotted sphere, sovereignty residual to the federal constitution, sovereignty shared with the central government. The definition, designed for the unitary and absolute sovereignty of the nation-state, cannot accommodate the layered and divided sovereignty characteristic of federal systems.

definition: An act A by a citizen C constitutes civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a deliberate violation of a law that C regards as unjust, (ii) A is performed publicly and non-violently, and (iii) C willingly accepts the legal consequences of A.
conditions: A is a deliberate violation of a law regarded as unjust
A is performed publicly and non-violently
C willingly accepts the legal consequences
counterexample: Consider a group of citizens who deliberately violate a law they regard as unjust — say, a law requiring racial segregation on public transport — by sitting in seats reserved for another race. Their action is public and non-violent, and they willingly accept arrest. All three conditions are met. But suppose these citizens do not regard the specific law they violate as unjust; rather, they violate the segregation law in order to protest a different injustice entirely — say, an unjust foreign policy. The law they break is not the law they oppose. The first condition requires that the violated law be the one regarded as unjust, but many acts of civil disobedience are indirect, targeting accessible laws to draw attention to inaccessible injustices.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate indirect civil disobedience, in which the law violated is not itself the object of protest but is broken as a means of drawing public attention to a different injustice.
passage: One might define civil disobedience as the deliberate, public, and non-violent violation of a law regarded as unjust, accompanied by a willingness to accept punishment. This definition captures the classic instances: the refusal to comply with a discriminatory law, performed openly and peacefully, with the protestor accepting the legal consequences as the price of conscience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises in connexion with what one might call indirect disobedience. Consider a group of citizens who are deeply opposed to their government's foreign policy, which they regard as unjust. The foreign policy is not embodied in any law that the citizens can meaningfully violate — it is enacted by the executive, conducted by diplomats and the military, and lies beyond the citizens' direct sphere of action. In protest, the citizens deliberately violate a domestic law — say, a law against trespassing on government property — by occupying a government building. Their action is public, non-violent, and they willingly accept arrest. They do not regard the trespassing law as unjust; they violate it solely as a means of drawing public attention to the foreign policy they oppose. The first condition of the definition is not satisfied — the violated law is not the one regarded as unjust. Yet the action possesses all the moral seriousness and political character of civil disobedience. What the definition overlooks is that civil disobedience may be indirect, targeting an accessible law as a vehicle for protesting an inaccessible injustice.

definition: An individual I possesses a right to X if and only if (i) I has a valid claim against others that they not interfere with I's pursuit or enjoyment of X, (ii) the claim is recognised and enforceable within I's legal system, and (iii) the claim derives from a general principle applicable to all persons in I's position.
conditions: I has a valid claim against interference
The claim is legally recognised and enforceable
The claim derives from a general principle
counterexample: Consider the rights claimed by the early abolitionists on behalf of enslaved persons. The abolitionists maintained that every human being had a right to liberty — a claim against interference derived from the general principle of human dignity. Yet the legal systems of the slaveholding states did not recognise or enforce any such claim. The second condition was emphatically not met. Yet the abolitionists were not speaking nonsense; they were asserting moral rights that they believed ought to be legal rights, and subsequent history vindicated their claim.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate moral or natural rights — rights that are claimed on the basis of moral principles independently of whether any legal system recognises them — and thus collapses the distinction between legal and moral rights.
passage: One might analyse the concept of a right as a valid claim against interference, recognised and enforceable at law, and derived from a general principle. This analysis captures many paradigmatic cases of legal rights: the right to property, the right to free movement, the right to assemble peacefully. In each case, there is a claim against interference, the claim is recognised by the legal system, and it derives from a general principle applicable to all citizens. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the history of moral reform. Consider the position of the early abolitionists who argued that enslaved persons possessed a right to liberty. Their claim was that every human being, by virtue of being human, had a right not to be held in bondage. This claim was derived from a general principle — the dignity and moral equality of all persons. And they maintained that others — slaveholders, legislators, judges — were bound to respect this right. But the legal systems of the slaveholding states did not recognise any such claim. Enslaved persons had no standing at law; their putative right to liberty was not enforceable in any court. The second condition of the definition is not satisfied. Yet the abolitionists were not asserting a mere wish or preference; they were asserting a moral right that they believed to hold independently of legal recognition. What the definition overlooks is the category of moral or natural rights — rights that exist by virtue of moral principles and that may be asserted against legal systems that fail to recognise them.

definition: A distribution of goods D in a society S is just if and only if (i) D is the result of a process to which all affected parties have consented, (ii) D is consistent with the legal rules of S, and (iii) D does not arise from fraud or coercion.
conditions: D results from a process to which all affected parties consented
D is consistent with legal rules
D does not arise from fraud or coercion
counterexample: Consider a society in which a feudal lord owns the vast majority of the land, and the peasants who work it receive only a bare subsistence. The distribution arose historically from a process — the feudal settlement — to which the ancestors of both lord and peasant are said to have consented. It is consistent with the legal rules of the society. And it arose not from fraud but from a long-established social arrangement. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that a distribution which leaves those who labour with almost nothing while granting abundance to those who do not is unjust, regardless of its procedural pedigree.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the distribution bear some reasonable relation to the needs, contributions, or deserts of the parties, thereby allowing grossly unequal distributions to count as just so long as the procedural conditions are met.
passage: A plausible analysis of distributive justice might require that a distribution result from a consented process, accord with legal rules, and be free of fraud or coercion. This analysis has the attraction of focusing upon procedure rather than outcome — it asks not whether the distribution is equal or proportional but whether it arose through fair processes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this purely procedural analysis is not difficult to construct. Consider a feudal society in which a lord holds the vast majority of the land and the peasants who work it receive only enough to sustain themselves. The feudal distribution arose from a historical settlement to which, it is maintained, the ancestors of both lord and peasant consented. The distribution is consistent with the legal rules of the society — the lord's title is recognised by law, and the peasants' obligations are legally defined. And the arrangement arose not from fraud but from a long-established social order. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet one might observe that a distribution which leaves the many who labour in grinding poverty while granting abundance to the few who do not can hardly be called just. The definition, by attending exclusively to procedure, ignores the substantive character of the distribution. What is needed is a further condition relating the distribution to the needs, contributions, or deserts of the affected parties.

definition: A citizen C has a political obligation to obey a law L if and only if (i) L was enacted by a government that C has consented to, (ii) L applies equally to all citizens, and (iii) obedience to L does not require C to act contrary to widely shared moral principles.
conditions: L was enacted by a government C consented to
L applies equally to all citizens
Obedience to L does not require acting contrary to widely shared moral principles
counterexample: Consider a citizen of a democratic state who has never voted, never participated in political life, and has taken no action that could be construed as consent to the government. She lives quietly within the state's borders, obeys its laws, and benefits from its services. A new law is enacted requiring all citizens to register for national service. The law applies equally to all and does not require her to violate any moral principle. But the first condition is not met — she has not consented to the government in any recognisable sense. Yet it seems strange to say she has no obligation to obey the law, given that she has lived under the government's protection all her life.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that political obligation may arise from sources other than consent — from the receipt of benefits, from fair play, or from the mere fact of long residence under a government's protection — and thus excludes those who have never consented yet appear to be bound.
passage: One might propose that a citizen has a political obligation to obey a law when it was enacted by a government to which the citizen has consented, applies equally to all, and does not demand morally objectionable conduct. This proposal locates obligation in consent, and consent has long been regarded as the most intuitively satisfying ground of political obligation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the citizen who has never consented. Consider a woman who has lived her entire life within a democratic state. She has never voted — not from principle, but from indifference. She has never signed any document, sworn any oath, or performed any act that could be interpreted as consent to the government. She has simply lived within the state's borders, obeyed its laws, used its roads and hospitals, and benefited from the order it maintains. A new law is enacted requiring national service. The law applies equally to all citizens and violates no moral principle. But the first condition of the definition is not met — the woman has not consented to the government. The definition compels us to say she has no obligation to obey. Yet this conclusion seems wrong: she has lived under the government's protection, benefited from its services, and participated in the social order it sustains. What the definition overlooks is that political obligation may arise from sources other than consent — from the receipt of benefits, from the demands of fair play, or from the simple fact of long-standing membership in a political community.

definition: A political system S is a democracy if and only if (i) the laws of S are enacted by representatives chosen in free and regular elections, (ii) all adult citizens of S have an equal right to vote in these elections, and (iii) the elections offer a genuine choice among competing candidates or parties.
conditions: Laws are enacted by elected representatives
All adult citizens have an equal right to vote
Elections offer genuine choice among competitors
counterexample: Consider a state in which free elections are held regularly, all adult citizens may vote, and multiple parties compete vigorously. The elected representatives enact the laws. All three conditions are met. But the constitution of this state places certain fundamental matters — the official religion, the structure of the military, the composition of the judiciary — beyond the reach of the elected legislature. An unelected council of religious authorities retains a veto over legislation that touches these matters. The elected representatives can legislate freely on most issues, but on the most consequential questions their power is circumscribed by an unelected body. The state satisfies the definition of democracy, yet the most important decisions are made by persons who are not subject to democratic accountability.
missing_condition: The definition requires elected representatives and free elections but does not require that the elected body possess supreme legislative authority, and thus cannot exclude systems in which an unelected body retains effective control over the most important matters of state.
passage: It appears natural to define a democracy as a political system in which laws are enacted by elected representatives, all adult citizens have an equal right to vote, and elections offer a genuine choice among competing candidates or parties. This definition captures the central features of the political systems most confidently called democratic: the representative assemblies, the universal franchise, the competitive elections. In the paradigmatic cases — the parliamentary democracies of Western Europe, for instance — these three conditions are jointly satisfied, and the system is democratic in substance as well as form. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by political systems in which free and competitive elections coexist with unelected bodies that retain effective power over the most important matters. Consider a state in which elections are free, the franchise is universal, and multiple parties compete. The elected parliament enacts legislation on a wide range of domestic issues. But the constitution reserves certain fundamental questions — the state religion, the structure of the armed forces, the appointment of senior judges — to an unelected council of religious authorities, which retains a veto over any legislation that touches these domains. The parliament is democratically elected, but its sovereignty is limited. The definition is satisfied in all three conditions, yet the most consequential decisions are made by a body that owes nothing to the democratic process. One might observe that the definition captures the electoral mechanism of democracy but not its substance — the requirement that the sovereign power be answerable to the people — and that a system in which the most important questions are decided by an unelected authority is democratic in form but not in substance.

definition: A state S is sovereign if and only if (i) S exercises supreme authority over a defined territory, (ii) S is recognised as a state by other states in the international community, and (iii) S has the capacity to enforce its laws within its territory without external interference.
conditions: S exercises supreme authority over a defined territory
S is recognised by other states
S has the capacity to enforce its laws without external interference
counterexample: Consider a small state that is recognised by the international community and exercises authority over its defined territory. However, its economy is entirely dependent upon a single powerful neighbour, which supplies its energy, controls its banking system, and stations military forces on its soil by treaty. The small state can enforce its laws — its police and courts function — but only because the powerful neighbour permits it to do so. If the neighbour withdrew its economic support or exercised its treaty right to intervene, the small state's authority would collapse. All three conditions of the definition are formally met, yet the small state's sovereignty is, in practice, nominal. Its authority persists at the sufferance of another power.
missing_condition: The definition requires the capacity to enforce laws without external interference but does not require that this capacity be independent of the will of another state, and thus cannot distinguish genuine sovereignty from a nominal sovereignty that persists only because a more powerful state permits it.
passage: One might define sovereignty as the condition in which a state exercises supreme authority over a defined territory, is recognised by other states, and has the capacity to enforce its laws without external interference. This definition captures the features of the paradigmatic sovereign states: they govern their territories, they are recognised in international law, and they maintain the apparatus of enforcement — police, courts, military — necessary to give effect to their laws. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by small states whose formal sovereignty is underwritten by the tolerance of a more powerful neighbour. Consider a small state that satisfies all three conditions. It exercises authority over its territory, it is recognised by the international community, and its police and courts function. But its economy depends entirely upon a single powerful neighbour that supplies its energy, controls its financial system, and maintains military forces on its soil under a treaty that the small state had little choice but to accept. The small state enforces its laws, but only because the powerful neighbour permits it to do so. Were the neighbour to withdraw economic support, the small state's government would be unable to pay its civil servants or maintain its armed forces. Were the neighbour to exercise its treaty right to intervene, the small state could offer no effective resistance. The definition is satisfied, but the sovereignty is nominal. One might observe that the definition captures the formal attributes of sovereignty — authority, recognition, enforcement — without requiring that these attributes be genuinely independent of external will, and that a state whose enforcement capacity exists at the pleasure of another power is sovereign only in name.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) P has a legitimate claim to X that others are obligated to respect, (ii) the claim to X is recognised by the legal or political order under which P lives, and (iii) there exist institutional mechanisms for the enforcement of P's claim.
conditions: P has a legitimate claim that others must respect
The claim is recognised by the legal or political order
Institutional mechanisms exist for enforcement
counterexample: Consider the inhabitants of a colony governed by a metropolitan power that denies them any legal standing. The colonists have no recognised legal claim to self-governance, and there are no institutional mechanisms through which they could enforce such a claim. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are not met. Yet one might hold — and many political thinkers have held — that the colonists have a right to self-governance that the metropolitan power violates. The right exists prior to and independently of any legal recognition or enforcement mechanism. The definition cannot accommodate rights that have not yet been recognised by the prevailing political order.
missing_condition: The definition makes legal recognition and institutional enforcement necessary conditions for the possession of a right, but moral and political rights may exist in advance of — and in opposition to — the legal order, as the history of struggles against colonial rule and unjust legislation amply demonstrates.
passage: It appears natural to propose that a person has a right to something if and only if the person has a legitimate claim that others are obligated to respect, the claim is recognised by the legal order, and institutional mechanisms exist for its enforcement. This definition captures the character of rights as they function in well-ordered legal systems: a citizen's right to property is recognised by law, enforced by the courts, and entails obligations on the part of others not to interfere. The definition connects rights to the institutional apparatus that gives them practical force. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the rights of persons living under political orders that deny them legal standing. Consider the inhabitants of a colony governed by a distant metropolitan power. The colonial subjects are denied the right to govern themselves; the legal order under which they live does not recognise any such claim, and there are no institutional mechanisms through which they might enforce it. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are unambiguously violated. Yet a long and distinguished tradition in political thought has maintained that these persons have a right to self-governance — a right that the colonial power violates — and that this right exists prior to and independently of any legal recognition. The American and French revolutionaries, among many others, appealed to rights that the prevailing legal orders did not recognise and had no mechanism to enforce. One might observe that the definition confuses the enforcement of rights with their existence, and that a right may be genuine, pressing, and morally binding even when no legal order recognises it and no institution stands ready to enforce it.

definition: A person P is free with respect to an action A if and only if (i) no external agent prevents P from performing A, (ii) no law prohibits P from performing A, and (iii) P faces no penalty or sanction for performing A.
conditions: No external agent prevents P from performing A
No law prohibits A
P faces no penalty for performing A
counterexample: Consider a citizen of a state in which there is no law against public assembly, no agent prevents assembly, and no penalty attaches to assembling. All three conditions are met. Yet the state's security apparatus maintains comprehensive surveillance of all public gatherings, and citizens know that those who assemble are placed on lists that are subsequently used to deny them employment, housing, and advancement. No one is prevented, prohibited, or penalised, yet the surveillance creates a climate of intimidation so pervasive that few citizens dare to assemble. The citizen is free by the definition's lights but unfree in any practically meaningful sense.
missing_condition: The definition addresses only overt prevention, prohibition, and sanction, and cannot accommodate the subtle forms of unfreedom — surveillance, intimidation, social coercion — that deter action without formally preventing, prohibiting, or penalising it.
passage: One might propose that a person is free with respect to an action if no external agent prevents the action, no law prohibits it, and the person faces no penalty for performing it. This definition captures the notion of freedom as the absence of overt interference: one is free to walk in the park if no one bars the gate, no ordinance forbids entry, and no sanction attaches to walking there. The definition is admirably clear and has the virtue of making freedom an empirically assessable condition. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the forms of unfreedom that operate through intimidation rather than overt coercion. Consider a state in which there is no law against public assembly, no agent physically prevents citizens from gathering, and no formal penalty attaches to those who do. The three conditions are satisfied: the citizen is free to assemble. But the state maintains a comprehensive apparatus of surveillance. Every public gathering is photographed and catalogued; the identities of participants are recorded; and these records are subsequently used — informally but effectively — to deny participants employment, housing, and professional advancement. No prohibition is enacted, no agent stands at the door, no penalty is formally imposed. Yet citizens know, from the experience of their fellows, that assembling carries grave practical consequences, and few dare to exercise their nominal freedom. One might observe that the definition captures freedom from overt interference but not freedom from the subtler forms of coercion — surveillance, informal sanction, the creation of a climate of fear — that are in practice far more effective at suppressing action than outright prohibition.

definition: A person or body A has political authority over a population P if and only if (i) A issues directives that P is expected to obey, (ii) A's directives are backed by the power to compel obedience, and (iii) P generally does in fact obey A's directives.
conditions: A issues directives P is expected to obey
A's directives are backed by compulsive power
P generally obeys A's directives
counterexample: Consider an occupying army that issues directives to a conquered population. The directives are backed by overwhelming military force, and the population generally complies out of fear. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to say that the occupying army has political authority over the population in the sense in which a legitimate government has authority over its citizens. The population obeys because it has no choice, not because it recognises any obligation to obey. The definition cannot distinguish authority from mere domination.
missing_condition: The definition captures the external features of authority — command, power, and compliance — but does not require that the population recognise A's directives as binding in a normative sense, and thus cannot distinguish legitimate authority from mere domination by force.
passage: One might propose that a person or body has political authority over a population if it issues directives the population is expected to obey, its directives are backed by the power to compel obedience, and the population generally does obey. This definition captures the observable features of political authority: a government issues laws, it maintains police and courts to enforce them, and the citizenry generally complies. The definition has the advantage of being empirically tractable — one can determine whether its conditions are met by observing the behaviour of the authority and the population. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is that of military occupation. An occupying army issues directives to the conquered population — curfews, requisitions, prohibitions on assembly. These directives are backed by overwhelming military force. The population, having no means of effective resistance, generally complies. All three conditions of the definition are met. But it would be wrong to say that the occupying army has political authority in the sense in which the population's own government, were it still in power, would have authority. The population obeys the occupier's directives not because it recognises them as binding but because disobedience would bring violent reprisal. There is compliance without recognition, obedience without obligation. One might observe that the definition has conflated two distinct phenomena — authority, which involves a normative claim that the governed recognise, and mere domination, which involves power and compliance without normative standing — and that any adequate analysis of political authority must include the recognition by the governed that the authority's directives carry a claim to obedience that does not rest solely upon the threat of force.

definition: A distribution of goods D in a society is just if and only if (i) D was arrived at through procedures that were fair and impartial, (ii) every member of the society had an equal opportunity to participate in the procedures, and (iii) no member of the society was subjected to coercion or fraud in the course of the procedures.
conditions: D was arrived at through fair and impartial procedures
Every member had equal opportunity to participate
No member was subjected to coercion or fraud
counterexample: Consider a society in which a fair lottery is used to distribute plots of agricultural land. The lottery is genuinely impartial, every citizen has an equal chance of receiving a plot, and no coercion or fraud is involved. All three conditions are satisfied. But suppose that, by the luck of the draw, a small number of citizens receive all the fertile valley land while the remainder receive only barren hillside. The procedural fairness is impeccable, but the outcome leaves some citizens with no means of subsistence while others enjoy abundance. One might hold that a distribution which leaves some members of society destitute through no fault of their own is unjust, regardless of the fairness of the procedure that produced it.
missing_condition: The definition attends only to the fairness of procedures and not to the substantive outcomes they produce, and thus cannot identify as unjust a distribution that results from fair procedures but leaves some members of society without the resources necessary for a minimally decent life.
passage: It appears natural to define a just distribution as one arrived at through fair and impartial procedures, in which every member of society had equal opportunity to participate, and which was free from coercion and fraud. This definition has the attraction of making justice a procedural matter: if the process is fair, the outcome is just, whatever it may be. In the paradigmatic cases — the impartial administration of a competitive examination, the fair conduct of a public auction — procedural fairness does seem to confer legitimacy on the outcome. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this purely procedural approach may be constructed as follows. Consider a society that distributes agricultural land by means of a lottery. The lottery is genuinely fair: each citizen's name is entered once, the drawing is conducted publicly, and the results are binding. No coercion or fraud is involved. Every citizen had an equal opportunity to participate. All three conditions of the definition are met. But suppose that the lottery assigns all the fertile valley land to a handful of citizens, while the remainder receive only barren hillside plots unsuitable for cultivation. The lucky few will prosper; the rest will be unable to feed their families. The procedure was impeccable, but the outcome is one in which some citizens, through no fault of their own, lack the resources for a minimally decent existence. One might observe that a definition of justice that attends only to procedural fairness and ignores the substantive character of the outcomes leaves open the possibility that a perfectly fair process produces an outcome that is, by any reasonable measure, unjust.

definition: A citizen C has a political obligation to obey a law L if and only if (i) L was enacted by a legitimate authority, (ii) C has received benefits from the political order that enacted L, and (iii) C has not been excluded from participation in the political processes by which L was enacted.
conditions: L was enacted by a legitimate authority
C has received benefits from the political order
C was not excluded from political participation
counterexample: Consider a citizen of a democratic state who has voted in elections, benefited from public services, and participated fully in political life. A law is enacted, through the ordinary democratic process, that requires all citizens to perform one year of military service. The law was enacted by a legitimate authority, the citizen has received benefits, and she was not excluded from the political process. All three conditions are met. Yet the citizen is a committed pacifist whose deepest moral convictions prohibit her from participating in military activity. One might hold that her moral obligation not to participate in violence overrides her political obligation to obey the conscription law, and that a definition which generates an obligation to obey in this case without any qualification fails to account for the limits of political obligation.
missing_condition: The definition generates political obligation without qualification, but it does not account for cases in which obedience to a law would require the citizen to violate fundamental moral convictions, and thus cannot accommodate the widely recognised limits of political obligation.
passage: One might propose that a citizen has a political obligation to obey a law if the law was enacted by a legitimate authority, the citizen has received benefits from the political order, and the citizen was not excluded from political participation. This definition combines three features that have figured prominently in discussions of political obligation: the legitimacy of the authority, the reciprocity of benefits, and the inclusiveness of the process. In the ordinary case — a citizen who has voted, benefited from public education and infrastructure, and lived under democratically enacted law — the definition yields the plausible result that the citizen is obligated to obey. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when obedience would require the citizen to violate deeply held moral convictions. Consider a citizen of a democratic state who has participated fully in political life and benefited amply from the political order. The state enacts, through the ordinary democratic process, a law requiring one year of compulsory military service. All three conditions of the definition are met: the authority is legitimate, the citizen has received benefits, and she was not excluded from the process. Yet the citizen is a committed pacifist whose most fundamental moral convictions prohibit her from participating in military activity of any kind. The definition generates an unqualified obligation to obey. But one might observe that an obligation to obey the law is not absolute, and that when obedience requires a citizen to act against her deepest moral convictions, the political obligation may be overridden. The definition, by attending only to legitimacy, benefit, and inclusion, generates obligations without acknowledging the moral limits that constrain them.

definition: A political order O practises toleration if and only if (i) O permits the expression of views and practices that the majority disapproves of, (ii) O refrains from imposing penalties on those who hold or practise minority views, and (iii) O does not require conformity to the majority's views as a condition of citizenship or civic participation.
conditions: O permits expression of disapproved views and practices
O refrains from penalising minority view-holders
O does not require conformity for citizenship or civic participation
counterexample: Consider a state that permits the expression of minority religious views, imposes no penalties on those who practise minority religions, and does not require religious conformity for citizenship. All three conditions are met. Yet the state provides substantial public funding for the majority religion — its schools, its clergy, its buildings — while providing nothing for minority religions. The state does not prohibit, penalise, or exclude; it simply favours. The minority religions are tolerated in the sense of the definition but systematically disadvantaged in practice. One might hold that toleration requires not merely the absence of prohibition and penalty but some measure of even-handedness in the distribution of public resources.
missing_condition: The definition captures toleration as the absence of prohibition and penalty but does not address the systematic favouring of majority views through the distribution of public resources, which can undermine toleration in practice while satisfying the definition in letter.
passage: One might define toleration as a political order's permitting the expression of disapproved views, refraining from penalising those who hold them, and not requiring conformity as a condition of civic participation. This definition captures the core of toleration as it has been understood in the liberal tradition: the state does not suppress dissent, does not punish dissenters, and does not exclude them from public life. In the paradigmatic cases — the toleration of religious dissent in states that have established an official religion but permit others — the definition yields correct verdicts. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by states that tolerate minority practices in the sense of not prohibiting them while systematically favouring the majority through the allocation of public resources. Consider a state in which minority religious communities are free to worship, face no penalties, and are not excluded from civic life. All three conditions are met. But the state funds the majority religion generously — its schools receive public money, its clergy receive state salaries, its buildings are maintained at public expense — while providing nothing comparable for minority religions. The minority faiths are tolerated in the letter of the definition but disadvantaged in practice. Their adherents must fund their own institutions from their own resources while contributing, through taxation, to the institutions of the majority faith. One might observe that toleration, understood merely as the absence of prohibition and penalty, is compatible with a degree of systematic favouritism that undermines its practical value, and that genuine toleration requires some measure of even-handedness in the distribution of the resources by which a state supports the activities of its citizens.

definition: An event E constitutes a political revolution if and only if (i) E involves the overthrow of the existing government by force or the credible threat of force, (ii) E results in a fundamental change in the form of government, and (iii) E is carried out by a substantial portion of the population or by agents acting on its behalf.
conditions: E involves overthrow of government by force or threat of force
E results in a fundamental change in the form of government
E is carried out by a substantial portion of the population or its agents
counterexample: Consider the so-called Glorious Revolution of 1688 in England, in which William of Orange was invited by a group of English nobles to assume the throne. James II fled the country without a battle being fought. The revolution resulted in a fundamental change — the establishment of parliamentary supremacy over royal prerogative — and it was supported by a substantial portion of the political nation. But it did not involve the overthrow of the government by force or the credible threat of force directed at the government. William brought an army, to be sure, but the government was not overthrown; the king departed voluntarily. The revolution was accomplished not by violence but by a combination of political manoeuvre and royal capitulation. Yet it is called a revolution by virtually every historian.
missing_condition: The definition requires the use or threat of force, but some of the most consequential political transformations — the Glorious Revolution being the most celebrated instance — are accomplished by political manoeuvre, negotiation, or the collapse of the existing authority's will to govern, without the forcible overthrow the definition demands.
passage: It appears natural to define a political revolution as an event that involves the overthrow of the existing government by force, results in a fundamental change in the form of government, and is carried out by a substantial portion of the population or its agents. This definition captures the character of the paradigmatic revolutions: the French Revolution of 1789, with its storming of the Bastille and its radical transformation of the state; the Russian Revolution of 1917, with its armed seizure of power and the destruction of the tsarist system. In these cases, force was used, the form of government was fundamentally altered, and the revolutionary actors claimed to represent the people. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is provided by the event that English historians call the Glorious Revolution of 1688. A group of English noblemen invited William of Orange to bring his army to England and assume the throne. King James II, finding his support melting away, fled to France without offering battle. The revolution resulted in a profound transformation of the English constitution: the principle of parliamentary supremacy was established, the royal prerogative was curtailed, and the basis of political authority was fundamentally altered. It was supported by a substantial body of the political nation. But it did not involve the forcible overthrow of the government. William brought an army, but it was not used against the government; the king simply departed. The revolution was accomplished through political manoeuvre and the collapse of the king's will to resist. One might observe that the definition, by requiring the use or threat of force, excludes a revolution that is universally regarded as one of the most consequential in European history, and that a fundamental change in the form of government may be accomplished by means other than violence.

definition: A state S is a welfare state if and only if (i) S provides a minimum standard of living to all its citizens through publicly funded programmes, (ii) S funds these programmes through compulsory taxation, and (iii) the programmes are administered by the state or its agencies.
conditions: S provides a minimum living standard through public programmes
Programmes are funded by compulsory taxation
Programmes are administered by the state or its agencies
counterexample: Consider a state in which the provision of welfare — education, healthcare, unemployment relief — is delegated by law to a network of religious and charitable organisations. The state levies compulsory taxes and distributes the revenue to these organisations, which administer the programmes according to standards set by the state. A minimum standard of living is effectively guaranteed. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met: the minimum standard is provided, and the funding comes from compulsory taxation. But condition (iii) is not met: the programmes are administered not by the state but by private organisations. Yet the arrangement achieves precisely what a welfare state is meant to achieve, and most political theorists would classify such a system as a form of welfare state.
missing_condition: The definition requires state administration of welfare programmes, but the essential feature of a welfare state is the public guarantee of a minimum standard of living, which may be achieved through delegation to non-state bodies as effectively as through direct state administration.
passage: One might propose that a welfare state is one that provides a minimum standard of living to all citizens through publicly funded programmes, funds these programmes by compulsory taxation, and administers them through state agencies. This definition captures the key features of the paradigmatic welfare states of the mid-twentieth century, in which the state operated public health services, state schools, and unemployment insurance schemes directly through its own bureaucratic apparatus. The three conditions identify the distinctive combination of provision, funding, and administration that characterises the welfare state as an institutional form. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is provided by states that achieve welfare objectives through delegation rather than direct administration. Consider a state that levies compulsory taxes and uses the revenue to fund a network of religious and charitable organisations that provide education, healthcare, and unemployment relief to all citizens. The state sets the standards, monitors compliance, and guarantees that every citizen has access to the services. A minimum standard of living is effectively assured. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied: the minimum standard is provided, and the funding comes from taxation. But the programmes are not administered by the state; they are administered by private organisations acting under state mandate. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the result is a system that achieves precisely the aims of a welfare state — the public guarantee of a decent minimum — through different institutional means. One might observe that the definition has confused one institutional arrangement for delivering welfare with the principle that defines the welfare state, and that what matters is the guarantee, not the administrative mechanism by which it is fulfilled.

definition: A person or body A possesses political authority over a population P if and only if (i) A issues directives that govern the conduct of the members of P, (ii) the members of P generally comply with A's directives, and (iii) A's directives are backed by the capacity to impose sanctions for non-compliance.
conditions: A issues directives that govern the conduct of the members of P
The members of P generally comply with A's directives
A's directives are backed by the capacity to impose sanctions for non-compliance
counterexample: Consider a military occupier who issues directives governing the conduct of a conquered population. The population generally complies, and the occupier has an overwhelming capacity to impose sanctions. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the occupier does not possess political authority in the fullest sense — the population complies out of fear rather than any recognition of the occupier's right to govern. What is present is coercive power, but what is absent is the moral or normative dimension that distinguishes authority from mere domination.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the population recognise A's directives as in some sense legitimate or as imposing a moral obligation of compliance, thereby conflating coercive domination with genuine political authority.
passage: One might define political authority as the condition in which a person or body issues directives governing a population's conduct, the population generally complies, and the directives are backed by the capacity to impose sanctions. This definition captures the observable features of authority: governments issue laws, citizens comply, and enforcement mechanisms exist. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers military occupation. An occupying army issues directives governing the conduct of the conquered population. The population generally complies — it has little choice. The occupier possesses an overwhelming capacity to impose sanctions for non-compliance. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that we should hesitate to say that the occupier possesses political authority in the full sense. The population's compliance is motivated by fear, not by any recognition of the occupier's right to govern. What is present is coercive power; what is absent is the normative dimension — the sense in which the population regards the directives as carrying a moral claim to obedience. The definition, by confining itself to the behavioural facts of directive-issuance, compliance, and sanction, has failed to distinguish genuine authority from mere domination.

definition: A political system S is a democracy if and only if (i) the laws and policies of S are determined by majority vote among the adult members of the community, (ii) all adult members of the community have an equal right to vote, and (iii) elections are held at regular intervals.
conditions: The laws and policies of S are determined by majority vote among the adult members of the community
All adult members of the community have an equal right to vote
Elections are held at regular intervals
counterexample: Consider a political system in which all adult citizens have an equal right to vote, elections are held at regular intervals, and laws are determined by majority vote, but in which a single political party controls all media, suppresses opposition parties, and manipulates the information available to voters in such a way that the majority consistently votes as the ruling party desires. All three conditions are satisfied, yet one might observe that such a system is a democracy in form only — it lacks the conditions of free political competition and open debate that give democratic procedures their value.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the political system provide conditions of free political competition, open debate, and access to diverse sources of information, without which the formal machinery of majority rule and equal suffrage is emptied of its democratic substance.
passage: A natural definition of democracy holds that a political system is democratic if and only if its laws are determined by majority vote among all adult members of the community, all adults have an equal right to vote, and elections are held at regular intervals. This definition has the merit of being precise and verifiable. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed as follows. Consider a political system in which all adult citizens vote, elections occur on schedule, and laws are determined by the majority. However, a single party controls all channels of public communication, suppresses rival parties, and systematically manipulates the information available to the electorate. The majority votes as the ruling party desires — not because the citizens are coerced into voting a particular way, but because they have been deprived of the information and the political alternatives that would enable them to vote otherwise. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that such a system is a democracy in form alone. It lacks the conditions of free political competition and open debate that give the formal machinery of democracy its point. The definition, by attending only to the procedural features of voting, has omitted the substantive conditions without which those procedures are hollow.

definition: A person P is free with respect to an action A if and only if (i) no external agent prevents P from performing A, (ii) no law or regulation prohibits P from performing A, and (iii) P faces no threat of punishment for performing A.
conditions: No external agent prevents P from performing A
No law or regulation prohibits P from performing A
P faces no threat of punishment for performing A
counterexample: Consider a person who is legally permitted to leave his village, faces no physical prevention, and is threatened with no punishment for doing so. Yet the only road out of the village crosses a privately owned bridge whose owner charges a toll that the man cannot afford. No one prevents him; no law prohibits him; no punishment awaits him. Yet it appears that he is not genuinely free to leave, for his poverty renders the formal absence of constraint practically meaningless.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P possess the means or capacity to perform A, thereby treating as free a person who faces no external prohibition but who is rendered incapable of action by material deprivation.
passage: One might define freedom with respect to an action as the condition in which no external agent prevents the person from performing it, no law prohibits it, and no threat of punishment attaches to it. This definition captures the negative conception of freedom — freedom as the absence of interference. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the following. A man living in a remote village is legally permitted to leave, faces no physical prevention, and is threatened with no punishment for departure. The road out of the village, however, crosses a privately owned bridge, and the bridge's owner charges a toll that the man cannot afford. No one prevents him from leaving; no law prohibits his departure; no punishment awaits him. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that we should hesitate to say the man is genuinely free to leave his village. His poverty renders the formal absence of constraint practically meaningless — the bridge toll is as effective a barrier as a locked gate. The definition, by confining itself to the absence of external prohibition and threat, has neglected the role of material capacity in determining whether freedom is real or merely nominal.

definition: A distribution of goods D in a society S is just if and only if (i) D was arrived at through procedures to which all members of S consented, (ii) D treats all members of S equally in the sense that no member receives a larger share without a corresponding justification, and (iii) D respects the prior entitlements of all members of S.
conditions: D was arrived at through procedures to which all members of S consented
D treats all members of S equally in the sense that no member receives a larger share without a corresponding justification
D respects the prior entitlements of all members of S
counterexample: Consider a society in which all members consented to a set of economic procedures — free exchange, voluntary contract, and so forth — and in which no prior entitlements are violated. Over several generations, the operation of these procedures results in a distribution in which a small minority controls nearly all the wealth, whilst the majority lacks access to basic necessities. The distribution was procedurally arrived at; prior entitlements were respected; and the inequality could be justified by reference to differences in effort and talent. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet one might observe that a distribution which leaves the majority in destitution is difficult to regard as just, regardless of the procedures that produced it.
missing_condition: The definition omits any requirement that the distribution ensure a minimum level of material welfare for all members of society, so that procedurally impeccable distributions which leave some in desperate poverty are not excluded.
passage: A natural definition of just distribution holds that a distribution of goods in a society is just if and only if it was arrived at through procedures to which all members consented, it treats all members equally in the sense that unequal shares require justification, and it respects the prior entitlements of all members. This definition captures the procedural and entitlement-based dimensions of justice. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed as follows. In a society whose members have freely consented to the principles of free exchange and voluntary contract, and whose prior entitlements are scrupulously respected, the operation of these principles over several generations produces a distribution in which a tiny minority controls nearly all the wealth, whilst the majority lacks access to food, shelter, and medical care. The inequality can be traced to differences in effort, talent, and inheritance — no fraud or coercion has occurred. All three conditions of the definition appear satisfied. Yet one might observe that a distribution which leaves the greater part of the population in destitution is difficult to call just, however impeccable the procedures that produced it. The definition, by confining itself to procedural consent, formal equality, and prior entitlement, has neglected the question of whether the distribution secures a minimum standard of welfare for all.

definition: A citizen C has a political obligation to obey a law L if and only if (i) L was enacted by a legitimate governmental authority, (ii) C has received benefits from the system of laws of which L is a part, and (iii) C had the opportunity to participate in the process by which L was enacted.
conditions: L was enacted by a legitimate governmental authority
C has received benefits from the system of laws of which L is a part
C had the opportunity to participate in the process by which L was enacted
counterexample: Consider a resident alien who lives and works in a country, benefits from its laws — the protection of the police, the use of public roads, the stability of the legal order — but who, as a non-citizen, had no opportunity to participate in the process by which the laws were enacted. The third condition fails. Yet one might observe that we ordinarily hold resident aliens to be obliged to obey the laws of the country in which they reside. The definition, by requiring participatory opportunity, would deny any political obligation on the part of those who benefit from a legal order but are excluded from its enactment.
passage: One might define political obligation as the condition in which a citizen is bound to obey a law that was enacted by a legitimate authority, from whose legal system the citizen has received benefits, and in whose enactment the citizen had the opportunity to participate. This definition brings together three important strands of thought about political obligation: legitimacy, benefit, and participation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the proposal arises when one considers the situation of a resident alien. Such a person lives and works in a country, benefits extensively from its legal order — the protection of the police, the use of public roads, the stability provided by contract law — but, as a non-citizen, had no opportunity whatever to participate in the enactment of the laws. The third condition fails. Yet one might observe that we ordinarily regard resident aliens as obliged to obey the laws of the countries in which they reside, and indeed the law itself imposes this obligation. The definition, by requiring participatory opportunity as a necessary condition, cannot account for the obligation of those who benefit from a legal order without having had any voice in its creation.

definition: An event R is a political revolution if and only if (i) R involves the overthrow of the existing government, (ii) R is carried out by a popular movement — a substantial portion of the general population, and (iii) R results in a fundamental change in the political order.
conditions: R involves the overthrow of the existing government
R is carried out by a popular movement — a substantial portion of the general population
R results in a fundamental change in the political order
counterexample: Consider a military coup in which a small group of army officers overthrows the existing government and establishes a new constitutional order with radically different political institutions. The existing government is overthrown; a fundamental change in the political order ensues. Yet the coup is carried out not by a popular movement but by a handful of military officers acting without popular participation. Historians would nonetheless describe such an event as a revolution if it produces a sufficiently radical transformation of the political order, as in the case of certain twentieth-century military revolutions.
passage: A natural definition of political revolution holds that an event is a revolution if and only if it involves the overthrow of the existing government, is carried out by a popular movement, and results in a fundamental change in the political order. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases — the French Revolution of 1789, in which a popular uprising overthrew the monarchy and established a new political order. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the military coup that produces radical political transformation. A small group of army officers overthrows the existing government and establishes a new constitutional order with fundamentally different political institutions, ideological foundations, and distributions of power. The first and third conditions are satisfied: the government is overthrown, and the political order is fundamentally changed. But the second condition fails, for the coup is carried out by a handful of officers, not by a popular movement. One might observe, however, that historians would not hesitate to describe certain such events as revolutions — the term 'military revolution' is well established in political historiography. The definition, by requiring popular participation, has excluded a well-recognised species of revolution.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) there exists a corresponding obligation on the part of some other person or body to provide, permit, or refrain from interfering with P's access to X, (ii) P is in a position to claim X as a matter of entitlement rather than charity or grace, and (iii) P's entitlement to X is recognised by the legal system of P's community.
conditions: There exists a corresponding obligation on the part of some other person or body to provide, permit, or refrain from interfering with P's access to X
P is in a position to claim X as a matter of entitlement rather than charity or grace
P's entitlement to X is recognised by the legal system of P's community
counterexample: Consider the inhabitants of a colonial territory in the eighteenth century whose legal system, imposed by the colonial power, does not recognise their entitlement to self-governance. If one were to argue, as many philosophers of the period did, that these inhabitants have a natural right to govern themselves, one would be asserting a right that satisfies the first two conditions — there is an obligation on the part of the colonial power, and the claim is made as a matter of entitlement — but that is not recognised by the prevailing legal system. The definition, by requiring legal recognition, cannot accommodate natural or moral rights that exist prior to or independently of legal systems.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the existence of moral or natural rights that are not recognised by any legal system, thereby collapsing the distinction between legal rights and moral rights.
passage: One might define a right as a condition in which there exists an obligation on another party to provide or permit something, the holder can claim it as an entitlement, and the entitlement is recognised by the legal system. This definition captures the structure of legal rights well: a property owner has a right because others are obliged not to trespass, the owner can claim this as an entitlement, and the law recognises it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises when one considers moral or natural rights asserted against a legal order that refuses to recognise them. The inhabitants of a colonial territory in the eighteenth century might assert a right to self-governance. There exists, one might argue, an obligation on the part of the colonial power to permit self-rule; the claim is made as a matter of entitlement, not as a petition for grace. Yet the colonial legal system does not recognise this entitlement. The third condition fails. One might observe, however, that the assertion of rights against oppressive legal orders has been one of the most important moral and political achievements in human history. A definition that requires legal recognition for the existence of a right cannot accommodate the very notion of a right that is being violated — and it is precisely in such cases that the concept of rights is most urgently needed.

definition: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme authority within its territory, (ii) E is not subject to the authority of any external power, and (iii) E possesses a monopoly on the legitimate use of force within its territory.
conditions: E exercises supreme authority within its territory
E is not subject to the authority of any external power
E possesses a monopoly on the legitimate use of force within its territory
counterexample: Consider a member state of a federal union that has transferred certain legislative and judicial powers to a supranational body. The member state does not exercise supreme authority within its territory, for the supranational body's directives take precedence in areas of shared competence. Nor is the member state entirely free from external authority. Yet the member state retains control of its armed forces, conducts its own foreign policy in many respects, and is universally recognised as a sovereign state. The definition, by requiring supremacy and freedom from external authority, would deny sovereignty to states that have voluntarily constrained their authority through international agreements.
passage: One might define sovereignty as the condition in which a political entity exercises supreme authority within its territory, is not subject to any external power, and possesses a monopoly on the legitimate use of force. This definition captures the classical Westphalian conception of sovereignty. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the member states of modern federal or supranational unions. A state that has joined such a union has transferred certain legislative and judicial competences to the supranational body, whose directives take precedence in areas of shared competence. The state does not exercise supreme authority within its territory in an unqualified sense, and it is, in the relevant areas, subject to external authority. Yet the state retains its armed forces, conducts foreign policy, and is universally recognised as sovereign. One might observe that the definition has taken an idealised, absolute conception of sovereignty and treated it as the only genuine form, thereby excluding the qualified sovereignty that characterises most modern states. The reality is that sovereignty admits of degrees and qualifications, and a definition that demands absoluteness fails to capture this.

definition: A political community exercises toleration with respect to a practice Q if and only if (i) Q is disapproved of by the majority of the community, (ii) the community refrains from prohibiting or suppressing Q, and (iii) the community's forbearance is the result of a principled commitment to permitting practices of which it disapproves.
conditions: Q is disapproved of by the majority of the community
The community refrains from prohibiting or suppressing Q
The community's forbearance is the result of a principled commitment to permitting practices of which it disapproves
counterexample: Consider a community that disapproves of a certain minority religious practice but refrains from suppressing it, not from any principled commitment to permitting disapproved practices, but simply because it lacks the administrative capacity to enforce a ban. The community would suppress the practice if it could; its forbearance is a matter of incapacity, not principle. The first two conditions are satisfied — the practice is disapproved of and not suppressed — but the third fails. Yet one might observe that the minority in question does in fact enjoy toleration in the practical sense: it is permitted to continue its practice undisturbed.
passage: A natural definition of toleration holds that a political community exercises toleration with respect to a practice when the majority disapproves of the practice, the community refrains from suppressing it, and the forbearance results from a principled commitment to permitting disapproved practices. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases: a society that disapproves of a minority religion but protects its practice out of commitment to liberty of conscience is exercising toleration in the fullest sense. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers a community that disapproves of a minority religious practice but refrains from suppressing it solely because it lacks the administrative means to enforce a ban. The community would suppress the practice if it could; its forbearance is a matter of practical incapacity, not principled commitment. The first two conditions are satisfied, but the third fails. Yet one might observe that the minority in question does in fact enjoy toleration in the practical sense: it is left undisturbed in its practice. The question is whether toleration is properly understood as a principled attitude or as a practical condition. The definition, by insisting upon principled commitment, captures only the former and excludes cases in which toleration exists as a de facto reality without a principled foundation.

definition: A state S promotes the welfare of its citizens if and only if (i) S provides material resources sufficient for subsistence to those citizens who cannot provide for themselves, (ii) S maintains institutions — hospitals, schools, public works — that serve the common good, and (iii) S distributes the costs of these provisions equitably among its citizens through taxation.
conditions: S provides material resources sufficient for subsistence to those citizens who cannot provide for themselves
S maintains institutions — hospitals, schools, public works — that serve the common good
S distributes the costs of these provisions equitably among its citizens through taxation
counterexample: Consider a state that provides subsistence to the destitute, maintains excellent hospitals and schools, and distributes the costs equitably through taxation, but that subjects its citizens to pervasive surveillance, restricts their freedom of association, and controls the information available to them. All three conditions of the definition are met — material welfare, institutional provision, and equitable distribution are all present. Yet one might observe that such a state does not promote the welfare of its citizens in any adequate sense, for it has sacrificed their liberty and dignity in the pursuit of material security.
missing_condition: The definition confines welfare to material and institutional provision, omitting the requirement that the state also secure the basic liberties and dignities of its citizens, without which material provision alone does not constitute genuine welfare.
passage: One might define the promotion of welfare as the condition in which a state provides subsistence to those who cannot provide for themselves, maintains institutions serving the common good, and distributes the costs equitably through taxation. This definition captures the material dimension of welfare admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers a state that satisfies all three conditions — it provides generously for the destitute, maintains excellent hospitals and schools, and funds these through equitable taxation — but that simultaneously subjects its citizens to pervasive surveillance, restricts their freedom of association, and controls the information available to them. The material conditions of welfare are met. But one might observe that we should hesitate to say that such a state promotes the welfare of its citizens in any full sense. The citizens are materially provided for but are not free; they are cared for but not respected. The definition, by confining itself to material and institutional provision, has omitted the dimension of liberty and human dignity that is essential to any adequate conception of welfare. A state that feeds its citizens but denies them the freedom to speak, to associate, and to learn has not secured their welfare — it has purchased their comfort at the price of their autonomy.

definition: A political system G is a democracy if and only if (i) the holders of political office in G are selected by periodic elections in which all adult citizens may vote, (ii) the elections are conducted by secret ballot, and (iii) there exist at least two competing parties or candidates.
conditions: The holders of political office in G are selected by periodic elections in which all adult citizens may vote
The elections are conducted by secret ballot
There exist at least two competing parties or candidates
counterexample: Consider a state in which elections are held periodically, the ballot is secret, and two parties compete vigorously. Yet one party controls all major media outlets, restricts the opposition's access to public assembly, and uses state resources to fund its own campaigns while denying public financing to its rival. The opposition is legal but effectively unable to communicate its programme to the electorate. All three conditions are formally satisfied. Yet one might observe that such a system is a democracy in name only, for the formal competition is rendered substantively meaningless by the inequality of conditions under which it takes place.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the competing parties or candidates have roughly equal access to the means of political communication and public resources, so that formal electoral competition is not hollowed out by asymmetries of power.
passage: One might define democracy as a political system in which office-holders are selected by periodic elections with universal adult suffrage, conducted by secret ballot, with at least two competing parties or candidates. This proposal captures the procedural core of democratic governance as it is commonly understood. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed without much difficulty. Suppose a state satisfies all three conditions: elections are held regularly, every adult citizen may vote by secret ballot, and two parties contest the elections. Yet one of the two parties controls all major newspapers, television stations, and radio broadcasts. It restricts the opposition's access to public spaces for rallies and meetings. It uses the resources of the state — public buildings, government employees, state funds — to promote its own candidates, while the opposition must rely on private donations alone. The opposition is legal, and it appears on the ballot. But it is effectively unable to reach the electorate with its message. All three conditions of the definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that such a system is a democracy in form but not in substance. The formal existence of electoral competition is rendered meaningless by the radical inequality of the conditions under which it takes place. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the procedural mechanics of elections, cannot distinguish genuine democracy from the mere appearance of it.

definition: A citizen C is free if and only if (i) C is not subject to physical restraint or imprisonment, (ii) C is not legally prohibited from performing any action that does not harm others, and (iii) no other person or institution exercises coercive power over C's choices.
conditions: C is not subject to physical restraint or imprisonment
C is not legally prohibited from performing any action that does not harm others
No other person or institution exercises coercive power over C's choices
counterexample: Consider a citizen in a laissez-faire state who is not imprisoned, faces no legal prohibitions on harmless actions, and is subject to no coercion. Yet she is desperately poor — she lacks the material resources to travel, to educate herself, or to participate in the cultural life of her society. She is at liberty to do all of these things in the sense that no one prevents her, but she is unable to do any of them. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that calling her free rings hollow when she lacks the means to exercise any of the liberties she formally possesses.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that C possess sufficient material resources or effective capabilities to exercise the freedoms that the absence of restraint, prohibition, and coercion formally makes available.
passage: A familiar analysis of freedom holds that a citizen is free if she is not physically restrained, faces no legal prohibition on actions that do not harm others, and is subject to no coercive power over her choices. This negative conception of freedom has a distinguished lineage in political thought and captures an important dimension of what it is to be free. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed as follows. Consider a citizen living in a state with minimal government interference. She is not imprisoned or physically restrained. The law does not prohibit her from travelling, from educating herself, from publishing her opinions, or from participating in the political and cultural life of her society. No person or institution exercises coercive power over her choices. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet she is destitute. She cannot afford to travel beyond her immediate neighbourhood. She cannot pay for education. She cannot purchase the materials needed to publish anything. She cannot take time away from subsistence labour to participate in political or cultural activities. She is at liberty to do all of these things in the sense that no one prevents her, but she is utterly unable to do any of them. One might observe that calling such a person free requires a very thin conception of freedom — one that attends only to the absence of external interference and ignores the presence or absence of the conditions needed to make use of that absence. It appears that the definition, by confining itself to negative liberty, cannot capture the distinction between formal freedom and effective freedom.
