definition: A society exhibits equality if and only if (i) all citizens possess the same legal rights, (ii) no citizen is subject to discrimination on the basis of birth, race, sex, or religion, and (iii) all citizens are equal before the law.
conditions: All citizens possess the same legal rights
No citizen is subject to discrimination on the basis of birth, race, sex, or religion
All citizens are equal before the law
counterexample: Consider a society in which all citizens possess identical legal rights, no citizen is discriminated against on any of the enumerated grounds, and the courts treat all citizens with scrupulous impartiality. Yet a small class of families controls the overwhelming majority of productive land and capital, while the rest of the population labours for subsistence wages. The children of the wealthy receive excellent education; the children of the poor do not. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the formal equality of legal rights coexists with, and indeed masks, a profound substantive inequality.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require any measure of equality in the distribution of material resources, opportunities, or social advantages, so that a society of extreme economic stratification may satisfy every condition while exhibiting gross inequality in any substantive sense.
passage: One might characterise equality by appeal to the legal and civic dimensions that are most readily codified: equal legal rights, freedom from discrimination, and equality before the law. This proposal captures the formal dimension of equality that has been central to liberal political thought since the eighteenth century. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be described as follows. Consider a society in which the constitution guarantees identical legal rights to every citizen, in which no citizen suffers discrimination on the basis of birth, race, sex, or religion, and in which the judiciary treats every citizen with exemplary impartiality. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet a small class of families — numbering perhaps one per cent of the population — controls ninety per cent of the productive land and capital. The remaining ninety-nine per cent work for wages that barely cover subsistence. The children of the wealthy attend excellent schools and inherit positions of influence; the children of the poor receive little education and inherit nothing. The society is one of profound and entrenched material inequality. One might observe that calling such a society equal, on the ground that legal rights are uniformly distributed, is to confuse the form of equality with its substance. It appears that the definition, by restricting its attention to the legal and civic sphere, cannot detect inequalities of wealth, opportunity, and social condition that may be far more consequential for the lives of citizens.

definition: A person or institution A has authority over a group G if and only if (i) A issues directives that purport to bind the members of G, (ii) the members of G generally comply with A's directives, and (iii) A possesses the capacity to impose sanctions on non-compliant members of G.
conditions: A issues directives that purport to bind the members of G
The members of G generally comply with A's directives
A possesses the capacity to impose sanctions on non-compliant members of G
counterexample: Consider a criminal gang that issues directives to shopkeepers in a neighbourhood, extracts compliance through intimidation, and possesses the capacity to impose violent sanctions on those who do not comply. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the gang does not possess authority in the normative sense that political philosophy typically intends. It possesses power — the capacity to coerce — but its directives carry no moral or political legitimacy.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that A's directives carry normative force — that the members of G have a moral or political reason to comply beyond the mere threat of sanctions — thereby conflating authority with mere power.
passage: One might characterise authority as the condition in which a person or institution issues binding directives, secures general compliance, and possesses the capacity to sanction non-compliance. This analysis has the advantage of being empirically identifiable: one can observe whether directives are issued, whether compliance follows, and whether sanctions are available. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is readily constructed. Consider a criminal gang operating in a city neighbourhood. The gang issues directives to local shopkeepers: they are to pay a monthly sum and to refuse service to the gang's rivals. The shopkeepers generally comply, for they have learned the consequences of refusal. The gang possesses a formidable capacity to impose sanctions — beatings, arson, worse — on those who do not comply. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to say that the gang possesses authority over the shopkeepers. What it possesses is power — the naked capacity to coerce. Authority, in the sense that political philosophy characteristically intends, involves something more: a normative claim to obedience, a right to direct, not merely the capacity to compel. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the issuance of directives, the fact of compliance, and the capacity for sanctions, cannot distinguish legitimate authority from the rule of force.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G came to power through procedures recognised as lawful by the constitution of the state, (ii) G governs with the consent of the majority of the governed, and (iii) G respects the fundamental civil liberties of its citizens.
conditions: G came to power through procedures recognised as lawful by the constitution of the state
G governs with the consent of the majority of the governed
G respects the fundamental civil liberties of its citizens
counterexample: Consider a government that came to power through constitutionally prescribed elections, enjoys the enthusiastic consent of a large majority of the population, and respects the civil liberties of most of its citizens. Yet it systematically denies fundamental civil liberties — freedom of assembly, freedom of speech, legal protection — to a small ethnic minority, with the full approval of the majority. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, and condition (iii) is satisfied with respect to most citizens. Yet we should hesitate to call such a government fully legitimate when it denies fundamental liberties to a minority with majoritarian sanction.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the protection of fundamental civil liberties extend to all citizens without exception, rather than merely to the majority whose consent sustains the government in power.
passage: A natural characterisation of political legitimacy holds that a government is legitimate if it came to power lawfully, governs with the consent of the governed, and respects fundamental civil liberties. This proposal combines procedural, democratic, and rights-based criteria in a manner that captures much of what political theorists have demanded of legitimate rule. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be described as follows. Suppose a government comes to power through free and fair elections conducted in strict accordance with the constitution. It governs with the enthusiastic consent of eighty-five per cent of the population. And it respects the civil liberties of the vast majority of its citizens — their freedom of speech, their freedom of assembly, their legal protections are all upheld. Yet there is a small ethnic minority, comprising perhaps five per cent of the population, whose members are denied these same liberties. They may not assemble freely, may not publish in their own language, and enjoy no recourse when their rights are violated. The majority approves of these restrictions. All three conditions of the definition are arguably satisfied: the government is constitutionally lawful, it enjoys majority consent, and it respects the civil liberties of its citizens — provided one reads 'its citizens' as referring to the majority rather than to all. One might observe that a government which purchases majority approval at the price of oppressing a minority is not fully legitimate. It appears that the definition, by failing to specify that civil liberties must be extended to all citizens without exception, permits a tyranny of the majority to pass as legitimate governance.

definition: A state S is sovereign if and only if (i) S exercises supreme authority within its territorial boundaries, (ii) S is not subject to the jurisdiction of any external power, and (iii) S possesses the capacity to enter into relations with other states.
conditions: S exercises supreme authority within its territorial boundaries
S is not subject to the jurisdiction of any external power
S possesses the capacity to enter into relations with other states
counterexample: Consider a member state of a supranational union that has voluntarily ceded authority over its trade policy, its monetary policy, and portions of its legal system to a supranational body whose decisions override domestic law. It does not exercise supreme authority within its borders on these matters, and it is subject to the jurisdiction of the supranational courts. Yet it maintains its own foreign ministry and enters freely into relations with other states. All three conditions are not fully met — conditions (i) and (ii) are partially violated. Yet the state is universally recognised as sovereign by the international community and by its own citizens.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the phenomenon of pooled or shared sovereignty, in which a state voluntarily transfers certain competences to supranational institutions without thereby ceasing to be sovereign.
passage: A traditional analysis of sovereignty holds that a state is sovereign if it exercises supreme authority within its territory, is not subject to external jurisdiction, and possesses the capacity to enter into relations with other states. This analysis captures the classical Westphalian conception of sovereignty that has structured international relations since the seventeenth century. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the contemporary phenomenon of supranational governance. Consider a state that is a member of a supranational union. It has voluntarily ceded authority over its trade policy, its monetary policy, and portions of its domestic legal system to the institutions of the union. The supranational body's regulations take direct effect within the state's territory, and the supranational court's rulings override domestic legislation. The state does not exercise supreme authority within its borders on these matters, and it is subject to external jurisdiction. Yet it maintains its own armed forces, its own foreign ministry, and its own diplomatic relations with states outside the union. It is recognised as sovereign by every other state in the international system. One might observe that the classical definition, by requiring unqualified supremacy and independence, cannot accommodate the graduated, shared, and pooled sovereignty that characterises the modern international order. It appears that sovereignty is not the all-or-nothing condition the definition presupposes, but admits of degrees and domains.

definition: An act A constitutes civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a deliberate violation of a law, (ii) A is performed publicly and non-violently, and (iii) A is motivated by the agent's conscientious objection to the law violated or to a policy that the law supports.
conditions: A is a deliberate violation of a law
A is performed publicly and non-violently
A is motivated by the agent's conscientious objection to the law violated or to a policy that the law supports
counterexample: Consider a group of citizens who publicly and non-violently refuse to pay a portion of their taxes as a protest against a military policy they find morally unconscionable. Their act is a deliberate violation of tax law, performed publicly and non-violently, and motivated by conscientious objection to the policy the tax revenue supports. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the group specifically intends to evade punishment by structuring their refusal so that enforcement would be prohibitively expensive for the state. They do not accept the legal consequences of their act. One might observe that this willingness to evade rather than accept punishment distinguishes their act from paradigmatic civil disobedience.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent be willing to accept the legal consequences of the act, which is widely regarded as essential to distinguishing civil disobedience from mere evasion of the law.
passage: A familiar analysis of civil disobedience holds that it consists in the deliberate, public, and non-violent violation of a law, motivated by conscientious objection. This characterisation captures the most prominent features of the acts that have historically been recognised as civil disobedience: the public sit-ins, the marches, the refusals to comply with laws deemed unjust. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Suppose a group of citizens publicly and non-violently refuses to pay a portion of their taxes as a protest against a military policy they regard as morally unconscionable. Their act is a deliberate violation of tax law, it is performed publicly, and it is entirely non-violent. Their motivation is conscientious objection to the military policy that their tax revenue would support. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet suppose further that the group has deliberately structured its refusal so as to make enforcement prohibitively expensive for the revenue authorities. They have dispersed the unpaid amounts across thousands of small transactions, each too insignificant to warrant individual prosecution. They do not intend to accept punishment — they intend to evade it. One might observe that there is an important difference between this carefully orchestrated evasion and the paradigmatic acts of civil disobedience, in which the agent stands before the court and accepts the penalty. It appears that the definition, by requiring only publicity and non-violence, fails to capture the element of willingness to bear the legal consequences that many theorists regard as essential.

definition: A claim R held by a person P constitutes a right if and only if (i) R imposes a correlative duty on some other person or institution to act or refrain from acting in a specified way, (ii) R is recognised by the legal system of the state in which P resides, and (iii) P possesses a remedy at law for the violation of R.
conditions: R imposes a correlative duty on some other person or institution to act or refrain from acting in a specified way
R is recognised by the legal system of the state in which P resides
P possesses a remedy at law for the violation of R
counterexample: Consider a person living under a totalitarian regime that recognises no right to free expression, provides no legal remedy for its suppression, and imposes no legal duty on the state to refrain from censorship. On the definition, this person possesses no right to free expression. Yet many political philosophers have argued that the right to free expression is a natural or moral right that obtains regardless of whether any legal system recognises it. If they are correct, the definition excludes an important class of rights.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate moral or natural rights — those rights that are held to obtain independently of positive law — by making legal recognition and legal remedy necessary conditions.
passage: A positivist analysis of rights holds that a claim constitutes a right if and only if it imposes a correlative duty, is recognised by the legal system, and is backed by a legal remedy. This analysis has the considerable merit of providing clear and determinate criteria: one can consult the statute books and the case law to determine whether a right exists. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the political circumstances under which legal recognition is most conspicuously absent. Suppose a person lives under a totalitarian regime that recognises no right to free expression, provides no legal remedy for censorship, and imposes no duty on the state to refrain from suppressing speech. On the definition offered, this person possesses no right to free expression. Yet a long tradition in political philosophy — running from the natural law theorists through the Enlightenment to the present day — holds that certain rights are not creatures of positive law but obtain independently of it. The right to free expression, on this view, is not conferred by the legal system but is a pre-political moral right that the legal system may recognise or fail to recognise. If this tradition is even partially correct, then the definition is too narrow. One might observe that it confuses the question of whether a right exists with the question of whether a particular legal system enforces it. It appears that the definition, by making legal recognition a necessary condition, cannot accommodate the moral rights that political philosophy has historically been most concerned to defend.

definition: A citizen C has a political obligation to obey a law L if and only if (i) L was enacted by a legitimate government, (ii) C has benefited from the system of laws of which L is a part, and (iii) C has not been subjected to serious injustice by the government that enacted L.
conditions: L was enacted by a legitimate government
C has benefited from the system of laws of which L is a part
C has not been subjected to serious injustice by the government that enacted L
counterexample: Consider a hermit who lives in a remote area, grows his own food, builds his own shelter, and has virtually no contact with civil society. The government that enacted the laws under which he nominally lives is legitimate. He has not been subjected to injustice. Yet it is difficult to say that he has benefited from the system of laws in any substantial sense — he neither uses public services nor participates in the social cooperation that the law facilitates. Conditions (i) and (iii) are met, and condition (ii) is arguably met in a minimal sense, yet one might question whether he has a genuine political obligation to obey laws that have conferred no meaningful benefit upon him.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that C's benefit from the system of laws be more than trivially connected to L and to C's actual circumstances, so that the obligation arises from genuine reciprocity rather than from the mere fact of residing within a jurisdiction.
passage: One might propose that a citizen has a political obligation to obey a law if the law was enacted by a legitimate government, the citizen has benefited from the system of laws, and the citizen has not been subjected to serious injustice. This analysis combines elements of consent theory, fair-play theory, and a justice proviso. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the figure of the hermit. Consider a person who lives in a remote and barely accessible area, grows his own food, builds his own shelter, draws water from a natural spring, and has virtually no contact with the society around him. He does not use public roads, does not send his children to public schools (he has none), does not call upon the police or the fire service, and does not participate in the economic or social life that the law facilitates. The government under which he nominally lives is legitimate. He has not been subjected to injustice. But in what sense has he benefited from the system of laws? One might argue that he benefits from the general stability and peace that the legal order provides, but this benefit is so remote and attenuated that it is difficult to regard it as a basis for genuine obligation. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the citizen have benefited from the system of laws, does not specify the nature or degree of benefit needed to ground a genuine obligation, and thus risks attributing political obligations to those who stand in only the most tenuous relationship to the legal order.

definition: A distribution of goods in a society is just if and only if (i) the distribution was produced by voluntary exchanges between consenting parties, (ii) no force or fraud was employed in producing the distribution, and (iii) the initial holdings from which the exchanges proceeded were legitimately acquired.
conditions: The distribution was produced by voluntary exchanges between consenting parties
No force or fraud was employed in producing the distribution
The initial holdings from which the exchanges proceeded were legitimately acquired
counterexample: Consider a society in which initial holdings were legitimately acquired and all subsequent exchanges have been voluntary and free from force or fraud. Over several generations, the cumulative effect of individually fair transactions has produced a distribution in which one per cent of the population controls virtually all productive resources, while the remaining ninety-nine per cent live in abject poverty and have no realistic prospect of improving their condition. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that a distribution this extreme, however clean its procedural history, is difficult to regard as just.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require any constraint on the cumulative outcome of individually fair transactions, so that a distribution may satisfy every procedural condition while producing a degree of material inequality that undermines the conditions necessary for meaningful voluntary exchange in subsequent generations.
passage: A procedural theory of distributive justice holds that a distribution of goods is just if it was produced by voluntary exchanges, free from force and fraud, beginning from legitimately acquired initial holdings. This theory has the attractive feature of respecting individual liberty: it does not impose any particular pattern upon the distribution of goods but requires only that the process by which the distribution arose be fair. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be described as follows. Suppose a society begins with initial holdings that are beyond reproach. Over the course of several generations, individuals engage in voluntary exchanges — buying, selling, investing, bequeathing — and no force or fraud is employed at any stage. Each individual transaction is fair. Yet the cumulative effect of these transactions, compounded over generations, is a distribution in which one per cent of the population controls virtually all productive land, capital, and resources, while the remaining ninety-nine per cent live in poverty so severe that they cannot feed their children, educate them, or provide medical care. All three conditions of the definition are met: the exchanges were voluntary, no force or fraud was used, and the initial holdings were legitimate. Yet one might observe that the resulting distribution, however procedurally clean, is difficult to regard as just. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the procedural history of the distribution, cannot detect injustice that arises from the cumulative and compounding effects of individually unobjectionable transactions.

definition: A political system G is genuinely democratic if and only if (i) all adult citizens of G have an equal right to vote, (ii) elections in G are free from coercion and fraud, and (iii) the outcomes of elections in G determine who holds political office.
conditions: All adult citizens have an equal right to vote
Elections are free from coercion and fraud
The outcomes of elections determine who holds political office
counterexample: Consider a state in which all adult citizens may vote, elections are scrupulously honest and free from any coercion, and the elected officials do in fact hold office. All three conditions are met. Yet the elected officials discover, upon taking office, that all significant policy decisions are made by an unelected permanent bureaucracy that controls the budget, the military, and the regulatory apparatus. The elected officials may speak, propose, and deliberate, but they cannot govern. The elections determine who holds the title of office but not who exercises power.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the elected officials possess effective power to govern — that the authority conferred by electoral victory extends to the actual making and implementation of policy, rather than being confined to a ceremonial role.
passage: One might characterise genuine democracy as a political system in which all adult citizens have an equal right to vote, elections are free from coercion and fraud, and electoral outcomes determine office-holders. This definition captures the procedural essentials of democratic governance as they are commonly understood. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed as follows. Suppose a state satisfies all three conditions impeccably. Every adult citizen votes freely. The elections are honest. The candidates who win take office. Yet upon assuming their positions, the elected officials discover that the actual governance of the state is conducted by an entrenched permanent bureaucracy. The bureaucracy controls the military, administers the budget, drafts all regulations, and manages the intelligence services. The elected officials may debate, propose, and speechify, but they lack the authority or the institutional mechanisms to translate their proposals into policy. The bureaucracy is courteous but immovable. One might observe that such a state satisfies every condition of the definition and yet is not, in any meaningful sense, a democracy. The elections determine who occupies the ceremonial trappings of office but not who governs. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the procedural mechanics of voting and the formal allocation of office, cannot distinguish genuine democratic governance from a system in which elected officials are figureheads presiding over an unaccountable administrative apparatus.

definition: A distribution of goods in a society is just if and only if (i) the distribution arises from voluntary exchanges among individuals, (ii) each individual's initial holdings were legitimately acquired, and (iii) no force or fraud was employed in any transaction.
conditions: The distribution arises from voluntary exchanges among individuals
Each individual's initial holdings were legitimately acquired
No force or fraud was employed in any transaction
counterexample: Consider a society in which, over several generations, a series of entirely voluntary and fraud-free exchanges results in one family accumulating ownership of all arable land, all water sources, and all means of production. Each transaction was legitimate, and the initial holdings were legitimately acquired. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the remaining citizens, owning nothing, must accept whatever terms the landowning family dictates in order to survive. The distribution is formally just by the definition, yet it has produced a condition of servitude indistinguishable in its practical effects from feudalism.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the resulting distribution preserve a minimum threshold of opportunity or material sufficiency for all members of society, so that formally voluntary transactions do not produce conditions of effective domination.
passage: One might characterise a just distribution of goods as one arising from voluntary exchanges, in which initial holdings were legitimately acquired and no force or fraud was employed. This procedural conception of justice has the attraction of respecting individual liberty and requiring no imposed pattern of distribution. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed by tracing the cumulative effects of procedurally impeccable transactions over time. Suppose that in a small agrarian society, a series of entirely voluntary exchanges over several generations results in a single family accumulating ownership of all arable land, all water sources, and all means of production. Each individual transaction was legitimate: no force was used, no fraud was practised, and the initial holdings of every party were properly acquired. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied at every stage. Yet the result is that the remaining citizens of the society own nothing of consequence. They must work the land on whatever terms the owning family dictates, for there is no alternative source of livelihood. They are free in the sense that they are not legally compelled to accept these terms, but they are unfree in the sense that the alternative is destitution. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the procedural legitimacy of individual transactions, cannot detect the emergence of structural domination from an accumulation of individually unobjectionable exchanges. It appears that procedural justice, however scrupulously maintained, may produce outcomes that are substantively indistinguishable from the most unjust forms of social organisation.

definition: A citizen C enjoys political freedom if and only if (i) C is not subject to arbitrary arrest or detention, (ii) C may express any opinion without legal penalty, and (iii) C may associate with any group or organisation of C's choosing.
conditions: C is not subject to arbitrary arrest or detention
C may express any opinion without legal penalty
C may associate with any group or organisation of C's choosing
counterexample: Consider a citizen in a state where there is no arbitrary arrest, no legal penalty for expressing opinions, and no restriction on association. All three conditions are met. Yet the state maintains a comprehensive surveillance apparatus that monitors every communication, tracks every movement, and records every association of every citizen. The information gathered is not used to arrest, penalise, or prohibit — but every citizen knows it is being collected. The chilling effect on speech and association is profound: citizens refrain from expressing controversial opinions and avoid joining unpopular groups, not because of any legal penalty but because of the pervasive awareness that they are watched.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that citizens be free from pervasive state surveillance that, even in the absence of legal penalties, inhibits the exercise of the very freedoms that the definition formally protects.
passage: One might characterise political freedom as the absence of arbitrary arrest, the right to express any opinion without legal penalty, and the right to associate freely. This definition captures the civil liberties that are most commonly invoked in discussions of free societies. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the effects of surveillance. Suppose a state satisfies all three conditions: there is no arbitrary arrest, no legal penalty for speech, and no restriction on association. The state is, by the definition, free. Yet the government maintains a comprehensive apparatus of surveillance. Every telephone call, every letter, every electronic communication is monitored. The movements of every citizen are tracked. The membership rolls of every organisation are known to the authorities. This information is not used to arrest anyone, to impose any legal penalty, or to prohibit any association. It is merely collected and stored. Yet the effect upon political freedom is devastating. Citizens who are aware of the surveillance — and eventually all become aware — refrain from expressing controversial opinions, avoid joining unpopular groups, and censor their private communications. The freedom that the definition guarantees in formal terms is hollowed out in practice by the awareness that one is watched. One might observe that the definition, by confining itself to the absence of legal penalties and formal prohibitions, cannot capture the chilling effect of surveillance upon the exercise of freedom. It appears that political freedom requires not only the absence of punishment but the absence of conditions that make the prospect of punishment perpetually salient.

definition: A state S is sovereign if and only if (i) S exercises supreme authority over a defined territory, (ii) S is recognised as legitimate by other states, and (iii) S possesses a monopoly on the legitimate use of force within its territory.
conditions: S exercises supreme authority over a defined territory
S is recognised as legitimate by other states
S possesses a monopoly on the legitimate use of force within its territory
counterexample: Consider a state that exercises supreme authority over its territory, is recognised by every other state in the international community, and possesses a monopoly on the legitimate use of force. All three conditions are met. Yet the state is so heavily indebted to international creditors that its fiscal policy, monetary policy, and even its domestic legislation are effectively dictated by the conditions attached to its loans. It cannot set its own tax rates, determine its own spending priorities, or enact social legislation without the approval of its creditors. It is sovereign by the definition but powerless to govern itself.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S possess effective autonomy in its policy-making — that its authority be exercised independently rather than being constrained by external economic or institutional dependencies that render formal sovereignty hollow.
passage: One might define sovereignty as the possession of supreme authority over a territory, international recognition, and a monopoly on the legitimate use of force. This definition captures the juridical essence of sovereignty as it has been understood since the early modern period. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the phenomenon of economic dependency. Suppose a state satisfies all three conditions. It exercises uncontested authority over its territory. Every other state recognises it as legitimate. It possesses an effective monopoly on force. Yet the state has accumulated such enormous debts to international financial institutions that the terms of its loans effectively dictate its domestic policy. It cannot set tax rates without the creditors' approval. Its spending priorities are determined by conditions attached to loans. It cannot enact social legislation that the creditors deem incompatible with fiscal consolidation. The state is formally sovereign but substantively powerless. One might observe that such a state is sovereign in the same way that a debtor is free — the legal form is intact but the capacity for autonomous action has been surrendered. It appears that the definition, by attending only to juridical authority and the monopoly of force, cannot distinguish genuine sovereignty from the formal shell that remains when a state has lost the capacity to determine its own affairs.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G came to power through a process accepted as valid by the political community, (ii) G governs in accordance with established laws and constitutional procedures, and (iii) G acts in the interest of the governed rather than in the interest of the rulers alone.
conditions: G came to power through a process accepted as valid by the political community
G governs in accordance with established laws and constitutional procedures
G acts in the interest of the governed rather than in the interest of the rulers alone
counterexample: Consider a government that came to power through a free election, governs strictly in accordance with its constitution, and enacts policies designed to promote the welfare of the citizenry. All three conditions are met. Yet the constitution under which it governs was drafted by a colonial power and imposed upon the population without consultation, and the electoral system it establishes systematically disadvantages the indigenous majority. The government is legitimate by the definition, for it followed the accepted process and acts within established law. Yet one might observe that the legitimacy of the government inherits the illegitimacy of the constitutional framework under which it operates.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the constitutional framework and procedural norms themselves be legitimate — that they were established through a process that included the meaningful participation and consent of the governed, rather than being imposed by an external or unrepresentative authority.
passage: One might characterise governmental legitimacy as arising from an accepted process of accession to power, adherence to established laws and constitutional procedures, and governance in the interest of the people. This definition combines procedural and substantive criteria in a way that captures the principal sources of political legitimacy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the origins of the constitutional framework itself. Suppose a former colony achieves formal independence and establishes a government through free and fair elections conducted under a constitution. The elected government governs in accordance with the constitution and enacts policies aimed at the welfare of the citizenry. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet the constitution under which the government operates was drafted by the departing colonial administration and imposed upon the population without meaningful consultation. The electoral system it establishes — single-member constituencies drawn to favour settler populations — systematically underrepresents the indigenous majority. The government is legitimate by the definition, for it follows the accepted process and acts within established law for the benefit of the governed. Yet one might observe that the legitimacy of the government is only as strong as the legitimacy of the framework within which it operates, and that framework was imposed rather than chosen. It appears that the definition, by taking the existing constitutional order as given, cannot detect cases in which procedural legitimacy is built upon a foundation that was itself established without the consent of the governed.

definition: A person or institution X has authority over a person Y if and only if (i) X occupies a recognised role or office that is sanctioned by the rules of the relevant social or political system, (ii) X issues directives that Y is expected to obey, and (iii) Y has an obligation to comply with X's directives in virtue of X's role.
conditions: X occupies a recognised role or office sanctioned by the rules of the relevant system
X issues directives that Y is expected to obey
Y has an obligation to comply with X's directives in virtue of X's role
counterexample: Consider a senior officer in an army who occupies a recognised rank, issues orders to subordinates, and whose subordinates have a clear obligation to obey in virtue of the military hierarchy. All three conditions are met. Now suppose the officer orders a subordinate to execute prisoners of war. The subordinate recognises that the order violates the laws of war. One might observe that while the officer satisfies every condition of the definition, his authority does not extend to issuing orders that contravene fundamental moral or legal principles. The subordinate may even have a duty to disobey.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the directives issued by X fall within the scope of X's legitimate competence and do not violate overriding moral or legal norms, so that the mere occupancy of a role and the issuance of directives do not constitute authority when the directives are themselves illegitimate.
passage: One might analyse authority as a relation in which a person occupying a recognised role issues directives that others are obligated to obey in virtue of that role. This analysis captures the institutional and normative character of authority as it operates in political, military, and organisational contexts. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when the directives issued by the authority conflict with overriding moral or legal norms. Consider a senior officer in an army who holds a recognised rank within the military hierarchy, issues orders to subordinates, and whose subordinates are bound by military law to obey. All three conditions are satisfied. Now suppose the officer orders a subordinate to execute unarmed prisoners of war. The subordinate recognises that the order violates the established laws of war and fundamental principles of humane conduct. One might observe that the officer's authority, though genuine in routine matters, does not extend to this directive. The definition, by grounding authority solely in the occupancy of a role and the obligation to comply, provides no resources for limiting the scope of authority or for recognising that certain directives fall outside the range of what any authority may legitimately command. It appears that authority is not merely a matter of institutional position and the expectation of obedience but is bounded by moral and legal constraints that the definition does not acknowledge.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) there exists a legal or moral rule that entitles P to X, (ii) others have a corresponding duty not to interfere with P's enjoyment of X, and (iii) P may legitimately demand enforcement of this entitlement if it is violated.
conditions: There exists a legal or moral rule that entitles P to X
Others have a corresponding duty not to interfere with P's enjoyment of X
P may legitimately demand enforcement of this entitlement if it is violated
counterexample: Consider a citizen of a state whose constitution guarantees the right to a fair trial. There exists a legal rule entitling her to it; others — including the state — have a duty not to interfere with this right; and she may demand its enforcement. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the state's judiciary is so underfunded and overburdened that no trial can be conducted within a reasonable period. Cases languish for decades. The citizen's right to a fair trial exists in law but cannot be exercised in fact. One might observe that calling her right genuine, when the institutional conditions for its exercise are entirely absent, stretches the concept beyond its useful application.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the institutional and material conditions necessary for the exercise of the right actually obtain, so that the right is not merely a formal entitlement on paper but a practically exercisable claim.
passage: One might characterise a right as an entitlement grounded in a legal or moral rule, correlative to a duty on the part of others not to interfere, and enforceable upon demand. This analysis captures the normative structure of rights as it has been articulated by jurists and political philosophers. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when the institutional conditions for the exercise of a right are absent. Consider a citizen of a state whose constitution solemnly guarantees the right to a fair trial. The legal rule exists. Others — including the organs of the state — have a duty not to interfere with this right. The citizen may legitimately demand its enforcement. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet the state's judiciary is so severely underfunded that cases are not heard for ten or fifteen years. Witnesses die or disappear. Evidence degrades. The citizen's right to a fair trial is, in any practical sense, meaningless. She possesses an entitlement that cannot be exercised, a claim that cannot be realised, and a demand that cannot be met. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the normative structure of rights — the existence of a rule, a correlative duty, and the capacity to demand enforcement — cannot distinguish between rights that are genuine, exercisable claims and rights that are mere declarations, formally intact but practically empty. It appears that a satisfactory account of rights would need to attend not only to the normative framework but to the institutional and material conditions that give rights their practical force.

definition: A citizen C has a political obligation to obey a law L if and only if (i) L was enacted by a legitimate authority, (ii) C has benefited from the legal order of which L is a part, and (iii) C has consented, explicitly or tacitly, to be governed by that legal order.
conditions: L was enacted by a legitimate authority
C has benefited from the legal order of which L is a part
C has consented explicitly or tacitly to be governed by that legal order
counterexample: Consider a person born into a state whose government she regards as legitimate, from whose legal order she has manifestly benefited, and in which she has continued to reside — thereby, on the usual account, giving tacit consent to its laws. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the legislature enacts a law requiring citizens to report their neighbours' private associations to the authorities. One might observe that the citizen's political obligation to obey the legal order in general does not obviously extend to a law whose content is so morally repugnant that compliance would require her to become an instrument of oppression.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the content of the specific law L be consistent with basic moral principles, so that political obligation is not an unconditional duty to obey any law enacted by legitimate authority but is bounded by the moral permissibility of what the law commands.
passage: One might analyse political obligation as the duty to obey laws enacted by a legitimate authority from whose legal order one has benefited and to which one has consented. This analysis draws together three of the most prominent grounds upon which political obligation has been defended: legitimacy, gratitude or reciprocity, and consent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the possibility of morally objectionable laws enacted by legitimate governments. Suppose a citizen lives in a state whose government she regards as entirely legitimate. She has benefited enormously from its legal order — its protections, its infrastructure, its institutions. She has continued to reside within its borders and to participate in its civic life, thereby giving what is usually taken to be tacit consent. All three conditions are met. Now suppose the legislature, through proper constitutional procedures, enacts a law requiring every citizen to report to the authorities any private meetings or associations conducted by their neighbours. The law is constitutionally valid, enacted by legitimate authority, and part of a legal order from which the citizen has benefited and to which she has consented. Yet one might observe that compliance with this law would require the citizen to become an instrument of surveillance and betrayal. It appears that the definition, by grounding political obligation solely in legitimacy, benefit, and consent, provides no basis for distinguishing between laws that one has an obligation to obey and laws whose content is so morally objectionable that the obligation lapses. It appears that political obligation is not unconditional but is bounded by the moral character of the specific law in question.

definition: A person P is a citizen of a state S if and only if (i) P was born within the territory of S or has been naturalised according to S's laws, (ii) P owes allegiance to S and is subject to S's laws, and (iii) P is entitled to the protections and benefits that S provides to its members.
conditions: P was born within the territory of S or has been naturalised according to S's laws
P owes allegiance to S and is subject to S's laws
P is entitled to the protections and benefits that S provides to its members
counterexample: Consider a person born within the territory of a state who has never been denaturalised, who owes allegiance to the state and is subject to its laws, and who is formally entitled to its protections and benefits. All three conditions are met. Yet this person has resided abroad for forty years, has no connection to the state beyond the accident of birth, pays no taxes to it, participates in none of its civic institutions, and has never voted in its elections. She is a citizen by the definition but has no substantive relationship to the political community.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P maintain a substantive connection to the political community of S — through participation, presence, or ongoing civic engagement — rather than possessing merely a formal legal status unaccompanied by any real ties.
passage: One might define citizenship as a status acquired by birth or naturalisation, involving allegiance and subjection to law, and entailing the protections and benefits of membership. This definition captures the formal legal structure of citizenship as it is codified in the nationality laws of most states. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the phenomenon of purely nominal citizenship. Suppose a person was born within the territory of a state and has never been denaturalised. She owes allegiance to the state and remains subject to its laws. She is formally entitled to its protections and benefits. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet she departed the state at the age of two and has resided abroad ever since. She has never returned. She pays no taxes to the state, participates in none of its civic or political institutions, and has never voted in its elections. She speaks none of its languages and has no cultural, social, or economic ties to it. She is, by the definition, a citizen. Yet one might observe that her citizenship is entirely formal — a legal fact without any corresponding political, social, or civic reality. It appears that the definition, by attending only to legal status and formal entitlement, cannot distinguish between citizenship as a living relationship to a political community and citizenship as a dormant legal classification with no substantive content.

definition: An event or series of events R constitutes a political revolution if and only if (i) R involves the overthrow of the existing government, (ii) R is carried out by a popular movement rather than by a palace coup or foreign intervention, and (iii) R results in a fundamental change in the form of government.
conditions: R involves the overthrow of the existing government
R is carried out by a popular movement
R results in a fundamental change in the form of government
counterexample: Consider a large-scale popular uprising that succeeds in overthrowing the existing government and is carried out entirely by a broad-based movement of ordinary citizens. All three conditions appear to be on the way to being met. Yet the popular movement, having overthrown the old government, proves unable to agree on a new form of government. After a period of disorder, a military general who participated in the uprising installs herself as head of state and governs by decree in a manner not fundamentally different from the regime that was overthrown. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) is not, for the form of government has not fundamentally changed. Yet historians would hesitate to deny that a revolution occurred.
passage: One might characterise a political revolution as a popular overthrow of the existing government resulting in a fundamental change in the form of government. This definition captures the dramatic and transformative character that the word revolution is ordinarily taken to imply. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers revolutions whose outcomes do not match the aspirations of those who carried them out. Suppose a broad-based popular movement, animated by genuine grievances and commanding wide support across social classes, succeeds in overthrowing a despotic government. The uprising is carried out by ordinary citizens — labourers, merchants, professionals — and not by military conspirators or foreign agents. Conditions (i) and (ii) are clearly satisfied. Yet in the aftermath of the overthrow, the popular movement fragments into rival factions. The constitutional convention collapses. A military general who had played a prominent role in the uprising steps into the vacuum and establishes a personal dictatorship that resembles, in all essential respects, the regime it replaced. The form of government has not fundamentally changed. Condition (iii) is not satisfied. Yet historians would be reluctant to deny that a revolution occurred. The popular uprising, the overthrow of the old order, and the brief flowering of new political possibilities are sufficient to warrant the term, even though the outcome was a restoration of autocratic rule in new hands. It appears that the definition, by requiring a fundamental change in the form of government as a necessary condition, cannot accommodate the well-attested phenomenon of revolutions that are betrayed or co-opted before they achieve their transformative aims.

definition: A political system S is democratic if and only if (i) the citizens of S select their leaders through periodic competitive elections, (ii) the franchise extends to all adult citizens without exclusion, and (iii) multiple political parties are permitted to compete for office.
conditions: The citizens select their leaders through periodic competitive elections
The franchise extends to all adult citizens without exclusion
Multiple political parties are permitted to compete for office
counterexample: Consider a state in which elections are held periodically, every adult citizen may vote, and several parties compete for office. All three conditions are met. Yet one party controls the national broadcasting service, owns the principal newspapers, and receives enormous funding from state-connected enterprises, while opposition parties are starved of funds and denied access to media. The elections are not rigged; the votes are counted honestly. But the electorate never hears the opposition's case. The ruling party wins every election by a wide margin, not through fraud but through informational monopoly.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the conditions of electoral competition be fair — specifically, that competing parties have reasonable and roughly comparable access to the means of public communication and the resources necessary to present their case to the electorate.
passage: One might define a democratic political system as one in which leaders are selected through periodic competitive elections, the franchise extends to all adults, and multiple parties are permitted to compete. This procedural definition captures the essential institutional features of electoral democracy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when the formal conditions of competition coexist with a profound inequality in the resources available to the competitors. Consider a state that satisfies all three conditions. Elections are held regularly. Every adult citizen votes. Half a dozen parties are legally registered and free to field candidates. Yet the ruling party controls the national broadcasting service. It owns, through allied enterprises, the principal newspapers and the dominant internet platforms. Its campaign budget, financed by state-connected corporations, exceeds the combined budgets of all opposition parties by a factor of fifty. The opposition parties are neither banned nor suppressed; they are simply invisible. Their candidates speak at rallies attended by dozens; the ruling party's messages reach millions daily. The elections are not rigged. The votes are counted honestly. Yet the electorate, having heard only one side of any question, votes overwhelmingly for the party whose voice it has heard. One might observe that such a system is democratic in form but not in substance. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the existence of elections, the universality of the franchise, and the legality of competition, cannot detect the informational asymmetries that render competition nominal rather than genuine.

definition: A legal system L is just if and only if (i) L applies the same rules to all persons regardless of their social standing, (ii) L provides an independent judiciary empowered to adjudicate disputes impartially, and (iii) L affords every accused person the opportunity to be heard in their own defence.
conditions: L applies the same rules to all persons regardless of social standing
L provides an independent judiciary empowered to adjudicate disputes impartially
L affords every accused person the opportunity to be heard in their own defence
counterexample: Consider a legal system that applies the same rules to all, maintains a scrupulously independent judiciary, and guarantees every accused person a hearing. All three conditions are met. Yet the cost of legal representation is so great that only the wealthy can afford competent counsel, while the poor must represent themselves. The rules are impartially applied, but the poor, unable to navigate the complexities of legal procedure, are convicted at far higher rates than the wealthy for the same offences. The system is formally just but substantively discriminatory.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the practical capacity to make use of the legal system's protections be available to all, so that formal equality before the law coexists with a material inequality that renders the system's protections inaccessible to those without resources.
passage: One might characterise a just legal system as one that applies the same rules to all persons, provides an independent judiciary, and affords every accused person the opportunity to be heard. This definition captures the formal principles of legal equality and procedural fairness that are widely regarded as the foundations of justice. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the practical conditions under which these formal guarantees are exercised. Consider a state whose legal system satisfies all three conditions. The same criminal code applies to all citizens. The judiciary is independent and incorruptible. Every accused person is entitled to appear before a court and present a defence. Yet the law is extraordinarily complex, and competent legal representation is costly. The wealthiest citizens retain skilled barristers who exploit procedural subtleties to secure acquittals or favourable settlements. The poorest citizens, unable to afford representation, must conduct their own defence before judges who, however impartial, cannot compensate for the accused's ignorance of the law. The result is a systematic disparity: the poor are convicted at far higher rates than the wealthy for identical offences. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the formal structure of the legal system, cannot detect the substantive injustice that arises when the capacity to make use of formal protections is distributed unequally. It appears that justice requires not only that the rules be the same for all but that the practical means of invoking them be accessible to all.

definition: A state S possesses sovereignty over its territory if and only if (i) S maintains effective control over the territory by means of its own administrative and military apparatus, (ii) no external power exercises authority within the territory without S's consent, and (iii) S has the capacity to enter into treaties and diplomatic relations with other states as an independent agent.
conditions: S maintains effective control over the territory by means of its own apparatus
No external power exercises authority within the territory without S's consent
S has the capacity to enter into treaties and diplomatic relations independently
counterexample: Consider a small island state that maintains effective control over its territory, conducts its own foreign affairs, and has consented to no external authority within its borders. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the state's entire economy depends upon a single commodity exported to a single large neighbour. The neighbour, by adjusting tariffs or refusing to purchase the commodity, can bring the island state to the brink of economic collapse within weeks. The island state, aware of this dependence, refrains from any foreign policy initiative that might displease its neighbour. It is formally sovereign but practically unable to act against the neighbour's interests.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S possess a degree of economic self-sufficiency or diversification sufficient to prevent its sovereignty from being effectively negated by the economic leverage of another state.
passage: One might define sovereignty as the effective control of a territory by means of the state's own apparatus, the absence of unconsented external authority, and the capacity to conduct independent diplomacy. This characterisation captures the juridical and administrative dimensions of sovereignty as they figure in international law. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the role of economic dependence. Consider a small island state whose territory is effectively controlled by its own government, whose borders admit no unconsented foreign authority, and which maintains embassies abroad and participates in international organisations. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet the state's economy rests entirely upon the export of a single commodity — copra, let us suppose — to a single large neighbouring power. The neighbour purchases the entirety of the island's output. By adjusting its tariffs or by threatening to source copra elsewhere, the neighbour can within weeks reduce the island's revenues to a fraction of what is needed to sustain its government and feed its population. The island's leaders, acutely aware of this dependence, systematically avoid any diplomatic or domestic initiative that might displease their sole customer. One might observe that such a state is sovereign in law but not in practice. Its formal independence is hollowed out by an economic dependence so complete that the neighbouring power need never exercise overt authority — the threat alone suffices. It appears that the definition, by confining sovereignty to juridical and administrative criteria, cannot detect the economic dependencies that render formal sovereignty a fiction.

definition: A citizen C is free if and only if (i) no law prohibits C from performing any action that does not harm others, (ii) C is not subject to compulsory labour or servitude, and (iii) C may travel freely within and beyond the borders of C's state.
conditions: No law prohibits C from performing any action that does not harm others
C is not subject to compulsory labour or servitude
C may travel freely within and beyond the borders of the state
counterexample: Consider a citizen whose state satisfies all three conditions. No law prohibits her from pursuing any harmless activity. She is not subject to compulsory labour. She may travel freely. Yet she is so impoverished that she cannot afford food, shelter, or transport. She is legally free to travel but has no means of doing so. She is legally free to pursue any career but lacks the education and resources to enter one. Her freedom is juridically complete but practically empty.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that C possess the material means to exercise the freedoms that the law formally guarantees, so that a citizen who is free in law but destitute in fact is classified as free despite being unable to act on any of their formal liberties.
passage: One might characterise the freedom of a citizen as the absence of legal prohibitions on harmless action, freedom from compulsory labour, and the right to travel. This negative conception of freedom captures the legal protections that are commonly associated with a free society. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the material conditions that are necessary for the exercise of formal liberties. Consider a citizen of a state that satisfies all three conditions. No law prohibits her from pursuing any occupation, engaging in any harmless recreation, or expressing any view. She is subject to no compulsory labour. She holds a passport and may cross any border. Yet she is destitute. She cannot afford a meal, a roof, or a railway ticket. She is free to travel but has no means of transport. She is free to pursue a profession but lacks the education required for any. She is free to express her views but has no access to any medium through which to express them. One might observe that this citizen is free in the same sense in which a woman locked in a room is free to fly — the absence of a legal prohibition is not the presence of a capacity. It appears that the definition, by confining freedom to the absence of legal constraints, cannot accommodate the thought that freedom requires not only the removal of prohibitions but the provision of the material conditions necessary to act upon them. The distinction between formal and substantive freedom is one that the definition cannot draw.

definition: A government G exercises legitimate authority if and only if (i) G has been chosen by a majority of the electorate in a free vote, (ii) G governs within the limits prescribed by a written constitution, and (iii) G submits to regular re-election at constitutionally mandated intervals.
conditions: G has been chosen by a majority of the electorate in a free vote
G governs within the limits prescribed by a written constitution
G submits to regular re-election at constitutionally mandated intervals
counterexample: Consider a government chosen by a free majority vote, operating within a written constitution, and submitting to regular elections. All three conditions are met. Yet the government systematically excludes a minority population from the political process — not by denying them the vote, which they formally possess, but by conducting all official proceedings in a language the minority does not speak, by locating polling stations at inconvenient distances from minority settlements, and by scheduling elections on days of religious observance for the minority. The exclusion is effective though nowhere stated in law.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the political process be genuinely inclusive of all segments of the population in practice, not merely in formal legal terms, so that a government may satisfy all three conditions while effectively disenfranchising a minority through administrative and cultural mechanisms.
passage: One might characterise legitimate governmental authority as resting upon majority election, constitutional governance, and regular re-election. This definition captures the electoral and constitutional foundations of democratic legitimacy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when the formal political process, though legally inclusive, is practically exclusionary. Consider a state in which a government has been freely elected by a majority, governs within a written constitution, and submits to elections at the prescribed intervals. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet the state contains a substantial linguistic minority whose language is not used in any official proceeding. All legislative debates, all court proceedings, all government publications, and all ballot papers are in the majority language. Polling stations in areas where the minority is concentrated are located at considerable distances from population centres and are open only during hours that coincide with the minority's customary times of work or worship. No law denies the minority the vote. No constitutional provision excludes them. Yet the practical effect of these administrative arrangements is that the minority participates in elections at a fraction of the rate of the majority population. One might observe that the government's legitimacy, though formally impeccable, rests upon a process from which a significant portion of the population is effectively excluded. It appears that the definition, by attending only to formal electoral and constitutional criteria, cannot detect the practical mechanisms by which a superficially inclusive process may be rendered substantively exclusionary.

definition: An institution I possesses political authority over a population P if and only if (i) I issues rules that P is expected to obey, (ii) I can enforce compliance through sanctions, and (iii) the members of P generally comply with I's rules.
conditions: I issues rules that P is expected to obey
I can enforce compliance through sanctions
The members of P generally comply with I's rules
counterexample: Consider an organised criminal syndicate that controls a district of a city. The syndicate issues rules — regarding the conduct of commerce, the resolution of disputes, and the behaviour of residents — that the population is expected to obey. It enforces compliance through severe sanctions. And the residents, out of fear, generally comply. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the syndicate does not possess political authority in any sense that carries normative weight. Its power rests entirely upon coercion, and the compliance it secures is the compliance of subjugation, not of allegiance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the institution's claim to obedience rest upon some normative basis — consent, tradition, constitutional sanction, or the promotion of the common good — rather than upon the mere capacity for coercion, so that any sufficiently powerful coercive body qualifies as a political authority.
passage: One might characterise political authority as the capacity to issue rules, enforce compliance through sanctions, and secure general obedience. This analysis captures the observable features of authority as they manifest in any functioning polity: rules are promulgated, sanctions are imposed, and people comply. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the rule of organised criminal syndicates. In certain cities, a criminal organisation may exercise de facto control over a district. It issues rules governing the conduct of local commerce, the resolution of disputes among residents, and even the permitted hours of public assembly. It enforces these rules through sanctions of considerable severity. And the residents, having no realistic alternative, generally comply. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that to call this political authority is to drain the concept of its normative content. The syndicate's power rests entirely upon intimidation and violence. The compliance it secures is the compliance of fear, not of recognition. No resident regards the syndicate's rules as legitimate or its sanctions as just. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the sociological facts of rule-issuance, enforcement, and compliance, cannot distinguish political authority from the brute exercise of coercive power. Authority, it seems, requires some normative basis — consent, constitutional sanction, or the promotion of a recognisable common good — that the definition does not require.

definition: A citizen C has a political obligation to obey a law L if and only if (i) L was enacted by a legitimate legislative body, (ii) C has benefited from the legal order of which L is a part, and (iii) C has not publicly repudiated the authority of the legislative body.
conditions: L was enacted by a legitimate legislative body
C has benefited from the legal order of which L is a part
C has not publicly repudiated the authority of the legislative body
counterexample: Consider a citizen of a state with a legitimate legislature who has benefited from the legal order — she has enjoyed the protection of property laws, the use of public roads, and the security provided by the police. She has never repudiated the legislature's authority. All three conditions are met. Now suppose the legislature enacts a law requiring citizens to report the whereabouts of political refugees to the authorities for deportation. The citizen believes, upon deep reflection, that compliance with this law would be gravely immoral. One might observe that the definition, by grounding obligation in legislative legitimacy, benefit, and non-repudiation, provides no mechanism for recognising that a citizen's moral duties may override her political obligation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that a law may be so gravely unjust that the citizen's moral duty to refuse compliance overrides whatever political obligation arises from legislative legitimacy and receipt of benefits, so that the three conditions cannot generate an absolute obligation in the face of manifest injustice.
passage: One might propose that a citizen has a political obligation to obey a law if it was enacted by a legitimate legislature, the citizen has benefited from the legal order, and the citizen has not repudiated the legislature's authority. This definition grounds obligation in the reciprocal relationship between a citizen and the legal order that protects and benefits her. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when the law in question demands something that is morally unconscionable. Consider a citizen who has benefited greatly from her state's legal order. She has enjoyed the protection of its property laws, used its public roads, and been safeguarded by its police. She has never questioned the authority of the legislature. Now suppose the legislature, in response to a political crisis, enacts a law requiring every citizen to report the location of any political refugee known to be hiding within the state, so that the refugees may be apprehended and deported to a country where they face persecution. The law was enacted through proper legislative channels by a duly constituted body. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet the citizen believes, upon the most careful reflection, that compliance would be gravely immoral — that it would make her complicit in the persecution of innocent persons. One might observe that the definition, by grounding obligation in legitimacy, benefit, and non-repudiation, cannot accommodate the possibility that a law's injustice may be so severe as to dissolve whatever obligation those conditions would otherwise generate.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) there exists a law or social norm that entitles P to X, (ii) others have a corresponding duty not to interfere with P's enjoyment of X, and (iii) P's entitlement to X can be enforced through institutional mechanisms.
conditions: There exists a law or social norm that entitles P to X
Others have a corresponding duty not to interfere with P's enjoyment of X
P's entitlement to X can be enforced through institutional mechanisms
counterexample: Consider a person living under a legal regime that does not recognise any entitlement to bodily integrity — a slave, for instance, in a society in which slavery is lawful. No law entitles the slave to be free from bodily harm by her owner. No social norm imposes a corresponding duty on the owner. No institutional mechanism exists to enforce such an entitlement. None of the three conditions is satisfied. Yet one might observe, with considerable force, that the slave has a right not to be beaten — a right that exists despite its non-recognition by any law, norm, or institution.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the existence of rights that are prior to or independent of any legal or social order, so that rights which rest upon the inherent dignity or moral standing of the person cannot be accommodated when no positive law recognises them.
passage: One might characterise rights as entitlements grounded in law or social norms, correlating with duties of non-interference in others, and enforceable through institutional mechanisms. This positivist analysis captures the institutional character of rights as they function in legal practice. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers persons who live under legal regimes that deny them rights which, upon reflection, they plainly possess. Consider a slave in a society whose law recognises the institution of slavery. No law entitles the slave to bodily integrity. No social norm imposes upon the slaveholder a duty to refrain from inflicting harm. No institutional mechanism exists through which the slave might seek redress. None of the three conditions of the definition is satisfied. By the definition, the slave has no right not to be beaten. Yet one might observe, with great force, that this conclusion is morally intolerable. The slave does have a right not to be beaten — a right that exists in spite of, and in opposition to, the legal order that denies it. The abolitionists of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries argued precisely this: that certain rights belong to persons as such, by virtue of their moral standing, and that a legal system that fails to recognise them is not merely incomplete but unjust. It appears that the definition, by grounding rights entirely in positive law and social norms, cannot accommodate rights whose existence is prior to and independent of any particular legal order.

definition: A citizen C has consented to the authority of a government G if and only if (i) C has performed an explicit act — such as an oath, a vote, or a signed declaration — that signifies acceptance of G's authority, (ii) C's act was voluntary and not performed under duress, and (iii) C understood the nature and implications of the act at the time of performance.
conditions: C has performed an explicit act signifying acceptance of G's authority
C's act was voluntary and not performed under duress
C understood the nature and implications of the act at the time of performance
counterexample: Consider a person who has never performed any explicit act of consent — she has never sworn an oath, never signed a declaration, and has chosen not to vote.
