She was born in the state, grew up in it, and continues to reside there. She benefits from its laws and uses its public services. Yet she has performed no act that satisfies condition (i). By the definition, she has not consented to the government's authority. Yet the state claims authority over her, and many political thinkers would argue that by residing in the state and accepting its benefits, she has tacitly consented to its authority.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the concept of tacit consent — the idea that continued residence, participation in public institutions, and acceptance of governmental benefits may constitute a form of consent even in the absence of any explicit act of acceptance.
passage: One might characterise political consent as an explicit, voluntary, and informed act signifying acceptance of governmental authority. This analysis captures the paradigmatic cases of consent — the naturalisation oath, the loyalty pledge, the constitutional referendum — in which citizens perform a discrete act that clearly indicates their acceptance. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the vast majority of citizens who have never performed any such act. Consider a woman born in a modern state who grows up, is educated in its schools, uses its public roads and hospitals, and earns her livelihood within its legal framework. She has never sworn an oath of allegiance. She has never signed a declaration of acceptance. She has, in fact, chosen not to vote, regarding the exercise as futile. None of the conditions of the definition is satisfied — no explicit act has been performed. Yet the state claims authority over her, taxes her income, and enforces its laws upon her person. And many political thinkers would argue that by continuing to reside in the state, by accepting the protection of its laws, and by making use of its public institutions, she has in some sense consented to its authority — if not explicitly, then tacitly. One might observe that the definition, by requiring an explicit act of consent, cannot accommodate this pervasive and philosophically influential notion of tacit consent, according to which residence and benefit-acceptance may themselves constitute a form of acceptance.

definition: A legislator L represents a constituency C if and only if (i) L was elected by the members of C in a free and fair election, (ii) L acts in accordance with the expressed wishes and interests of the majority of C, and (iii) L is accountable to C through the mechanism of periodic re-election.
conditions: L was elected by the members of C in a free and fair election
L acts in accordance with the expressed wishes and interests of the majority of C
L is accountable to C through periodic re-election
counterexample: Consider a legislator elected in a free and fair contest who discovers, upon taking office, that the expressed wish of her constituency is that a neighbouring community be denied access to a shared water source. She believes, upon careful deliberation, that complying with this wish would be unjust and would cause grave harm to innocent persons. She acts against the majority's expressed preference and votes to preserve the shared access. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. Yet one might observe that in doing so, she represents her constituency more nobly than if she had complied — she exercises the independent judgement that, on one influential account, is the very essence of representation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the exercise of independent moral and deliberative judgement by the representative, so that a legislator who acts against the expressed wishes of them constituents in order to prevent injustice is classified as failing in their representative role.
passage: One might propose that a legislator represents a constituency when she is elected by its members, acts according to their expressed wishes, and is accountable through re-election. This analysis captures the democratic intuition that representatives are delegates of the popular will. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when the expressed will of the constituency is unjust. Consider a legislator elected in an exemplary free and fair contest who finds, upon taking office, that a strong majority of her constituents wish her to vote for a measure that would deny a neighbouring community access to a shared river — the sole water source for both populations. The measure, if enacted, would cause grave hardship to the neighbouring community. The legislator deliberates carefully and concludes that compliance with the majority's wish would be unjust. She votes against the measure, knowing that she will face the displeasure of her constituents at the next election. Condition (ii) of the definition is not satisfied: she has acted against the expressed wishes of the majority. By the definition, she has failed in her representative capacity. Yet one might observe that her conduct exemplifies a form of representation that is, on certain accounts, more admirable than mere delegation — the exercise of independent judgement in the service of justice. It appears that the definition, by requiring conformity with the expressed wishes of the constituency, cannot accommodate the view that a representative may sometimes best serve her constituents by acting against their stated preferences when those preferences are manifestly unjust.

definition: A political system P is democratic if and only if (i) the adult citizens of P have the right to vote in regular elections, (ii) the elections are competitive — more than one candidate or party stands for office, and (iii) the government is formed by the party or coalition that wins the most votes.
conditions: The adult citizens have the right to vote in regular elections
The elections are competitive
The government is formed by the party winning the most votes
counterexample: Consider a political system in which elections are held regularly, multiple parties compete, and the government is formed by the party winning the most votes — but in which the press is controlled by the ruling party, opposition candidates are routinely harassed, and the electoral rolls are manipulated to exclude certain ethnic or social groups. All three conditions are formally satisfied, yet the system is not democratic in any substantive sense.
missing_condition: The definition captures the formal machinery of electoral democracy but fails to require the substantive conditions — freedom of the press, freedom of association, the rule of law, equal access to the franchise — without which elections are mere theatre.
passage: One might define democracy as a system in which citizens vote in regular competitive elections and the government is formed by the winning party. This characterisation captures the formal machinery of electoral democracy with precision. Yet a case which tells against the sufficiency of these formal conditions may be drawn from the practice of authoritarian regimes that maintain a democratic facade. Consider a system in which elections are held at regular intervals, multiple parties compete for office, and the government is duly formed by the party receiving the most votes. All three conditions are satisfied. But suppose further that the press is controlled by the ruling party, opposition candidates are subjected to harassment and intimidation, the judiciary serves the interests of the government, and the electoral rolls are manipulated to exclude unfavoured ethnic or social groups from voting. The elections are competitive in form but not in substance; the franchise is universal in law but restricted in practice. The system satisfies every stated condition yet is not democratic in any meaningful sense. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the formal machinery of elections, fails to require the substantive conditions — freedom of expression, freedom of association, the independence of the judiciary, and genuine equality of access to the franchise — without which the forms of democracy are emptied of their content.

definition: A person P is free if and only if (i) P is not subject to physical coercion or restraint, (ii) P is not legally prohibited from acting as P chooses, and (iii) no other person deliberately interferes with P's choices.
conditions: P is not subject to physical coercion or restraint
P is not legally prohibited from acting as P chooses
No other person deliberately interferes with P's choices
counterexample: Consider a person living in extreme poverty, who is not physically restrained, faces no legal prohibitions, and is not deliberately interfered with by any other person. She is formally free to travel, to educate herself, to start a business. Yet she cannot do any of these things because she lacks the material resources — the money, the nutrition, the access to education — that would make these options genuinely available. All three conditions are met, yet she is not free in any substantive sense: the options that are formally open to her are practically inaccessible.
missing_condition: The definition captures freedom from external interference but fails to account for the positive conditions — the material resources, capabilities, and opportunities — that must be present for formal freedom to translate into effective freedom, the freedom to actually do and be what one values.
passage: One might define freedom as the absence of physical coercion, legal prohibition, and deliberate interference by others. This characterisation, in the tradition of negative liberty, captures a recognisable and important conception of freedom. Yet a case which tells against its sufficiency may be drawn from the condition of the very poor. Consider a person living in extreme poverty — without adequate food, shelter, or education. She is not physically restrained; no chains bind her. She faces no legal prohibition against travelling, learning, or pursuing a livelihood. No other person deliberately interferes with her choices. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet she cannot travel, for she has no money. She cannot educate herself, for she has no access to schools. She cannot pursue a livelihood, for she lacks the nutrition and health required for sustained labour. She is formally free but substantively unfree: the options that are formally available to her are practically inaccessible. It appears that the definition, by confining freedom to the absence of external interference, overlooks the positive conditions — the material resources, capabilities, and opportunities — without which formal freedom is an empty privilege. Freedom, one might observe, requires not merely the absence of obstacles but the presence of the means by which obstacles can be overcome.

definition: A society S achieves equality if and only if (i) all citizens of S have the same legal rights, (ii) no citizen is discriminated against on the basis of birth, sex, or social origin, and (iii) all citizens have equal access to the institutions of government.
conditions: All citizens have the same legal rights
No citizen is discriminated against on the basis of birth, sex, or social origin
All citizens have equal access to the institutions of government
counterexample: Consider a society in which all citizens have identical legal rights, no formal discrimination exists, and every citizen may stand for office and vote. Yet the distribution of wealth is such that a small class of families controls the vast majority of economic resources, while the majority of citizens live in conditions of material deprivation. The wealthy enjoy superior education, health care, housing, and social connections. The legal equality is real, but the practical opportunities available to citizens differ enormously. The three conditions are met, yet the society is profoundly unequal.
missing_condition: The definition captures formal or legal equality but fails to account for economic and social inequality — the vast disparities in wealth, education, and opportunity that may coexist with perfect legal equality and render it practically meaningless.
passage: One might define equality as a condition in which all citizens have the same legal rights, are free from discrimination, and have equal access to government. This characterisation captures the ideal of formal or legal equality with considerable precision. Yet a case which tells against the sufficiency of formal equality may be drawn from the structure of an economically stratified society. Consider a society in which every citizen holds identical legal rights, no formal discrimination on the basis of birth, sex, or social origin exists, and every citizen may vote, stand for office, and petition the government. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the distribution of wealth is such that a small number of families control the majority of economic resources, while most citizens live in material deprivation. The wealthy enjoy superior education, health care, housing, and social influence. The poor, though legally equal, find that the opportunities formally available to them are practically inaccessible. It appears that the definition, by attending only to legal and political equality, cannot accommodate the profound social and economic inequalities that may coexist with perfect formal equality. Equality before the law, one might observe, is a necessary but not a sufficient condition of the equality that a just society requires.

definition: A person or body B has political authority over a group G if and only if (i) B has the power to issue commands that G is expected to obey, (ii) B's power is recognised as legitimate by the members of G, and (iii) B obtained power through an established procedure — election, appointment, or succession.
conditions: B has the power to issue commands that G is expected to obey
B's power is recognised as legitimate by the members of G
B obtained power through an established procedure
counterexample: Consider a revolutionary leader who seizes power by force, overthrowing an established government. She has not obtained power through any established procedure — indeed, she has destroyed the established procedure. Yet her commands are obeyed and her authority is recognised as legitimate by the population, which regards the revolution as justified. All three conditions are met except the third. Yet her authority is real and recognised — more so, perhaps, than that of the government she replaced.
missing_condition: The definition ties authority to established procedures of succession and thereby cannot accommodate the authority that arises from revolution, popular uprising, or charismatic leadership — cases in which legitimacy is conferred by popular recognition rather than by procedural regularity.
passage: One might define political authority as the power to issue commands, recognised as legitimate, and obtained through an established procedure. This analysis captures the authority of elected officials, appointed judges, and hereditary monarchs. Yet a case which tells against the procedural condition may be drawn from the history of revolution. Consider a leader who seizes power by overthrowing an established government. She has not obtained power through any established procedure — election, appointment, or succession. She has destroyed the established procedure. Yet her commands are obeyed by the population, and her authority is recognised as legitimate by the vast majority of citizens, who regard the revolution as a justified response to tyranny. The first two conditions are satisfied; the third is violated. Yet her authority is real — more effective and more widely recognised, perhaps, than that of the government she displaced. It appears that the definition, by requiring procedural regularity, cannot accommodate the authority that arises from revolution, popular uprising, or charismatic leadership. Authority, one might observe, is ultimately grounded in the recognition of the governed, and this recognition may be conferred upon leaders who have come to power by entirely extra-procedural means.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G was established with the consent of the governed, (ii) G exercises power in accordance with a constitution or fundamental law, and (iii) G serves the common good of the population it governs.
conditions: G was established with the consent of the governed
G exercises power in accordance with a constitution
G serves the common good
counterexample: Consider a benevolent colonial administration that governs a territory with scrupulous adherence to a constitutional framework and demonstrably promotes the welfare of the population — building schools, hospitals, and infrastructure. It exercises power constitutionally and serves the common good. But it was never established with the consent of the governed; it was imposed by a foreign power. The first condition is violated. Yet one might argue that a government serving the common good under the rule of law has a claim to legitimacy — whilst another might insist, equally plausibly, that the absence of consent is fatal. The definition cannot resolve this tension because it treats consent, constitutionalism, and the common good as jointly necessary without acknowledging that they may conflict.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify which condition takes precedence when consent, constitutionalism, and the common good conflict — it presents three necessary conditions that may not be jointly satisfiable and offers no principle for adjudicating between them.
passage: One might define governmental legitimacy as requiring the consent of the governed, constitutional exercise of power, and service of the common good. This analysis captures an appealing ideal: a government chosen by its people, constrained by law, and devoted to the public welfare. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this tripartite requirement may be constructed from the history of colonial governance. Consider a benevolent colonial administration that governs a territory with scrupulous adherence to a constitutional framework, builds schools and hospitals, promotes economic development, and demonstrably serves the welfare of the population. The second and third conditions are satisfied. But the government was never established with the consent of the governed; it was imposed by a foreign power without consulting the population. The first condition is violated. Yet the government governs well and under the rule of law. One might argue that its service of the common good and its constitutional regularity confer a degree of legitimacy; another might insist that the absence of consent is fatal, regardless of the quality of governance. The definition, by treating all three conditions as jointly necessary, cannot resolve this conflict and provides no principle for determining which condition takes precedence when the three cannot be simultaneously satisfied.

definition: A state S is sovereign if and only if (i) S exercises supreme authority within its territory, (ii) S is not subject to the authority of any external power, and (iii) S has the capacity to enforce its laws within its borders.
conditions: S exercises supreme authority within its territory
S is not subject to any external authority
S has the capacity to enforce its laws within its borders
counterexample: Consider a member state of a political and economic union that has voluntarily transferred certain legislative powers to supranational institutions — agreeing, for instance, that regulations issued by the union shall have direct effect within its territory and shall override conflicting national legislation. The state is subject to the authority of an external power in the relevant domains; the second condition is violated. Yet the state is universally recognised as sovereign, maintains its own armed forces, conducts its own foreign policy in most respects, and could, in principle, withdraw from the union.
missing_condition: The definition treats sovereignty as an absolute and indivisible property, failing to accommodate the graduated and divisible character of sovereignty in practice, where states routinely share, delegate, or limit their authority through treaties, unions, and international agreements without ceasing to be sovereign.
passage: One might define sovereignty as the exercise of supreme authority within a territory, freedom from external authority, and the capacity to enforce domestic law. This characterisation captures the classical Westphalian ideal of the sovereign state. Yet a case which tells against the requirement of freedom from all external authority may be drawn from the contemporary practice of political unions. Consider a state that has voluntarily joined an economic and political union, agreeing that certain regulations issued by supranational institutions shall have direct effect within its territory and shall override conflicting national legislation. In these domains, the state is subject to the authority of an external power. The second condition is violated. Yet the state is universally recognised as sovereign. It maintains its own armed forces, conducts its own foreign policy, and retains the legal right to withdraw from the union. It appears that the definition, by treating sovereignty as an absolute and indivisible property, cannot accommodate the graduated and divisible character of sovereignty in practice. States routinely share, delegate, and limit their authority through treaties and international agreements without forfeiting their sovereignty. Sovereignty, one might observe, admits of degrees and domains rather than being an all-or-nothing condition.

definition: An act A constitutes civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a deliberate violation of a law, (ii) A is performed publicly and non-violently, and (iii) A is motivated by a desire to change an unjust law or policy.
conditions: A is a deliberate violation of a law
A is performed publicly and non-violently
A is motivated by a desire to change an unjust law or policy
counterexample: Consider a group of workers who secretly refuse to comply with a government order to produce munitions for an unjust war. Their refusal is a deliberate violation of the law, motivated by a desire to oppose an unjust policy. But it is performed secretly rather than publicly — the workers fear imprisonment and conceal their non-compliance. The second condition is violated (with respect to publicity). Yet their act of resistance has a strong claim to be regarded as civil disobedience, differing from the paradigmatic case only in its secrecy.
missing_condition: The definition's publicity requirement excludes acts of principled law-breaking that are performed covertly due to the practical impossibility or extreme danger of public protest, failing to recognise that the moral character of civil disobedience may inhere in the principled motivation rather than in the public performance.
passage: One might define civil disobedience as a deliberate, public, non-violent violation of law motivated by the desire to change an unjust law or policy. This characterisation captures the paradigmatic cases — the sit-in at a segregated lunch counter, the peaceful march in defiance of a discriminatory ordinance. Yet a case which tells against the publicity requirement may be drawn from situations of severe repression. Consider a group of workers in an authoritarian state who secretly refuse to comply with a government order to manufacture munitions for an unjust war of aggression. Their refusal is deliberate and non-violent. It is motivated by a sincere conviction that the war is unjust and that compliance would make them complicit in injustice. But they perform their act of resistance secretly, for the regime punishes public dissent with imprisonment or worse. The second condition — publicity — is violated. Yet their act of principled non-compliance has a strong claim to be regarded as civil disobedience. Its moral character — the deliberate, non-violent, conscientious refusal to comply with an unjust law — is the same as that of the public protester. It appears that the definition, by requiring publicity, privileges one form of principled resistance and excludes another that is morally indistinguishable but practically constrained by the danger of public protest.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) P has a valid claim to X that others are obligated to respect, (ii) P's claim is recognised by the legal system, and (iii) P can enforce the claim through legal proceedings.
conditions: P has a valid claim that others are obligated to respect
P's claim is recognised by the legal system
P can enforce the claim through legal proceedings
counterexample: Consider a person living under a tyrannical regime that denies freedom of speech. She has no legal recognition of her right to speak freely, and she cannot enforce such a claim through legal proceedings. The second and third conditions are violated. Yet many would insist that she has a right to freedom of speech — a natural or moral right that exists independently of legal recognition and that the tyrannical regime violates precisely because it fails to recognise it.
missing_condition: The definition reduces rights to legally recognised and enforceable claims, failing to accommodate the concept of moral or natural rights — rights that persons possess independently of whether any legal system recognises or enforces them.
passage: One might define a right as a valid claim recognised by the legal system and enforceable through legal proceedings. This analysis captures the rights of citizens in well-ordered legal systems: the right to property, the right to a fair trial, the right to vote. Yet a case which tells against the legal recognition requirement may be drawn from the condition of persons living under tyranny. Consider a person whose government denies freedom of speech — criminalising dissent, censoring the press, and imprisoning those who speak against the regime. She has no legal recognition of any right to speak freely. She cannot enforce such a claim through legal proceedings; the courts are instruments of the regime. The second and third conditions are violated. Yet many would insist — and many political traditions have insisted — that she has a right to freedom of speech. This right is not created by law; it exists prior to and independently of legal recognition. The tyrannical regime violates it precisely because the right exists whether the regime acknowledges it or not. It appears that the definition, by reducing rights to legally recognised claims, cannot accommodate the concept of moral or natural rights — rights that persons possess by virtue of their humanity rather than by virtue of the legal system under which they happen to live.

definition: A decision D is democratically made if and only if (i) D is determined by majority vote among the eligible voters, (ii) every eligible voter has had an equal opportunity to participate, and (iii) D is binding upon all members of the community.
conditions: D is determined by majority vote
Every eligible voter has had an equal opportunity to participate
D is binding upon all members of the community
counterexample: Consider a majority vote by the citizens of a town to confiscate the property of an unpopular ethnic minority and distribute it among the majority. The decision is determined by majority vote; every eligible voter has had an equal opportunity to participate; and the decision is binding upon all members of the community. All three conditions are met. Yet the decision is not democratic in any substantive sense — it is an exercise of majoritarian tyranny that violates the fundamental rights of the minority.
missing_condition: The definition treats majority rule as sufficient for democratic legitimacy but fails to require the protection of individual and minority rights — it cannot distinguish between democratic self-governance and the tyranny of the majority.
passage: One might define a democratic decision as one determined by majority vote, with equal opportunity for participation, and binding upon the community. This characterisation captures the procedural core of democratic decision-making. Yet a case which tells against the sufficiency of majority rule may be constructed straightforwardly. Consider a town whose citizens vote, by a large majority, to confiscate the property of an unpopular ethnic minority and distribute it among the majority population. The vote is conducted fairly: every eligible voter has had an equal opportunity to participate. The decision is determined by majority vote and is binding upon all members of the community. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the decision is not democratic in any substantive sense. It is an exercise of majoritarian tyranny — the use of democratic procedures to violate the fundamental rights of a vulnerable minority. It appears that the definition, by treating majority rule as the essence of democratic decision-making, cannot distinguish between democratic self-governance and the oppression of minorities. Democracy, one might observe, requires not merely majority rule but the protection of individual and minority rights against the will of the majority.

definition: An institution I has legitimate authority if and only if (i) I was established according to recognised legal or customary procedures, (ii) those subject to I's authority have had a meaningful opportunity to participate in the process of establishing I, and (iii) I exercises its power within the limits prescribed by its founding instruments.
conditions: I was established according to recognised procedures
Those subject to I's authority participated in its establishment
I exercises power within prescribed limits
counterexample: Consider a customary court in a traditional society that adjudicates disputes according to ancestral practices. It was not established by any formal procedure or founding instrument — it emerged gradually through centuries of customary practice. Those subject to its authority never participated in a discrete establishment process, for there was no such process. Yet the court exercises legitimate authority that is recognised and respected by the community. Its authority derives from tradition, communal acceptance, and the perceived wisdom of its judgements rather than from any procedural origin.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes a discrete founding event with procedural regularity and participatory legitimacy, failing to accommodate institutions whose authority derives from tradition, long usage, and communal acceptance rather than from any identifiable act of establishment.
passage: One might define legitimate institutional authority as requiring establishment by recognised procedure, participatory opportunity, and exercise of power within prescribed limits. This analysis captures the legitimacy of modern constitutional institutions — legislatures, courts, regulatory agencies — with considerable accuracy. Yet a case which tells against the requirement of a discrete establishment process may be drawn from the authority of customary institutions. Consider a traditional court in a pre-literate society that has adjudicated disputes for generations according to ancestral practices. It was not established by any formal procedure or founding instrument. There was no founding moment at which the community participated in creating the institution; the court emerged gradually through centuries of practice, accumulating authority through the perceived wisdom and fairness of its decisions. Those subject to its authority never had an opportunity to participate in its establishment, for there was no establishment to participate in. The first two conditions are violated. Yet the court exercises legitimate authority that is recognised and respected by the entire community. It appears that the definition cannot accommodate institutions whose legitimacy derives from tradition, long usage, and communal acceptance rather than from procedural origin.

definition: A political system P is a democracy if and only if (i) the citizens of P have the right to vote in regular elections, (ii) the elections determine who holds governmental power, and (iii) all adult citizens are eligible to vote on equal terms.
conditions: The citizens of P have the right to vote in regular elections
The elections determine who holds governmental power
All adult citizens are eligible to vote on equal terms
counterexample: Suppose a nation holds regular elections in which every adult citizen may vote on equal terms, and the results of these elections determine who occupies the offices of government. Yet suppose that a single wealthy individual controls every newspaper, television station, and internet platform in the country, and uses this control to ensure that only candidates favourable to his interests receive any public exposure. The elections are held; the votes are counted; the results are respected. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the citizens' choices are so thoroughly shaped by a single private actor's monopoly on information that the elections merely ratify decisions effectively made by that individual.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that citizens have meaningful access to diverse and independent sources of information — that the conditions under which they form their political preferences be free from monopolistic control, so that voting reflects genuine choice rather than manufactured consent.
passage: One might propose that a political system is a democracy when its citizens vote in regular elections, those elections determine who holds power, and all adult citizens vote on equal terms. This characterisation captures the procedural core of democratic governance: universal suffrage, regular elections, and the binding character of electoral results. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Suppose a nation conducts elections at regular intervals — every four years, say — in which every adult citizen is entitled to vote. The ballots are secret, the counting is fair, and the results determine who occupies the offices of government. Yet suppose that a single wealthy individual owns every major newspaper, every television network, and every significant internet platform in the country. This individual uses his monopoly over the channels of public communication to ensure that only candidates favourable to his economic interests receive any significant coverage, while opposing candidates are ignored, ridiculed, or misrepresented. The citizens vote freely and on equal terms, and the elections are procedurally impeccable. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this system is a democracy in form only. The citizens' preferences have been shaped so thoroughly by a single private actor's control of information that their choices in the voting booth merely ratify decisions effectively made elsewhere. The definition, by attending only to the procedural features of elections — the right to vote, the determination of power, and equal eligibility — fails to require that the conditions under which citizens form their political judgements be conducive to genuine and independent deliberation. Without access to diverse and independent sources of information, the equal right to vote becomes the equal right to endorse a pre-selected outcome.

definition: A citizen C is free if and only if (i) C is not subject to physical restraint by another person or by the state, (ii) C is not threatened with punishment for engaging in lawful activities, and (iii) no law prohibits C from pursuing the activities that C wishes to pursue.
conditions: C is not subject to physical restraint by another person or by the state
C is not threatened with punishment for engaging in lawful activities
No law prohibits C from pursuing the activities that C wishes to pursue
counterexample: Suppose a woman lives in a society where no law restricts her from pursuing an education, no one physically restrains her, and she faces no threat of punishment for attending a university. Yet suppose that deep-seated social norms in her community dictate that women do not seek higher education, and any woman who does so is ostracised by her family and neighbours, denied employment, and excluded from all communal life. The woman is free by the proposed definition — no physical restraint, no legal prohibition, no threat of punishment. Yet her freedom to pursue an education is, in practice, as thoroughly curtailed as if it had been prohibited by law.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the constraining force of entrenched social norms and informal sanctions — forms of coercion that do not operate through physical restraint or legal prohibition but that may, in practice, be equally effective in preventing citizens from pursuing the activities they wish to pursue.
passage: One might define a citizen's freedom as the absence of physical restraint, the absence of threats of punishment for lawful activity, and the absence of legal prohibitions on the activities the citizen wishes to pursue. This characterisation captures what many have taken to be the essential conditions of liberty: that one not be chained, threatened, or legally barred from acting as one chooses. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the workings of social convention. Suppose a woman lives in a society where no law prevents her from attending a university, no one physically restrains her from doing so, and she faces no threat of legal punishment for seeking an education. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied, and she is, on this account, free. Yet suppose further that the social norms of her community dictate, with unwavering force, that women do not pursue higher education. Any woman who defies this norm is swiftly and thoroughly ostracised: her family disowns her, her neighbours refuse to speak to her, no employer in the region will hire her, and she is excluded from every form of communal life. These consequences are not enforced by law or by physical coercion; they are the product of social convention operating with the weight of unspoken but absolute authority. One might observe that the woman's freedom to pursue an education is, in practical terms, as thoroughly curtailed as if a statute had forbidden it. The definition, by restricting its attention to physical restraint, legal prohibition, and threats of punishment, overlooks the constraining force of entrenched social norms and the informal sanctions that enforce them. It provides an account of formal liberty that is consistent with a practical unfreedom so thorough as to be indistinguishable from coercion.

definition: A society S achieves political equality if and only if (i) every citizen of S possesses the same legal rights, (ii) no citizen is granted legal privileges on the basis of birth, wealth, or social standing, and (iii) the laws of S apply uniformly to all citizens.
conditions: Every citizen of S possesses the same legal rights
No citizen is granted legal privileges on the basis of birth, wealth, or social standing
The laws of S apply uniformly to all citizens
counterexample: Suppose a society grants every citizen identical legal rights, confers no privileges on the basis of birth or wealth, and applies its laws with strict uniformity. Yet suppose that access to the judicial system requires the payment of substantial fees, the engagement of costly legal counsel, and the ability to sustain proceedings over many months. A wealthy citizen can exercise her rights fully; a poor citizen, though possessing the same rights on paper, cannot afford to vindicate them. All three conditions are met. Yet the political equality is nominal rather than real, for the capacity to exercise one's rights is distributed according to economic resources that the definition does not address.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that citizens possess the practical means — the economic resources, the access to institutions, the capacity to bear the costs of legal proceedings — necessary to exercise the rights that the law formally grants them, so that equality is effective rather than merely nominal.
passage: One might propose that a society achieves political equality when every citizen possesses the same legal rights, no privileges are conferred on the basis of birth or wealth, and the laws apply uniformly. This characterisation captures the formal dimension of political equality — the idea that the law must regard all citizens alike. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the administration of justice. Suppose a society has carefully ensured that its legal code grants every citizen the same rights: the right to own property, to make contracts, to seek redress for injuries, to participate in political life. No privilege of birth, wealth, or standing is recognised in the law. The courts apply the law with scrupulous uniformity. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet suppose that access to the judicial system requires the payment of filing fees, the engagement of solicitors and barristers, and the capacity to sustain legal proceedings over many months. A wealthy citizen can afford these costs and can exercise her legal rights to the full. A citizen without means, though possessing the same rights in law, cannot afford to bring a case, cannot hire competent counsel, and cannot sustain a prolonged legal contest. Her rights exist on paper but cannot be vindicated in practice. One might observe that the equality described by the definition is nominal rather than real. The same laws apply to all, yet the capacity to make use of those laws is distributed according to economic resources that the definition does not address. It appears that the definition, by attending only to legal rights, the absence of formal privilege, and the uniform application of law, fails to require that citizens possess the practical means necessary to exercise their rights effectively.

definition: A person or institution X possesses legitimate political authority over a group G if and only if (i) X has the power to issue directives that G is expected to obey, (ii) G has consented to X's rule, and (iii) X exercises power within the limits set by the terms of G's consent.
conditions: X has the power to issue directives that G is expected to obey
X has received the consent of G to rule
X exercises power within the limits set by the terms of G's consent
counterexample: Suppose a charismatic leader persuades the citizens of a small community to consent to her rule by making extravagant promises — peace, prosperity, the resolution of all disputes — that she sincerely believes she can fulfil but in fact cannot. The citizens consent on the basis of these promises. She issues directives and exercises power within the agreed limits. All three conditions are met. Yet the consent was obtained through promises that were, though sincerely made, radically mistaken, and the citizens would not have consented had they known the truth. We should hesitate to regard such authority as fully legitimate.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the consent upon which authority rests be adequately informed — that the citizens consented on the basis of a substantially accurate understanding of the relevant facts, rather than on the basis of materially false representations, however sincerely offered.
passage: One might propose that a person or institution possesses legitimate political authority when it can issue directives, has received the consent of those over whom it rules, and exercises its power within the limits of that consent. This characterisation roots political legitimacy in the voluntary agreement of the governed and is meant to exclude tyrannical or arbitrary rule. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Suppose a charismatic individual presents herself to the citizens of a small, newly formed community as a capable leader. She promises peace, prosperity, the swift resolution of all disputes, and the establishment of institutions that will serve the common good. The citizens, impressed by her eloquence and apparent competence, consent unanimously to her rule and agree upon the limits of her authority. She issues directives and governs within those limits. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet suppose that her promises were, though sincerely offered, radically mistaken. She lacks the knowledge and ability to deliver what she has promised. The prosperity she forecast does not materialise; the peace she guaranteed depends on circumstances beyond her control; the institutions she establishes are inadequate to the challenges they face. Had the citizens known these facts, they would not have consented. One might observe that the consent upon which her authority rests was vitiated by a fundamental misunderstanding of the relevant facts. The citizens agreed to her rule on the basis of representations that, though not deliberately deceptive, were materially false. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that consent be given and that power be exercised within the consented limits, fails to require that the consent be adequately informed — that the citizens have consented on the basis of a substantially accurate understanding of the circumstances.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G came to power through a procedure recognised as lawful by the existing constitutional order, (ii) G governs in accordance with established law, and (iii) the constitutional order under which G operates was itself established through a recognised founding process.
conditions: G came to power through a procedure recognised as lawful by the existing constitutional order
G governs in accordance with established law
The constitutional order under which G operates was itself established through a recognised founding process
counterexample: Suppose a constitutional order was established through a founding process that excluded the majority of the population — say, a constitution drafted and ratified exclusively by a propertied male elite, with no input from women, the poor, or ethnic minorities. A government comes to power through the procedures specified by this constitution and governs in strict accordance with its laws. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to call this government legitimate, for the constitutional order upon which its claim rests was established through a process that systematically excluded the very people over whom the government now claims authority.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the founding process through which the constitutional order was established be inclusive — that it afford meaningful participation to those who will be subject to the resulting government, rather than merely being recognised as a founding process by those who happened to control it.
passage: One might define a legitimate government as one that comes to power through a constitutionally lawful procedure, governs in accordance with established law, and operates under a constitutional order that was itself established through a recognised founding process. This characterisation roots legitimacy in legality and constitutional continuity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of constitutional founding. Suppose a constitutional order was established in the eighteenth century through a convention of propertied gentlemen who drafted and ratified a constitution among themselves. Women were excluded from the process. The poor were excluded. Members of certain ethnic groups were excluded. The resulting constitution specified the procedures by which governments would thereafter come to power and the laws by which they would govern. Two centuries later, a government comes to power through the procedures specified in this constitution and governs with strict fidelity to its provisions. The constitutional order was established through a recognised founding process — the convention. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should hesitate to regard this government as fully legitimate. The constitutional order upon which its claim rests was established through a process that systematically excluded the majority of the population over whom the government now exercises authority. The founding was recognised as legitimate only by those who controlled it. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the founding process be recognised, fails to specify by whom it must be recognised and fails to demand that the process be inclusive — that it afford meaningful participation to those who will be subject to the resulting authority.

definition: A state S is sovereign if and only if (i) S possesses supreme authority within its territory, (ii) no external power exercises authority over S without S's consent, and (iii) S has the capacity to enforce its laws throughout its territory.
conditions: S possesses supreme authority within its territory
No external power exercises authority over S without S's consent
S has the capacity to enforce its laws throughout its territory
counterexample: Suppose a state possesses supreme authority within its borders, enforces its laws throughout its territory with perfect effectiveness, and has consented to no external authority. Yet suppose that the state's economy is entirely dependent upon the export of a single commodity, the price of which is set by a cartel of foreign nations. By adjusting the commodity price, the foreign cartel can, at will, plunge the state into economic crisis, compel changes of government, or force the adoption of unwelcome policies. The state meets all three conditions. Yet its effective independence is illusory, for its capacity to pursue its own policies is wholly contingent upon the decisions of the foreign cartel.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the state's capacity for self-governance be substantially independent of external economic coercion — that its sovereignty extend beyond formal legal supremacy and enforcement capacity to include a degree of practical autonomy in the economic conditions that underwrite its political life.
passage: One might propose that a state is sovereign when it possesses supreme authority within its territory, is free from external authority to which it has not consented, and has the capacity to enforce its laws throughout its domain. This characterisation captures the formal and coercive dimensions of sovereignty: internal supremacy and external independence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the realities of economic dependence. Suppose a state satisfies all three conditions. Its authority within its borders is unchallenged. No foreign power exercises authority over it without its consent. Its police and military enforce its laws throughout the whole of its territory. Yet the state's economy depends entirely upon the export of a single commodity — say, a mineral essential to foreign industry — and the price of this commodity is determined by a cartel of purchasing nations. By raising or lowering the price, the cartel can at will impoverish the exporting state, cause social unrest, compel changes of government, or extract policy concessions. The state has never formally consented to the cartel's authority; the cartel exercises no legal power over it. Yet the cartel's economic leverage is so complete that the state cannot pursue any significant policy contrary to the cartel's interests without courting economic ruin. One might observe that the state's sovereignty is, in practical terms, an illusion. Its formal supremacy and enforcement capacity remain intact, yet its capacity for genuine self-governance is wholly contingent upon the decisions of foreign actors. The definition, by attending only to legal authority and enforcement, fails to require that the state possess a degree of practical autonomy in the economic conditions that sustain its political life.

definition: An act A constitutes civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a deliberate violation of a law, (ii) A is performed publicly, and (iii) A is motivated by the agent's moral opposition to the law or policy being protested.
conditions: A is a deliberate violation of a law
A is performed publicly
A is motivated by the agent's moral opposition to the law or policy being protested
counterexample: Suppose a factory owner publicly violates newly enacted environmental regulations by discharging pollutants into a river. He does so deliberately and in full public view, and he declares that his action is motivated by his moral conviction that the regulations represent an unjust infringement upon individual liberty and the right to conduct business as one sees fit. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call this civil disobedience, for the act is performed not out of concern for the common good but for private economic benefit, and it inflicts direct harm upon others — the downstream communities whose water is contaminated.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the act of disobedience be constrained by a concern for the common good and an avoidance of direct harm to others — that the agent's moral motivation be directed toward justice or the public interest rather than toward private advantage, and that the means employed not inflict injury upon innocent parties.
passage: One might define civil disobedience as a deliberate, public violation of a law motivated by the agent's moral opposition to the law or policy in question. This characterisation captures the intentionality, the transparency, and the moral seriousness that distinguish civil disobedience from ordinary criminality. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the sphere of commercial regulation. Suppose a factory owner, upon the enactment of new environmental regulations that restrict the discharge of certain pollutants, publicly and deliberately violates the regulations by directing his factory's waste into a river. He makes no attempt to conceal his actions; indeed, he announces them to the press and invites scrutiny. He declares that his violation is motivated by a sincere moral conviction that the regulations constitute an unjust interference with individual liberty and the right to conduct one's business without governmental intrusion. His violation is deliberate. It is public. And it is motivated, by his own account, by moral opposition to the policy. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should hesitate to call this civil disobedience. The factory owner acts for private economic advantage under the banner of moral conviction. His violation inflicts direct and tangible harm upon downstream communities whose drinking water is contaminated. Civil disobedience, as we ordinarily understand it, involves a willingness to accept the consequences of one's violation and a concern for the welfare of the community, not the pursuit of private interest at the expense of others. The definition, by attending only to deliberateness, publicity, and moral motivation, fails to require that the act be constrained by concern for the common good and by the avoidance of direct harm to innocent parties.

definition: A citizen C possesses a right R if and only if (i) R is recognised by the legal system of C's state, (ii) C can invoke R to constrain the actions of others, and (iii) the violation of R gives rise to a legal remedy available to C.
conditions: R is recognised by the legal system of C's state
C can invoke R to constrain the actions of others
The violation of R gives rise to a legal remedy available to C
counterexample: Suppose a state has enacted a comprehensive legal code that recognises an expansive list of citizen rights, including the right to peaceful assembly. Any citizen can invoke this right to prevent others from interfering with gatherings, and the law provides remedies — injunctions, damages — for violations. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the state's security apparatus systematically surveils, intimidates, and detains citizens who organise assemblies, using extralegal means that the judiciary is powerless to check. The right exists in law and is formally enforceable, yet it cannot be exercised without retaliation that the legal system cannot prevent.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the legal system possess the effective independence and coercive capacity needed to protect the right against interference by the state's own agents — that the right be not merely formally recognised and legally remedied but practically exercisable without extralegal retaliation.
passage: One might define a citizen's right as something recognised by the legal system, invocable to constrain others, and backed by a legal remedy. This characterisation roots rights in positive law and captures the claim that a right must have institutional support. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the politics of repression. Suppose a state formally recognises an extensive catalogue of citizen rights, including the right to peaceful assembly. The right is inscribed in the constitution and elaborated in statute. Any citizen can invoke it in court to constrain those who would interfere with lawful gatherings, and the courts provide remedies — injunctions, damages, and criminal penalties for violators. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet suppose that the state's security apparatus — its intelligence services and police — systematically surveils citizens who organise assemblies, intimidates their families, and detains them on fabricated charges unrelated to the assembly itself. These extralegal measures are carried out with sufficient discretion and institutional power that the judiciary, though formally independent, is unable to check them. A citizen who exercises her right to assemble will find herself subjected to retaliation that the legal system cannot prevent or remedy. One might observe that the right to assembly exists in this state only on paper. The legal recognition is genuine, the remedy is available, and the right can be invoked — but it cannot be exercised without consequences that fall outside the reach of the legal system. The definition, by attending only to formal recognition, invocability, and legal remedy, fails to require that the institutional framework possess the effective independence and coercive capacity necessary to protect the right against interference by the state's own agents.

definition: A political decision D is democratically made if and only if (i) D is reached through a vote in which every affected citizen may participate, (ii) the vote is conducted by majority rule, and (iii) the outcome of the vote is binding upon all citizens.
conditions: D is reached through a vote in which every affected citizen may participate
The vote is conducted by majority rule
The outcome of the vote is binding upon all citizens
counterexample: Suppose a town of one thousand citizens holds a vote on whether to confiscate all property belonging to the town's fifty wealthiest residents and redistribute it equally among the remaining nine hundred and fifty. Every citizen votes; the decision is reached by majority rule; the outcome is binding. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the decision strips a minority of citizens of their property through nothing more than the numerical superiority of the majority, without any consideration of whether such a measure is just or whether the rights of the affected minority have been respected. We should hesitate to call this a democratic decision in the fullest sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that majority rule be constrained by the protection of fundamental rights — that the democratic process not be used to override the basic entitlements of a minority, no matter how large the majority that favours doing so.
passage: One might propose that a political decision is democratically made when it is reached through a vote open to all affected citizens, conducted by majority rule, and binding upon all. This characterisation captures the participatory and majoritarian dimensions of democracy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from a familiar type of political situation. Suppose a town of one thousand citizens holds a binding vote on whether to confiscate all property belonging to the fifty wealthiest residents and distribute it equally among the remaining nine hundred and fifty. Every affected citizen participates. The vote is conducted by straightforward majority rule. Six hundred citizens vote in favour; the measure passes and is binding. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should hesitate to regard this as a democratic decision in the fullest sense. The majority has used its numerical superiority to strip a minority of citizens of their property without any consideration of the justice of the measure or the rights of those affected. The fifty citizens whose property is confiscated have participated in the vote and have been outnumbered, but their fundamental entitlements have been overridden by nothing more than the arithmetic of majority rule. It appears that the definition, by requiring only participation, majority rule, and bindingness, provides no protection against the use of democratic procedures to violate the basic rights of a minority. A conception of democracy that permits the majority to do anything it pleases to the minority, provided only that the proper voting procedure is followed, seems to omit something essential.

definition: A citizen C enjoys freedom of expression if and only if (i) C may communicate their views without prior restraint by the state, (ii) C faces no criminal penalty for the content of them speech, and (iii) no governmental body exercises censorship over the channels through which C communicates.
conditions: C may communicate their views without prior restraint by the state
C faces no criminal penalty for the content of them speech
No governmental body exercises censorship over the channels through which C communicates
counterexample: Suppose a citizen lives in a state that imposes no prior restraint on speech, levies no criminal penalty for any expressed opinion, and exercises no censorship over any channel of communication. Yet the citizen's employer — a large private corporation — has adopted a policy of dismissing any employee who publicly expresses a political view contrary to the corporation's interests. Since the corporation is the dominant employer in the region, the citizen cannot speak freely without risking her livelihood. The state imposes no restraint, yet the citizen's freedom of expression is effectively curtailed by a private actor whose power the definition does not address.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that freedom of expression be protected not only from governmental restraint but also from the coercive power of private actors — employers, monopolistic platforms, economic gatekeepers — whose capacity to punish speech may be as effective as state censorship.
passage: One might define freedom of expression as the absence of prior restraint by the state, the absence of criminal penalties for the content of one's speech, and the absence of governmental censorship over the channels of communication. This characterisation captures the core of what classical liberal theory has understood by freedom of expression: the state must not silence the citizen. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the realities of economic life. Suppose a citizen lives in a state that imposes no prior restraint upon speech, prescribes no criminal penalty for any opinion, and exercises no censorship over any medium of communication. The citizen is, by the proposed definition, entirely free to express her views. Yet suppose her employer — a large private corporation that dominates the local economy — has adopted a policy of dismissing any employee who publicly expresses political views contrary to the corporation's commercial interests. The citizen depends upon her employment for her livelihood and that of her family. She knows that if she speaks her mind on a range of political questions, she will lose her position, and no comparable employment is available in the region. The state has done nothing to restrict her speech. No governmental body has censored or punished her. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the citizen's freedom of expression has been as effectively curtailed as if the state itself had imposed a prohibition. The coercive power that silences her emanates from a private actor rather than from the government, but its practical effect is indistinguishable from state censorship. The definition, by attending only to state action, fails to address the capacity of powerful private actors to suppress expression through economic coercion.

definition: A political system S is a democracy if and only if (i) all adult citizens of S have the right to vote, (ii) political offices are filled by competitive elections, and (iii) the government of S acts in accordance with the will of the majority.
conditions: All adult citizens of S have the right to vote
Political offices are filled by competitive elections
The government of S acts in accordance with the will of the majority
counterexample: Suppose a state grants all adult citizens the right to vote and fills its offices by competitive elections. The majority of the electorate, animated by ethnic hostility, votes to disenfranchise a religious minority, to confiscate their property, and to expel them from the territory. The government, faithfully reflecting the will of the majority, enacts these measures. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: universal suffrage obtains, elections are competitive, and the government acts in accordance with the majority's will. Yet we should hesitate to regard this as a genuine democracy, for the system has used majoritarian procedures to destroy the very conditions of equal citizenship upon which democratic governance depends.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the democratic process be constrained by the protection of fundamental rights and liberties, such that the will of the majority cannot be exercised to destroy the equal standing of any citizen or group. Without this condition, majoritarian tyranny satisfies the definition while violating the spirit of democratic governance.
passage: One might propose that a democracy is a political system in which all adult citizens have the right to vote, political offices are filled by competitive elections, and the government acts in accordance with the will of the majority. This characterisation captures the participatory, competitive, and majoritarian elements commonly associated with democratic governance. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be imagined as follows. Suppose a state satisfies all three conditions: every adult citizen may vote, elections are vigorously contested, and the government faithfully enacts the preferences of the electoral majority. The majority, however, animated by ethnic hostility, votes to disenfranchise a religious minority, to confiscate their property, and to expel them from the territory. The government, acting precisely in accordance with the majority's will, carries out these measures with scrupulous adherence to electoral mandates. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet one might observe that this is not a genuine democracy but a tyranny of the majority — a system in which majoritarian procedures are employed to destroy the equal standing of a portion of the citizenry. It appears that the definition, by requiring only universal suffrage, competitive elections, and majority rule, fails to demand that the democratic process be constrained by the protection of fundamental rights. Without such a constraint, the will of the majority may be exercised to undermine the very conditions of equal citizenship upon which democratic legitimacy depends. One is led to conclude that a further condition is required, ensuring that democratic governance cannot be turned against the rights and liberties of any citizen or group.

definition: A citizen C is free if and only if (i) C is not subject to physical coercion or restraint by the state or by other persons, (ii) C is permitted by law to pursue any course of action not explicitly prohibited, and (iii) no legal penalty attaches to C's expression of opinion.
conditions: C is not subject to physical coercion or restraint by the state or by other persons
C is permitted by law to pursue any course of action not explicitly prohibited
No legal penalty attaches to C's expression of opinion
counterexample: Suppose a citizen lives in a state where no physical coercion is applied, where the law permits any action not explicitly prohibited, and where no legal penalty attaches to the expression of opinion. Yet the citizen is born into extreme poverty, with no access to education, health care, or the material resources needed to pursue any of the legally permitted courses of action. The law permits him to start a business, to travel, to stand for office — but he lacks the means to do any of these things. All three conditions are met: he suffers no coercion, faces no legal prohibition, and may express his opinions freely. Yet his freedom is, in a significant sense, merely formal; the absence of material means renders it empty.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the citizen possess the material conditions — education, health, economic resources — necessary to exercise the liberties that the law formally grants. Without such a condition, the analysis confuses the mere absence of legal and physical constraint with the genuine capacity to act freely.
passage: One might characterise a free citizen as one who is not subject to physical coercion, who is legally permitted to pursue any course of action not explicitly prohibited, and upon whom no legal penalty is imposed for the expression of opinion. This formulation captures what many have taken to be the essential elements of political freedom: the absence of force, the breadth of legal permission, and the protection of speech. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the circumstances of poverty. Suppose a citizen lives under a regime that satisfies all three conditions admirably. No agent of the state lays a hand upon him; the law is generous in the range of actions it permits; and he may speak his mind on any subject without fear of prosecution. Yet the citizen was born into destitution. He has no access to education, no means of securing adequate nourishment or medical treatment, and no capital with which to avail himself of the opportunities the law formally provides. The law permits him to travel, but he cannot afford passage; it permits him to stand for public office, but he lacks the resources to mount a campaign; it permits him to engage in commerce, but he has nothing to trade. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied, yet his freedom is, in a most significant sense, merely nominal. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the absence of coercion and legal restriction, fails to require that the citizen possess the material conditions necessary to exercise the liberties the law affords. One is led to conclude that freedom, properly understood, demands not merely the absence of constraint but the presence of the resources needed to act upon one's legal permissions.

definition: A society S achieves political equality if and only if (i) every citizen of S has one and only one vote of equal weight, (ii) every citizen is eligible for public office, and (iii) the laws of S apply uniformly to all citizens without distinction.
conditions: Every citizen of S has one and only one vote of equal weight
Every citizen is eligible for public office
The laws of S apply uniformly to all citizens without distinction
counterexample: Suppose a society satisfies all three conditions: every citizen has one equally weighted vote, every citizen is eligible for public office, and the laws apply uniformly. Yet a small number of citizens possess vast fortunes, which they use to fund political campaigns, control the principal organs of public information, and lobby officials. These citizens thereby exercise an influence over political outcomes that is enormously disproportionate to their single votes. The remaining citizens, though legally equal in every respect, find their preferences systematically overridden by the political power that wealth confers. All three conditions of the definition are met, yet political equality in any substantive sense has not been achieved.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that citizens possess roughly comparable capacities to influence political outcomes in practice, not merely equal formal entitlements. A condition addressing the distribution of resources that affect political influence — wealth, access to information, organisational capacity — is needed.
passage: One might define political equality as obtaining in a society where every citizen possesses one equally weighted vote, where every citizen is eligible for public office, and where the laws apply uniformly to all without distinction. This formulation captures the formal features most closely associated with the ideal of political equality: equal suffrage, equal eligibility, and equal legal treatment. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be imagined as follows. Suppose a society satisfies each of the three conditions. Every citizen has a single vote, and no vote carries more weight than any other. Every citizen, regardless of birth or station, is legally eligible to hold any public office. The laws are drafted and enforced without distinction among persons. Yet a small number of citizens possess vast personal fortunes, which they employ to fund political campaigns, to acquire ownership of the principal organs of public communication, and to retain advocates who lobby public officials on their behalf. These citizens thereby exercise a degree of influence over political outcomes that is enormously disproportionate to the single vote each of them formally possesses. The remaining citizens, though they enjoy every formal entitlement stipulated by the definition, find their political preferences routinely overridden by the concentrated power of private wealth. It appears that the definition, by attending only to formal entitlements — equal votes, equal eligibility, uniform laws — fails to require that citizens possess roughly comparable capacities to influence political outcomes in fact. One is led to conclude that political equality demands not merely the formal equality of legal rights but some measure of substantive equality in the resources that determine political influence.

definition: A person or body P possesses political authority over a group G if and only if (i) P's directives are generally obeyed by the members of G, (ii) P occupies an office or role that is recognised by the members of G as carrying the right to issue directives, and (iii) P's directives are backed by the capacity to impose sanctions for disobedience.
conditions: P's directives are generally obeyed by the members of G
P occupies an office or role recognised by the members of G as carrying the right to issue directives
P's directives are backed by the capacity to impose sanctions for disobedience
counterexample: Suppose a military commander seizes control of a government by force. The populace, having no realistic prospect of resistance, generally obeys his directives. He installs himself in the presidential office and, through a combination of propaganda and intimidation, secures widespread acknowledgement that the occupant of that office has the right to issue commands. His directives are backed by a formidable capacity for sanctions: imprisonment, confiscation of property, and worse. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this commander does not possess genuine political authority; his position rests upon coercion rather than upon any legitimate entitlement to rule, and the recognition he secures is extracted by fear rather than freely given.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the recognition of P's right to issue directives be given freely and without coercion — that the acknowledgement of authority arise from the genuine consent or considered judgement of the members of G, rather than from fear or the absence of any realistic alternative.
passage: One might propose that a person or body possesses political authority over a group when its directives are generally obeyed, when it occupies a role recognised as carrying the right to command, and when its directives are backed by the capacity for sanctions. This characterisation captures the behavioural compliance, institutional recognition, and coercive capacity that we commonly associate with political authority. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of coups d'etat. Suppose a military commander seizes control of a government by force, dismisses the elected officials, and installs himself in the presidential office. The populace, confronted with armoured vehicles in the streets and soldiers at every corner, generally obeys his directives; resistance would be futile and dangerous. Through sustained propaganda and the suppression of dissent, the commander secures widespread acknowledgement that the occupant of the presidential office possesses the right to issue commands. His capacity for sanctions is beyond question: imprisonment, confiscation of property, and summary punishment await those who disobey. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: directives are obeyed, the office is recognised, and sanctions are available. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to say that this commander possesses genuine political authority. His position rests upon coercion, and the recognition he enjoys is extracted by fear rather than given freely. It appears that the definition, by requiring only general obedience, recognised office, and coercive capacity, fails to demand that the recognition of authority be freely conferred. One is led to conclude that a further condition is needed: the acknowledgement of the right to rule must arise from the genuine consent or considered judgement of the governed, not from intimidation.
