definition: A government G is politically legitimate if and only if (i) G came to power through procedures accepted by the political community, (ii) G governs in accordance with established law, and (iii) G enjoys the general acquiescence of the population.
conditions: G came to power through procedures accepted by the political community
G governs in accordance with established law
G enjoys the general acquiescence of the population
counterexample: Suppose a colonial power establishes a government over a foreign territory, following procedures accepted within the colonial administrative system. The government administers the territory in strict accordance with the colonial legal code. The indigenous population, lacking the military or organisational resources to resist, generally acquiesces. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to regard this government as politically legitimate, for the procedures by which it came to power were not accepted by the governed population but imposed upon them, and the acquiescence of the people reflects powerlessness rather than endorsement.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the procedures by which G came to power be accepted by those over whom G governs, rather than merely by some external political community, and that the acquiescence of the population be genuine and voluntary rather than the product of powerlessness or the absence of any viable alternative.
passage: One might characterise political legitimacy as obtaining when a government came to power through accepted procedures, governs in accordance with established law, and enjoys the general acquiescence of the population. This formulation captures the procedural, legal, and popular dimensions of legitimate governance. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of colonial rule. Suppose a European power establishes a government over a distant territory, following the administrative and diplomatic procedures accepted within the colonial system — the signing of treaties, the appointment of officials, the promulgation of a legal code. The colonial government administers the territory in strict conformity with the laws it has established. The indigenous population, lacking the military capacity or organisational resources to mount effective resistance, generally acquiesces in the arrangements imposed upon them. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: the government came to power through procedures accepted by the relevant political community, it governs according to established law, and it enjoys general acquiescence. Nevertheless, one might observe that this government is not politically legitimate in any defensible sense. The procedures by which it assumed power were accepted by the colonial power, not by the people it governs. The acquiescence of the indigenous population reflects not endorsement but the absence of any realistic alternative. It appears that the definition, by failing to specify that the accepted procedures must be accepted by the governed and that the acquiescence must be voluntary, cannot distinguish between legitimate governance and the imposition of power by an external force upon a subject people.

definition: An act A constitutes civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a deliberate violation of a law, (ii) A is performed publicly, and (iii) A is motivated by the agent's moral or political convictions.
conditions: A is a deliberate violation of a law
A is performed publicly
A is motivated by the agent's moral or political convictions
counterexample: Suppose a man, animated by the sincere moral conviction that taxation is theft, publicly refuses to pay his taxes and announces his refusal in the newspapers. He is prepared to evade any attempt to collect the tax and has hidden his assets to prevent seizure. His act is a deliberate violation of the tax code, performed publicly, and motivated by deep moral conviction. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call this civil disobedience, for the man does not accept the legal consequences of his violation; he evades and resists rather than submitting to the authority of the legal system he challenges. His act is closer to open defiance or resistance than to civil disobedience in its proper sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent willingly accept the legal consequences of their violation — that they submit to arrest, trial, and punishment — thereby demonstrating their continued respect for the legal order even as they challenge a particular law. Without this condition, the definition cannot distinguish civil disobedience from mere lawbreaking driven by conviction.
passage: One might define civil disobedience as the deliberate, public violation of a law motivated by moral or political conviction. This characterisation captures the intentionality, transparency, and principled motivation that we commonly associate with acts of civil disobedience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Suppose a man holds the sincere moral conviction that taxation constitutes a form of theft and that compliance with the tax code is morally impermissible. Acting upon this conviction, he publicly refuses to pay his taxes, publishing a letter in the newspapers explaining his reasons. His act is deliberate: he knows exactly which law he violates and does so with full awareness. It is public: he takes pains to ensure that his refusal is widely known. And it is motivated by deep moral conviction: he regards the tax system as fundamentally unjust. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to describe his act as civil disobedience, for the man has taken elaborate steps to hide his assets and evade any attempt by the authorities to collect the tax. He is prepared to flee the jurisdiction rather than submit to legal proceedings. He does not accept the legal consequences of his violation; he resists them. It appears that the definition, by requiring only deliberateness, publicity, and moral motivation, fails to demand that the agent willingly accept the penalties imposed by the legal system he challenges. One is led to conclude that a further condition is essential: the civilly disobedient agent must submit to the legal consequences of his act, thereby demonstrating his respect for the rule of law even as he contests a particular statute.

definition: A citizen C possesses a political right R if and only if (i) R is explicitly guaranteed by the constitution or fundamental law of C's state, (ii) C can invoke R in a court of law to obtain redress for its violation, and (iii) the state's institutions are structured so as to protect R.
conditions: R is explicitly guaranteed by the constitution or fundamental law of C's state
C can invoke R in a court of law to obtain redress for its violation
The state's institutions are structured so as to protect R
counterexample: Consider the situation of citizens in a state whose constitution explicitly guarantees freedom of assembly, whose courts formally recognise the right and permit its invocation, and whose police force is nominally charged with protecting assemblies. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the courts are thoroughly corrupt and the police routinely disperse peaceful gatherings on fabricated pretexts, and that no citizen who invokes the right of assembly in court ever receives genuine redress. The constitutional guarantee exists, the formal mechanisms of invocation exist, and the institutional structures nominally devoted to protection exist — yet the right is empty, for none of these formal provisions functions as intended.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the constitutional guarantee, the judicial mechanism, and the institutional protections be effective in practice — that they actually function to secure the right, rather than merely existing on paper. A condition distinguishing formal or nominal guarantees from substantive, functioning protections is needed.
passage: One might propose that a citizen possesses a political right when that right is explicitly guaranteed by the constitution, when the citizen may invoke it in court, and when the state's institutions are structured to protect it. This characterisation captures the formal, justiciable, and institutional dimensions that we associate with genuine rights. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the practice of nominally constitutional states. Suppose a state's constitution explicitly guarantees freedom of assembly. The courts formally recognise the right and permit citizens to file suits alleging its violation. The police force is nominally charged with protecting peaceful assemblies from disruption. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet suppose the courts are thoroughly corrupt: judges are appointed by the ruling party, decisions are dictated by political considerations, and no citizen who invokes the right of assembly ever receives genuine redress. The police, meanwhile, routinely disperse peaceful gatherings on fabricated pretexts, arresting organisers under vague public-order statutes. The constitutional guarantee exists on paper, the formal mechanism of judicial invocation exists, and the institutional structure of police protection exists — but none of these provisions functions as intended. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the formal existence of guarantees, judicial mechanisms, and protective institutions, fails to demand that these provisions be effective in practice. One is led to conclude that a political right, to be genuinely possessed, must be not merely formally guaranteed but substantively protected — that the institutions charged with its defence must actually function to secure it.

definition: A state S is sovereign if and only if (i) S exercises supreme authority over a defined territory, (ii) no external power has the right to intervene in S's internal affairs, and (iii) S is recognised as a state by other states in the international community.
conditions: S exercises supreme authority over a defined territory
No external power has the right to intervene in S's internal affairs
S is recognised as a state by other states in the international community
counterexample: Suppose a state is universally recognised by the international community, exercises supreme authority over its territory, and no external power claims the right to intervene in its internal affairs. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the state has entered into a binding international treaty that subjects it to the jurisdiction of an international court, which may override its domestic legislation on matters of human rights. The state voluntarily accepted this constraint, but the effect is that an external body now possesses the authority to invalidate the state's own laws. The second condition appears to be violated in spirit, though the state's consent might be taken to preserve its sovereignty. The case reveals that the definition cannot clearly accommodate situations in which a state freely cedes elements of its supreme authority to international bodies.
missing_condition: The definition fails to address the distinction between sovereignty that is unlimited and sovereignty that is voluntarily constrained by international commitments. A condition specifying whether sovereignty requires absolute freedom from external authority or is compatible with consensual limitation is needed.
passage: One might define sovereignty as obtaining when a state exercises supreme authority over a defined territory, when no external power has the right to intervene in its internal affairs, and when it is recognised by other states. This formulation captures the territorial, non-interventionist, and recognitional dimensions of sovereignty as traditionally understood. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the practice of international treaty-making. Suppose a state is universally recognised, exercises supreme authority within its borders, and enjoys freedom from external intervention. It then enters into a binding international treaty establishing a court with the authority to review and, if necessary, invalidate domestic legislation that contravenes certain human rights standards. The state accepted this arrangement voluntarily, by the free act of its own legislature. Yet the practical effect is that an external body now possesses the power to override the state's laws. If a citizen appeals to the international court and the court rules against the state, the state is obligated to amend its legislation. The second condition of the proposed definition — that no external power have the right to intervene — appears to be compromised, for an external judicial body now exercises a form of authority over internal affairs. Yet the state entered the arrangement of its own free will. It appears that the definition cannot determine whether the state remains sovereign: if sovereignty requires absolute freedom from external authority, the state has lost it; if sovereignty is compatible with voluntary self-limitation, the definition's second condition is too strict. One is led to conclude that the analysis must address the relationship between sovereignty and consensual constraint.

definition: A right R is a natural right if and only if (i) R belongs to every human being regardless of the laws of any particular state, (ii) R is not conferred by any social institution or convention, and (iii) R is discoverable by reason alone, without recourse to revelation or tradition.
conditions: R belongs to every human being regardless of the laws of any particular state
R is not conferred by any social institution or convention
R is discoverable by reason alone
counterexample: Suppose one considers the right to property. Some philosophers have maintained that the right to property belongs to every human being regardless of positive law, that it is not conferred by any social institution, and that it is discoverable by reason. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet the specific content of the right to property — what counts as property, how it may be acquired, what uses are permissible, how it may be transferred — depends entirely upon social institutions and conventions. In a society without conventions of ownership, the right to property has no determinate content. The right, considered abstractly, may satisfy the conditions, but it cannot be exercised or even fully specified without the very social institutions and conventions that the definition excludes as sources.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between the abstract existence of a right and its determinate content, neglecting the fact that some rights, even if they exist independently of institutions in principle, require social conventions for their specification and exercise. A condition addressing the relationship between the right's existence and its institutional articulation is needed.
passage: One might define a natural right as one that belongs to every human being regardless of the laws of any particular state, that is not conferred by social institutions, and that is discoverable by reason alone. This characterisation captures the universality, non-conventional origin, and rational accessibility traditionally ascribed to natural rights. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the philosophy of property. Some thinkers have maintained that the right to property is a natural right: it belongs to every human being by virtue of his rational nature, it is not created by any convention, and it is discoverable through the exercise of reason. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Yet one might observe that the right to property, considered in its concrete exercise, depends entirely upon social institutions and conventions. What counts as property, how ownership is established, what uses of property are permissible, how it may be transferred or forfeited — all of these determinations require the very conventions that the definition excludes as sources of the right. In a community wholly without conventions of ownership, the right to property has no determinate content; it is an abstraction without practical application. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the right not be conferred by social institutions, cannot accommodate rights whose abstract existence may be non-conventional but whose specific content and exercise depend upon institutional arrangements. One is led to conclude that the analysis must address the distinction between the existence of a right and the conditions necessary for its articulation and exercise.

definition: A political system S is a democracy if and only if (i) the holders of political office in S are chosen through periodic elections in which all adult citizens are eligible to vote, (ii) the decisions of office-holders are subject to public deliberation and criticism, and (iii) political power in S is exercised in accordance with the will of the majority.
conditions: Holders of political office are chosen through periodic elections in which all adult citizens are eligible to vote
Decisions of office-holders are subject to public deliberation and criticism
Political power is exercised in accordance with the will of the majority
counterexample: Suppose a state holds periodic elections in which all adult citizens are eligible to vote. The decisions of office-holders are openly deliberated and criticised in a free press. Political power is exercised in strict accordance with the will of the majority. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the majority, having elected its representatives, proceeds to enact legislation that strips a religious minority of its civil liberties, confiscates its property, and bars its members from public employment. The system functions precisely as the definition requires, yet the result is a form of tyranny exercised by the majority against a vulnerable minority.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the exercise of majority power be constrained by protections for the fundamental rights of minorities, so that the democratic process cannot be used as an instrument of systematic oppression against those who find themselves outnumbered.
passage: One might define democracy as a political system in which office-holders are chosen through periodic elections open to all adult citizens, in which decisions are subject to public deliberation and criticism, and in which political power is exercised in accordance with the will of the majority. This characterisation captures the electoral, deliberative, and majoritarian features that we ordinarily associate with democratic governance. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of majoritarian excess. Suppose a state holds periodic elections in which all adult citizens vote freely, the decisions of office-holders are openly debated and criticised in a vigorous press, and political power is exercised in strict accordance with the majority's will. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, the majority, having elected its representatives, proceeds to enact legislation that strips a religious minority of its civil liberties, confiscates its property, and bars its members from public employment. The system operates precisely as the definition requires, yet the outcome is a form of tyranny exercised by the many against the few. It appears that the definition, by requiring only elections, deliberation, and majority rule, fails to demand that the exercise of majority power be constrained by protections for the fundamental rights of minorities. Without such protections, democracy becomes indistinguishable from majoritarian despotism, a result that we should be reluctant to accept.

definition: A citizen C is free in a political society if and only if (i) C is not subject to coercion by the state or by other citizens in the exercise of C's lawful activities, (ii) C has the legal right to pursue any course of action not prohibited by law, and (iii) the laws to which C is subject have been enacted through procedures in which C had the opportunity to participate.
conditions: C is not subject to coercion by the state or by other citizens in the exercise of lawful activities
C has the legal right to pursue any course of action not prohibited by law
The laws to which C is subject have been enacted through procedures in which C had the opportunity to participate
counterexample: Suppose a citizen is not coerced by the state or by other citizens, has the legal right to pursue any lawful course of action, and is subject only to laws enacted through procedures in which she participated. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the citizen lives in grinding poverty, unable to afford food, shelter, or medical care. She possesses formal legal rights to do as she pleases, but her material circumstances render these rights entirely notional. She is free in the legal sense but unable to exercise her freedom in any substantive way.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that C possess the material resources or conditions necessary for the effective exercise of the formal rights and legal permissions that the definition guarantees, so that freedom is not merely a legal abstraction but a practical reality.
passage: One might analyse political freedom as the absence of coercion in the exercise of lawful activities, the legal right to pursue any course of action not prohibited by law, and subjection only to laws enacted through participatory procedures. This analysis captures the negative, permissive, and procedural dimensions of freedom that are central to the liberal tradition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the conditions of material deprivation. Suppose a citizen is entirely free from coercion, possesses the legal right to pursue any lawful activity, and is subject only to laws enacted through procedures in which she participated. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, the citizen lives in grinding poverty, unable to afford food, shelter, or medical care. She possesses the formal legal right to travel, to seek employment, to educate herself, and to participate in public life, but her material circumstances render every one of these rights entirely notional. She is free in the legal sense yet unable to exercise her freedom in any substantive way. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the absence of coercion, legal permission, and participatory legislation, neglects the material conditions necessary for the effective exercise of formal rights. Without these conditions, freedom remains a mere legal abstraction, available in principle but inaccessible in practice.

definition: Citizens of a political society enjoy equality if and only if (i) they possess the same legal rights and are subject to the same legal obligations, (ii) they have equal access to public offices and positions of influence, and (iii) the state treats them with equal concern and respect regardless of their social characteristics.
conditions: Citizens possess the same legal rights and are subject to the same legal obligations
Citizens have equal access to public offices and positions of influence
The state treats citizens with equal concern and respect regardless of social characteristics
counterexample: Suppose a society grants all citizens identical legal rights and obligations, provides formally equal access to public offices, and treats all citizens with ostensibly equal concern and respect. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the society permits vast inherited wealth to accumulate without restraint, so that children of wealthy families enjoy private education, extensive social networks, and financial security that effectively guarantee their success in competing for public offices, whilst children of poor families cannot afford the preparation necessary to compete. The formal equality of access conceals a substantive inequality of opportunity produced by the unregulated transmission of economic advantage.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the social and economic conditions under which citizens compete for offices and positions of influence be sufficiently fair that formal equality of access translates into genuine equality of opportunity, rather than being undermined by inherited advantages that the state permits to persist.
passage: One might propose that political equality consists in identical legal rights and obligations, equal access to public offices, and treatment by the state with equal concern and respect. This characterisation captures the legal, institutional, and attitudinal dimensions of equality that are most prominent in the political tradition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the effects of inherited wealth. Suppose a society grants all citizens identical legal rights and obligations, provides formally equal access to all public offices, and treats every citizen with ostensible equal concern. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, the society permits vast inherited wealth to accumulate without restraint. Children of wealthy families enjoy private education, extensive social connections, and financial security that effectively guarantee their success in competing for public positions, whilst children of poor families cannot afford the preparation necessary to compete on equal terms. The formal equality of access masks a substantive inequality of opportunity produced by the unregulated transmission of economic advantage. It appears that the definition, by requiring only legal equality, equal access, and equal state concern, neglects the social and economic conditions that determine whether formal equality translates into genuine equality of opportunity. Without attention to these conditions, the analysis declares equality where inequality is most deeply entrenched.

definition: An agent A possesses legitimate political authority over subjects B if and only if (i) A has been invested with the power to issue binding directives through a procedure recognised as valid by the political community, (ii) A exercises this power within the limits prescribed by the constitutional framework, and (iii) the subjects B have consented, either explicitly or tacitly, to A's authority.
conditions: A has been invested with power to issue binding directives through a procedure recognised as valid by the political community
A exercises this power within the limits prescribed by the constitutional framework
The subjects B have consented either explicitly or tacitly to A's authority
counterexample: Suppose a colonial governor has been invested with power to issue binding directives through procedures recognised as valid by the colonising nation's political community. He exercises this power within the limits prescribed by the colonial constitution. And the colonised subjects have tacitly consented, in the sense that they have not revolted and have generally complied with the governor's directives. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the colonised population had no voice in establishing the procedures that invested the governor with power, no role in framing the constitutional framework, and their compliance reflects not genuine consent but the practical impossibility of effective resistance. The authority is legitimate according to the definition but manifestly illegitimate by any reasonable moral standard.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the procedures through which A is invested with authority and the constitutional framework within which A operates be themselves the product of a process in which the subjects B had a genuine and effective voice, so that consent is not merely tacit acquiescence under conditions of subjugation.
passage: One might define legitimate political authority as obtaining when an agent has been invested with the power to issue binding directives through a recognised procedure, exercises that power within constitutional limits, and governs subjects who have consented to the arrangement. This characterisation captures the procedural validity, constitutional constraint, and consensual basis that we associate with legitimate rule. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of colonial governance. Suppose a colonial governor has been invested with directive power through procedures recognised as valid by the colonising nation's political community. He exercises his authority within the bounds of the colonial constitution. And the colonised population has tacitly consented, in the sense that they have not revolted and have generally complied with the governor's directives. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this authority as legitimate. The colonised subjects had no voice in establishing the procedures that invested the governor with power, no role in framing the constitutional framework, and their compliance reflects not genuine consent but the practical impossibility of effective resistance against a militarily superior power. It appears that the definition, by requiring only procedural investiture, constitutional constraint, and consent, fails to demand that the procedures and constitutional framework themselves be the product of a process in which the subjects had a genuine and effective voice. Without this requirement, the definition confers legitimacy upon arrangements that are, by any reasonable moral standard, instruments of subjugation.

definition: A political regime R is legitimate if and only if (i) R came to power through a process that was not vitiated by fraud or violence, (ii) R governs in accordance with publicly promulgated laws, and (iii) the citizens under R's jurisdiction generally acquiesce in R's rule.
conditions: R came to power through a process not vitiated by fraud or violence
R governs in accordance with publicly promulgated laws
The citizens under R's jurisdiction generally acquiesce in R's rule
counterexample: Suppose a regime comes to power through a process free from fraud and violence, governs in strict accordance with publicly promulgated laws, and enjoys the general acquiescence of its citizens. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the citizens acquiesce only because the regime controls all channels of information, presenting itself as the sole viable form of government and systematically distorting the citizens' understanding of political alternatives. The acquiescence is manufactured by the regime's monopoly on information rather than being the product of genuine assessment and voluntary acceptance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the citizens' acquiescence be informed and unmanipulated, arising from conditions in which they have access to a plurality of viewpoints and are free to form their political judgements without systematic distortion by the regime itself.
passage: One might propose that a political regime is legitimate when it came to power without fraud or violence, governs by publicly promulgated laws, and enjoys the general acquiescence of its citizens. This characterisation captures the procedural integrity, rule of law, and popular acceptance that we associate with legitimate governance. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenon of information control. Suppose a regime ascends to power through a process entirely free from fraud and violence, governs in strict accordance with laws that are publicly promulgated and consistently applied, and enjoys the broad acquiescence of the populace. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the citizens acquiesce only because the regime controls every channel of information, presenting itself as the sole viable form of government and systematically distorting the citizens' understanding of political alternatives. The acquiescence is not the product of genuine assessment and voluntary acceptance but is manufactured by the regime's monopoly on information. It appears that the definition, by requiring only procedural integrity, lawful governance, and general acquiescence, fails to demand that the acquiescence be informed and unmanipulated. Without the requirement that citizens have access to a plurality of viewpoints and freedom to form their political judgements without systematic distortion, the definition confers legitimacy upon regimes that maintain their position through the engineering of consent.

definition: An act A constitutes civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a deliberate and public violation of a law, (ii) A is motivated by a sincere moral or political conviction that the law violated is unjust, and (iii) the agent who performs A willingly accepts the legal consequences of the violation.
conditions: A is a deliberate and public violation of a law
A is motivated by a sincere moral or political conviction that the law violated is unjust
The agent willingly accepts the legal consequences of the violation
counterexample: Suppose a group of citizens deliberately and publicly violates a law requiring the registration of printing presses, motivated by the sincere conviction that this registration law is unjust because it enables censorship. The citizens willingly accept arrest and prosecution. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the law the citizens violate is not the law they regard as unjust; they violate the registration law as a means of protesting a different statute that restricts the content of published material. The violated law is merely a convenient vehicle for the protest, not itself the object of moral objection. The definition, which requires that the violated law be the one judged unjust, cannot accommodate this common form of indirect civil disobedience.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for indirect civil disobedience, in which the law deliberately violated is not itself the law judged to be unjust but is violated as a means of drawing attention to a different unjust law or policy, so that the moral conviction and the legal violation need not have the same object.
passage: One might define civil disobedience as a deliberate and public violation of a law, motivated by a sincere conviction that the violated law is unjust, with willing acceptance of the legal consequences. This characterisation captures the deliberateness, publicity, moral seriousness, and legal accountability that we associate with the tradition of civil disobedience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the practice of indirect protest. Suppose a group of citizens deliberately and publicly violates a law requiring the registration of printing presses, motivated by the sincere conviction that this registration requirement facilitates censorship. The citizens willingly accept arrest and prosecution. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the registration law is not itself the law the citizens regard as unjust. They violate it as a means of protesting a different statute that restricts the content of published material. The registration law is merely a convenient vehicle for the demonstration, not the object of their moral objection. The definition, by requiring that the violated law be the one judged unjust, cannot accommodate this common and historically important form of indirect civil disobedience, in which the law broken and the law protested are distinct. It appears that the analysis, without provision for this indirectness, excludes a significant class of acts that we should wish to count as genuine civil disobedience.

definition: A citizen C possesses a right R in a political society if and only if (i) there exists a law or constitutional provision that recognises R as belonging to C, (ii) there is an identifiable party or institution that has a corresponding duty to respect or fulfil R, and (iii) C has access to a legal mechanism for the enforcement of R.
conditions: There exists a law or constitutional provision recognising R as belonging to C
There is an identifiable party or institution with a corresponding duty to respect or fulfil R
C has access to a legal mechanism for the enforcement of R
counterexample: Suppose a constitution recognises the right of every citizen to a fair trial. The judiciary has a clearly identified duty to provide fair trials. And citizens have access to an appellate system through which the denial of a fair trial can be challenged. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the judiciary is so thoroughly corrupt that no fair trial has been conducted in the country for a generation. Every citizen possesses the right in the formal sense required by the definition, yet no citizen can in practice obtain what the right promises. The right exists on paper but is entirely without substance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the institutions charged with respecting and enforcing R function with sufficient integrity and competence to make the right effective in practice, so that a right is not merely a formal legal entitlement but a guarantee that has real force in the lives of those who hold it.
passage: One might propose that a citizen possesses a right when the right is recognised by law, a corresponding duty-bearer is identifiable, and a legal mechanism for enforcement exists. This characterisation captures the legal recognition, correlative duty, and enforceability that are central to the concept of a right. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenon of institutional corruption. Suppose a constitution recognises the right of every citizen to a fair trial. The judiciary is clearly identified as the institution charged with the duty of providing fair trials. And citizens have access to an appellate system through which the denial of a fair trial may be challenged. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the judiciary is so thoroughly corrupt that no fair trial has been conducted in the country for a generation. Judges accept bribes, proceedings are predetermined, and the appellate system merely ratifies the decisions of the lower courts. Every citizen possesses the right in the formal sense that the definition requires, yet no citizen can in practice obtain what the right promises. It appears that the definition, by requiring only legal recognition, an identifiable duty-bearer, and access to an enforcement mechanism, fails to demand that the institutions charged with respecting and enforcing the right function with sufficient integrity to make it effective. Without this requirement, the analysis confers the status of a genuine right upon what is, in reality, no more than a hollow legal formality.

definition: A state S is sovereign if and only if (i) S exercises supreme authority within its territory, such that no domestic body has the power to override S's decisions, (ii) S is recognised by other states as an independent political entity, and (iii) S has the capacity to enter into binding agreements with other states.
conditions: S exercises supreme authority within its territory such that no domestic body can override its decisions
S is recognised by other states as an independent political entity
S has the capacity to enter into binding agreements with other states
counterexample: Suppose a small island state exercises supreme authority within its territory, is recognised by the international community as an independent entity, and regularly enters into binding treaties with other states. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the island state is entirely dependent upon a neighbouring great power for its military defence, its economic survival, and the enforcement of its own laws. The great power stations troops on the island, controls its trade routes, and can at any time withdraw its support, rendering the island state incapable of maintaining order. The island state satisfies the formal criteria of sovereignty yet is, in substance, a dependency of the great power.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S possess a degree of effective independence sufficient to exercise its authority and fulfil its agreements without existential reliance upon the support of another state, so that sovereignty is not merely formal recognition but reflects genuine political self-sufficiency.
passage: One might define sovereignty as the exercise of supreme domestic authority, recognition by other states, and the capacity to enter into binding international agreements. This characterisation captures the internal supremacy, external recognition, and treaty-making power that are traditionally attributed to sovereign states. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenon of dependent statehood. Suppose a small island state exercises supreme authority within its territory, is recognised by the international community as an independent political entity, and regularly concludes binding treaties with other states. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, the island state is entirely dependent upon a neighbouring great power for its military defence, its economic survival, and even the enforcement of its own domestic laws. The great power stations troops on the island, controls its principal trade routes, and can withdraw its support at any time, rendering the island state incapable of maintaining internal order. The island state is sovereign in the formal sense that the definition requires, yet it is, in substance, a dependency of the great power. It appears that the definition, by requiring only domestic supremacy, international recognition, and treaty capacity, neglects the requirement that the state possess effective independence, a degree of political and material self-sufficiency that enables it to exercise its authority without existential reliance upon another state's continued goodwill.

definition: A natural right R belongs to all human beings if and only if (i) R is not conferred by any government or legal system but is inherent in human nature, (ii) R is universal, applying to all persons regardless of nationality, culture, or historical period, and (iii) R is inalienable, in the sense that it cannot be surrendered or taken away.
conditions: R is not conferred by any government or legal system but is inherent in human nature
R is universal applying to all persons regardless of nationality culture or historical period
R is inalienable in the sense that it cannot be surrendered or taken away
counterexample: Suppose the right to own property is declared a natural right. It is said to be inherent in human nature, universal across all cultures and historical periods, and inalienable. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet in certain historical periods and cultural contexts, the very concept of private ownership of land is absent from the society's conceptual repertoire. Nomadic communities that move across territories without recognising fixed claims on land do not violate a right to property; the concept simply has no application in their form of life. A right that presupposes institutional arrangements peculiar to certain forms of social organisation cannot plausibly be described as inherent in human nature across all cultures and periods.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that R be specifiable without reference to institutional arrangements that are not universally present in all human societies, so that the right truly inheres in human nature rather than in the contingent social structures that give it content.
passage: One might propose that a natural right is one that is inherent in human nature rather than conferred by any government, is universal across all persons regardless of culture or period, and is inalienable. This characterisation captures the pre-political, universal, and indefeasible character that the natural-rights tradition attributes to fundamental rights. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the right to property. Suppose the right to own property is declared to be a natural right. It is said to be inherent in human nature, universal, and inalienable, satisfying all three conditions. Nevertheless, one might observe that in certain historical periods and cultural contexts, the very concept of private ownership of land is absent from the society's conceptual repertoire. Nomadic communities that traverse territories without recognising fixed claims upon the land do not violate a right to property; the concept simply has no application within their form of life. A right whose content presupposes institutional arrangements peculiar to certain modes of social organisation, such as settled agriculture and legal systems of title, cannot plausibly be described as inherent in human nature across all cultures and periods. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the right be inherent, universal, and inalienable, fails to require that the right be specifiable without reference to institutional arrangements that are not universally present. Without this condition, the analysis risks elevating the contingent products of particular social structures to the status of natural features of the human condition.

definition: A political system is a democracy if and only if (i) political authority derives from the consent of the governed, (ii) decisions are reached by majority vote, and (iii) all adult citizens have an equal right to participate in the decision-making process.
conditions: political authority derives from the consent of the governed
decisions are reached by majority vote
all adult citizens have an equal right to participate in the decision-making process
counterexample: Suppose that in a certain state all three conditions are satisfied: the government rules by consent, decisions are made by majority vote, and every adult citizen may participate equally. Yet the majority consistently votes to strip a linguistic minority of its property and civil standing. The system meets every condition of the proposed definition and yet operates in a manner that most would regard as incompatible with genuine democratic governance, for the majority exercises its power without any constraint upon the oppression of the few.
missing_condition: The definition omits any requirement that democratic decision-making be subject to constraints protecting the fundamental rights of minorities against the unchecked will of the majority.
passage: It is commonly held that a political system qualifies as democratic when authority springs from the consent of the governed, decisions are taken by majority vote, and every adult citizen enjoys an equal right of participation. These conditions appear, at first inspection, to exhaust what we mean by the term. Yet one may construct a case which reveals a significant gap in this analysis. Let us imagine a state in which all three conditions are impeccably satisfied. The citizens have freely consented to the constitutional order; decisions are reached through fair and open balloting in which each citizen's vote carries equal weight; and participation is universal among the adult population. Suppose, however, that the majority, exercising its power in perfect accordance with the prescribed procedures, repeatedly votes to confiscate the property of a small linguistic minority and to exclude its members from civic life. The system remains, by the lights of the definition, a democracy in good standing, for none of the three conditions has been violated. Yet it appears that most reflective observers would hesitate to call such a regime democratic in any robust sense, for the very procedures of consent and majority rule have been turned to the oppression of a vulnerable group. We are thus led to conclude that the definition is incomplete: a satisfactory account of democracy must include some provision for the protection of fundamental rights against majoritarian encroachment.

definition: A person is free if and only if (i) they are not subject to physical coercion by another agent, (ii) they are not subject to the threat of punishment for acting on their choices, and (iii) no legal prohibition prevents their from pursuing their chosen course of action.
conditions: they are not subject to physical coercion by another agent
they are not subject to the threat of punishment for acting on their choices
no legal prohibition prevents their from pursuing their chosen course of action
counterexample: Suppose that a destitute labourer faces no physical coercion, no threat of punishment, and no legal prohibition against leaving her place of employment. Yet her circumstances are such that departure would mean starvation for her family. She satisfies all three conditions and yet cannot, in any meaningful sense, be said to be free to leave, for economic necessity constrains her as effectively as any chain or statute.
missing_condition: The definition attends only to external legal and physical impediments to action, neglecting the role of material and economic conditions that may constrain a person's freedom as effectively as coercion or legal prohibition.
passage: One prominent analysis of freedom holds that a person is free when she suffers no physical coercion from another agent, faces no threat of punishment for her chosen conduct, and encounters no legal prohibition upon her intended course of action. The definition has a certain attractive simplicity, restricting attention to clearly identifiable impediments — force, threat, and law — and pronouncing the agent free whenever these are absent. Yet we should examine whether the absence of such external obstacles truly suffices for freedom. Let us imagine a labourer employed in harsh conditions at a meagre wage. No person coerces her physically; she is threatened with no punishment should she choose to leave; and the law places no bar upon her departure. In every respect the three conditions are met. Yet her circumstances are such that to leave would be to condemn her children to hunger, for she possesses no savings and no alternative employment is available. It appears that she is not free to leave in any sense that matters, notwithstanding the satisfaction of the formal conditions. We are brought to recognise that freedom may be constrained not merely by force, threat, and law but by the material conditions of a person's existence, and that a satisfactory philosophical analysis of freedom must take account of such economic and social impediments.

definition: A society is egalitarian if and only if (i) all citizens possess the same legal rights, (ii) no citizen is accorded privilege on the basis of birth or hereditary status, and (iii) public offices are open to all citizens on equal terms.
conditions: all citizens possess the same legal rights
no citizen is accorded privilege on the basis of birth or hereditary status
public offices are open to all citizens on equal terms
counterexample: Consider a case in which a state grants identical legal rights to every citizen, confers no hereditary privilege, and opens all public offices without restriction. Yet the distribution of wealth is so extreme that a small class of the very rich can, as a practical matter, monopolise access to education, legal representation, and political candidacy, while the poor, though formally entitled to the same offices, cannot realistically compete. All three conditions are satisfied, and yet one would hesitate to describe the resulting society as genuinely egalitarian.
missing_condition: The definition addresses only formal legal equality and ignores the material conditions — such as wealth, education, and social capital — that may render formal equality practically hollow.
passage: It is often proposed that a society may be called egalitarian when it accords identical legal rights to all its citizens, confers no privilege by birth or hereditary standing, and opens every public office on equal terms. So defined, the concept appears to capture the core of what we mean by political equality. Yet we should ask whether these conditions are truly sufficient. Let us imagine a commonwealth in which the constitution is scrupulously observed: every citizen enjoys the same rights before the law, hereditary titles have been abolished, and any citizen may stand for any public office. Suppose, however, that the distribution of wealth within this commonwealth is profoundly unequal. A small number of families command resources so vast that they alone can afford the education, the legal counsel, and the political campaigns necessary to exercise their formal entitlements effectively. The poor, though legally entitled to stand for office, find the practical obstacles insuperable. It appears that the three conditions are fully met and yet the society fails to be egalitarian in any substantive sense. We are led to conclude that formal legal equality, unaided by some measure of material equality, is insufficient to ground a genuine egalitarianism, and that the analysis must be broadened to encompass the social and economic conditions under which formal rights are exercised.

definition: A person or institution possesses political authority if and only if (i) the subjects have consented to be governed by that person or institution, (ii) the person or institution issues directives that the subjects recognise as binding, and (iii) the person or institution has the power to enforce compliance.
conditions: the subjects have consented to be governed by that person or institution
the person or institution issues directives that the subjects recognise as binding
the person or institution has the power to enforce compliance
counterexample: Suppose that in a remote district the central government possesses the formal consent of the populace (given at the founding of the state), issues laws that the citizens nominally recognise as binding, and maintains a police force with the power to enforce compliance. Yet the district is in practice governed by a local elder to whom the inhabitants defer in all matters of dispute, though he holds no official position, commands no enforcement apparatus, and has received no formal consent. The elder appears to possess a form of genuine authority that the central government, despite satisfying all three conditions, effectively lacks in that community.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the authority be actually effective — that subjects in practice defer to the directives in question — and thus cannot distinguish between nominal authority and genuine authority.
passage: It has been widely maintained that political authority rests upon three pillars: the consent of the governed, the issuance of directives recognised as binding, and the capacity to enforce compliance. When these conditions converge, we are told, the person or institution in question possesses genuine authority over the relevant subjects. The analysis has an appealing clarity, and in many ordinary cases it serves well enough. Yet one may construct a case that exposes its limitations. Let us imagine a remote district within a large state. The central government was established with the consent of the populace at the state's founding; it issues laws that citizens formally acknowledge as binding; and it maintains a body of constables with the power to enforce obedience. Yet the inhabitants of this district, in all practical matters — the resolution of disputes, the allocation of common resources, the regulation of daily life — defer not to the central government but to a village elder. The elder holds no office, has no enforcement apparatus at his disposal, and has never received any formal act of consent. Nevertheless, his word is treated as authoritative, and the directives of the distant government are largely ignored. It appears that the elder possesses real authority in that community, while the central government, though satisfying every condition of the definition, does not. We are thus led to recognise that authority involves not merely formal consent, recognition, and enforcement but actual practical effectiveness in guiding conduct.

definition: A government is legitimate if and only if (i) it was established through procedures accepted by the governed, (ii) it acts within the limits set by its founding charter or constitution, and (iii) it serves the common good of the populace.
conditions: it was established through procedures accepted by the governed
it acts within the limits set by its founding charter or constitution
it serves the common good of the populace
counterexample: Suppose that a military junta seizes power without any procedural acceptance by the governed and in flagrant violation of the existing constitution. Yet the junta proceeds to govern wisely, serving the common good with conspicuous success — ending a famine, establishing public order, and improving the material conditions of the population. The third condition is satisfied but the first two are not, and yet the populace, far from rejecting the junta, comes to regard it as a more legitimate authority than the corrupt elected government it replaced. One would hesitate to say that legitimacy is wholly absent here.
missing_condition: The definition treats procedural establishment and constitutional fidelity as necessary conditions, failing to account for cases in which a government acquires practical legitimacy through effective governance and popular acceptance despite extra-constitutional origins.
passage: A familiar analysis of governmental legitimacy holds that a government is legitimate when it was established through accepted procedures, operates within its constitutional bounds, and serves the common good. The conditions appear to capture the intuitions both of those who stress procedural correctness and of those who emphasise substantive outcomes. Yet one may devise a scenario in which they come apart. Let us imagine a state plagued by corruption, in which the constitutionally established government has so thoroughly neglected the welfare of the populace that famine and disorder prevail. A group of military officers, moved by the suffering they witness, seizes power in a coup d'état. The new regime has no procedural warrant — the populace was never consulted — and its assumption of authority violates the existing constitution in every particular. Yet the junta governs with remarkable competence: the famine is ended, public services are restored, and the material conditions of the citizenry improve markedly. Over time, the populace comes to accept the junta as its rightful authority, and its directives are obeyed with a willingness that the former government could never command. The first two conditions are unmet, yet there is a strong intuitive case that the junta possesses a form of legitimacy — one grounded in effective service and popular acceptance rather than in procedure and parchment. We are thus led to conclude that the proposed definition is too narrow and that legitimacy may arise from sources that the analysis, as stated, does not acknowledge.

definition: A state is sovereign if and only if (i) it possesses supreme authority over its territory, (ii) no external power exercises jurisdiction within its borders, and (iii) it has the capacity to enter into relations with other states as an independent entity.
conditions: it possesses supreme authority over its territory
no external power exercises jurisdiction within its borders
it has the capacity to enter into relations with other states as an independent entity
counterexample: Suppose that a small nation signs a treaty permitting a neighbouring power to station troops on its soil and to exercise criminal jurisdiction over those troops. An external power now exercises a form of jurisdiction within the nation's borders, violating the second condition. Yet the nation retains supreme authority in all other respects, conducts its own foreign policy, and is universally recognised as a sovereign state. One would hesitate to deny its sovereignty on the ground that it has consented to a limited foreign jurisdiction.
missing_condition: The definition treats any external jurisdiction within a state's borders as incompatible with sovereignty, failing to distinguish between imposed foreign domination and voluntarily ceded jurisdictional rights that leave the state's general authority intact.
passage: The traditional analysis of sovereignty identifies three conditions: supreme authority over territory, the absence of external jurisdiction within the state's borders, and the capacity to conduct independent foreign relations. This tripartite formulation has long served as the standard account and appears to correspond to what we ordinarily mean when we call a state sovereign. Yet one may construct a case that calls the second condition into question. Let us imagine a small but independent nation that enters into a treaty with a larger neighbour, permitting the latter to station a military garrison upon its soil and to exercise criminal jurisdiction over the garrisoned troops. The treaty is freely negotiated and may be renounced at any time. In every other respect the small nation governs itself: it levies taxes, administers justice over its own citizens, and conducts diplomatic relations with states around the world. The second condition — that no external power exercises jurisdiction within its borders — is plainly unsatisfied, for the neighbour's military courts operate on the nation's territory. Yet it appears that the small nation remains sovereign in every meaningful sense; it has not been subjected to foreign domination but has merely consented, revocably, to a limited jurisdictional arrangement. We are thus brought to recognise that the second condition, as stated, is too stringent, and that a more nuanced analysis must distinguish between the imposition of foreign authority and the voluntary concession of limited jurisdictional rights.

definition: An act is one of civil disobedience if and only if (i) it is a deliberate violation of law, (ii) it is performed publicly and openly, and (iii) the agent accepts the legal consequences of the violation.
conditions: it is a deliberate violation of law
it is performed publicly and openly
the agent accepts the legal consequences of the violation
counterexample: Suppose that a group of citizens, convinced that a particular censorship law is unjust, secretly distributes prohibited literature through an underground network. Their act is a deliberate violation of law and they are prepared to accept punishment if caught. Yet they operate in secrecy because public action would result in the immediate suppression of the literature and the imprisonment of its authors before the message could reach the public. The second condition — publicity — is unsatisfied, yet the act has every hallmark of civil disobedience: it is conscientious, law-breaking, and aimed at challenging an unjust statute.
missing_condition: The definition requires publicity as a necessary condition, failing to accommodate clandestine acts of principled lawbreaking undertaken in circumstances where openness would defeat the very moral purpose of the disobedience.
passage: A widely endorsed analysis of civil disobedience holds that it consists in three elements: a deliberate breach of law, performed in the public eye, by an agent who willingly accepts the legal penalties that follow. The requirement of publicity is thought to distinguish civil disobedience from mere criminality, for it signals the agent's moral earnestness and invites public deliberation upon the justice of the law in question. Yet we should examine whether publicity is truly a necessary condition. Let us imagine a group of citizens living under a regime that has enacted a stringent censorship law, prohibiting the distribution of certain works of political philosophy. The citizens are persuaded that the law is gravely unjust. They wish to challenge it, but they recognise that any public act of distribution would be crushed immediately — the literature confiscated, its distributors imprisoned, and its intended readers left unenlightened. Accordingly, they establish a secret network through which the prohibited texts are quietly passed from hand to hand. Their action is deliberate, conscientious, and law-breaking; they are prepared to accept punishment if discovered. Yet the action is not public, for circumstances make publicity self-defeating. It appears that the act possesses the moral character of civil disobedience despite its clandestine nature. We are led to conclude that the insistence upon publicity, though appropriate in many contexts, is not a necessary condition and that a more adequate analysis must accommodate principled lawbreaking undertaken in secrecy when openness would frustrate its moral aims.

definition: A person has a right to X if and only if (i) there exists a corresponding duty on the part of others to respect or provide X, (ii) the person can waive the right at their discretion, and (iii) the right is recognised by the legal or moral framework governing the community.
conditions: there exists a corresponding duty on the part of others to respect or provide X
the person can waive the right at their discretion
the right is recognised by the legal or moral framework governing the community
counterexample: Suppose that a person has a right not to be enslaved. There is a corresponding duty on others not to enslave her, and the right is recognised by the prevailing moral and legal framework. Yet one would be reluctant to say that she can waive this right at her discretion — that she can consent to her own enslavement and thereby release others from the duty not to enslave her. The right appears to be inalienable, incapable of being waived, and yet it is plainly a right. The second condition fails.
missing_condition: The definition treats waivability as a necessary condition of all rights, failing to account for inalienable rights that the right-holder cannot surrender, transfer, or waive even by voluntary consent.
passage: A familiar analysis of rights holds that a person has a right to some good or liberty when others bear a corresponding duty to respect or provide it, the person may waive the right at her discretion, and the right is recognised within the governing legal or moral framework. The first and third conditions capture the correlativity of rights and duties and the social embeddedness of rights, respectively; the second is intended to mark the right-holder's authority over the right. Yet the second condition raises a difficulty that the analysis does not resolve. Let us imagine the right not to be enslaved — a right that most moral and legal traditions recognise as fundamental. Others bear a clear duty not to enslave the right-holder, and the right is enshrined in law and morality alike. The first and third conditions are satisfied. But can the right-holder waive this right? Can she, by a voluntary act of consent, authorise another to enslave her and thereby release that person from the duty? Most considered judgements would say not: the right against enslavement is inalienable, meaning that it cannot be surrendered, transferred, or waived even by the willing consent of the right-holder. If this is correct, then the second condition fails in the case of inalienable rights. We are thus led to conclude that the proposed analysis is too narrow, for it excludes from the category of rights precisely those that are most fundamental — those which the holder cannot choose to relinquish.
